<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**  
Define dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their SI units and values for air at standard sea-level conditions.

**Model Answer:**  
Dynamic viscosity (μ) is the property of a fluid that quantifies its internal resistance to shearing deformation. Mathematically, it is the ratio of shear stress to the velocity gradient perpendicular to the flow direction:

$$ \mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy} $$

SI unit: **Pa·s** or **kg/(m·s)**. For air at 15 °C, μ = 1.789 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s.

Kinematic viscosity (ν) is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to density:

$$ \nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} $$

SI unit: **m²/s**. For air at sea level, ν = 1.46 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition of μ, 1 mark for ν, 1 mark for units and values)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
State and explain the Ideal Gas Law as applied to air. Why is it relevant in vehicle aerodynamics?

**Model Answer:**  
The ideal gas law relates pressure, density, and temperature of a gas:

$$ p = \rho R T $$

where p = absolute pressure [Pa], ρ = density [kg/m³], R = specific gas constant for air = **287 J/(kg·K)**, T = absolute temperature [K].

**Relevance:** Vehicle aerodynamic analyses (especially CFD simulations and wind tunnel calibrations) require accurate density values, which depend on local temperature and pressure. The ideal gas law allows engineers to compute density at any altitude or test condition. It also forms the basis of the speed of sound formula a = √(γRT), which determines compressibility effects at high vehicle speeds.

*(3 marks — 1 mark formula, 1 mark values, 1 mark relevance)*

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**  
Derive the relationship between the speed of sound in a fluid and its Bulk Modulus. Hence, obtain the expression a = √(γRT) for an ideal gas.

*(7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Equation of motion of a fluid element:**  
Consider a fluid element of cross-section A and thickness dx. Applying Newton's second law:

$$ \rho A dx \cdot \frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial t^2} = -A \, dP $$

**Step 2 — Definition of Bulk Modulus:**  
For a small compression, the volumetric strain is dV/V = ∂ξ/∂x. Therefore:

$$ dP = -K \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial x} $$

**Step 3 — Substituting:**

$$ \rho \frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial t^2} = K \frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial x^2} $$

**Step 4 — Comparing with the standard wave equation** ∂²ξ/∂t² = c² ∂²ξ/∂x²:

$$ a = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}} $$

**Step 5 — For adiabatic process in ideal gas:**  
K = γp. Substituting in the ideal gas law p = ρRT:

$$ a = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma p}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\gamma R T} $$

**Numerical Verification:**  
At T = 300 K, a = √(1.4 × 287 × 300) = √120,540 = **347.19 m/s** ✓

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**  
Air at 25 °C flows over a car moving at 120 km/h. Calculate:  
(i) Dynamic pressure, (ii) Mach number, (iii) Reynolds number based on a characteristic length of 2.5 m.  
Given: ρ = 1.184 kg/m³, μ = 1.849 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s, γ = 1.4, R = 287 J/(kg·K).

*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- T = 25 °C = 298.15 K
- V = 120 km/h = 120 × (1000/3600) = **33.33 m/s**
- L = 2.5 m
- ρ = 1.184 kg/m³
- μ = 1.849 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s

**(i) Dynamic Pressure:**

$$
q = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = \frac{1}{2}(1.184)(33.33)^2
$$

$$
q = \frac{1}{2}(1.184)(1111.09) = 657.77 \text{ Pa}
$$

**(ii) Mach Number:**  
First compute speed of sound:

$$
a = \sqrt{\gamma R T} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 298.15}
$$

$$
a = \sqrt{119{,}815.67} = 346.15 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
M = \frac{V}{a} = \frac{33.33}{346.15} = 0.0963
$$

Since M < 0.3, the flow can be treated as **incompressible**.

**(iii) Reynolds Number:**

$$
Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{1.184 \times 33.33 \times 2.5}{1.849 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
Re = \frac{98.66}{1.849 \times 10^{-5}} = 5.336 \times 10^6
$$

This is a **high Reynolds number**, fully turbulent flow regime — typical for full-scale automobiles.

*(7 marks — 2 marks each for (i) and (ii), 3 marks for (iii) including interpretation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
Define Mach number and explain its significance in classifying flow regimes. A car travels at 240 km/h at an altitude where the temperature is −10 °C. Determine the Mach number. Comment on whether compressibility effects must be considered.

*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Mach number is the ratio of the flow velocity to the local speed of sound:

$$ M = \frac{V}{a} $$

**Classification of Flow Regimes:**
- **Incompressible:** M < 0.3  
- **Subsonic:** 0.3 ≤ M < 0.85  
- **Transonic:** 0.85 ≤ M ≤ 1.2  
- **Supersonic:** M > 1.2  
- **Hypersonic:** M > 5

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: V = 240 km/h = 66.67 m/s, T = −10 °C = 263.15 K

Speed of sound:

$$
a = \sqrt{\gamma R T} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 263.15}
$$

$$
a = \sqrt{105{,}713.47} = 325.14 \text{ m/s}
$$

Mach number:

$$
M = \frac{66.67}{325.14} = 0.205
$$

**Comment:** Since M = 0.205 < 0.3, the flow regime is **incompressible**, and compressibility effects can be safely **neglected**. Standard aerodynamic equations (using constant density) remain valid.

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
Using Sutherland's Law, calculate the dynamic viscosity of air at 50 °C. Comment on how viscosity changes with altitude during a hill-climb race.

*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Sutherland's Law:

$$
\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}
$$

**Given:**
- T = 50 °C = 323.15 K
- T₀ = 273.15 K
- μ₀ = 1.716 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s
- S = 110.4 K

**Step 1 — Compute (T/T₀)^(3/2):**

$$
\left(\frac{323.15}{273.15}\right)^{3/2} = (1.1831)^{1.5} = 1.2868
$$

**Step 2 — Compute (T₀ + S)/(T + S):**

$$
\frac{273.15 + 110.4}{323.15 + 110.4} = \frac{383.55}{433.55} = 0.8847
$$

**Step 3 — Compute μ:**

$$
\mu = 1.716 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.2868 \times 0.8847
$$

$$
\mu = 1.716 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.1385 = 1.954 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}
$$

**Comment on Altitude Effects:**  
During a hill-climb, as altitude increases:
- Temperature decreases (lapse rate ≈ −6.5 K/km)
- Density decreases (ρ ∝ p/RT)
- Dynamic viscosity μ **decreases** (because T drops)
- Kinematic viscosity ν = μ/ρ **increases dramatically** (ρ drops faster than μ)

This means the **boundary layer thickens** at altitude, increasing form drag and friction drag, while reducing Reynolds number — which can shift the flow regime and alter aerodynamic balance, a critical consideration for racing engineers.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert temperature to Kelvin** — a single °C slip-up leads to a wrong Mach number and zero credit on full sub-parts.
> 2. **Using wrong R value** — always use **R = 287 J/(kg·K) for air**, not the universal gas constant 8.314.
> 3. **Mixing up dynamic and kinematic viscosity** — ν is a derived property, not an independent one. Marks lost when units are swapped.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** — in derivations, always specify "incompressible," "steady," and "adiabatic" where applicable.
> 5. **Skipping units** — KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for missing SI units in final answers.
> 6. **Sutherland's Law formula error** — students often forget the +S term in the denominator, leading to a 30% error in μ.
> 7. **Velocity conversion error** — 120 km/h ≠ 120 m/s. Always convert km/h to m/s by multiplying by **5/18**.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Density (ρ)** of air at sea level = **1.225 kg/m³**; decreases with altitude.
- ✅ **Dynamic viscosity (μ)** of air at 15 °C = **1.789 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s**; **increases** with temperature.
- ✅ **Kinematic viscosity (ν)** = μ/ρ = **1.46 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s**; **increases** with altitude.
- ✅ **Speed of sound** a = √(γRT); depends **only on temperature**, not pressure.
- ✅ **Mach number** M = V/a; flow is **incompressible if M < 0.3**.
- ✅ **Ideal gas law** p = ρRT with **R = 287 J/(kg·K)** for air.
- ✅ **Bulk Modulus** K = γp for adiabatic processes; K = p for isothermal.
- ✅ **Dynamic pressure** q = ½ρV²; the pressure a moving car "feels" from oncoming air.
- ✅ **Reynolds number** Re = ρVL/μ; high Re means turbulent flow (cars usually have Re > 10⁶).
- ✅ **Specific gas constant** R is **NOT** the universal gas constant R_u = 8.314 J/(mol·K).
- ✅ **Sutherland's constant** S = **110.4 K** — memorize this.
- ✅ **Continuity equation** for incompressible flow: A₁V₁ = A₂V₂.
- ✅ **ISA sea-level values:** p₀ = 101,325 Pa; T₀ = 288.15 K; ρ₀ = 1.225 kg/m³; a₀ = 340.29 m/s.
- ✅ **γ (gamma) for air** = **1.4** (ratio of specific heats, C_p/C_v).
- ✅ Always **convert temperature to Kelvin** before using in any gas law formula.
- ✅ Always **convert km/h to m/s** (multiply by 5/18) before velocity calculations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forces in a Fluid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Forces in a Fluid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌀 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📘 Formal Academic Definition

In continuum mechanics and fluid dynamics, **Forces in a Fluid** are the various internal and external loads that act on a fluid element or on a body immersed in a moving/stationary fluid. These forces govern the acceleration, deformation, pressure distribution, and equilibrium state of the fluid mass.

For analytical convenience, all fluid forces are mathematically decomposed into two fundamental categories:

1. **Body Forces (Volumetric Forces):** Forces that act on the entire volume of the fluid element. They are proportional to the mass (or volume) of the element. The SI unit is **N/m³** (force per unit volume) or **N/kg** (force per unit mass).
2. **Surface Forces (Contact Forces):** Forces that act on the exposed surfaces of the fluid element. They are proportional to the surface area. The SI unit is **N/m² = Pa (Pascal)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PEAUT752 / Module 1**
> The classification of fluid forces into **body forces** and **surface forces**, along with the formal introduction of the **stress tensor** (normal stress = pressure, tangential stress = viscous shear), forms the foundational prerequisite for deriving the **Navier-Stokes equations** and the **Euler equations** in subsequent modules of Vehicle Aerodynamics.

---

## 🌊 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge in a Bathtub"

Imagine you take a small cube-shaped sponge and hold it underwater in a bathtub.

- The **weight of the sponge** (pulled downward by Earth) is a **body force** — it acts on the *entire mass* of the sponge, no matter how big or small you cut it.
- The **water pressing inward on every face** of the sponge is a **pressure force** (a normal surface force) — if you squeeze the sponge, water pushes back on each face perpendicular to the surface.
- The **sticky, dragging feel** as you try to slide the sponge through the water is the **viscous shear force** (a tangential surface force) — this is the friction between water layers sliding past each other.

Together, these three forces (gravity, pressure, viscosity) plus the **inertia** of the fluid element completely describe how the fluid moves. For a **road vehicle moving through air**, the analogous "bathtub" is the surrounding atmosphere, and the "sponge" is any parcel of air the vehicle disturbs.

---

## 🧭 Taxonomy of Forces in a Fluid

| S.No. | Force Type | Category | Mathematical Symbol | Acts On | Common Examples |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Pressure Force | Surface (Normal) | $p$ | Faces of element | Hydrostatic thrust, aerodynamic pressure |
| 2 | Viscous Shear Force | Surface (Tangential) | $\tau$ | Faces of element | Skin friction drag, pipe flow friction |
| 3 | Gravity Force | Body | $\rho \vec{g}$ | Volume of element | Hydrostatic pressure variation |
| 4 | Inertia Force | Apparent (D'Alembert) | $\rho \vec{a}$ | Volume of element | Virtual force in accelerating frames |
| 5 | Buoyancy Force | Body (resultant) | $\rho_f V \vec{g}$ | Immersed body | Uplift on submerged car |
| 6 | Electromagnetic Force | Body | $\rho_e \vec{E}$ | Volume of element | MHD flows, plasma aerodynamics |
| 7 | Centrifugal/Coriolis Force | Body (rotating frame) | $\rho \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r})$ | Volume of element | Rotating machinery, turbomachinery |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric & Constants Used in This Module**
> - Density of air (sea level, 15°C): $\rho = \mathbf{1.225 \; kg/m^3}$
> - Dynamic viscosity of air (15°C): $\mu = \mathbf{1.81 \times 10^{-5} \; kg/(m \cdot s)}$
> - Kinematic viscosity of air: $\nu = \mu / \rho = \mathbf{1.46 \times 10^{-5} \; m^2/s}$
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: $p_{atm} = \mathbf{101325 \; Pa}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## ⚙️ I. Body Forces — Forces Distributed Over the Fluid Mass

Body forces are those that act on every infinitesimal volume element of the fluid, with the magnitude proportional to the mass (or volume) of that element. They **do not require physical contact** with a bounding surface.

### (a) Gravity Force
The most ubiquitous body force on Earth. For a fluid element of volume $dV$ and density $\rho$:

$$d\vec{F}_g = \rho \vec{g} \, dV$$

For a fluid of mass $m$:

$$\vec{F}_g = m \vec{g}$$

**Why it matters in vehicle aerodynamics:** Gravity establishes the **hydrostatic pressure gradient** in still air ($\partial p / \partial z = -\rho g$), which becomes significant for tall vehicles (buses, trucks) where the bottom and roof experience different static pressures.

### (b) Electromagnetic Force (Lorentz Force)
Relevant only in **magnetohydrodynamic (MHD)** flows or ionized gases:

$$d\vec{F}_{em} = \rho_e (\vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \, dV$$

where $\rho_e$ is the charge density, $\vec{E}$ the electric field, and $\vec{B}$ the magnetic flux density.

### (c) Rotational Pseudo-Forces (Centrifugal & Coriolis)
Appearing in **non-inertial rotating reference frames** (e.g., studying flow inside a rotating turbocharger):

$$\vec{F}_{cf} = -\rho \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}) \quad ; \quad \vec{F}_{cor} = -2\rho \vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}$$

---

## ⚙️ II. Surface Forces — Forces Acting on the Boundary of the Element

These forces require a **physical bounding surface** and are characterized by a **stress** (force per unit area). The complete description requires the **Cauchy stress tensor** $\sigma_{ij}$.

### (a) Normal Stress — Pressure Force
The pressure $p$ is the **compressive normal stress** that a fluid exerts **perpendicular** to any imaginary surface. It is defined as:

$$p = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F_n}{\Delta A}$$

A fluid at rest cannot sustain shear — it can only push or pull normally. **Pascal's Law** (1653) states:

> *The pressure at a point in a static fluid is the same in all directions (isotropic).*

**Hydrostatic Pressure Variation** (in a fluid of constant density $\rho$):

$$p_2 - p_1 = -\rho g (z_2 - z_1) \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{dp}{dz} = -\rho g$$

**Dynamic Pressure** (kinetic energy per unit volume of a moving fluid):

$$q = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2$$

This is the cornerstone quantity for defining the **pressure coefficient** in vehicle aerodynamics:

$$C_p = \frac{p - p_\infty}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2}$$

### (b) Tangential Stress — Viscous Shear Force
Unlike pressure, the viscous shear stress $\tau$ acts **parallel (tangential)** to the surface. For a **Newtonian fluid**, the shear stress is proportional to the velocity gradient (Newton's Law of Viscosity, 1687):

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \dot{\gamma}$$

where $\mu$ is the **dynamic viscosity** $[\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}]$ and $\dot{\gamma}$ is the **shear rate** $[\text{s}^{-1}]$. For air, $\mu$ is typically $\sim 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$ at 15°C.

**Generalized Newtonian form (3D):**

$$\tau_{ij} = \mu \left( \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i} \right) \quad \text{with} \quad i \neq j$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Shear Stress Matters in Vehicle Aerodynamics:** The viscous shear stress integrated over the entire wetted surface of a car gives the **skin friction drag** — a dominant component of total aerodynamic drag for streamlined cars (especially at low speeds, $C_D \approx 0.25$).

---

## ⚙️ III. The Generalized Stress Tensor

A complete mathematical description of surface forces in a fluid element is provided by the **Cauchy stress tensor** $\sigma_{ij}$ (a 2nd-order tensor, 9 components):

$$\sigma_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_{zz} \end{bmatrix} = -p \delta_{ij} + \tau_{ij}$$

where:
- $\sigma_{ii}$ = normal stress components
- $\tau_{ij}$ = tangential (shear) stress components
- $\delta_{ij}$ = **Kronecker delta** ($\delta_{ij} = 1$ if $i=j$, else $0$)
- The **negative sign** convention indicates compression (pressure pushes inward).

For a **static fluid**: $\tau_{ij} = 0$, and $\sigma_{ij} = -p \delta_{ij}$ (perfectly isotropic).
For an **inviscid (frictionless) fluid**: $\tau_{ij} = 0$, but $p$ varies with position and velocity (Bernoulli's theorem applies).
For a **viscous Newtonian fluid**: full stress tensor is active.

---

## ⚙️ IV. The Resultant Force on a Fluid Element

The net force on a fluid element of volume $dV = dx \, dy \, dz$ is the **sum of body forces and the net surface traction**:

$$d\vec{F} = \rho \vec{f}_b \, dV + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma} \, dV$$

where $\vec{f}_b$ is the body force per unit mass, and $\nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma}$ is the divergence of the stress tensor.

Applying Newton's Second Law ($\sum \vec{F} = m \vec{a}$):

$$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \rho \vec{f}_b + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma}$$

This compact form is the **Cauchy momentum equation** — the parent of all fluid motion equations (Euler, Navier-Stokes, etc.).

---

## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Name | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $dF_g = \rho g \, dV$ | Gravity body force | Weight of fluid element | N |
| 2 | $p = F/A$ | Pressure | Normal force per area | Pa |
| 3 | $dp/dz = -\rho g$ | Hydrostatic eqn. | Pressure variation with depth | Pa/m |
| 4 | $p_2 = p_1 + \rho g h$ | Manometer eqn. | Pressure under fluid column | Pa |
| 5 | $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ | Dynamic pressure | Kinetic energy / volume | Pa |
| 6 | $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ | Newton's viscosity law | Shear stress = viscosity × strain rate | Pa |
| 7 | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | Kinematic viscosity | Momentum diffusivity | m²/s |
| 8 | $\sigma_{ij} = -p\delta_{ij} + \tau_{ij}$ | Cauchy stress tensor | Complete surface force state | Pa |
| 9 | $C_p = (p - p_\infty)/q$ | Pressure coefficient | Normalized pressure difference | — |
| 10 | $Re = \rho V L / \mu$ | Reynolds number | Inertia $\div$ Viscous | — |
| 11 | $Eu = p / (\rho V^2)$ | Euler number | Pressure $\div$ Inertia | — |
| 12 | $Fr = V / \sqrt{gL}$ | Froude number | Inertia $\div$ Gravity | — |
| 13 | $F_B = \rho_f g V_{disp}$ | Buoyancy (Archimedes) | Upthrust on immersed body | N |
| 14 | $\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \rho \vec{f}_b + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma}$ | Cauchy momentum eqn. | Newton's 2nd law for fluid | N/m³ |

---

## 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Automotive Aerodynamics:** Pressure and shear forces integrated over a car's body yield **drag**, **lift**, and **side force** coefficients — critical for fuel economy and high-speed stability.
2. **Wind Engineering:** Pressure differences on building surfaces drive **cladding loads**; viscous shear drives **wind-induced vibrations**.
3. **Hydraulic Machinery:** Body forces (gravity) and pressure forces govern **pump and turbine** performance.
4. **Aerospace:** Combined with electromagnetic forces, these govern **re-entry plasmas** and **MHD propulsion**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 🔬 Derivation 1 — Net Pressure Force on a Fluid Element (1D)

**Objective:** Derive the net pressure force on a fluid element of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ in the $x$-direction.

### Step 1: Identify the pressure on the left and right faces.

Consider a small fluid element centered at point $(x, y, z)$ with face area $dA = dy \cdot dz$. The pressure on the left face (at $x - dx/2$) is $p_L$ and on the right face (at $x + dx/2$) is $p_R$.

### Step 2: Express the pressures using a Taylor series expansion.

$$\begin{aligned}
p_L &= p - \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \cdot \frac{dx}{2} \\
p_R &= p + \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \cdot \frac{dx}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Compute the force on each face (pressure × area).

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{L,x} &= p_L \cdot dA = \left( p - \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \frac{dx}{2} \right) dy \, dz \\
F_{R,x} &= p_R \cdot dA = \left( p + \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \frac{dx}{2} \right) dy \, dz
\end{aligned}$$

> *(Note: Pressure pushes inward, so the right face force is in the $-x$ direction, but the algebraic sum already accounts for direction via the sign of $p$.)*

### Step 4: Sum the forces to obtain the net force in $x$.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{net,x} &= F_{L,x} - F_{R,x} \\
&= \left( p - \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \frac{dx}{2} - p - \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \frac{dx}{2} \right) dy \, dz \\
&= -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \cdot dx \, dy \, dz
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Recognize the net force per unit volume.

$$\boxed{\frac{F_{net,x}}{dV} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}}$$

> *This is the **pressure gradient force per unit volume** — one of the most important results in fluid mechanics. Fluid naturally accelerates from high pressure to low pressure.*

---

## 🔬 Derivation 2 — Net Viscous Shear Force on a Fluid Element (1D)

**Objective:** Derive the net viscous force in the $x$-direction on a fluid element assuming a velocity gradient in $y$.

### Step 1: Identify shear stresses on the top and bottom faces.

The element has top face area $dx \cdot dz$ at $y + dy/2$ and bottom face at $y - dy/2$. Shear stress $\tau_{yx}$ acts in the $x$-direction on a face normal to $y$.

### Step 2: Taylor expand the shear stress.

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{yx, \text{bottom}} &= \tau_{yx} - \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} \frac{dy}{2} \\
\tau_{yx, \text{top}} &= \tau_{yx} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} \frac{dy}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Compute shear forces on top and bottom faces.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{top}, x} &= \left( \tau_{yx} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} \frac{dy}{2} \right) dx \, dz \\
F_{\text{bottom}, x} &= \left( \tau_{yx} - \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} \frac{dy}{2} \right) dx \, dz
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Net viscous force in $x$.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{visc, x} &= F_{\text{top}, x} - F_{\text{bottom}, x} \\
&= \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} \cdot dx \, dy \, dz
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Force per unit volume.

$$\boxed{\frac{F_{visc, x}}{dV} = \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y}}$$

### Step 6: Substitute Newton's law of viscosity.

Using $\tau_{yx} = \mu (\partial u / \partial y)$:

$$\frac{F_{visc, x}}{dV} = \mu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

> *Generalizing to 3D, the viscous force per unit volume becomes $\mu \nabla^2 \vec{V}$ (for incompressible Newtonian fluid with constant $\mu$).*

---

## 🔬 Derivation 3 — Force Balance & Inertia (Cauchy Momentum Equation)

**Objective:** Combine all forces on a fluid element and equate to inertia.

### Step 1: Assemble all forces per unit volume in $x$.

From the derivations above and including body force $\rho g_x$ (gravity component in $x$):

$$\sum F_x / dV = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z} + \rho g_x$$

### Step 2: Equate to inertia (mass × acceleration per unit volume).

The mass of the element is $\rho \, dV$, and its acceleration is $Du/Dt$ (the **material derivative**):

$$\rho \frac{Du}{Dt} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z} + \rho g_x$$

### Step 3: Substitute the full Newtonian stress tensor.

For a Newtonian fluid, the normal and shear viscous stresses are:

$$\tau_{xx} = 2\mu \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \tau_{yx} = \mu \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} \right), \quad \tau_{zx} = \mu \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial x} \right)$$

### Step 4: Simplify for incompressible flow ($\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$).

After substituting and simplifying (the detailed algebra is shown in advanced texts), the $x$-momentum equation reduces to the celebrated **Navier-Stokes Equation**:

$$\boxed{\rho \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} \right) + \rho g_x}$$

> *Each term has a clear physical meaning:*
> - **LHS:** Inertia (mass × acceleration)
> - **RHS term 1:** Pressure force (normal surface)
> - **RHS term 2:** Viscous force (tangential surface)
> - **RHS term 3:** Body force (gravity)

---

## 🔬 Derivation 4 — Hydrostatic Pressure Variation

**Objective:** Show that $p$ increases linearly with depth in a static, incompressible fluid.

### Step 1: Apply the static equilibrium condition.

A static fluid has zero acceleration. Setting $Du/Dt = 0$ and $\tau_{ij} = 0$ (no motion, no shear), the momentum equation reduces to:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} - \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial x}$$

(assuming gravity acts in $-z$ direction).

### Step 2: Recognize that pressure depends only on $z$.

For a horizontal fluid layer, $\partial p / \partial x = 0$ and $\partial p / \partial y = 0$, so $p = p(z)$ only.

### Step 3: Integrate.

$$\frac{dp}{dz} = -\rho g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \int_{p_1}^{p_2} dp = -\rho g \int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz$$

### Step 4: Final result.

$$\boxed{p_2 - p_1 = -\rho g (z_2 - z_1) \quad \text{or} \quad p = p_0 + \rho g h}$$

> *For air ($\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$), a height difference of 1 m causes only $\sim 12 \text{ Pa}$ of pressure change — negligible compared to $p_{atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa}$. This is why the atmosphere is treated as incompressible for low-speed vehicle aerodynamics.*

---

## 🔬 Derivation 5 — Dimensional Analysis of Key Force Ratios

### (a) Reynolds Number — Inertia vs. Viscous

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Inertia force} &\sim \rho V^2 L^2 \\
\text{Viscous force} &\sim \mu V L \\
\Rightarrow \quad Re &= \frac{\text{Inertia}}{\text{Viscous}} = \frac{\rho V^2 L^2}{\mu V L} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}
\end{aligned}$$

### (b) Euler Number — Pressure vs. Inertia

$$Eu = \frac{\text{Pressure force}}{\text{Inertia force}} = \frac{p L^2}{\rho V^2 L^2} = \frac{p}{\rho V^2}$$

### (c) Froude Number — Inertia vs. Gravity

$$Fr = \frac{\text{Inertia force}}{\text{Gravity force}} = \frac{\rho V^2 L^2}{\rho g L^3} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$$

---

## 📋 Analytical Matrix — Identification of Dominant Forces

| Physical Scenario | Dominant Force(s) | Negligible Force(s) | Key Non-Dim. Number |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Slow-flow water in pipe | Viscous, Pressure | Inertia, Gravity | $Re \ll 1$ |
| Aerodynamic flow over a car (90 km/h) | Inertia, Pressure | Viscous (locally), Gravity | $Re \sim 10^6$–$10^7$ |
| Wind over a tall skyscraper | Inertia, Pressure | Viscous (boundary layer only) | $Re \sim 10^7$ |
| Tsunami / dam-break wave | Gravity, Inertia | Viscous | $Fr \sim 1$ |
| Boundary layer near a wing | Viscous, Inertia | Pressure gradient (within BL), Gravity | $Re_{BL} \sim 10^5$ |
| Static fluid in a tank | Gravity, Pressure | Viscous, Inertia | — |
| Storm surge in ocean | Gravity, Pressure | Viscous | $Fr \ll 1$ |
| Aircraft at high altitude | Inertia, Pressure | Viscous, Gravity | $Re \sim 10^7$, $Eu \sim 1$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 📊 Diagram 1: Master Classification of Forces in a Fluid (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Forces in a Fluid]) --> A1[Classification of Forces]
    A1 --> B1[Body Forces]
    A1 --> B2[Surface Forces]
    A1 --> B3[Inertia Force]
    A1 --> B4[Buoyancy Force]

    B1 --> C1[Gravity]
    B1 --> C2[Electromagnetic Lorentz Force]
    B1 --> C3[Centrifugal Force]
    B1 --> C4[Coriolis Force]

    B2 --> D1[Normal Stress]
    B2 --> D2[Tangential Stress]

    D1 --> E1[Pressure p]
    E1 --> E2[Static Pressure]
    E1 --> E3[Dynamic Pressure q]
    E1 --> E4[Stagnation Pressure]

    D2 --> F1[Viscous Shear Stress tau]
    F1 --> F2[Newtonian tau = mu du/dy]
    F1 --> F3[Non-Newtonian Power Law]

    B3 --> G1[Mass times Acceleration]
    B3 --> G2[DAlembert Virtual Force]

    B4 --> H1[Archimedes Upthrust]
    B4 --> H2[Center of Buoyancy]

    Start --> Final[Governing Equation Cauchy Momentum]
    Final --> FinalEq[rho DV/Dt = rho fb + div sigma]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The flowchart first bifurcates forces into four primary families, then recursively decomposes each into physically meaningful sub-forces, finally converging into the governing **Cauchy Momentum Equation** that unifies all of them.

---

## 📊 Diagram 2: Stress Tensor on a Fluid Element (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ELEMENT[FLUID ELEMENT dx dy dz]
        N1[X face normal stress sigma_xx]
        N2[Y face normal stress sigma_yy]
        N3[Z face normal stress sigma_zz]
        S1[X face shear tau_xy tau_xz]
        S2[Y face shear tau_yx tau_yz]
        S3[Z face shear tau_zx tau_zy]
    end

    N1 --> M1[All normal stresses are minus p plus viscous]
    N2 --> M1
    N3 --> M1
    S1 --> M2[All shear stresses obey Newton viscosity law]
    S2 --> M2
    S3 --> M2

    M1 --> SUM[Sum of all surface forces]
    M2 --> SUM

    SUM --> COMBINE[Add body force rho g]
    COMBINE --> OUT[EQUATION OF MOTION]
    OUT --> OUTEQ[rho DV/Dt = minus grad p + mu Laplacian V + rho g]
```

> **Interpretation:** The 3 normal stresses and 6 shear stresses (the stress tensor) act on the 6 faces of the element, contributing to the net surface force. After adding the body force, the result is the Navier-Stokes equation.

---

## 📊 Diagram 3: Force Dominance Map for Vehicle Aerodynamics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SCEN[Vehicle Speed Regime] --> LOW[Low Speed under 30 km/h]
    SCEN --> MED[Medium Speed 30 to 120 km/h]
    SCEN --> HIGH[High Speed above 120 km/h]

    LOW --> LOW_F[DOMINANT Viscous and Inertia Equal Re order 10 to 5]
    MED --> MED_F[DOMINANT Inertia and Pressure Re order 10 to 6]
    HIGH --> HIGH_F[DOMINANT Inertia and Compressibility Effects Re order 10 to 7 M above 0.3]

    LOW_F --> FORMULA1[Re equals rho V L over mu]
    MED_F --> FORMULA2[Cp equals p minus p_inf over 0.5 rho V squared]
    HIGH_F --> FORMULA3[M equals V over a Mach number]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The dominant force regime shifts as vehicle speed increases. At low speeds, viscous (skin friction) and inertia forces are comparable; at typical highway speeds, inertia and pressure dominate; at racing/supersonic speeds, compressibility (Mach) effects emerge.

---

## 📊 Diagram 4: Body-Force vs Surface-Force Interaction in a Moving Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle at Velocity V] --> B[Surrounding Air Parcel]
    B --> C1[Surface Force 1 Pressure on Body Surface]
    B --> C2[Surface Force 2 Shear on Body Surface]
    B --> C3[Body Force Gravity Acts on Air Volume]
    B --> C4[Inertia Force Air Parcel Acceleration]

    C1 --> R1[Integrated over surface equals Aerodynamic Drag plus Lift plus Side Force]
    C2 --> R2[Integrated over surface equals Skin Friction Drag]
    C3 --> R3[Negligible at vehicle scale density ratio is small]
    C4 --> R4[Equal to Net Force in Momentum Equation]
```

> **Note:** The body force (gravity) on the air parcel is generally negligible compared to the pressure and shear forces on the vehicle surface because the density ratio $\rho_{air}/\rho_{vehicle} \ll 1$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🟢 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Classify the forces acting on a fluid element. Give one example for each category.**

**Model Answer:**

Forces acting on a fluid element are broadly classified into two categories:

1. **Body Forces:** These act on the entire volume of the fluid element without requiring physical contact. Examples: **gravity**, electromagnetic force, centrifugal force.
2. **Surface Forces:** These act on the bounding surface of the element and are proportional to the surface area. They are further divided into:
   - **Normal surface force (pressure):** Acts perpendicular to the surface. Example: aerodynamic pressure on a car bonnet.
   - **Tangential surface force (viscous shear):** Acts parallel to the surface. Example: skin friction drag on an aircraft wing.

> **Mark Distribution (as per KTU pattern):** Definition of body force (1M) + Example (0.5M) + Definition of surface force (1M) + Example (0.5M) = **3 Marks**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**State and explain Newton's Law of Viscosity. Mention its limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Newton's Law of Viscosity (1687):** The shear stress in a fluid is directly proportional to the velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) at a point.

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where $\tau$ = shear stress [Pa], $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity [Pa·s], and $du/dy$ = velocity gradient perpendicular to the surface [1/s].

**Explanation:** When two fluid layers move relative to each other, the faster layer pulls the slower layer forward, while the slower layer drags the faster layer backward. This internal friction manifests as shear stress. Fluids obeying this linear relationship (constant $\mu$) are called **Newtonian fluids** (air, water, gasoline, oil). Fluids where $\mu$ varies with shear rate (blood, paint, ketchup) are **non-Newtonian**.

**Limitations:**
1. Valid only for **laminar** flow, not turbulent.
2. Does not apply to **non-Newtonian** fluids (polymers, slurries, blood).
3. Assumes the fluid is a **continuum** (fails at nano/micro scales — rarefied gas dynamics).
4. Assumes **isothermal** conditions; $\mu$ varies with temperature significantly.

> **Mark Distribution:** Statement (1M) + Equation (1M) + Limitations (1M) = **3 Marks**.

---

## 🔵 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Students must answer EITHER the entire Q from OR (a) + (b) of Q-A OR (a) + (b) of Q-B. This is the **"either-or" internal choice** mandated by the 2024 scheme.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]**

#### (a) **Derive the expression for the net pressure force acting on a fluid element of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ in the $x$-direction.** [7 Marks]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5M):** Define the element and faces.
Consider a fluid element of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$. The faces perpendicular to the $x$-axis have area $dA = dy \cdot dz$. Let $p$ be the pressure at the centre of the element $(x, y, z)$.

**Step 2 (1.5M):** Express pressures on the two faces using Taylor expansion.
- Pressure on the left face (at $x - dx/2$): $p_L = p - (\partial p/\partial x) \cdot (dx/2)$
- Pressure on the right face (at $x + dx/2$): $p_R = p + (\partial p/\partial x) \cdot (dx/2)$

**Step 3 (1.5M):** Compute forces.
- Force on left face: $F_L = p_L \cdot dy \cdot dz$ (acts in $+x$ direction)
- Force on right face: $F_R = p_R \cdot dy \cdot dz$ (acts in $-x$ direction)

**Step 4 (1.5M):** Compute net force.
$$F_{net, x} = F_L - F_R = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$$

**Step 5 (1M):** Force per unit volume.
$$\frac{F_{net, x}}{dV} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$

**Conclusion:** The negative sign indicates the fluid accelerates from high pressure to low pressure.

---

#### (b) **A car is travelling at 108 km/h. The density of air is 1.2 kg/m³. Calculate the dynamic pressure acting on the car. If the pressure coefficient at the front bumper is measured as $C_p = -0.8$, find the static pressure difference between the bumper and the freestream.** [7 Marks]

**Given:** $V = 108$ km/h, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $C_p = -0.8$

**Step 1 (1M):** Convert velocity to SI units.
$$V = 108 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 30 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 (2M):** Calculate dynamic pressure.
$$q_\infty = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.2 \times (30)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.2 \times 900 = 540 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 (2M):** Apply pressure coefficient definition.
$$C_p = \frac{p - p_\infty}{q_\infty} \quad \Rightarrow \quad p - p_\infty = C_p \times q_\infty$$

**Step 4 (2M):** Calculate static pressure difference.
$$p - p_\infty = -0.8 \times 540 = -432 \text{ Pa}$$

**Conclusion:** The pressure on the bumper is **432 Pa below** the freestream static pressure. This suction contributes to **aerodynamic drag** (pressure drag component).

> **Mark Distribution:** Unit conversion (1M) + Formula + Dynamic pressure (2M) + Cp formula (2M) + Final answer with units (2M) = **7 Marks**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**

#### (a) **Derive the Cauchy Momentum Equation for a fluid element, listing the physical meaning of each term.** [7 Marks]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5M):** State Newton's Second Law for a fluid element of mass $dm = \rho \, dV$.
$$\sum \vec{F} = dm \cdot \vec{a} = \rho \, dV \cdot \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}$$

**Step 2 (1.5M):** Identify and list all force types.
- Body force per unit volume: $\rho \vec{f}_b$ (e.g., $\rho \vec{g}$ for gravity)
- Surface force per unit volume: $\nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma}$ (divergence of stress tensor)

**Step 3 (1.5M):** Write the equation in compact tensor form.
$$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \rho \vec{f}_b + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma}$$

**Step 4 (1.5M):** Expand the stress tensor for a Newtonian viscous fluid.
$$\sigma_{ij} = -p \delta_{ij} + \mu \left( \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i} \right)$$

**Step 5 (1M):** Final form (Navier-Stokes).
$$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$

**Physical meaning of each term (table for full marks):**

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|:---|:---|:---|
| LHS | $\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}$ | Inertia (mass × acceleration) per unit volume |
| RHS-1 | $-\nabla p$ | Pressure gradient force per unit volume |
| RHS-2 | $\mu \nabla^2 \vec{V}$ | Viscous (shear) force per unit volume |
| RHS-3 | $\rho \vec{g}$ | Body force (gravity) per unit volume |

---

#### (b) **For the flow over a wing, the local Reynolds number is $5 \times 10^6$. If the chord length is 2 m and dynamic viscosity is $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s), determine the freestream velocity. Comment on the dominant force regime.** [7 Marks]

**Given:** $Re = 5 \times 10^6$, $L = 2$ m, $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s), $\rho \approx 1.225$ kg/m³ (standard air).

**Step 1 (1M):** Recall the Reynolds number definition.
$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}$$

**Step 2 (1.5M):** Solve for velocity.
$$V = \frac{Re \cdot \mu}{\rho \cdot L} = \frac{5 \times 10^6 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5}}{1.225 \times 2}$$

**Step 3 (1.5M):** Compute the numerator.
$$Re \cdot \mu = 5 \times 10^6 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} = 90 \text{ kg/(m·s)}$$

**Step 4 (1.5M):** Compute denominator and final answer.
$$\rho L = 1.225 \times 2 = 2.45 \text{ kg/m}^2$$
$$V = \frac{90}{2.45} = 36.73 \text{ m/s} \approx 132.2 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 5 (1.5M):** Comment on dominant forces.
Since $Re = 5 \times 10^6 \gg 1$, **inertia forces dominate over viscous forces**. The flow is overwhelmingly inertia-driven, with viscosity acting only in a thin **boundary layer** near the wing surface. This is the typical regime for full-scale aircraft and high-speed cars.

> **Mark Distribution:** Formula (1M) + Algebraic rearrangement (1.5M) + Numerical substitution (2M) + Final answer (1M) + Physical interpretation (1.5M) = **7 Marks**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions:** Many students lose 1 mark by not converting km/h to m/s in problems involving vehicle aerodynamics. Always convert to SI first.
> 2. **Sign error in pressure gradient:** The net pressure force per unit volume is $-\partial p / \partial x$, NOT $+\partial p / \partial x$. The negative sign is critical.
> 3. **Mixing up $C_p$ and $q$:** $C_p$ is **dimensionless**, while $q$ has units of Pa. Never write $C_p$ in Pa or $q$ as a pure number.
> 4. **Confusing body and surface forces:** Gravity acts on the **mass**, not the surface. Pressure acts on the **surface**, not the volume.
> 5. **Forgetting the material derivative:** In the Cauchy momentum equation, $\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$. Writing only $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Missing the "either-or" choice:** KTU Part B questions have internal choice. Read the entire question paper before answering.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Forces in a fluid** = **Body forces** (volume-based: gravity, EM, centrifugal) + **Surface forces** (area-based: pressure, viscous shear).
- [x] **Pressure** is a **normal, compressive** surface force, isotropic in a static fluid (Pascal's Law).
- [x] **Shear stress** is a **tangential** surface force, present only when fluid is in motion (or has a velocity gradient).
- [x] **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ — the foundational constitutive equation for Newtonian fluids.
- [x] **Dynamic pressure:** $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ — the kinetic energy per unit volume of a moving fluid; the reference scale for $C_p$ and $C_D$.
- [x] **Pressure coefficient:** $C_p = (p - p_\infty)/q$ — the primary diagnostic quantity in vehicle aerodynamics.
- [x] **Stress tensor:** $\sigma_{ij} = -p\delta_{ij} + \tau_{ij}$ — complete mathematical description of surface forces (9 components).
- [x] **Cauchy Momentum Equation:** $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \rho \vec{f}_b + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma}$ — Newton's 2nd Law for a fluid element.
- [x] **Navier-Stokes Equation (incompressible Newtonian):** $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$.
- [x] **Hydrostatic equation:** $dp/dz = -\rho g$; integrated: $p = p_0 + \rho g h$.
- [x] **Reynolds number $Re = \rho V L / \mu$** — ratio of **inertia to viscous** forces.
- [x] **Euler number $Eu = p/(\rho V^2)$** — ratio of **pressure to inertia** forces.
- [x] **Froude number $Fr = V/\sqrt{gL}$** — ratio of **inertia to gravity** forces.
- [x] **Buoyancy (Archimedes):** $F_B = \rho_f g V_{disp}$, acts upward through the centroid of the displaced fluid.
- [x] In **typical vehicle aerodynamics** ($V \sim 30$–$150$ km/h), the dominant forces are **inertia and pressure**; viscous effects are confined to a **thin boundary layer** on the body surface.
- [x] **Standard air properties (15°C, sea level):** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $p_{atm} = 101325$ Pa.
- [x] **Continuum assumption** is valid when the Knudsen number $Kn \ll 0.001$ (always true for full-scale vehicle flows).

---

> **End of Module 1 / Topic: Forces in a Fluid — KTU 2024 Scheme Study Notes**

topics.title: Compressibility and Incompressibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Compressibility and Incompressibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Vehicle Aerodynamics (PEAUT752)**, *Compressibility* and *Incompressibility* are fundamental classification criteria for fluid flow, based on whether the fluid density ($\rho$) undergoes a measurable variation under applied pressure or velocity fields.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compressibility** is formally defined as the fractional change in fluid density ($\rho$) per unit change in pressure ($p$), expressed mathematically as the **coefficient of compressibility** ($\beta$ or $\tau$):
> $$\beta = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{dV}{dp} = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{d\rho}{dp}$$
> where $V$ is the specific volume. The negative sign indicates that volume decreases as pressure increases.

**Incompressibility** is the limiting condition where the fluid density is treated as a **constant** ($\rho = \text{constant}$) irrespective of the local pressure and temperature variations. For an incompressible flow, $\beta \to 0$ or $d\rho = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A fluid is classified as **incompressible** only when the *local* density change at any point in the flow field is less than **5%** of the freestream density. Beyond this threshold, compressibility effects become aerodynamically significant and must be modeled.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **sponge (incompressible analogue)** versus a **balloon filled with air (compressible analogue)**:

- **Sponge analogy (Incompressible flow):** When you press one side of a wet sponge, water is displaced, but the water molecules themselves do not compress — they simply rearrange. The total mass per unit volume in any local region remains essentially constant. This mirrors **liquid water** or **air at very low speeds**.

- **Balloon analogy (Compressible flow):** When you squeeze the balloon, the air molecules inside get packed closer together — the *density actually changes*. At higher vehicle speeds (approaching or exceeding the speed of sound), the air behaves like the balloon: it gets locally compressed around the vehicle, changing density and producing shock waves, drag divergence, and compressibility losses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world KTU Context:** For a typical road vehicle traveling at 60–120 km/h, the Mach number is approximately **M ≈ 0.045 to 0.09**. Since this is far below the **M = 0.3 threshold**, automotive aerodynamics is overwhelmingly treated as **incompressible flow**. However, for **high-speed trains (300+ km/h)**, **formula cars**, and **supersonic aircraft**, compressibility effects become critical.

## 1.3 Governing Physical Constants

The key non-dimensional parameter that universally governs the compressibility classification is the **Mach Number ($M$)**, defined as:

$$M = \frac{V}{a}$$

where:
- **$V$** = local flow velocity (in **m/s**)
- **$a$** = local speed of sound (in **m/s**), given by $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$
- **$\gamma$** = ratio of specific heats ($c_p / c_v$) = **1.4** for air
- **$R$** = specific gas constant for air = **287 J/(kg·K)**
- **$T$** = absolute temperature (in **Kelvin**)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value/Unit |
|:------:|:---------|:--------------------|
| $\gamma$ | Ratio of specific heats (air) | **1.4** (dimensionless) |
| $R$ | Specific gas constant | **287 J/(kg·K)** |
| $a_{SL}$ | Speed of sound at sea level (15°C) | **340.3 m/s** ≈ **1225 km/h** |
| $\rho_{SL}$ | Air density at sea level | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| $p_{SL}$ | Standard atmospheric pressure | **101,325 Pa** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of the Compressibility Concept

The classification of flow into compressible/incompressible regimes is not arbitrary — it emerges from the **relative magnitude of kinetic energy** of the flow versus its **internal (thermodynamic) energy**.

**Step-by-step logical decomposition:**

- **Step 1 — Energy Comparison:** As a fluid element accelerates from rest to velocity $V$, the kinetic energy per unit mass gained is $V^2/2$. The internal energy per unit mass is $c_v T$.

- **Step 2 — The Ratio:** The dimensionless ratio $\dfrac{V^2/2}{c_p T} = \dfrac{V^2}{2 c_p T} = \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2$ quantifies the relative importance of compressibility.

- **Step 3 — Threshold Criterion:** When this ratio is small ($\ll 1$, i.e., $M < 0.3$), density changes are negligible (less than 5%) and the flow is **incompressible**.

- **Step 4 — Consequence:** The **Continuity Equation** for steady flow simplifies from its general compressible form to the simple **divergence-free condition** $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$, and **Bernoulli's Equation** becomes exactly valid along a streamline.

## 2.2 Speed of Sound — The Foundation of Compressibility

The **speed of sound ($a$)** is the speed at which an infinitesimally small pressure disturbance propagates through a fluid at rest. It is derived by combining the **continuity equation** with the **momentum equation** and assuming an **isentropic (reversible adiabatic)** process.

For a perfect gas undergoing an isentropic process:

$$a^2 = \left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial \rho}\right)_s$$

Applying the isentropic relation $p/\rho^\gamma = \text{constant}$, we obtain:

$$a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The speed of sound sets the **natural velocity scale** for any aerodynamic problem. Every compressible flow phenomenon — shock waves, expansion fans, choked nozzles, and drag divergence — is referenced to this scale via the Mach number $M$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Expression | Application / Domain |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Coefficient of compressibility | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{d\rho}{dp}$ | Measure of fluid compressibility |
| 2 | Bulk Modulus | $K = \rho \, a^2 = \gamma p$ | Resistance to compression |
| 3 | Speed of sound (perfect gas) | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Compressible flow reference velocity |
| 4 | Mach number | $M = \dfrac{V}{a}$ | Compressibility classifier |
| 5 | Critical Mach number | $M_{cr} = \dfrac{V_{\infty} - \Delta V}{a_{\infty}}$ | Onset of local sonic flow |
| 6 | Incompressible continuity | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | Density is constant |
| 7 | Compressible continuity (steady) | $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | Density varies with position |
| 8 | Bernoulli's (incompressible) | $p + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = \text{const}$ | Valid only when $M < 0.3$ |
| 9 | Density ratio (isentropic) | $\dfrac{\rho}{\rho_0} = \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2\right)^{-1}$ | Compressible density variation |
| 10 | Pressure coefficient (incompressible) | $C_p = 1 - \left(\dfrac{V}{V_{\infty}}\right)^2$ | Surface pressure in low-speed flow |
| 11 | Pressure coefficient (compressible) | $C_p = \dfrac{2}{\gamma M_{\infty}^2}\left[\left(\dfrac{p}{p_{\infty}}\right) - 1\right]$ | Surface pressure in high-speed flow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Students frequently confuse **incompressible substance** (water) with **incompressible flow** (any fluid at low Mach number). Even air, despite being a gas, exhibits *incompressible flow behavior* when $M < 0.3$. The classification is on the **flow regime**, not on the **fluid type**.

## 2.4 Flow Classification by Mach Number

| Mach Number Range | Flow Regime | Compressibility Treatment | Vehicle Example |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| $M < 0.3$ | **Incompressible** | Neglect $\Delta \rho$ | Cars, trucks, low-speed trains |
| $0.3 \le M < 0.8$ | **Subsonic** | Weak compressibility | High-speed trains (TGV, Shinkansen) |
| $0.8 \le M \le 1.2$ | **Transonic** | Mixed subsonic/supersonic | Supersonic aircraft near cruise |
| $1.2 < M < 5.0$ | **Supersonic** | Full compressibility + shocks | Fighter jets (near M = 1.5) |
| $M \ge 5.0$ | **Hypersonic** | High-temperature effects | Re-entry vehicles, missiles |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Speed of Sound from First Principles

We consider a **one-dimensional sound wave** propagating through a quiescent fluid. A weak pressure disturbance moves with speed $a$, and we analyze the flow relative to a control volume moving with the wavefront.

**Step 1 — Control Volume Setup:**
Let the wave propagate to the right with speed $a$. In the wave-fixed frame, undisturbed fluid enters from the right with velocity $a$, and disturbed fluid exits on the left with velocity $(a - dV)$, where $dV$ is the small velocity change induced by the pressure rise $dp$.

**Step 2 — Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass):**

Mass flow rate entering = Mass flow rate leaving:

$$\rho \cdot a = (\rho + d\rho)(a - dV)$$

Expanding the right-hand side:

$$\rho \, a = \rho \, a - \rho \, dV + a \, d\rho - d\rho \, dV$$

Neglecting the second-order term $d\rho \, dV$ (since both $d\rho$ and $dV$ are infinitesimally small):

$$\rho \, dV = a \, d\rho$$

$$\therefore \quad dV = a \cdot \frac{d\rho}{\rho} \quad \text{...(Equation A)}$$

**Step 3 — Momentum Equation (Newton's Second Law):**

The net pressure force on the control volume equals the rate of change of momentum:

$$p \cdot A - (p + dp) \cdot A = \rho \cdot a \cdot A \cdot [0 - (-dV)]$$

The left side simplifies to $-dp \cdot A$. On the right side, fluid enters with velocity $-a$ (relative to waveframe) and exits with velocity $-(a - dV)$, so the change in velocity is $+dV$.

$$-dp \cdot A = \rho \, a \cdot A \cdot dV$$

$$dp = -\rho \, a \cdot dV \quad \text{...(Equation B)}$$

**Step 4 — Eliminating $dV$:**

From Equation A: $dV = a \cdot \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}$

Substituting into Equation B:

$$dp = -\rho \, a \cdot \left(a \cdot \frac{d\rho}{\rho}\right) = -a^2 \, d\rho$$

**Step 5 — Final Result:**

$$a^2 = -\frac{dp}{d\rho}$$

For an **isentropic** process, $p = C \rho^\gamma$ (where $C$ is a constant), so:

$$\frac{dp}{d\rho} = \gamma C \rho^{\gamma - 1} = \gamma \frac{p}{\rho}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{a^2 = \gamma \frac{p}{\rho} = \gamma R T}$$

This is the fundamental speed of sound relation for a perfect gas.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Mach Number

The **critical Mach number ($M_{cr}$)** is defined as the freestream Mach number at which the *local* flow velocity at some point on the body first reaches the local speed of sound ($M_{\text{local}} = 1$).

**Step 1 — Local velocity from pressure coefficient:**

In compressible flow, the local velocity is related to the local Mach number. From the isentropic relation:

$$\frac{V_{\text{local}}^2}{2} + \frac{a^2}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{V_{\infty}^2}{2} + \frac{a_{\infty}^2}{\gamma - 1}$$

Using energy conservation and the isentropic identity $\dfrac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2$:

$$M_{\text{local}}^2 = \frac{2}{\gamma - 1}\left[\left(\frac{p_0}{p}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]^{-1} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{\text{local}}}{a}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Simplification for small perturbations (Prandtl-Glauert):**

For thin bodies at small disturbance, the **Prandtl-Glauert transformation** relates compressible and incompressible pressure coefficients:

$$C_{p,\text{compressible}} = \frac{C_{p,\text{incompressible}}}{\sqrt{\vert 1 - M_{\infty}^2 \vert}}$$

**Step 3 — Critical condition:**

At the point where $M_{\text{local}} = 1$, using the isentropic relation for local Mach number:

$$\frac{p_{\text{local}}}{p_{\infty}} = \left(\frac{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M_{\infty}^2}{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M_{\text{local}}^2}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$$

For $M_{\text{local}} = 1$:

$$\frac{p_{\text{local}}}{p_{\infty}} = \left(\frac{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M_{\infty}^2}{\frac{\gamma + 1}{2}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$$

Setting $p_{\text{local}} = p_{\infty} + \Delta p$ and using the standard subsonic critical Mach number approximation:

$$\boxed{M_{cr} = 1 - \frac{\vert \Delta p / p_{\infty} \vert_{\min}}{\gamma} - \frac{\gamma + 1}{2\gamma} M_{cr}^2 + \ldots}$$

For a symmetric airfoil at small angles of attack, this reduces to:

$$M_{cr} \approx 1 - \sqrt{\frac{2}{\gamma - 1}\left[\left(\frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}} - 1\right]^{-1} \cdot \vert C_{p,\min} \vert}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The critical Mach number is the design threshold that aircraft aerodynamicists must avoid. For modern commercial jetliners, $M_{cr} \approx 0.78$–$0.82$, allowing cruise at $M_{\infty} \approx 0.78$–$0.85$ without encountering local sonic conditions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Continuity Equation (Compressible vs Incompressible)

**Step 1 — General (Compressible) Continuity:**

For a steady, 3D flow, the differential form of the continuity equation is:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$$

In vector form:

$$\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Incompressible Limit:**

When $\rho = \text{constant}$, the density can be factored out:

$$\rho \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}\right) = 0$$

Since $\rho \neq 0$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Physical Interpretation:**

| Aspect | Compressible Flow | Incompressible Flow |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Mass conservation | $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ |
| Volume conservation | Not conserved | Conserved |
| Density | Variable: $\rho(x,y,z)$ | Constant: $\rho$ |
| Applicable equation | Full Navier-Stokes + energy | Simplified Navier-Stokes |
| Valid range | All Mach numbers | $M < 0.3$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Compressibility Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Flow Problem] --> B{Vehicle Velocity V}
    B --> C[Compute Local Mach Number M = V / a]
    C --> D{M < 0.3?}

    D -->|No| F{0.3 ≤ M < 0.8?}

    H -->|No| J{1.2 < M < 5.0?}

    J -->|No| L[Hypersonic High Temp]
    E --> M[Use Bernoulli + Continuity]
    G --> N[Use Prandtl Glauert Correction]
    I --> O[Use Linearised Supersonic Theory]
```


## 4.3 Isentropic Flow Relation Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Isentropic Process Assumption] --> B[Speed of Sound a equals sqrt gamma R T]
    A --> C[Isentropic Pressure Relation p over p0]
    A --> D[Isentropic Density Relation rho over rho0]
    A --> E[Isentropic Temperature Relation T over T0]
    B --> F[Mach Number M equals V over a]
    F --> G{Critical Condition M equals 1}
    G --> H[Critical Pressure Ratio equals 0.528]
    G --> I[Critical Density Ratio equals 0.634]
    G --> J[Critical Temperature Ratio equals 0.833]
```
