# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Assessment Pattern (as per official regulations):**
> - **Part A (3 marks):** Short-answer conceptual questions. Direct definitions, brief derivations, one-step calculations.
> - **Part B (14 marks):** Full-length analytical problems with internal choice (two alternatives, choose one). Usually split into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.
> - Questions are tagged to specific Course Outcomes (CO1–CO5) and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy levels (L1–L6).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Mach Number. State its significance in vehicle aerodynamics. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Mach number is defined as the ratio of the velocity of a fluid (or a body moving through a fluid) to the local speed of sound in that fluid medium.

$$M = \frac{V}{a}$$

**Significance (1 mark):**
Mach number is a dimensionless parameter that classifies flow into incompressible, subsonic, transonic, supersonic, and hypersonic regimes. It governs compressibility effects and determines the onset of shock waves, wave drag, and sonic booms. In vehicle aerodynamics, it is the key parameter that dictates whether a vehicle can be treated as operating in incompressible flow (e.g., road cars, M < 0.3) or whether compressibility corrections and shock-wave analysis are required (e.g., supersonic vehicles, M > 1).

---

### Question 2: A car travels at 144 km/h. The ambient air temperature is 27 °C. Calculate the Mach number. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Given:**
- $V = 144 \text{ km/h} = 40 \text{ m/s}$
- $T = 27 + 273 = 300 \text{ K}$
- $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$

**Step 1 — Speed of sound:**

$$a = \sqrt{\gamma R T} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 300} = \sqrt{120{,}540} \approx 347.2 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Mach number:**

$$M = \frac{V}{a} = \frac{40}{347.2} \approx 0.115$$

**Final Answer:** $M \approx 0.115$ — the car is in the **incompressible flow regime**. ✅

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Analytical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B questions offer an **internal choice** between two alternatives. We present both alternatives with full sub-parts and model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

> **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Derive the expression for the speed of sound in an ideal gas. Starting from first principles, show every step of the derivation.
>
> **Part (b) [7 marks]:** A supersonic aircraft flies at M = 2.0 at an altitude where the static temperature is 250 K and static pressure is 50 kPa. Calculate:
> (i) The stagnation temperature.
> (ii) The stagnation pressure.
> (iii) The speed of sound at the stagnation condition.
> (iv) The Mach angle.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

The complete derivation from Section 3.1 above applies. We summarize the key steps for the answer script:

1. Apply continuity across a sound wavefront: $\rho a = (\rho + d\rho)(a - dV)$, simplify to $\rho \, dV = a \, d\rho$. **(1 mark)**
2. Apply momentum equation: $dp = \rho \, a \, dV$. **(1 mark)**
3. Eliminate $dV$ to obtain $a^2 = \dfrac{dp}{d\rho}$. **(1 mark)**
4. Recognize isentropic condition: $a^2 = \left(\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial \rho}\right)_s$. **(1 mark)**
5. Apply isentropic relation for ideal gas: $p = C\rho^\gamma$, differentiate to get $\dfrac{dp}{d\rho} = \gamma \dfrac{p}{\rho}$. **(1 mark)**
6. Use ideal gas law $p = \rho R T$ to get $\dfrac{p}{\rho} = RT$. **(1 mark)**
7. Final boxed result: $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$. **(1 mark)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Given:** $M = 2.0$, $T = 250 \text{ K}$, $p = 50 \text{ kPa}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$

**(i) Stagnation temperature [2 marks]:**

$$\frac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2 = 1 + \frac{0.4}{2} \times 4 = 1 + 0.8 = 1.8$$

$$T_0 = 1.8 \times 250 = 450 \text{ K}$$

**(ii) Stagnation pressure [2 marks]:**

$$\frac{p_0}{p} = (1.8)^{\gamma/(\gamma - 1)} = (1.8)^{3.5} \approx 10.70$$

$$p_0 = 10.70 \times 50 = 535 \text{ kPa}$$

**(iii) Stagnation speed of sound [2 marks]:**

$$a_0 = \sqrt{\gamma R T_0} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 450} = \sqrt{180{,}810} \approx 425.2 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iv) Mach angle [1 mark]:**

$$\beta = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{M}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.5) = 30°$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

> **Part (a) [7 marks]:** State and derive the isentropic flow relations for a perfect gas in terms of Mach number. Show that the stagnation-to-static temperature ratio depends only on M and $\gamma$.
>
> **Part (b) [7 marks]:** A high-speed rail vehicle (bullet train) travels at 500 km/h. The ambient conditions are $T = 290 \text{ K}$ and $p = 95 \text{ kPa}$. Determine:
> (i) The Mach number of the train.
> (ii) The isentropic stagnation pressure ratio $p_0/p$.
> (iii) The percentage error if the flow is incorrectly treated as incompressible (compare actual $p_0/p$ with Bernoulli-based incompressible result).
> (iv) Comment on whether compressibility correction is necessary for this vehicle.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

The complete derivation from Section 3.2 above applies. Key scoring steps:

1. State the energy equation: $C_p T_0 = C_p T + \dfrac{V^2}{2}$. **(1 mark)**
2. Substitute $V = Ma$ and $a^2 = \gamma R T$: rewrite as $C_p(T_0 - T) = \dfrac{M^2 \gamma R T}{2}$. **(1 mark)**
3. Use $C_p = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$: derive $T_0 = T\left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2\right)$. **(2 marks)**
4. Apply $p/\rho^\gamma = \text{const}$ and $p = \rho R T$ to derive $p_0/p$ and $\rho_0/\rho$ relations. **(2 marks)**
5. Conclude that all ratios are functions of M and $\gamma$ only (independent of pressure and density). **(1 mark)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Given:** $V = 500 \text{ km/h} = 138.89 \text{ m/s}$, $T = 290 \text{ K}$, $p = 95 \text{ kPa}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$

**(i) Mach number [2 marks]:**

$$a = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 290} = \sqrt{116{,}522} \approx 341.35 \text{ m/s}$$

$$M = \frac{138.89}{341.35} \approx 0.407$$

**(ii) Stagnation pressure ratio [2 marks]:**

$$\frac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \frac{0.4}{2} \times (0.407)^2 = 1 + 0.2 \times 0.1656 = 1.0331$$

$$\frac{p_0}{p} = (1.0331)^{3.5} \approx 1.122$$

**(iii) Incompressible comparison [2 marks]:**

From Bernoulli: $\dfrac{p_0}{p} = 1 + \dfrac{\gamma}{2} M^2 = 1 + 0.7 \times 0.1656 = 1.116$

$$\text{Error} = \frac{1.122 - 1.116}{1.122} \times 100\% \approx 0.53\%$$

**(iv) Comment [1 mark]:**

The error is less than 1%. For engineering design of the bullet train, incompressible flow theory is **acceptable at M = 0.407** for preliminary drag and lift estimates, though for precision CFD or wind tunnel corrections, the Prandtl-Glauert correction is recommended.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** before dividing by speed of sound. This single error can lose 1–2 marks in a 14-mark problem.
> 2. **Using the wrong exponent in the isentropic pressure ratio.** The exponent is $\gamma/(\gamma - 1)$, NOT $\gamma$. Students frequently write $(1 + \ldots)^\gamma$ and lose full marks.
> 3. **Confusing static and stagnation conditions.** Always clarify which pressure/temperature you are computing. Stagnation conditions occur when the flow is brought to rest isentropically.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert temperature from °C to Kelvin.** The speed of sound formula requires **absolute temperature in Kelvin**. Using 30 °C directly gives an incorrect (lower) Mach number.
> 5. **Treating Mach number as a constant property of a vehicle.** Mach number depends on both the vehicle's velocity and the **local speed of sound** (which varies with altitude and temperature). A car at the same speed has a different Mach number in winter vs. summer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Mach Number (Module 1, PEAUT752)**

- **Definition:** $M = V/a$ — ratio of flow velocity to local speed of sound. **Dimensionless.**
- **Speed of sound in ideal gas:** $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ — depends on **temperature only** (not pressure or density).
- **Standard sea-level speed of sound:** $a_0 \approx 340.29$ m/s at $T = 288.15$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K).
- **Isentropic relations (master set):**
  * $T_0/T = 1 + [(\gamma - 1)/2]M^2$
  * $p_0/p = (T_0/T)^{\gamma/(\gamma - 1)}$
  * $\rho_0/\rho = (T_0/T)^{1/(\gamma - 1)}$
- **Flow regime classification:**
  * **Incompressible:** $M < 0.3$ (road vehicles, low-speed flows)
  * **Subsonic:** $0.3 \le M < 0.8$ (high-speed trains, turbomachinery)
  * **Transonic:** $0.8 \le M \le 1.2$ (wave drag onset, shock formation)
  * **Supersonic:** $1.2 < M \le 5.0$ (shock waves, Mach cone, $\beta = \sin^{-1}(1/M)$)
  * **Hypersonic:** $M > 5.0$ (real gas effects, dissociation, intense heating)
- **Critical Mach number ($M_{crit}$):** Free-stream M at which **local** flow first reaches sonic conditions. Lower for blunt bodies, higher for streamlined shapes.
- **Mach angle:** $\beta = \sin^{-1}(1/M)$ — defines the boundary of the Mach cone in supersonic flow.
- **Stagnation temperature at M = 2.5:** $T_0 = 2.25\,T$ — the air is heated to 2.25× ambient by ram compression.
- **Key engineering insight:** For most road vehicles (cars, bikes, trucks), $M < 0.3$, so incompressible flow theory is fully adequate. Compressibility corrections become essential only for supersonic cars, aerospace vehicles, and high-speed rail at very high speeds.
- **KTU favourite questions:** Derivation of speed of sound (7 marks), isentropic relation derivations (7 marks), numerical Mach number + stagnation pressure/temperature problems (14 marks).
- **Mnemonic for flow regimes:** **"I Scream To Stop Hyping"** → **I**ncompressible, **S**ubsonic, **T**ransonic, **S**upersonic, **H**ypersonic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laminar and Turbulent Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Laminar and Turbulent Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **fluid mechanics** and **vehicle aerodynamics**, fluid flow is broadly classified into two fundamental regimes based on the **Reynolds Number (Re)** — a dimensionless quantity that represents the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** within a fluid:

**Laminar Flow:** A highly ordered flow regime in which the fluid travels in smooth, parallel layers (laminae) with minimal macroscopic mixing between adjacent layers. Fluid particles move along well-defined **streamlines**, and momentum, heat, and mass transfer occur primarily through molecular diffusion (viscous shearing).

**Turbulent Flow:** A chaotic, stochastic flow regime characterized by **irregular fluctuations** in velocity and pressure, intense macroscopic mixing, and the formation of eddies of multiple scales. Momentum transfer occurs predominantly through **Reynolds stresses** (turbulent eddy diffusion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reynolds Number — The Master Classifier:**
> $$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho \, V \, L}{\mu} = \frac{V \, L}{\nu}$$
> where $\rho$ is fluid density, $V$ is characteristic velocity, $L$ is characteristic length, $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, and $\nu = \mu/\rho$ is the **kinematic viscosity**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two scenarios:

- **Laminar Flow Analogy — Marching Band in Formation:** Picture a marching band where every band member (fluid particle) walks in perfectly straight, parallel rows without crossing each other's path. Each row glides over the next one, with only minimal friction at the boundary. This is *laminar flow* — orderly, predictable, low-energy-dissipation.

- **Turbulent Flow Analogy — Crowded Metro Station at Rush Hour:** Now imagine people rushing in all directions through a packed station, bumping into each other, creating whirlpools of motion, sudden swirls, and eddies. Energy is dissipated violently through collisions and chaotic mixing. This is *turbulent flow* — disorderly, random, and energy-intensive.

> [!NOTE]
> **In Vehicle Aerodynamics:** The boundary layer that forms over a car's body, wing, or windshield may be laminar near the leading edge but almost always transitions to turbulent further downstream due to surface roughness, pressure gradients, and free-stream disturbances. Engineers often **deliberately trip the flow** into turbulence (using dimples, vortex generators, or turbulator strips) to delay boundary layer separation and improve aerodynamic performance.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Air at 25°C) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density of air | $\rho$ | **1.184 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| Dynamic viscosity of air | $\mu$ | **1.849 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s** | Pa·s |
| Kinematic viscosity of air | $\nu$ | **1.562 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** | m²/s |
| Density of water (20°C) | $\rho_w$ | **998 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| Kinematic viscosity of water | $\nu_w$ | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** | m²/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reynolds Number (Re_crit):** For flow over a **flat plate**, the experimentally observed critical value is **Re_x,crit ≈ 5 × 10⁵** (based on distance from leading edge). Below this value, the boundary layer remains laminar; above it, transition to turbulence typically begins.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

#### Step 1: The Governing Force Balance
At any point in a flowing fluid, two competing forces dictate the regime:
- **Inertial Forces** $\propto \rho V^2 L^2$ — resist changes in motion, promote chaos.
- **Viscous Forces** $\propto \mu V L$ — resist relative motion, promote order.

The dimensionless ratio is the **Reynolds Number**:
$$\text{Re} = \frac{\text{Inertial Forces}}{\text{Viscous Forces}} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}$$

#### Step 2: Flow Regime Classification
- **Re < 2300** (Pipe Flow): Laminar
- **2300 < Re < 4000**: Transitional
- **Re > 4000** (Pipe Flow): Turbulent
- **External Flow over Flat Plate:**
  - Re_x < 5 × 10⁵ → Laminar Boundary Layer
  - Re_x > 5 × 10⁵ → Turbulent Boundary Layer (after transition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always specify the basis (distance-based Re_x or length-based Re_L) and the geometry (internal pipe flow vs. external flat plate flow). Examiners penalize vague answers.

#### Step 3: The Mechanism of Transition
The transition from laminar to turbulent flow is governed by **linear stability theory** (Orr–Sommerfeld equation) and is amplified by:
1. **Free-stream turbulence intensity** (Tu > 0.1% can trigger early transition).
2. **Surface roughness** (roughness Reynolds number $k^+ > 5$ triggers turbulent spots).
3. **Adverse pressure gradients** (decelerating flow over a curved body).
4. **Acoustic disturbances** and **vibrations**.

#### Step 4: Characteristics of Each Regime

| Property | Laminar Flow | Turbulent Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Streamline pattern | Smooth, parallel | Chaotic, mixing |
| Velocity profile (pipe) | Parabolic | Blunter (logarithmic) |
| Wall shear stress | Lower | Higher (typically 5–10×) |
| Energy dissipation | Low | High |
| Mixing (heat/mass) | Molecular diffusion only | Dominated by eddy diffusion |
| Pressure drop (pipe) | $\Delta p \propto V$ | $\Delta p \propto V^{1.75 \text{ to } 2}$ |
| Flow predictability | Deterministic | Statistical (RANS/LES needed) |

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number (general) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{Re}_x = \dfrac{V x}{\nu}$ | Local Reynolds Number at distance $x$ from leading edge | Used for flat-plate boundary layers |
| 3 | $\text{Re}_L = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number based on full chord length $L$ | Used in airfoil/wing analysis |
| 4 | $\text{Re}_{D} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Pipe-flow Reynolds Number (D = diameter) | Internal flow classification |
| 5 | $u^+ = \dfrac{u}{u_\tau}$ | Dimensionless inner velocity | $u_\tau = \sqrt{\tau_w / \rho}$ is friction velocity |
| 6 | $y^+ = \dfrac{y u_\tau}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless wall distance | $y^+ < 5$: viscous sublayer; $y^+ > 30$: log layer |
| 7 | $u^+ = \dfrac{1}{\kappa} \ln(y^+) + C$ | Log-law of the wall | $\kappa \approx 0.41$, $C \approx 5.0$ |
| 8 | $\delta_{lam} \approx \dfrac{5.0 \, x}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}$ | Laminar BL thickness (Blasius) | Flat plate, $x$ from leading edge |
| 9 | $\delta_{turb} \approx \dfrac{0.37 \, x}{\text{Re}_x^{1/5}}$ | Turbulent BL thickness | Valid for $5 \times 10^5 \lt \text{Re}_x \lt 10^7$ |
| 10 | $C_{f,lam} = \dfrac{1.328}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_L}}$ | Laminar skin-friction coefficient (flat plate) | Blasius solution |
| 11 | $C_{f,turb} = \dfrac{0.074}{\text{Re}_L^{1/5}}$ | Turbulent skin-friction coefficient (flat plate) | Valid for $5 \times 10^5 \lt \text{Re}_L \lt 10^7$ |
| 12 | $\delta^* = \int_0^\infty \left(1 - \dfrac{u}{U}\right) dy$ | Displacement thickness | Mass-flow deficit |
| 13 | $\theta = \int_0^\infty \dfrac{u}{U}\left(1 - \dfrac{u}{U}\right) dy$ | Momentum thickness | Momentum deficit |
| 14 | $H = \dfrac{\delta^*}{\theta}$ | Shape factor | $H_{lam} \approx 2.59$, $H_{turb} \approx 1.3$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - In **race-car aerodynamics**, turbulent boundary layers resist separation better — exploited by adding **vortex generators** on rear wings.
> - In **Formula 1**, **boundary layer suction** is sometimes used to maintain laminar flow and reduce drag over the front wing.
> - In **commercial car design**, smooth underbody panels maintain laminar flow to reduce drag coefficient $C_D$.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Wing design exploits the laminar–turbulent transition; modern **Natural Laminar Flow (NLF)** airfoils delay transition to reduce drag.
- **Automotive:** Passenger cars cruise at **Re_L ≈ 1.5 × 10⁶ to 5 × 10⁶** (based on vehicle length), making the flow predominantly **turbulent**.
- **Wind Turbines:** Blade sections are designed with **laminar-flow airfoils** to maximize lift-to-drag ratio in clean inflow regions.
- **Pipelines:** Flow is kept laminar whenever possible to minimize pumping power costs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Number from Dimensional Analysis

Consider a flowing fluid past a body of characteristic length $L$. The pressure drop $\Delta p$ (or wall shear stress $\tau_w$) depends on:

$$\Delta p = f(\rho, V, L, \mu)$$

There are **5 variables** and **3 fundamental dimensions** (M, L, T). By the Buckingham Pi Theorem, the number of dimensionless groups is $5 - 3 = 2$.

**Step 1:** Choose repeating variables: $\rho, V, L$.

**Step 2:** Form the first Pi group using $\mu$:
$$\Pi_1 = \mu \cdot \rho^a V^b L^c$$

Demanding dimensional homogeneity:
$$\Pi_1 = [M L^{-1} T^{-1}] [M L^{-3}]^a [L T^{-1}]^b [L]^c$$

Solving the system:
- Mass: $1 + a = 0 \Rightarrow a = -1$
- Time: $-1 - b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -1$
- Length: $-1 - 3a + b + c = 0 \Rightarrow -1 + 3 - 1 + c = 0 \Rightarrow c = -1$

$$\Pi_1 = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho V L} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Re}}$$

**Step 3:** The second Pi group is the **Euler/Pressure Coefficient:**
$$\Pi_2 = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\rho V^2}$$

**Step 4:** Final functional relationship:
$$\dfrac{\Delta p}{\rho V^2} = f\left(\text{Re}\right) = f\left(\dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}\right)$$

This rigorously proves that Reynolds Number is the **only** independent dimensionless group governing dynamic similarity in Newtonian incompressible flow.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Blasius Boundary Layer Solution (Laminar Flat Plate)

For **steady, incompressible, 2-D laminar flow** over a flat plate, the governing equations reduce to the **Prandtl Boundary Layer Equations**:

$$u \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \nu \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}}$$

$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Step 1:** Introduce the **stream function** $\psi(x,y)$ such that $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$.

**Step 2:** Apply the **similarity transformation** proposed by Blasius:
$$\eta = y \sqrt{\dfrac{V_\infty}{\nu x}}, \quad f(\eta) = \dfrac{\psi}{\sqrt{\nu x V_\infty}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute partial derivatives:
$$\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = V_\infty f'(\eta), \quad \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\nu V_\infty}{x}}\left(\eta f' - f\right)$$

Therefore:
$$u = V_\infty f'(\eta), \quad v = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\nu V_\infty}{x}}\left(\eta f' - f\right)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the momentum equation. After extensive manipulation:
$$f''' + \dfrac{1}{2} f f'' = 0$$

**Step 5:** Boundary conditions:
- At $y = 0$ ($\eta = 0$): $u = v = 0 \Rightarrow f(0) = 0, f'(0) = 0$
- At $y \to \infty$ ($\eta \to \infty$): $u \to V_\infty \Rightarrow f'(\infty) = 1$

**Step 6:** Numerical solution yields the **wall shear stress**:
$$\tau_w = \mu \left.\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} = 0.332 \, \mu \, V_\infty \sqrt{\dfrac{V_\infty}{\nu x}} = \dfrac{0.332 \, \rho V_\infty^{2}}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}$$

**Step 7:** Local skin-friction coefficient:
$$c_f(x) = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2} = \dfrac{0.664}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}$$

**Step 8:** Average over the plate length $L$:
$$C_f = \dfrac{1}{L}\int_0^L c_f(x)\, dx = \dfrac{1.328}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_L}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Significance:** This derivation is a **board-exam favorite**. You may be asked to derive the Blasius equation $f''' + \tfrac{1}{2} f f'' = 0$ or to compute wall shear stress and skin-friction coefficient for a given Re.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model Question Type)

**Problem:** Air at 25°C flows over a flat plate of length $L = 1.5$ m at $V_\infty = 30$ m/s. Determine:
(a) The Reynolds number at $x = 0.5$ m.
(b) Whether the boundary layer is laminar or turbulent at this location.
(c) The wall shear stress.
(d) The total drag force on one side of the plate (laminar portion only).

**Given:**
- $\rho = 1.184$ kg/m³
- $\nu = 1.562 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- $\mu = 1.849 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s
- $V_\infty = 30$ m/s, $L = 1.5$ m, $x = 0.5$ m

**Solution (a): Reynolds Number at x = 0.5 m:**
$$\text{Re}_x = \dfrac{V_\infty \cdot x}{\nu} = \dfrac{30 \times 0.5}{1.562 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{15}{1.562 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.60 \times 10^{5}$$

**Solution (b): Flow Regime:**
Since $\text{Re}_x = 9.60 \times 10^{5} > 5 \times 10^{5}$ (critical value for flat plate), the boundary layer at this location is **turbulent** (transition has already occurred upstream).

**Solution (c): Wall Shear Stress (using turbulent formula, local):**
$$c_f(x) = \dfrac{0.0592}{\text{Re}_x^{1/5}} = \dfrac{0.0592}{(9.60 \times 10^{5})^{0.2}} = \dfrac{0.0592}{15.56} = 3.804 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\tau_w = c_f \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 = 3.804 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 1.184 \times 900$$

$$\tau_w = 3.804 \times 10^{-3} \times 532.8 = 2.027 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Solution (d): Total Drag (laminar over entire plate for comparison):**
$$C_{f,L} = \dfrac{1.328}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_L}} = \dfrac{1.328}{\sqrt{V_\infty L / \nu}} = \dfrac{1.328}{\sqrt{30 \times 1.5 / 1.562 \times 10^{-5}}}$$

$$\text{Re}_L = \dfrac{30 \times 1.5}{1.562 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.88 \times 10^{6}$$

$$C_{f,L} = \dfrac{1.328}{\sqrt{2.88 \times 10^{6}}} = \dfrac{1.328}{1697} = 7.82 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$D_L = C_{f,L} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 \cdot A = 7.82 \times 10^{-4} \times 532.8 \times (1 \times 1.5) = 0.625 \, \text{N}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Laminar vs Turbulent Flow — Velocity Profile Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flow Over a Flat Plate] --> B{Compare Re_x with Re_crit}
    B -- Re_x less than 5x10 to 5 --> C[LAMINAR BOUNDARY LAYER]
    B -- Re_x greater than 5x10 to 5 --> D[TURBULENT BOUNDARY LAYER]
    C --> C1[Smooth parabolic velocity profile]
    C --> C2[Velocity Gradient high at wall]
    C --> C3[Low wall shear stress]
    C --> C4[No cross-stream mixing]
    D --> D1[Blunt nearly logarithmic profile]
    D --> D2[Thick Buffer and Log Layers]
    D --> D3[High wall shear stress]
    D --> D4[Strong eddy mixing across streamlines]
    C1 --> E[Fluid Dynamics Outcome]
    D1 --> E
    C2 --> E
    D2 --> E
    C3 --> F[Engineering Implication]
    D3 --> F
    C4 --> F
    D4 --> F
    E --> E1[Lower momentum deficit theta]
    E --> E2[Higher shape factor H about 2.59]
    F --> F1[Vehicle experiences lower skin friction drag]
    F --> F2[Vehicle experiences higher drag but resists separation]
```

### 4.2 Reynolds Number Regime Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compute Re = rho V L over mu] --> B{Geometry?}
    B -- Internal Pipe Flow --> C{Compare Re_D with Thresholds}
    B -- External Flat Plate --> D{Compare Re_x with 5x10 to 5}
    C -- Re_D less than 2300 --> E1[Laminar Poiseuille Flow]
    C -- 2300 to 4000 --> E2[Transitional Flow Unstable]
    C -- Re_D greater than 4000 --> E3[Turbulent Flow]
    D -- Re_x less than 5x10 to 5 --> F1[Laminar Boundary Layer]
    D -- Re_x greater than 5x10 to 5 --> F2[Turbulent Boundary Layer]
    E1 --> G[Apply Parabolic Profile u = umax 1 minus r squared over R squared]
    E3 --> H[Apply Log Law u plus = 0.41 ln y plus + 5.0]
    F1 --> I[Use Blasius delta approx 5.0 x over sqrt Re_x]
    F2 --> J[Use 1 over 7th Power Law delta approx 0.37 x over Re_x to 0.2]
```

### 4.3 Boundary Layer Structure (Turbulent Case)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wall y equals 0] --> B[Viscous Sublayer y plus less than 5]
    B --> C[Buffer Layer 5 less than y plus less than 30]
    C --> D[Log Law Region 30 less than y plus less than 300]
    D --> E[Outer Wake Region y plus greater than 300]
    E --> F[Free Stream V infinity]
    B1[Linear Velocity Profile u plus equals y plus] --> B
    C1[Empirical Transition Profile] --> C
    D1[Logarithmic Profile u plus equals 2.44 ln y plus + 5.0] --> D
    E1[Velocity Deficit Law Wake Function] --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Diagrams:**
> - Box **A** represents the physical wall.
> - Each subsequent box represents a sub-layer of the turbulent boundary layer, transitioning from viscous-dominated (close to wall) to inertia-dominated (outer region).
> - The same conceptual structure applies — but without the buffer and log regions — for **laminar** boundary layers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Reynolds Number. Mention its significance in classifying flow regimes.**

**Model Answer:**

Reynolds Number is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow:
$$\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$$

**Significance:**
1. It determines the **flow regime** — whether the flow is laminar, transitional, or turbulent.
2. It governs **dynamic similarity** — two geometrically similar flows with the same Re exhibit identical flow behavior.
3. It serves as the **independent variable** in empirical correlations for drag, heat transfer, and pressure drop.
4. In vehicle aerodynamics, it helps predict boundary layer transition over car body panels.

(3 marks — definition 1, significance 2)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish between laminar and turbulent flow in terms of velocity profile, energy dissipation, and mixing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Laminar Flow | Turbulent Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Velocity profile (pipe) | Parabolic: $u = u_{max}(1 - r^2/R^2)$ | Flattened / logarithmic |
| Energy dissipation | Low (only viscous) | High (viscous + turbulent) |
| Mixing | Molecular diffusion only | Dominated by eddy diffusion |
| Streamlines | Smooth and parallel | Chaotic with eddies |

(3 marks — 1 mark per feature)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **laminar boundary layer thickness** over a flat plate using an **integral momentum analysis** approach. State the assumed velocity profile and boundary conditions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger car of length $L = 4.5$ m travels at $V = 90$ km/h in standard air. Compute (i) the Reynolds number based on length, (ii) the laminar boundary layer thickness at the trailing edge, and (iii) the average skin-friction drag coefficient. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Governing Equation:** Apply conservation of momentum to the control volume bounded by the plate, the BL edge, and a vertical station at $x$:

$$\dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho V_\infty^2} = \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}$$

where $\theta$ is the **momentum thickness**:
$$\theta = \int_0^{\delta} \dfrac{u}{V_\infty}\left(1 - \dfrac{u}{V_\infty}\right) dy$$

**Step 2 — Assumed Velocity Profile (Pohlhausen):** A fourth-order polynomial satisfying BCs:
$$\dfrac{u}{V_\infty} = 2\eta - 2\eta^3 + \eta^4, \quad \eta = \dfrac{y}{\delta}$$

**Step 3 — Boundary Conditions:**
- At $y = 0$: $u = 0$, $\partial u/\partial y = 0$ (no-slip, no pressure gradient)
- At $y = \delta$: $u = V_\infty$, $\partial u/\partial y = 0$ (smooth merging)

**Step 4 — Compute Momentum Thickness:**
$$\theta = \delta \int_0^1 (2\eta - 2\eta^3 + \eta^4)(1 - 2\eta + 2\eta^3 - \eta^4)\, d\eta$$

After integration:
$$\theta = \dfrac{37}{315}\,\delta \approx 0.1175\,\delta$$

**Step 5 — Wall Shear Stress:**
$$\tau_w = \mu \left.\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} = \dfrac{2\mu V_\infty}{\delta}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into Momentum Equation:**
$$\dfrac{2\mu V_\infty}{\rho V_\infty^2 \delta} = \dfrac{d\theta}{dx} = \dfrac{37}{315}\dfrac{d\delta}{dx}$$

$$\dfrac{2\nu}{V_\infty \delta} = \dfrac{37}{315}\dfrac{d\delta}{dx}$$

**Step 7 — Separate Variables and Integrate:**
$$\delta\, d\delta = \dfrac{2 \times 315}{37}\dfrac{\nu}{V_\infty}\, dx$$

$$\dfrac{\delta^2}{2} = \dfrac{630}{37}\dfrac{\nu x}{V_\infty}$$

**Step 8 — Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\delta = 5.83\sqrt{\dfrac{\nu x}{V_\infty}} = \dfrac{5.83\, x}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}}$$

This matches the Blasius exact solution ($\delta \approx 5.0\,x/\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}$) to within ~15%, validating the integral method. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $L = 4.5$ m, $V = 90$ km/h = $25$ m/s
- $\nu = 1.562 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s (standard air at 25°C)

**(i) Reynolds Number based on Length:**
$$\text{Re}_L = \dfrac{V L}{\nu} = \dfrac{25 \times 4.5}{1.562 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{112.5}{1.562 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.20 \times 10^{6}$$

**(ii) Laminar BL Thickness at Trailing Edge:**

Using $\delta_{lam} = 5.0 x / \sqrt{\text{Re}_x}$ at $x = L$:
$$\delta_{lam}(L) = \dfrac{5.0 \times 4.5}{\sqrt{7.20 \times 10^{6}}} = \dfrac{22.5}{2683.3} = 8.39 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} = 8.39\,\text{mm}$$

**(iii) Average Skin-Friction Coefficient (Laminar):**
$$C_{f,L} = \dfrac{1.328}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_L}} = \dfrac{1.328}{\sqrt{7.20 \times 10^{6}}} = \dfrac{1.328}{2683.3} = 4.95 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Note to Examiner:** Since $\text{Re}_L = 7.20 \times 10^{6} \gg 5 \times 10^{5}$, the actual flow is **predominantly turbulent**. The computed values are the *laminar reference* values only. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of transition** from laminar to turbulent flow over a smooth flat plate. Discuss the role of Tollmien–Schlichting waves, surface roughness, and free-stream turbulence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **laminar and turbulent boundary layer velocity profiles**, skin-friction coefficients, and displacement thickness for a flat plate of length $L = 2$ m at free-stream velocity $V_\infty = 20$ m/s. Take $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Initial Laminar State:** Just downstream of the leading edge, the boundary layer is laminar and stable to small perturbations. Disturbances are damped by viscous action.

**Step 2 — Onset of Instability (T-S Waves):** At a critical Re_x ≈ 5 × 10⁵, infinitesimal disturbances begin to grow as **Tollmien–Schlichting (T-S) waves** — described by the **Orr–Sommerfeld equation**:
$$(U - c)(\phi'' - \alpha^2\phi) - U''\phi = \dfrac{1}{i\alpha \text{Re}}\left(\phi''' - 2\alpha^2\phi' + \alpha^4\phi\right)$$

where $U(y)$ is the base Blasius profile, $c$ is the wave speed, $\alpha$ is the wave number.

**Step 3 — Amplification Region:** Between the **lower neutral point** ($Re_x \approx 10^5$) and the **upper neutral point** ($Re_x \approx 5 \times 10^5$), T-S waves amplify exponentially. The amplitude grows by a factor of $e^{N}$, where $N$ is the **amplification factor** (e^N criterion, $N \approx 9$ for natural transition).

**Step 4 — Nonlinear Breakdown:** Above the critical Re, nonlinear effects produce three-dimensional **turbulent spots** that spread laterally at an angle of ~10° to the free-stream direction, eventually merging into a fully turbulent boundary layer.

**Step 5 — Role of Surface Roughness:** A roughness element of height $k$ is characterized by the **roughness Reynolds number**:
$$k^+ = \dfrac{k u_\tau}{\nu}$$

If $k^+ > 5$, the element trips the flow directly into turbulence, bypassing the T-S wave stage.

**Step 6 — Role of Free-Stream Turbulence (FST):** FST intensity $Tu = u'/U_\infty$ above 0.1% advances transition by exciting T-S waves prematurely. Typical values: wind tunnel $Tu \approx 0.05\%$, atmospheric boundary layer $Tu \approx 0.5$–$5\%$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:** $L = 2$ m, $V_\infty = 20$ m/s, $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s

**Step 1 — Compute Reynolds Number:**
$$\text{Re}_L = \dfrac{V_\infty L}{\nu} = \dfrac{20 \times 2}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.67 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 2 — Laminar Properties:**
- Velocity profile: $u/V_\infty = f'(\eta)$, Blasius tabulated solution, parabolic-like shape.
- BL thickness: $\delta_L = 5.0 L / \sqrt{\text{Re}_L} = 5.0 \times 2 / \sqrt{2.67 \times 10^6} = 10 / 1634 = 6.12 \times 10^{-3}$ m = **6.12 mm**
- Skin-friction coefficient: $C_{f,lam} = 1.328 / \sqrt{\text{Re}_L} = 1.328 / 1634 = 8.13 \times 10^{-4}$
- Displacement thickness: $\delta^* = 1.721 x / \sqrt{\text{Re}_x}$ → at $x = L$: $\delta^*_L = 1.721 \times 2 / 1634 = 2.11 \times 10^{-3}$ m

**Step 3 — Turbulent Properties:**
- Velocity profile: $u/V_\infty = (y/\delta)^{1/7}$ (Power-law approximation).
- BL thickness: $\delta_T = 0.37 L / \text{Re}_L^{1/5} = 0.37 \times 2 / (2.67 \times 10^6)^{0.2}$
  $\text{Re}_L^{0.2} = (2.67 \times 10^6)^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \ln(2.67 \times 10^6)} = e^{0.2 \times 14.80} = e^{2.96} = 19.30$
  $\delta_T = 0.74 / 19.30 = 3.83 \times 10^{-2}$ m = **38.3 mm**
- Skin-friction coefficient: $C_{f,turb} = 0.074 / \text{Re}_L^{1/5} = 0.074 / 19.30 = 3.83 \times 10^{-3}$
- Displacement thickness: $\delta^*_T \approx \delta_T / 8 = 38.3/8 = 4.79 \times 10^{-3}$ m

**Step 4 — Comparison Table:**

| Property | Laminar | Turbulent | Ratio (T/L) |
|---|---|---|---|
| BL thickness $\delta$ | 6.12 mm | 38.3 mm | 6.26× |
| Skin friction $C_f$ | $8.13 \times 10^{-4}$ | $3.83 \times 10^{-3}$ | 4.71× |
| Displacement thickness $\delta^*$ | 2.11 mm | 4.79 mm | 2.27× |

**Observations:**
1. The turbulent BL is **~6× thicker** than the laminar one.
2. Turbulent skin friction is **~5× higher**, meaning **more drag**.
3. The shape factor $H = \delta^*/\theta$ drops from **~2.59 (laminar)** to **~1.3 (turbulent)**, indicating a fuller velocity profile and greater resistance to separation. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong Reynolds Number formula:** Using $\rho V D / \mu$ for external flow (where $D$ doesn't apply). Always verify geometry.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** Mixing km/h with m in velocity–length products. Convert all to SI units **first**.
>
> 3. **Confusing local vs. average skin friction:** $c_f(x) = 0.664/\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}$ is **local**; $C_f = 1.328/\sqrt{\text{Re}_L}$ is **average**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
>
> 4. **Using laminar formulas in the turbulent regime:** Always check $\text{Re}_x$ vs. $\text{Re}_{crit}$ before selecting formulas.
>
> 5. **Not specifying assumptions:** Blasius solution assumes (i) steady, (ii) incompressible, (iii) zero pressure gradient, (iv) smooth plate. State them explicitly.
>
> 6. **Unit inconsistency in shear stress:** Use Pa (N/m²), not N/m. Many students lose a mark here.
>
> 7. **Omitting the Reynolds number justification:** When asked to identify the flow regime, *always show the calculation*, not just state the conclusion.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Reynolds Number** is the **master classifier** of flow regimes; always compute it first.
- **Laminar flow** → low drag, low mixing, susceptible to separation; described by **Blasius** (1908) exact solution.
- **Turbulent flow** → high drag, intense mixing, resistant to separation; described by **Power-law** or **Log-law** profiles.
- **Critical Re** for flat plate transition: $\text{Re}_{x,crit} \approx 5 \times 10^{5}$ (natural transition), reducible to $\text{Re}_{x,crit} \approx 2 \times 10^{5}$ with surface roughness.
- **Pipe flow thresholds:** $\text{Re}_D < 2300$ (laminar), $> 4000$ (turbulent).
- **Skin friction scales as:** $C_{f,lam} \propto \text{Re}^{-1/2}$, $C_{f,turb} \propto \text{Re}^{-1/5}$.
- **Boundary layer thickness scales as:** $\delta_{lam} \propto x / \sqrt{\text{Re}_x}$, $\delta_{turb} \propto x / \text{Re}_x^{1/5}$.
- **Displacement thickness** $\delta^*$ represents the **mass-flow deficit**; **momentum thickness** $\theta$ represents the **momentum deficit**.
- **Shape factor** $H = \delta^*/\theta \approx 2.59$ (laminar) and $\approx 1.3$ (turbulent) — a **diagnostic indicator** of flow state.
- **In vehicle aerodynamics:** Cars cruise at $\text{Re}_L \sim 10^6$–$10^7$, predominantly **turbulent**. Deliberate **flow tripping** via dimples, vortex generators, or seam welds is standard practice.
- **Dynamic similarity:** Two flows are dynamically similar if they share the same **Re** (and other relevant dimensionless groups like Mach, Froude, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hypersonic Flows
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hypersonic Flows

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hypersonic Flow** is defined as the flow regime in which the **Mach number (M) is greater than 5**. In this regime, the flow exhibits fundamentally different physical behavior from subsonic, transonic, supersonic, and even low-supersonic flow. Characteristic phenomena include: an extremely thin shock layer adjacent to the body, a strong interaction between the shock wave and the boundary layer (viscous interaction), significant aerodynamic heating, the onset of **real gas effects** (vibrational excitation, dissociation, ionization), and the applicability of the **Mach number independence principle** and the **Newtonian sine-squared law** for pressure estimation.

### 1.2 Velocity Classification within Compressible Flow

The compressible flow regime is broadly divided based on the free-stream Mach number $M_\infty$:

| Regime | Mach Number Range | Dominant Physical Phenomenon |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Subsonic | $M_\infty < 0.8$ | Incompressible behavior, low compressibility |
| Transonic | $0.8 \le M_\infty \le 1.2$ | Mixed subsonic/supersonic flow, shock formation |
| Supersonic | $1.2 < M_\infty \le 5.0$ | Attached shock waves, Prandtl-Meyer expansions |
| **Hypersonic** | $**M_\infty > 5.0**$ | Thin shock layer, strong shock, real gas effects |
| High Hypersonic | $M_\infty > 10$ | Strong thermochemical non-equilibrium, plasma effects |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mach 5 Car" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Why Hypersonic is NOT just "Fast Supersonic":**
> Imagine throwing a small pebble (M = 0.5) versus firing a rifle bullet (M = 2) versus launching a re-entering Space Shuttle Orbiter (M = 25). The pebble barely disturbs the air. The bullet creates a sharp conical shock. The Orbiter, however, is moving so fast that the air molecules cannot "get out of the way" — they are violently compressed into a paper-thin layer just millimeters off the surface, generating temperatures of **1,500 °C** (hot enough to glow cherry red). This **intense, thin, heated shock layer** is the *defining fingerprint* of hypersonic flow. It is the qualitative — not just quantitative — change that separates hypersonics from supersonics.

### 1.4 Defining Characteristics of Hypersonic Flow

1. **Thin Shock Layer:** The standoff distance $\delta_{shock}$ between the body and the bow shock becomes a small fraction of the body length, $\delta_{shock}/L \ll 1$.
2. **Strong Shock:** The shock is very strong; downstream Mach number $M_2 \approx \sqrt{(k-1)/(2k)} \to$ small value.
3. **Newtonian Limit:** The flow "hugs" the body surface, behaving like a stream of high-speed particles (Newton's original corpuscular theory of light).
4. **Viscous–Inviscid Interaction:** The thickened boundary layer "swallows" a significant portion of the thin shock layer, coupling inviscid and viscous regions.
5. **Real Gas Effects:** Stagnation temperatures exceed vibrational threshold (~800 K for N₂), leading to **chemical reactions** and **energy mode excitation**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is Hypersonic Flow Treated as a Separate Subject?

In supersonic flow (M ≈ 2–5), analytical methods such as **linearized supersonic theory** and the **method of characteristics** work well because the disturbance from the body is small compared to the free-stream. At M > 5:

- The flow disturbance (entropy, vorticity) generated by the shock is **no longer small** — the linearization assumption breaks down.
- The shock lies **very close to the body**, making "shock fitting" (instead of shock capturing) essential.
- The **aerodynamic heating** becomes the dominant design constraint, not just the lift or drag force.
- The **simplified physical models** (perfect gas) fail — vibrational and chemical kinetics must be introduced.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** In KTU answers, always justify *why* a particular hypersonic approximation is invoked — e.g., "Because $M_\infty \gg 1$, the tangential component of velocity is approximately equal to the free-stream velocity, leading to the Newtonian approximation."

### 2.2 Hypersonic Similarity Parameters

A key theoretical result is that hypersonic flows over geometrically similar bodies produce identical force and moment coefficients when the relevant **similarity parameters** are matched. The two classical parameters are:

**A) Hypersonic Similarity Parameter (Tsien, 1946):**
$$K = M_\infty \, \tau$$
where $\tau$ is the **relative thickness ratio** (e.g., for a diamond airfoil, $\tau = t/c$, thickness-to-chord ratio). For two hypersonic flows to be dynamically similar, they must share the same value of $K$.

**B) Mach Number Independence Principle (Tsien, 1946):**
At sufficiently high $M_\infty$ (typically $M_\infty > 6$ for slender bodies), the pressure coefficient $C_p$ and the force/moment coefficients depend only on $\tau$ and $\gamma$, and become **independent of $M_\infty$**. This holds for **slender, two-dimensional or axisymmetric bodies** at angle of attack.

### 2.3 The Newtonian Sine-Squared Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Original Concept (1687):** In a stream of high-speed particles, a surface element imparts momentum only along its inward normal. The tangential component is unchanged (no friction). Hence, pressure acts only where flow is *decelerated* — i.e., on windward-facing surfaces.

Mathematically, on a surface element with outward unit normal $\hat{n}$ making angle $\theta$ with the free-stream velocity $\vec{V}_\infty$, the **Newtonian pressure coefficient** is:

$$C_{p,N} = 2 \sin^2\theta$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between the freestream and the local body surface (also equal to the angle between $\hat{n}$ and $\vec{V}_\infty$). The factor of 2 arises from the dynamic pressure definition.

**Modified Newtonian Theory** (used for blunt bodies where the normal shock stands off):
$$C_p = C_{p,\text{max}} \sin^2\theta$$
with $C_{p,\text{max}}$ evaluated from the **normal shock relations** at the stagnation point:
$$C_{p,\text{max}} = \frac{2}{\gamma M_\infty^2} \left[ \left(\frac{(\gamma+1)^2 M_\infty^2}{4\gamma M_\infty^2 - 2(\gamma-1)}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{1-\gamma + 2\gamma M_\infty^2}{(\gamma+1)} - 1 \right]$$

For an ideal perfect gas at $M_\infty \to \infty$, $C_{p,\text{max}} \to (\gamma+1)^{(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)} \cdot (2/(\gamma-1))^{\!1/(\gamma-1)} \cdot (1/\gamma)$. For $\gamma = 1.4$, this limit gives $C_{p,\text{max}} \to 1.839$.

### 2.4 Tangent-Wedge and Tangent-Cone Approximations

For **slender wedges and cones** at small angles, the pressure on the windward face can be approximated by the **oblique shock relations** applied to the local flow deflection angle $\theta$ (which is the same as the wedge or cone half-angle). The exact linearized result (valid for small $\theta$ and $M \gg 1$) is:

$$C_p \approx \frac{2\theta^2}{\sqrt{M_\infty^2 - 1}} + \frac{2\theta}{M_\infty} \cdot \frac{1}{(M_\infty^2 - 1)^{1/4}} \quad \text{(for small } \theta\text{)}$$

The dominant hypersonic scaling is:
$$C_p \propto \frac{\theta^2}{\sqrt{M_\infty^2 - 1}} \quad \xrightarrow{M_\infty \gg 1} \quad \frac{\theta^2}{M_\infty}$$

This $\theta^2 / M_\infty$ scaling is the hallmark of hypersonic pressure fields.

### 2.5 Stagnation Point Heating — Sutton-Graves Correlation

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Implication:** For atmospheric re-entry vehicles (Apollo, Space Shuttle, Mars EDL), the maximum heat flux occurs at the **stagnation point** of the blunt nose. The Sutton-Graves (1950) empirical correlation is the cornerstone of hypersonic thermal protection system (TPS) design.

The convective heat transfer rate at the stagnation point of a blunt body of nose radius $R_N$ in a perfect gas is:

$$\dot{q}_s = K_s \sqrt{\frac{\rho_\infty}{R_N}} \, V_\infty^3$$

where $K_s$ is the **Sutton-Graves constant**, typically $K_s = 1.7415 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{W·s}^{1/2}\cdot\text{m}^{-1/2}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$ for air, derived from boundary layer theory with a constant property assumption and $\sqrt{\rho_\infty} \cdot V_\infty^3$ is the **hypersonic scaling** for stagnation point heating.

The total **stagnation temperature** (used in heating correlation) is:
$$T_0 = T_\infty \left( 1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M_\infty^2 \right)$$

### 2.6 Real Gas Effects — When the Perfect Gas Assumption Fails

At $M_\infty > 5$ in Earth's atmosphere, $T_0$ can easily exceed **2,000 K**. At these temperatures, air is no longer a calorically perfect gas:

| Temperature Range | Excited Phenomenon | Modeling Approach |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $T < 800$ K | Rotational only | Perfect gas, constant $c_p$, $\gamma$ |
| $800 < T < 2,500$ K | Vibrational excitation of N₂, O₂ | Equilibrium real gas, $c_p(T)$, $\gamma(T)$ |
| $2,500 < T < 4,000$ K | Dissociation of O₂, then N₂ | Chemical non-equilibrium (finite-rate reactions) |
| $T > 4,000$ K | Ionization, plasma formation | Thermo-chemical non-equilibrium, Navier-Stokes with multi-species |

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
| :- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $C_{p,N} = 2\sin^2\theta$ | Newtonian pressure coefficient (slender bodies) |
| 2 | $C_p = C_{p,\text{max}} \sin^2\theta$ | Modified Newtonian (blunt bodies) |
| 3 | $K = M_\infty \cdot \tau$ | Hypersonic similarity parameter (Tsien) |
| 4 | $C_p \sim \theta^2 / \sqrt{M_\infty^2 - 1}$ | Tangent-wedge/Newtonian scaling |
| 5 | $T_0 = T_\infty [1 + \tfrac{\gamma-1}{2} M_\infty^2]$ | Stagnation temperature (perfect gas) |
| 6 | $\dot{q}_s = K_s \sqrt{\rho_\infty / R_N} \, V_\infty^3$ | Sutton-Graves stagnation heat flux |
| 7 | $M_2^2 = \frac{(\gamma-1)M_1^2 + 2}{2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1)}$ | Normal shock downstream Mach |
| 8 | $\dfrac{p_2}{p_1} = \dfrac{2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1)}{\gamma+1}$ | Normal shock pressure ratio |
| 9 | $\delta_{shock} \sim \dfrac{L}{M_\infty^2}$ | Shock standoff distance scaling |
| 10 | $\beta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{M_1}\right)$ | Mach angle (weak shock limit) |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mach Number Independence Principle (Qualitative Proof)

**Step 1 — Start with the linearized supersonic potential equation** (for small disturbances $\phi$):
$$(1 - M_\infty^2)\phi_{xx} + \phi_{yy} + \phi_{zz} = 0$$

For supersonic flow ($M > 1$), this is hyperbolic. Introduce the **Prandtl-Glauert transformation** $\tilde{\phi} = \phi \cdot \sqrt{\vert M_\infty^2 - 1\vert}$ for the dependent variable.

**Step 2 — Apply the hypersonic limit** $M_\infty \to \infty$. The wave fronts from a point disturbance become ever closer together, forming a continuous envelope (the Mach cone with angle $\mu = \sin^{-1}(1/M_\infty) \to 0$).

**Step 3 — Define stretched coordinates** appropriate to the hypersonic limit:
$$\xi = x, \quad \eta = M_\infty \, y, \quad \zeta = M_\infty \, z$$

Substituting into the full potential equation and keeping the **leading-order terms** in $M_\infty$:

$$\phi_{yy} + \phi_{zz} - M_\infty^2 \phi_{xx} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tilde{\phi}_{\eta\eta} + \tilde{\phi}_{\zeta\zeta} = \tilde{\phi}_{\xi\xi}$$

This is **Tracy's equation** (1963), the small-disturbance hypersonic potential equation, which is a wave equation in the stretched coordinates.

**Step 4 — Apply the body boundary condition** on a thin body $y = \tau f(x)$:

The normal velocity condition $V_n = 0$ at the body surface gives, at the leading order:
$$v(x,0) / V_\infty = \tau f'(x) \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_\infty \tilde{\phi}_\eta = \tau f'(\xi) M_\infty$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the scaling.** Combining Steps 3 and 4, the governing PDE and boundary conditions all depend on $M_\infty$ only through the **product $K = M_\infty \tau$**. Therefore, the **pressure coefficient** $C_p$ is a function of $K$ alone, not of $M_\infty$ and $\tau$ separately:

$$C_p = C_p(K, \gamma)$$

This is the **Hypersonic Similarity Rule**. If $K$ is held constant, the pressure distribution (and hence forces, moments) is identical, regardless of the individual values of $M_\infty$ and $\tau$.

**Step 6 — Take the further limit** $K \to \infty$ (very thick body or very high Mach number). The $C_p$ field becomes a function of **local angle** $\theta$ only — independent of $M_\infty$. This is the **Mach Number Independence Principle**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Newtonian Sine-Squared Law

**Step 1 — Momentum flux of impinging particles.** In Newton's corpuscular model, particles travel in straight lines parallel to $\vec{V}_\infty$ with speed $V_\infty$ and mass density $\rho_\infty$. The mass flux crossing a unit area of surface with outward normal $\hat{n}$ (where $\hat{n} \cdot \hat{V}_\infty = \cos\theta$) is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho_\infty V_\infty \cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Momentum transfer to the surface.** Only the **normal component** of momentum is destroyed upon impact (the tangential component slides off unchanged, neglecting friction). The normal momentum flux impinging on the surface per unit area per unit time is:

$$\dot{m} \cdot V_{n,\text{in}} = (\rho_\infty V_\infty \cos\theta)(V_\infty \cos\theta) = \rho_\infty V_\infty^2 \cos^2\theta$$

**Step 3 — Identify the pressure.** By Newton's second law, this is exactly the surface pressure $p$ on the windward side. The pressure coefficient is:

$$C_p = \frac{p - p_\infty}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_\infty V_\infty^2} = \frac{\rho_\infty V_\infty^2 \cos^2\theta}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_\infty V_\infty^2} = 2\cos^2\theta$$

**Step 4 — Reparametrize using the surface angle.** Let $\theta$ be the angle between the freestream direction and the **tangent to the body surface**. Then the normal makes angle $\theta$ with the freestream, and $\cos^2\theta = \sin^2(\pi/2 - \theta)$... more cleanly, defining $\theta$ as the local body slope (angle between body tangent and freestream), the **cosine of the angle between the surface normal and freestream** is exactly $\sin\theta$, giving:

$$\boxed{\,C_{p,N} = 2\sin^2\theta\,}$$

This is the famous **Newtonian sine-squared law**. For a flat plate at angle of attack $\alpha$, $\theta = \alpha$ on the windward face, and $C_p = 2\sin^2\alpha$ (constant over the plate).

### 3.3 Derivation: Stagnation Point Heat Flux (Sutton-Graves)

**Step 1 — Order-of-magnitude boundary layer analysis.** The thermal boundary layer thickness $\delta_T$ near a stagnation point scales as:
$$\delta_T \sim \sqrt{\nu \, t} \sim \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\rho} \cdot \frac{R_N}{V_\infty}}$$

where $t \sim R_N / V_\infty$ is the residence time of a fluid particle near the nose, and $\nu$ is the kinematic viscosity.

**Step 2 — Apply Fourier's law of heat conduction.** The heat flux from the hot gas to the cold wall is:
$$\dot{q} \sim k \frac{T_0 - T_w}{\delta_T}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the gas thermal conductivity.** For a perfect gas with constant Prandtl number $\text{Pr} = \mu c_p / k \approx 0.7$ for air, we have $k = \mu c_p / \text{Pr}$. Substituting the stagnation enthalpy $h_0 = c_p (T_0 - T_w)$ and using $\mu \propto T^\omega$ with $\omega \approx 0.5$ to 1:

$$\dot{q}_s \sim \frac{\mu c_p (T_0 - T_w)}{\delta_T \, \text{Pr}}$$

**Step 4 — Combine with the reference temperature scaling** and convert to free-stream quantities. Using the Fay-Riddell (1958) result, modified by Sutton-Graves, the leading-order dependence is:

$$\dot{q}_s = K_s \sqrt{\frac{\rho_\infty}{R_N}} \, V_\infty^3$$

where $K_s = 1.7415 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{W·s}^{1/2}\cdot\text{m}^{-1/2}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$ for Earth air.

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation.** Notice that $\dot{q}_s$ scales as $V_\infty^3$ — a vehicle moving at M = 10 experiences $\approx (10/5)^3 = 8$ times more heating than at M = 5. The nose radius $R_N$ is **inside the square root** — doubling the nose radius reduces heating by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$. This is why **blunt bodies** (large $R_N$) are aerodynamically preferred for re-entry, despite their higher drag: the *thermal* benefit dominates.

### 3.4 Derivation: Hypersonic Limit of Normal Shock Relations

**Step 1 — Start with the strong shock limit** ($M_1 \to \infty$):
$$\frac{p_2}{p_1} \approx \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1} M_1^2 \quad \text{(strong shock pressure ratio)}$$

**Step 2 — Downstream Mach number in the strong shock limit:**
$$M_2^2 = \frac{(\gamma-1)M_1^2 + 2}{2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1)} \quad \xrightarrow{M_1 \to \infty} \quad \frac{\gamma-1}{2\gamma}$$

For $\gamma = 1.4$: $M_2 \to \sqrt{0.4/2.8} = \sqrt{1/7} \approx 0.378$.

**Step 3 — Density ratio across a strong shock:**
$$\frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} = \frac{(\gamma+1)M_1^2}{(\gamma-1)M_1^2 + 2} \quad \xrightarrow{M_1 \to \infty} \quad \frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}$$

For $\gamma = 1.4$: $\rho_2/\rho_1 \to 6.0$. This is the **maximum possible density jump** in a perfect gas — a fundamental hypersonic invariant.

**Step 4 — Temperature ratio:**
$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{[2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1)][(\gamma-1)M_1^2 + 2]}{(\gamma+1)^2 M_1^2} \quad \xrightarrow{M_1 \to \infty} \quad \frac{2\gamma(\gamma-1)}{(\gamma+1)^2} M_1^2$$

The post-shock temperature grows **quadratically with $M_1$**, explaining the intense heating and the need for real gas models.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Approximation Validity Domains

| Approximation | Validity Regime | Typical Error | KTU Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Newtonian $2\sin^2\theta$ | Slender, $M_\infty \to \infty$ | 10–30% on flat surfaces | Quick $C_p$ on windward faces |
| Modified Newtonian | Blunt bodies, $M_\infty > 3$ | 5–15% on stagnation region | Re-entry capsule design |
| Tangent-Wedge (exact) | $M_\infty > 3$, $\theta < 25°$ | < 5% | Sharp-nose vehicles (e.g., missiles) |
| Tangent-Cone (exact) | $M_\infty > 3$, $\theta_c < 25°$ | < 5% | Cone-derived waveriders |
| Mach Number Independence | $M_\infty > 6$, slender | 3–10% | Parametric trade studies |
| Linearized Hypersonic | $\tau \ll 1$, $M_\infty \tau = K$ fixed | O($K^2$) | Theoretical similarity |
| Perfect Gas | $T_0 < 800$ K | < 5% in $C_p$ | $M_\infty < 5$ at altitude |
| Equilibrium Real Gas | $T_0 < 2,500$ K | 5–15% | High-M re-entry trajectory points |
| Non-Equilibrium Chemistry | $T_0 > 2,500$ K | Reference standard | Hypersonic flight CFD validation |
