# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are framed per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** (3-mark short answer and 14-mark long answer with internal choice). Bloom's taxonomy levels are indicated in brackets.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP Set A]`
**(3 Marks) [Bloom Level: Understand]**
**Define flow separation. State the mathematical condition for the onset of separation and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

Flow separation is the phenomenon in which the boundary layer detaches from a solid surface due to a sufficiently strong adverse pressure gradient, causing the wall shear stress to vanish and the near-wall fluid to reverse direction.

The mathematical condition for the separation point is:

$$\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0} = 0$$

**Physical significance:** At this point, the viscous sublayer can no longer supply enough momentum to overcome the rising pressure in the flow direction. Just downstream, the wall gradient becomes negative, indicating reverse flow. The separation point is the **upstream boundary of the wake** and the **primary origin of pressure drag** on bluff bodies such as road vehicles.

> [!TIP]
> Examiner's Note: A complete answer must include (i) the definition, (ii) the formula, AND (iii) the physical interpretation. Missing the third part costs 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP Set B]`
**(3 Marks) [Bloom Level: Remember / Understand]**
**Differentiate between favourable and adverse pressure gradients. Which one causes flow separation, and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Favourable Pressure Gradient | Adverse Pressure Gradient |
|---|---|---|
| Sign of $dP/dx$ | Negative ($dP/dx < 0$) | Positive ($dP/dx > 0$) |
| Edge velocity | Accelerating ($dU_e/dx > 0$) | Decelerating ($dU_e/dx < 0$) |
| Effect on boundary layer | Thins, stabilizes (laminar → turbulent transition delayed) | Thickens, destabilizes, may separate |
| Wall shear stress | Increases | Decreases, can reach zero |
| Geometry example | Front portion of an airfoil, hood of a car | Aft of car's roof, leeward of wing's max thickness |

**Adverse pressure gradients cause flow separation** because the decelerating outer flow requires the near-wall slow fluid to "climb" a rising pressure hill. With insufficient kinetic energy, the boundary layer cannot sustain itself against the wall and detaches.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice Option A]`
**(14 Marks) [Bloom Level: Apply / Analyze]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Starting from the boundary layer x-momentum equation, derive the mathematical condition that defines the point of flow separation. Clearly state the assumptions used in your derivation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible, 2D flow.
2. Boundary layer approximations: $\partial/\partial x \ll \partial/\partial y$ and $v \ll u$.
3. Newtonian fluid: $\tau = \mu\, \partial u/\partial y$.

**Step 1 — Write the boundary layer x-momentum equation:**

$$\rho \left( u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right) = -\frac{dP}{dx} + \mu\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the no-slip condition at the wall ($y = 0$):**

At $y = 0$: $u = 0$ and $v = 0$. The convective terms vanish, leaving:

$$0 = -\frac{dP}{dx} + \mu \left(\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}\right)_{y=0}$$

**Step 3 — Express the wall shear stress in terms of velocity gradient:**

$$\tau_w = \mu \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate the boundary layer equation with respect to $y$ and evaluate at $y = 0$:**

Differentiating the original BL equation and applying the chain rule, the inertial terms remain bounded. The key conclusion is that if $dP/dx > 0$ (adverse), the wall curvature $(\partial^2 u / \partial y^2)_{y=0} > 0$.

**Step 5 — State the separation criterion:**

The separation point is the unique location along the surface where the wall shear stress first becomes zero:

$$\boxed{\tau_w = \mu \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{dP}{dx} > 0 \ \text{at that point}}$$

Immediately downstream, $(\partial u/\partial y)_{y=0} < 0$, indicating reverse flow and the formation of a separated region.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**For a laminar boundary layer over a flat plate, the velocity profile is approximated as $\dfrac{u}{U_e} = 2\eta - \eta^2$, where $\eta = y/\delta$. Determine: (i) the displacement thickness $\delta^*$, (ii) the momentum thickness $\theta$, and (iii) the shape factor $H$. Comment on the value of $H$ relative to the Blasius exact solution.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Displacement thickness $\delta^*$:**

$$\delta^* = \int_0^{\delta}\left(1 - \frac{u}{U_e}\right)dy = \delta\int_0^1(1 - 2\eta + \eta^2)\,d\eta$$

$$= \delta\left[\eta - \eta^2 + \frac{\eta^3}{3}\right]_0^1 = \delta\left(1 - 1 + \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{\delta}{3}$$

**(ii) Momentum thickness $\theta$:**

$$\theta = \int_0^{\delta}\frac{u}{U_e}\left(1 - \frac{u}{U_e}\right)dy = \delta\int_0^1(2\eta - \eta^2)(1 - 2\eta + \eta^2)\,d\eta$$

Expanding the integrand:

$$(2\eta - \eta^2)(1 - 2\eta + \eta^2) = 2\eta - 4\eta^2 + 2\eta^3 - \eta^2 + 2\eta^3 - \eta^4 = 2\eta - 5\eta^2 + 4\eta^3 - \eta^4$$

Integrating from 0 to 1:

$$\int_0^1 = 2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) - 5\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) + 4\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) - \frac{1}{5} = 1 - \frac{5}{3} + 1 - \frac{1}{5} = 2 - 1.6667 - 0.2 = 0.1333$$

Therefore: $\theta = 0.1333\,\delta = \dfrac{2\delta}{15}$.

**(iii) Shape factor $H$:**

$$H = \frac{\delta^*}{\theta} = \frac{\delta/3}{2\delta/15} = \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{15}{2} = \frac{15}{6} = 2.5$$

**Comment:** The parabolic profile gives $H = 2.5$, which is **slightly less than the exact Blasius value of $H = 2.59$**. This indicates that the simple parabolic profile is a reasonable but not exact representation of the true laminar boundary layer. Both values are well below the experimental separation value of $H \approx 3.5$–$4.0$, confirming that the parabolic profile is far from separation under attached-flow conditions.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice Option B]`
**(14 Marks) [Bloom Level: Apply / Analyze]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain with suitable sketches how the pressure gradient parameter $\beta$ governs the onset of flow separation in a laminar boundary layer. Define $\beta$ and identify the critical value at which separation occurs.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The pressure gradient parameter, originally introduced by **Clauser** and later refined by **Pohlhausen**, is a dimensionless measure of the strength of the pressure gradient experienced by the boundary layer:

$$\beta = \frac{\delta^*}{\tau_w}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

Using the Euler relation $dP/dx = -\rho U_e\, dU_e/dx$, this can be rewritten as:

$$\beta = -\frac{\rho\, \delta^*\, U_e}{\tau_w}\frac{dU_e}{dx}$$

**Physical interpretation:**
- $\beta = 0$: Zero pressure gradient (e.g., flat plate, Blasius BL).
- $\beta < 0$: Favourable pressure gradient (accelerating flow); the boundary layer thins and stabilizes.
- $\beta > 0$ corresponds to favourable gradient; the **separation-promoting** regime is in the **adverse** sense when the **velocity** decreases, leading to a critical **negative** value of the **velocity-gradient** form.

In Pohlhausen's formulation based on a polynomial profile family, the separation criterion is:

$$\beta_{sep,\,laminar} = -0.090$$

When the local value of $\beta$ reaches this critical negative value, the wall shear stress vanishes and separation occurs.

**Sketch description (verbal — to be drawn by student):**

```
   Favourable  |  Zero  |  Mild Adverse  |  Strong Adverse
   β < -0.09   | β = 0  |  -0.09 < β < 0  |  β approaches -0.09 from above side
   --------------------------------------------------->
                                                          β = -0.090 (separation)
```

**Behaviour near separation:** As $\beta$ approaches the critical value, the wall shear $\tau_w$ drops to zero, the displacement thickness $\delta^*$ grows rapidly, and the shape factor $H$ inflates. Just beyond the critical value, the wall shear becomes **negative** (mathematically), indicating reverse flow.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A car is travelling at $V = 30$ m/s. The boundary layer along its roof is turbulent. At a particular point near the rear window, the local free-stream velocity is $U_e = 25$ m/s and the local boundary layer thickness is $\delta = 12$ mm. Assuming a 1/7th power-law turbulent velocity profile, calculate (i) the displacement thickness $\delta^*$, (ii) the momentum thickness $\theta$, and (iii) the local skin friction coefficient $C_f$. Take air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ and dynamic viscosity $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s).**

**Model Solution:**

**Standard results for 1/7th power-law profile:**

$$\frac{u}{U_e} = \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^{1/7}$$

The corresponding integrated quantities are:
- $\delta^*/\delta = 1/8 = 0.125$
- $\theta/\delta = 7/72 \approx 0.09722$
- $C_f = 0.0576 / Re_x^{1/5}$ (approximate local turbulent form)

**(i) Displacement thickness:**

$$\delta^* = 0.125 \times 0.012\ \text{m} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} = 1.5\ \text{mm}$$

**(ii) Momentum thickness:**

$$\theta = 0.09722 \times 0.012\ \text{m} = 1.167 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} \approx 1.17\ \text{mm}$$

**(iii) Skin friction coefficient:**

We need the local Reynolds number. The boundary layer thickness for a turbulent BL is given by:

$$\delta(x) \approx \frac{0.37\,x}{Re_x^{1/5}} \quad \text{and} \quad Re_x = \frac{\rho U_e x}{\mu}$$

From the thickness equation:

$$Re_x^{1/5} = \frac{0.37\,x}{\delta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Re_x = \left(\frac{0.37\,x}{\delta}\right)^5$$

We need $x$. Rearranging $\delta = 0.37 x / Re_x^{1/5}$ with $Re_x = \rho U_e x / \mu$:

$$\delta = \frac{0.37\,x}{\left(\rho U_e x / \mu\right)^{1/5}} = 0.37\,x^{4/5}\left(\frac{\mu}{\rho U_e}\right)^{1/5}$$

Solve for $x$:

$$x^{4/5} = \frac{\delta}{0.37}\left(\frac{\rho U_e}{\mu}\right)^{1/5}$$

$$\left(\frac{\rho U_e}{\mu}\right)^{1/5} = \left(\frac{1.225 \times 25}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}}\right)^{1/5} = \left(1.655 \times 10^{6}\right)^{1/5}$$

Taking the fifth root:

$$1.655 \times 10^{6} = 1.655 \times 10^6$$

$$(10^6)^{1/5} = 10^{1.2} \approx 15.849 \quad ; \quad (1.655)^{1/5} \approx 1.106$$

$$\left(\frac{\rho U_e}{\mu}\right)^{1/5} \approx 1.106 \times 15.849 \approx 17.53\ \text{m}^{-1}$$

Now:

$$x^{4/5} = \frac{0.012}{0.37} \times 17.53 = 0.03243 \times 17.53 = 0.5686$$

$$x = (0.5686)^{5/4} = (0.5686)^{1.25}$$

Computing:

$$(0.5686)^{1.25} = \exp(1.25 \times \ln 0.5686) = \exp(1.25 \times (-0.5642)) = \exp(-0.7053) = 0.494\ \text{m}$$

Therefore $x \approx 0.494$ m.

**Local Reynolds number:**

$$Re_x = \frac{\rho U_e x}{\mu} = \frac{1.225 \times 25 \times 0.494}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{15.13}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}} = 8.18 \times 10^{5}$$

**Skin friction coefficient:**

$$C_f = \frac{0.0576}{Re_x^{1/5}} = \frac{0.0576}{(8.18 \times 10^{5})^{0.2}}$$

$$(8.18 \times 10^5)^{0.2} = (8.18)^{0.2} \times (10^5)^{0.2} = 1.521 \times 10 = 15.21$$

$$C_f = \frac{0.0576}{15.21} = 3.79 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Final answers:**
- $\delta^* = 1.5\ \text{mm}$
- $\theta \approx 1.17\ \text{mm}$
- $C_f \approx 3.79 \times 10^{-3}$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks on Flow Separation Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the inequality alongside the equality:** Many students write only $\tau_w = 0$ and forget to state that $dP/dx > 0$ at the separation point. The condition $\tau_w = 0$ alone is *necessary* but not *sufficient*; it is also a stagnation point of the velocity profile, which can occur in favourable gradients without separation. **Cost: 2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Confusing the Pohlhausen $H_{sep}$ with the experimental value:** Different textbooks give different numbers ($1.46$ vs $3.5$–$4.0$). Read the question wording: if it specifies "Pohlhausen polynomial profile," use $1.46$; if it says "experimentally observed," use $\sim 3.5$–$4.0$. **Cost: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 3. **Mixing up displacement and momentum thickness integrals:** Sign errors in $(1 - u/U_e)$ vs $u/U_e \cdot (1 - u/U_e)$ are extremely common. Write the integrals explicitly on the answer sheet. **Cost: 2–3 marks per occurrence.**
>
> 4. **Not stating boundary layer assumptions:** When deriving separation conditions from Navier–Stokes, students often skip stating the boundary layer approximations ($\partial/\partial x \ll \partial/\partial y$, $v \ll u$). Examiners expect these explicitly. **Cost: 1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical problems:** $C_f$ is dimensionless — report it as such. Boundary layer thickness must be in metres for any subsequent Reynolds number calculation. **Cost: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Flow Separation**

- **Definition:** Detachment of the boundary layer from a surface due to a strong adverse pressure gradient.
- **Mathematical condition at separation:** $\left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0} = 0$ AND $\dfrac{dP}{dx} > 0$ at that point.
- **Wall shear stress** in terms of velocity gradient: $\tau_w = \mu \left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0}$.
- **Adverse pressure gradient** = pressure rises in the flow direction = $\dfrac{dP}{dx} > 0$ = $\dfrac{dU_e}{dx} < 0$.
- **Favourable pressure gradient** = pressure falls in the flow direction = $\dfrac{dP}{dx} < 0$ = boundary layer stabilizes.
- **Shape factor** $H = \delta^* / \theta$: attached laminar $\approx 2.59$, Pohlhausen laminar separation $\approx 1.46$, experimental laminar separation $\approx 3.5$–$4.0$, attached turbulent $\approx 1.3$.
- **Pressure gradient parameter** (Clauser/Pohlhausen): $\beta = \dfrac{\delta^*}{\tau_w}\dfrac{dP}{dx}$; critical value for laminar separation $\beta_{sep} \approx -0.090$.
- **von Kármán momentum integral equation:** $\dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho U_e^2} = \dfrac{d\theta}{dx} + (2 + H)\dfrac{\theta}{U_e}\dfrac{dU_e}{dx}$.
- **Turbulent separation** is **delayed** compared to laminar separation because turbulent eddies transport momentum more effectively, energizing the near-wall layer.
- **Engineering controls** for separation: boat-tail geometry, vortex generators, boundary layer suction, blowing, faired edges, diffusers.
- **Vehicle applications:** roof-to-rear-window region, side mirrors, wheel wells, underbody, and truck-trailer rear faces are the principal separation zones on a road vehicle.
- **Consequences:** Pressure drag dominance, wake-induced buffeting, lift loss, increased fuel consumption, aerodynamic noise, and crosswind sensitivity.
- **Exam tip:** Always draw a velocity profile sketch (with arrows) showing the change from attached to separated state — this alone can earn 1–2 marks in a 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Streamlines, Streak Lines and Path Lines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Streamlines, Streak Lines and Path Lines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Streamlines (The Instantaneous Flow Map)

A **streamline** is an imaginary curve drawn through a flowing fluid such that the tangent to the curve at every point is in the direction of the velocity vector of the fluid at that point **at a given instant of time**. It represents a *snapshot* of how the fluid is moving at that exact moment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A streamline is a continuous line in the flow field such that the velocity vector is everywhere tangent to it. The differential equation governing a streamline in a 3-D flow with velocity components $(u, v, w)$ is:
> $$\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$$

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you take a high-speed photograph of a river. The tiny white foamy streaks on the surface — those lines along which the water is *currently* moving — are streamlines. They are frozen in time and tell you the direction of flow at this very instant.

### 1.2 Pathlines (The Travel Diary of a Single Particle)

A **pathline** is the actual trajectory (or "road map") traced by a single fluid particle as it moves through space over a period of time. It is the *history* of one specific particle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A pathline is the locus of positions successively occupied by a single fluid particle over a time interval. Mathematically, if a particle is at position $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ at time $t = 0$, then its pathline is given by integrating:
> $$\frac{dx_p}{dt} = u(x_p, y_p, z_p, t), \quad \frac{dy_p}{dt} = v(x_p, y_p, z_p, t), \quad \frac{dz_p}{dt} = w(x_p, y_p, z_p, t)$$

**Conceptual Analogy:** Take a single floating leaf on a river. If you mark its position at 10:00 AM, 10:01 AM, 10:02 AM, ... and connect those points, the curve you draw is the *pathline* of that one leaf. It is one particle's personal journey.

### 1.3 Streaklines (The Dye-Injection Trace)

A **streakline** (also spelled *streak line*) is the locus of all fluid particles that have *previously passed through a prescribed point* in the flow. It is a continuous record of where all particles emanating from a fixed point have gone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A streakline is the instantaneous locus of all fluid particles that have passed through (or originated from) a given fixed point in the flow at some earlier time $\tau \le t$. For each $\tau$ in $[-\infty, t]$, the particle that was at the reference point $(x_P, y_P, z_P)$ at time $\tau$ is now at a new position; the curve joining all these positions is the streakline.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you stand on a bridge and continuously drop a drop of food coloring into a river at the *same spot* below the bridge. The colored thread that forms on the water surface is a **streakline**. It contains pieces of *many* different particles, all of which happened to be at that injection point at some moment in the past.

### 1.4 The Three Lines — A Unified View

| Feature | Streamline | Pathline | Streakline |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Describes** | Direction of flow at an instant | History of one particle | History of all particles from a point |
| **Time basis** | Single instant (snapshot) | Time history (one particle) | Time history (many particles) |
| **In steady flow** | Fixed in space | Coincides with streamline | Coincides with streamline |
| **In unsteady flow** | Changes with time | Different from streamline | Different from streamline |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick:** *Stream*line = the **stream** as you see it **now**. *Path*line = the **path** one particle **walked**. *Streak*line = the **streak** of dye from a fixed point.

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- The velocity components are conventionally denoted in m/s.
- The standard reference velocity in vehicle aerodynamics is the **free-stream velocity $U_\infty$** (m/s).
- The **Reynolds number** $Re = \dfrac{\rho U_\infty L}{\mu}$ dictates whether flow streamlines remain attached or separate — a key concern in vehicle aerodynamics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematics of a Streamline

Consider a small element $d\vec{s} = dx\,\hat{i} + dy\,\hat{j} + dz\,\hat{k}$ of a streamline. By definition, this element is parallel to the local velocity vector $\vec{V} = u\,\hat{i} + v\,\hat{j} + w\,\hat{k}$.

For two vectors to be parallel, their cross product must vanish:
$$\vec{V} \times d\vec{s} = \vec{0}$$

Expanding this in 3-D:
$$\begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ u & v & w \\ dx & dy & dz \end{vmatrix} = \vec{0}$$

This yields the **streamline differential equation**:
$$\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$$

The "Why" behind this: since $d\vec{s}$ and $\vec{V}$ are parallel, the ratios of corresponding components must be equal — this is precisely what the equation states.

### 2.2 The Mathematics of a Pathline

A pathline is a parametric curve in time. Starting from an initial position $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ at $t = 0$, the particle's position at any later time is:
$$x_p(t) = x_0 + \int_0^t u(x_p, y_p, z_p, \tau)\,d\tau$$
$$y_p(t) = y_0 + \int_0^t v(x_p, y_p, z_p, \tau)\,d\tau$$
$$z_p(t) = z_0 + \int_0^t w(x_p, y_p, z_p, \tau)\,d\tau$$

In **steady flow**, $u, v, w$ do not depend on time, so the velocity can be treated as a function of position only, and the pathline is just the time-parametrized form of the streamline.

### 2.3 The Mathematics of a Streakline

A streakline requires us to track *all* particles that ever passed through the fixed point $\vec{r}_P = (x_P, y_P, z_P)$ at some past time $\tau \le t$. For each $\tau$:
$$x_s(t; \tau) = x_P + \int_\tau^t u(x_s, y_s, z_s, \tau')\,d\tau'$$

The streakline is the curve traced by $x_s(t; \tau)$ as $\tau$ varies over $(-\infty, t]$ with $(x_s(\tau; \tau), y_s(\tau; \tau), z_s(\tau; \tau)) = (x_P, y_P, z_P)$.

### 2.4 The Critical Theorem: Steady vs. Unsteady

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steady Flow Equivalence Theorem:** In a *steady* flow (where $\partial \vec{V} / \partial t = 0$), **streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines coincide**. In an *unsteady* flow, all three are generally different.

**Why?** In steady flow, the velocity field is frozen in time. So a particle's future trajectory, a snapshot of the flow direction, and a dye trace from a fixed point all give the same curve.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Streamline condition | $\vec{V} \times d\vec{s} = 0$ | Velocity parallel to streamline element |
| 2 | Streamline differential form | $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$ | Slope of streamline equals $v/u$ in 2-D |
| 3 | 2-D streamline | $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{v(x,y,t)}{u(x,y,t)}$ | Time $t$ is a parameter (frozen at instant) |
| 4 | Pathline (parametric) | $x_p = x_p(t),\ y_p = y_p(t)$ | Trajectory of one particle |
| 5 | Pathline ODE | $\dfrac{dx_p}{dt} = u,\ \dfrac{dy_p}{dt} = v$ | Kinematic definition |
| 6 | Streakline (parametric) | $x_s = x_s(t,\tau),\ y_s = y_s(t,\tau)$ | Locus for varying $\tau$ at fixed $t$ |
| 7 | Steady flow condition | $\partial \vec{V} / \partial t = 0$ | All three lines coincide |
| 8 | Volumetric flow rate (per unit depth) | $Q = \int u\,dA$ | Mass between streamlines is conserved |
| 9 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho U_\infty L}{\mu}$ | Governs flow separation in vehicles |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility in Vehicle Aerodynamics:** Understanding streamlines is critical in designing vehicle body shapes, rear diffusers, and underbody airflow channels. Designers use streamline plots from CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics) to visualize *where* the flow attaches, separates, or forms vortices — all of which directly affect drag, lift, and fuel economy.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Streamline Equation in 2-D

**Given:** A 2-D, incompressible, steady flow field with velocity components $u(x, y)$ and $v(x, y)$.

**Goal:** Derive the differential equation that defines a streamline.

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup:**

Let the streamline be a curve $y = f(x)$ in the $xy$-plane. A small arc-length element along the curve is:
$$d\vec{s} = dx\,\hat{i} + dy\,\hat{j}$$

The velocity vector at that point is:
$$\vec{V} = u\,\hat{i} + v\,\hat{j}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Tangency Condition:**

Since the streamline is *tangent* to the velocity vector, the two vectors must be parallel. The cross product must vanish:
$$\vec{V} \times d\vec{s} = (u\,dy - v\,dx)\,\hat{k} = \vec{0}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the Slope:**

$$u\,dy - v\,dx = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad u\,dy = v\,dx \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{v}{u}$$

**Step 4 — Final Streamline Equation (2-D):**

$$\boxed{\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{v(x, y, t)}{u(x, y, t)}}$$

For 3-D, this generalizes to the symmetric form:
$$\boxed{\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic — Step 3 to 4:** The ratio $v/u$ tells us that the slope of a streamline at any point equals the ratio of vertical to horizontal velocity at that point. For 3-D, since three components must be in proportion, we equate pairwise ratios of differentials to components.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Streamline of a Non-Uniform Flow

**Given Velocity Field:**
$$u(x, y) = 1\ \text{m/s} \quad (\text{constant})$$
$$v(x, y) = x\ \text{m/s} \quad (\text{linear in } x)$$

This is a steady, 2-D flow.

**Step 1 — Identify the streamline ODE:**

Using the 2-D streamline equation:
$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{x}{1} = x$$

**Step 2 — Integrate:**

$$dy = x\,dx \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = \int x\,dx = \frac{x^2}{2} + C$$

**Step 3 — Family of Streamlines:**

The general streamline is the family of parabolas:
$$\boxed{y = \frac{x^2}{2} + C, \quad C \in \mathbb{R}}$$

**Step 4 — Verification at a sample point:**

At the point $(2, 3)$, we require $3 = \dfrac{2^2}{2} + C = 2 + C$, giving $C = 1$. So the streamline through $(2, 3)$ is:
$$y = \frac{x^2}{2} + 1$$

At $x = 2$: slope $= 2$ (matches $v/u = 2/1 = 2$). ✓

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for vehicles:** Parabolic-like streamline patterns appear near the front of bluff bodies (e.g., the nose of a truck), where flow accelerates. Visualizing such streamlines helps designers tune the curvature of the bonnet/windshield transition.

---

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation of Pathline for a Time-Dependent Field

**Given Velocity Field (Unsteady):**
$$u(x, t) = U_0 (1 + \epsilon \sin \omega t), \quad v = 0$$

**Step 1 — Write the Pathline ODEs:**

$$\frac{dx_p}{dt} = U_0(1 + \epsilon \sin \omega t), \quad \frac{dy_p}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve the $y$-equation:**

$$y_p(t) = y_0 = \text{constant}$$

The particle moves along a horizontal line.

**Step 3 — Solve the $x$-equation (assuming $x_p(0) = x_0$):**

$$x_p(t) = x_0 + U_0 \int_0^t (1 + \epsilon \sin \omega \tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$x_p(t) = x_0 + U_0 \left[ t - \frac{\epsilon}{\omega}\cos \omega t + \frac{\epsilon}{\omega} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Final Pathline Equation:**

$$\boxed{x_p(t) = x_0 + U_0 t + \frac{U_0 \epsilon}{\omega}(1 - \cos \omega t), \quad y_p(t) = y_0}$$

---

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Streamline vs. Pathline vs. Streakline

| Aspect | Streamline | Pathline | Streakline |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Differential definition** | $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v}$ at fixed $t$ | $\dfrac{dx_p}{dt} = u,\ \dfrac{dy_p}{dt} = v$ | $x_s = \int_\tau^t u\,d\tau'$ with $\tau$ varying |
| **Independent variable** | Position $(x, y)$ | Time $t$ | Time $t$ and emission time $\tau$ |
| **Number of particles tracked** | None — virtual line | One | Infinitely many (all from one point) |
| **Experiment to visualize** | Hydrogen-bubble wire at instant | Single tracer particle, photograph over time | Continuous dye injector at fixed point |
| **Coincides with others when?** | — | Always, in **steady** flow | Always, in **steady** flow |
| **Use in CFD post-processing** | Plot of velocity vectors | Particle tracking (Lagrangian) | Dye-penetration plots |
| **Computational cost** | Cheap (algebraic in 2-D) | Moderate (ODE integration) | High (many particles per $\tau$ slice) |

---

### 3.5 Detailed Derivation of Streakline (Parametric)

**Given the same unsteady field as Section 3.3** with the reference emission point at $(x_P, 0) = (0, 0)$.

**Step 1 — Particle path from emission time $\tau$:**

The particle released at time $\tau$ at position $(0, 0)$ will be at:
$$x_s(t; \tau) = 0 + U_0 (t - \tau) + \frac{U_0 \epsilon}{\omega}(\cos \omega \tau - \cos \omega t)$$
$$y_s(t; \tau) = 0$$

**Step 2 — The streakline at time $t$:**

For a *fixed* observation time $t$, the streakline is the locus of $(x_s, y_s)$ as $\tau$ varies over $[-\infty, t]$:
$$\boxed{x_s(t; \tau) = U_0 (t - \tau) + \frac{U_0 \epsilon}{\omega}(\cos \omega \tau - \cos \omega t), \quad y_s = 0}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the streamline at time $t$:**

The streamline (at fixed $t$) has slope:
$$\frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{t\ \text{fixed}} = \frac{v}{u} = 0$$

So the streamline is also a horizontal line. But the streamline and streakline only coincide exactly in steady flow (when $\epsilon = 0$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** When $\epsilon \to 0$, the field becomes steady, and all three expressions reduce to $x = U_0 t + \text{const}$, $y = \text{const}$ — confirming the **Steady Flow Equivalence Theorem**.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Summary — When Do the Three Lines Differ?

| Flow Type | Streamline = Pathline? | Streamline = Streakline? | Pathline = Streakline? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Steady** | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes |
| **Unsteady, uniform** | ✗ No | ✗ No | ✗ No (in general) |
| **Unsteady, periodic** | ✗ No | ✗ No | ✓ Yes (if integrated over full period) |
| **Steady, rotating** | ✓ Yes (azimuthal) | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — How the Three Lines Relate to the Velocity Field

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Velocity Field V x,y,t] --> B{Is the flow steady?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Steady Flow Equivalence]
    C --> D[Streamlines = Pathlines = Streaklines]
    D --> E[Fixed pattern in space, time-independent]
    B -- No --> F[Unsteady Flow]
    F --> G[Three Distinct Line Families]
    G --> H[Streamlines: instantaneous tangent field]
    G --> I[Pathlines: single particle trajectory]
    G --> J[Streaklines: dye from fixed point]
    H --> K[Snapshots at t1, t2, t3 differ]
    I --> L[ODE integration in t]
    J --> M[Parametric in emission time tau]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    end

    pA[Mark one particle at t0] --> pB[Track position at t0, t1, t2]
    pB --> pC[Connect positions]
    pC --> pD[Pathline ready]
    end
    subgraph STREAMLINE
    stA[Pick instant t*] --> stB[Plot velocity vectors at all grid points]
    stB --> stC[Connect tangentially]
    stC --> stD[Streamline ready]
    end
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Flow — Computational Determination of Each Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Given V x,y,t] --> Q1{Steady?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> W1[Algebraic solve of dy over dx = v over u]
    W1 --> W2[Streamline = Pathline = Streakline]
    Q1 -- No --> X1[Streamline: dy over dx at fixed t]
    Q1 --> X2[Pathline: integrate d x over dt = u]
    Q1 --> X3[Streakline: integrate for many tau values]
    X1 --> Y1[Time snapshot curve]
    X2 --> Y2[Lagrangian trajectory]
    X3 --> Y3[Dye trace locus]
```


## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a streamline. Derive the differential equation of a streamline in a 2-D flow field.*

**Model Answer:**

A streamline is a curve in the flow field whose tangent at every point is parallel to the instantaneous velocity vector at that point. It is defined for a particular instant of time.

For a 2-D flow with velocity components $u(x, y, t)$ and $v(x, y, t)$, let $d\vec{s} = dx\,\hat{i} + dy\,\hat{j}$ be an element of the streamline. Tangency requires:
$$\vec{V} \times d\vec{s} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (u\,dy - v\,dx)\,\hat{k} = 0$$

Therefore, the **streamline equation** is:
$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{v(x, y, t)}{u(x, y, t)}$$

Time $t$ is treated as a fixed parameter while integrating. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the condition under which streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines coincide. Justify your answer.*

**Model Answer:**

All three lines coincide when the flow is **steady**, i.e., when:
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Justification:** In steady flow, the velocity at any fixed point in space is constant with time. Hence:
- The streamline (a snapshot of velocity directions) is fixed in space.
- A particle's pathline is determined only by its position, not by the time of release.
- A streakline from a fixed point is the union of all such pathlines, and they too lie along the same fixed curve.

In contrast, for an unsteady flow, the velocity field changes with time, so a streamline at $t_1$ differs from that at $t_2$, and pathlines and streaklines deviate from any instantaneous streamline. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Define streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines. Explain with a clear diagram how they differ in an unsteady flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-D velocity field is given by $u = 2x$, $v = -2y$ (in m/s, with $x$ and $y$ in metres). Determine:
  (i) The equation of the streamlines.  **(3 marks)**
  (ii) The equation of the pathline of a particle starting from $(1, 1)$ at $t = 0$.  **(4 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

- **Streamline:** A line in the flow field tangent to the velocity vector at a given instant $t = t^*$. It gives an instantaneous picture of flow direction. Snapshot changes with $t^*$.
- **Pathline:** The trajectory of a single identifiable fluid particle as it moves through space and time. It is found by integrating the kinematic equations.
- **Streakline:** The locus of all fluid particles that have passed through a fixed point (e.g., a dye injection location) at some past time $\tau \le t$. It is a continuous dye trace.

In a steady flow, all three coincide. In an unsteady flow:
- Streamlines at $t = t_1$ and $t = t_2$ are different curves.
- A pathline of a particle released at $t = 0$ will not, in general, follow any of the instantaneous streamlines.
- A streakline from a fixed point is the union of all pathlines of particles released at that point and lies along a different curve from any single streamline.

**Diagram (described):** Three sets of curves on the same flow domain — (i) the streamline snapshot at $t = t^*$, (ii) the pathline of a specific particle starting at $t = 0$, and (iii) the streakline of dye from a fixed point — all clearly differ in shape.

> **(Full 7 marks: 2 marks each for definitions, 1 mark for "when they differ" condition, 2 marks for conceptual clarity with example.)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**(i) Streamline Equation:**

The 2-D streamline ODE is:
$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{-2y}{2x} = -\frac{y}{x}$$

Separate variables:
$$\frac{dy}{y} = -\frac{dx}{x}$$

Integrate:
$$\ln \vert y \vert = -\ln \vert x \vert + C_1$$

$$\ln \vert xy \vert = C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert xy \vert = K$$

The family of streamlines is therefore:
$$\boxed{xy = C, \quad C = \text{constant}}$$

These are rectangular hyperbolas. **(3 marks)**

**(ii) Pathline Equation:**

The pathline ODEs are:
$$\frac{dx_p}{dt} = 2 x_p, \quad \frac{dy_p}{dt} = -2 y_p$$

Solve the $x$-ODE:
$$\frac{dx_p}{x_p} = 2\,dt \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln x_p = 2t + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_p(t) = A\,e^{2t}$$

With $x_p(0) = 1$: $A = 1$, so $x_p(t) = e^{2t}$.

Solve the $y$-ODE:
$$\frac{dy_p}{y_p} = -2\,dt \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln y_p = -2t + C_3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_p(t) = B\,e^{-2t}$$

With $y_p(0) = 1$: $B = 1$, so $y_p(t) = e^{-2t}$.

Eliminate $t$: from $x_p = e^{2t}$, we get $e^{-2t} = 1/x_p$. Therefore:
$$y_p = \frac{1}{x_p}$$

The pathline is:
$$\boxed{x_p \cdot y_p = 1, \quad \text{i.e.,}\ xy = 1}$$

**Note:** In this particular case, the pathline happens to coincide with the streamline $xy = 1$ because the flow is **steady** (no $t$-dependence in $u$ and $v$). This validates the steady-flow equivalence theorem. **(4 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, distinguish between pathlines, streamlines, and streaklines for a flow past a cylinder where the free-stream velocity varies with time as $U_\infty(t) = U_0 (1 + 0.1 \sin \omega t)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the streamline equation for a flow field given by $u = 3$ m/s, $v = 2x$ m/s. Plot the streamline passing through the origin. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

- **Setup:** The free-stream velocity is time-varying: $U_\infty(t) = U_0(1 + 0.1 \sin \omega t)$. The flow past the cylinder is therefore **unsteady**.
- **Streamlines:** At any instant $t^*$, the streamlines around the cylinder are roughly the familiar steady-cylinder pattern, but the *stagnation point shifts* and the *wake angle* changes from one instant to the next. Hence the streamline pattern is not fixed in time.
- **Pathlines:** A specific fluid particle (e.g., one released upstream on the centerline at $t = 0$) will follow a wavy trajectory, oscillating in $x$-direction as the free-stream speed oscillates. Its pathline will *not* coincide with any instantaneous streamline.
- **Streaklines:** A dye injection point fixed slightly upstream of the cylinder will produce a wavy colored thread downstream that does not coincide with the pathline of any single particle or any instantaneous streamline.

**Sketch elements required (described):**
- Cylinder shown as a circle.
- Streamlines drawn as symmetric, attached curves at $t_1$, and a slightly different pattern at $t_2$.
- Pathline drawn as a single wavy curve approaching and leaving the cylinder.
- Streakline drawn as a separate wavy curve emanating from the dye source.

> **(7 marks: 2 marks for correctly identifying the flow as unsteady, 3 marks for the three distinct descriptions, 2 marks for the sketch quality and labeling.)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Given:** $u = 3$ m/s, $v = 2x$ m/s. (Note: this is *not* dependent on $t$, so it is a **steady** flow.)

**Step 1 — Streamline ODE:**

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{2x}{3}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate:**

$$dy = \frac{2x}{3}\,dx \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac{x^2}{3} + C$$

**Step 3 — Apply Initial Condition for the Streamline through the Origin:**

At $(0, 0)$: $0 = 0 + C \Rightarrow C = 0$.

**Streamline through Origin:**
$$\boxed{y = \frac{x^2}{3}}$$

**Step 4 — Plot Description:**

The streamline is a parabola opening upward, passing through $(0, 0)$, with vertex at the origin. Sample points:
- At $x = 1$ m: $y = 1/3$ m
- At $x = 2$ m: $y = 4/3$ m
- At $x = -1$ m: $y = 1/3$ m (symmetric about $y$-axis)

> **(7 marks: 2 marks for ODE, 2 marks for integration, 1 mark for applying the initial condition, 2 marks for the correct parabola shape and tabulated points.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to fix $t$ for streamlines:** Many students treat $t$ as a variable when finding streamlines. Markers deduct up to **2 marks** if the distinction between a streamline (fixed $t$) and a pathline (variable $t$) is not maintained.
> 2. **Confusing $u, v$ with displacement components:** In pathline equations, $u = dx_p/dt$ — students sometimes write $x_p = u$ (no integration), losing **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Not stating "steady vs. unsteady":** A common 1-mark deduction occurs when students solve streamline and pathline problems without explicitly identifying whether the flow is steady. Always begin with: *"The given field is steady/unsteady because…"*
> 4. **Missing initial condition for pathlines:** A pathline must satisfy an initial position. Showing only the family $y = x^2/3 + C$ without specifying $C$ from the given point is incomplete and costs **1 mark**.
> 5. **Streamline ≠ trajectory of a particle:** This conceptual error appears frequently in viva and short-answer sections. Remember: a streamline is a *geometric* locus at one instant; a pathline is a *time history* of one particle.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Streamline:** Tangent to $\vec{V}$ at a fixed instant $t = t^*$. Equation: $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$.
- **Pathline:** Trajectory of a *single particle*. Equation: $\dfrac{dx_p}{dt} = u$, $\dfrac{dy_p}{dt} = v$ with IC at $t = 0$.
- **Streakline:** Locus of *all particles* that passed through a fixed point. Parametric in emission time $\tau$.
- **Steady Flow Equivalence:** $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$ ⟹ all three lines coincide.
- **Unsteady Flow:** All three lines are generally distinct.
- **2-D Streamline:** $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{v(x, y, t)}{u(x, y, t)}$ at fixed $t$.
- **Cross-Product Origin:** $\vec{V} \times d\vec{s} = 0$ is the master condition for streamlines.
- **CFD Visualization:** Streamlines = Eulerian; Pathlines = Lagrangian; Streaklines = dye-injection plots.
- **Vehicle Relevance:** Streamlines over the bonnet, A-pillars, and rear window directly impact drag coefficient $C_D$ and lift coefficient $C_L$.
- **In 2-D steady flow with $u = 1$, $v = x$:** Streamlines are $y = x^2/2 + C$ (parabolas).
- **In 2-D steady flow with $u = 2x$, $v = -2y$:** Streamlines are $xy = C$ (hyperbolas).
- **For periodic flows (e.g., oscillating $U_\infty$):** Pathline ≠ Streakline in general; careful distinction required in answers.
- **Examiner's favourite 3-mark question:** *"Define streamline and derive its equation in 2-D."* — always include the cross-product derivation.
- **Examiner's favourite 14-mark question:** *"Given a velocity field, find the streamline and pathline of a particle, and comment on whether the flow is steady."* — practice this template.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Visualization Techniques - Types and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flow Visualization Techniques - Types and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Flow Visualization Techniques — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Flow Visualization** is defined as the set of experimental methods and optical techniques employed to make the normally invisible characteristics of a fluid flow field (velocity direction, streamline patterns, density gradients, vortices, boundary layer behavior, and shock structures) directly observable to the human eye or to a recording instrument.

In the specific context of **Vehicle Aerodynamics (PEAUT752)**, flow visualization refers to the qualitative and quantitative experimental procedures used in subsonic and transonic wind tunnels, full-scale road tests, and CFD validation studies to investigate the **external airflow around a road vehicle**, the **internal cooling flows through heat exchangers**, and the **wake structure** behind moving automobiles, trucks, and racing cars.

The governing physical principle behind most visualization techniques is that a flow property (velocity, density, pressure, temperature) is converted into an **optical signal** — either by direct addition of a tracer, or by modifying the refractive index of the fluid medium itself.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine pouring a few drops of dark food coloring into a transparent river or into your glass of water. The dye does not change the water itself, but it *rides* on the currents and reveals the path the water is taking, the swirls it forms, and the stagnant pockets where it barely moves. Flow visualization in aerodynamics is essentially the engineering equivalent of that food coloring — except we use controlled smoke, laser-illuminated oil particles, or even air's own refractive properties as our "dye."

In the same way that a leaking roof reveals the path of water along a wooden beam, the **streamlines and separation bubbles** around a car body are revealed by smoke streaks in a wind tunnel, helping engineers pinpoint where drag is generated and how to reduce it.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Refractive index of air at STP | $n$ | **1.000293** (dimensionless) | Basis of optical techniques |
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | **$2.998 \times 10^{8}$ m/s** | Used in Mach number relations |
| Density of standard air | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** | Reference for compressibility effects |
| Specific gas constant for air | $R$ | **287 J/(kg·K)** | Used in density–temperature relations |
| Gladstone–Dale constant | $K_{GD}$ | **$2.27 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/kg** | Links density to refractive index |
| Kinematic viscosity of air | $\nu$ | **$1.46 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s** | Tracer particle settling |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Gladstone–Dale constant** is a critical link between a thermodynamic property (density) and an optical property (refractive index). All optical density-based flow visualization methods — Schlieren, Shadowgraph, Interferometry — fundamentally depend on this constant.

## 1.4 Historical Context and Relevance to Vehicle Aerodynamics

Flow visualization originated in the **19th century** with Ludwig Mach and August Toepler (Schlieren photography, 1864) and Ernst Mach (shadowgraph, 1867). With the rise of road-vehicle wind tunnels (Pininfarina, MIRA, FKFS) and motorsport aerodynamics in the 1960s and 70s, surface-flow oil visualization and tuft methods became the **workhorse qualitative techniques**. Modern Formula 1 teams now combine these classical methods with **Pressure-Sensitive Paints (PSP)** and **Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV)** to extract thousands of velocity vectors in milliseconds.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Flow Visualization Techniques

Flow visualization techniques can be classified along **four orthogonal axes**. Understanding these axes is essential for KTU exam answers because examiners frequently ask for "classification of techniques" for 3 marks.

### A. By Tracer Type
1. **Foreign Tracer Methods** — A physical substance (smoke, dye, oil, helium bubbles) is introduced.
2. **Native Tracer Methods** — The fluid's own properties (refractive index, density) are exploited.

### B. By Flow Medium
1. **Liquid flow visualization** (water tunnel, towing tank) — dye injection, hydrogen bubbles.
2. **Gas/Air flow visualization** (wind tunnel) — smoke, schlieren, tufts.

### C. By Information Type
1. **Qualitative** — direction of flow, separation points (smoke, tuft, oil).
2. **Quantitative** — exact velocity or density values (PIV, interferometry).

### D. By Temporal Resolution
1. **Steady-state / time-averaged** — oil film, sublimation.
2. **Time-resolved / instantaneous** — high-speed PIV, pulsed schlieren.

## 2.2 Detailed Operation of the Major Techniques

### 2.2.1 Smoke Visualization (Most common in vehicle wind tunnels)

1. A non-toxic glycol or paraffin-based fog is generated upstream of the test section.
2. The fog particles (typical diameter **0.5 – 5 µm**) are small enough to faithfully follow the streamlines (Stokes number << 1).
3. Light sheet illumination from above (using halogen or LED arrays) scatters off the droplets.
4. The captured streak pattern is the streamline field at the plane of the light sheet.

**Engineering utility:** Identifies separation points on a sedan boot-lid, A-pillar vortices, and the wake of a truck-trailer gap.

### 2.2.2 Surface Oil-Flow Technique

1. The vehicle body is painted with a **kerosene–titanium-dioxide–dye mixture**.
2. The oil migrates under shear stress from the boundary layer.
3. After the run, **oil-streak patterns** are visible: aligned lines indicate attached flow, divergence indicates separation, and convergence indicates reattachment.

**Engineering utility:** Excellent for detecting the position of the **rear-glass flow separation** on a notchback sedan — a critical drag-generation region.

### 2.2.3 Tuft and Streamer Method

1. Lightweight yarn tufts (~10–15 mm long) are attached at grid points on the model surface.
2. Tufts align with the local velocity vector.
3. Photographs/video yield a *time-averaged* qualitative map.

**Engineering utility:** Used extensively on race cars to validate the on-track performance of front-wing flap elements.

### 2.2.4 Schlieren Photography

1. A point light source is collimated, passed through the test section, and focused onto a knife-edge.
2. Density gradients (i.e., $\partial \rho / \partial x$ or $\partial \rho / \partial y$) refract the light beam.
3. The knife-edge blocks a portion of the refracted ray → intensity change on screen → visible flow structure.

### 2.2.5 Shadowgraph

1. Similar to schlieren but uses **no knife-edge**; the disturbance is recorded as a *second-derivative* of density, $\nabla^2 n$.
2. Renders shocks, plume fronts, and hot-air columns above warm vehicle surfaces.

### 2.2.6 Interferometry (Mach–Zehnder)

1. A coherent laser beam is split into two paths: one through the test section, one through reference air.
2. The recombined beam produces **interference fringes**.
3. Fringe shift $\Delta N$ is directly proportional to the integrated density change.

### 2.2.7 Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV)

1. The flow is seeded with micron-sized tracer particles (olive oil fog, DEHS).
2. A thin laser light sheet (~1 mm thick) illuminates the plane at time $t_1$.
3. A second pulse fires at time $t_1 + \Delta t$.
4. A CCD camera captures both frames; a cross-correlation algorithm computes the **displacement vector** $\Delta \vec{x}$ for every interrogation window.
5. Velocity: $\vec{V} = \Delta \vec{x} / \Delta t$.

### 2.2.8 Pressure-Sensitive Paint (PSP)

1. A luminescent coating is applied to the model.
2. Oxygen in the air *quenches* the luminescence in proportion to local partial pressure $p_{O_2}$.
3. Luminescence intensity $I$ → pressure $p$ via the Stern–Volmer equation.

### 2.2.9 Infrared Thermography

1. As air is compressed or expanded in the flow, its **static temperature** changes (recovery temperature).
2. A calibrated IR camera reads surface temperatures of a thin foil coating.
3. Temperature map is converted to pressure map via the isentropic relation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Compact Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the formulas in the table below. They appear regularly in 14-mark derivations.

| # | Equation | Meaning / Variables | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n - 1 = K_{GD} \cdot \rho$ | **Gladstone–Dale Law** (refractive index vs density) | Schlieren, Interferometry |
| 2 | $\Delta N = \dfrac{K_{GD}}{\lambda} \int (\rho_2 - \rho_1)\, dl$ | Fringe shift in interferometer | Quantitative density |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_y = L \int \dfrac{1}{n}\dfrac{\partial n}{\partial y}\, dz$ | Light deflection angle in $y$ | Schlieren |
| 4 | $M = \dfrac{V}{a}$ where $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Mach number definition | Compressible flow |
| 5 | $\dfrac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2$ | Stagnation temperature ratio | Thermography |
| 6 | $\dfrac{p_0}{p} = \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma - 1)}$ | Stagnation pressure ratio | PSP, probes |
| 7 | $I / I_0 = A(p) + B(p) \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + K_{SV} \cdot p_{O_2}}$ | Stern–Volmer PSP model | Pressure paints |
| 8 | $\vec{V}(x,y) = \dfrac{\Delta \vec{x}}{\Delta t}$ | Instantaneous PIV velocity | Particle tracking |
| 9 | $\rho = \dfrac{p}{R T}$ | Ideal gas equation | All compressible methods |
| 10 | $\tau_w = \mu \left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0}$ | Wall shear stress | Oil-flow streak theory |
| 11 | $St = \dfrac{\rho_p d_p^2 V}{18 \mu L}$ | Stokes number for tracer | PIV tracer design |
| 12 | $\dfrac{I}{I_0} = \exp\!\left(-K_{GD} L \rho\right)$ | Beer–Lambert absorption form | Some optical methods |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility and Selection Heuristic

> [!NOTE]
> The **selection of a flow visualization technique** in a vehicle-aerodynamics laboratory is governed by four questions: (1) What flow property do I want to measure? (2) Is the flow steady or unsteady? (3) What spatial resolution is required? (4) Is the test in a wind tunnel or on the road?

| Desired Information | Recommended Technique |
|---|---|
| Surface streamline pattern | Oil-flow, tuft, sublimation |
| Wake vortex identification | Smoke, PIV |
| Local velocity vector field | PIV, Laser Doppler Velocimetry (LDV) |
| Shock wave / compressibility feature | Schlieren, shadowgraph |
| Quantitative pressure map on body | PSP, pressure taps |
| Underbody flow & cooling | Dye in water channel, infrared thermography |
| Real-road, open environment | Tuft, smoke rake, on-board cameras |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gladstone–Dale Relation (Optical Foundation of Schlieren/Interferometry)

### Step 1 — Empirical observation
Experimentally, for a given gas and a given wavelength of light $\lambda$, the **refractive index $n$** is found to be a linear function of the gas **density $\rho$**:

$$n(\lambda, \rho) = 1 + \alpha(\lambda) \cdot \rho$$

### Step 2 — Define the Gladstone–Dale constant
The coefficient of proportionality is called the **Gladstone–Dale constant** $K_{GD}(\lambda)$:

$$\alpha(\lambda) \equiv K_{GD}(\lambda)$$

Hence,

$$n - 1 = K_{GD}(\lambda) \cdot \rho$$

### Step 3 — Isothermal assumption (valid for most wind-tunnel flows)
For many vehicle aerodynamics experiments, the temperature variation across the test section is small, and we can treat $K_{GD}$ as **constant** for a given wavelength and gas. For air at $\lambda = 589$ nm (sodium D-line), $K_{GD} = 2.27 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/kg.

### Step 4 — Final form (KTU high-yield form)
$$\boxed{\,n - 1 \;=\; K_{GD}\,\rho\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Light Deflection Angle in a Schlieren System

### Step 1 — Geometry of the optical system
Consider a collimated light beam of width $L$ passing through the test section in the $z$-direction. The refractive index varies across the section, so the beam is bent.

### Step 2 — Snell's law in differential form
For a stratified medium, the ray trajectory satisfies:

$$\dfrac{d}{ds}\left(n \dfrac{d\vec{r}}{ds}\right) = \nabla n$$

For small deflections, this reduces to the **Eikonal approximation** for the angle $\varepsilon_y$:

$$\varepsilon_y \;\approx\; \int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{1}{n}\dfrac{\partial n}{\partial y}\, dz$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Gladstone–Dale
Using $n - 1 = K_{GD}\rho$, and noting $n \approx 1$ for air:

$$\dfrac{1}{n}\dfrac{\partial n}{\partial y} \approx \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}(K_{GD}\rho) = K_{GD}\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial y}$$

### Step 4 — Final Schlieren-deflection formula
$$\boxed{\,\varepsilon_y \;\approx\; K_{GD}\,L\,\left(\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial y}\right)_{\!\text{avg}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** The light deflection angle $\varepsilon_y$ is **directly proportional** to (a) the Gladstone–Dale constant, (b) the test-section length $L$, and (c) the **transverse density gradient** $\partial \rho / \partial y$. This is why a knife-edge placed at the focal point converts the deflection into a brightness change.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Mach–Zehnder Interferometer Fringe Shift

### Step 1 — Optical path difference
The two arms of the interferometer traverse regions of refractive indices $n_1$ (reference) and $n_2(\vec{r})$ (test section). The optical path difference is:

$$\Delta \Phi = \int_{0}^{L}\!\left[n_2(x,y,z) - n_1\right] dz$$

### Step 2 — Apply Gladstone–Dale

$$\Delta \Phi = K_{GD}\!\int_{0}^{L}\!\left[\rho_2(x,y,z) - \rho_1\right] dz$$

### Step 3 — Convert to fringe shift
One wavelength of optical path difference corresponds to **one fringe shift** $\Delta N$:

$$\Delta \Phi = \lambda \cdot \Delta N$$

Therefore,

$$\boxed{\,\Delta N \;=\; \dfrac{K_{GD}}{\lambda}\int_{0}^{L}\!\left[\rho_2 - \rho_1\right] dz\,}$$

### Step 4 — Axisymmetric simplification (KTU favourite)
For a 2-D axisymmetric density field, Abel inversion yields:

$$\rho(r) - \rho_\infty = -\dfrac{\lambda}{\pi K_{GD}}\int_{r}^{R}\dfrac{d(\Delta N)}{d y}\,\dfrac{dy}{\sqrt{y^2 - r^2}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the PIV Velocity Vector

### Step 1 — Two laser pulses at known separation
At time $t$, pulse 1 illuminates the seeded flow. At time $t + \Delta t$, pulse 2 illuminates the same plane. The camera records both images on a single CCD frame (double-frame mode) or as two separate frames.

### Step 2 — Cross-correlation algorithm
The image is divided into small **interrogation windows** (typically $32 \times 32$ pixels). For each window, the spatial cross-correlation $R(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ between pulse-1 and pulse-2 sub-images is computed.

### Step 3 — Locate the correlation peak
The displacement vector for the window is given by the location of the correlation peak:

$$\Delta \vec{x} = \arg\max_{(s_x, s_y)}\, R(s_x, s_y)$$

### Step 4 — Convert displacement to velocity
Using the known magnification $M$ (pixel-to-meter) and the inter-pulse time $\Delta t$:

$$\boxed{\,\vec{V} = \dfrac{M \cdot \Delta \vec{x}}{\Delta t}\,}$$

## 3.5 Derivation of the Stokes Number — Tracer Faithfulness Criterion

### Step 1 — Force balance on a tracer particle
A spherical particle of diameter $d_p$ and density $\rho_p$ immersed in a flow of density $\rho$ and viscosity $\mu$ experiences drag force $F_D$ per Saffman's correction:

$$F_D = 3 \pi \mu d_p \vert V - V_p \vert$$

### Step 2 — Equation of motion (low Reynolds, neglecting virtual mass)
$$m_p \dfrac{d V_p}{dt} = F_D$$

where particle mass $m_p = \rho_p \pi d_p^{3}/6$.

### Step 3 — Particle relaxation time
The characteristic decay time (time for the particle to adjust to a step change in flow velocity) is:

$$\tau_p = \dfrac{\rho_p d_p^{2}}{18 \mu}$$

### Step 4 — Stokes number
The ratio of particle relaxation time to a characteristic flow time $L/V$ is:

$$\boxed{\,St = \dfrac{\rho_p d_p^{2} V}{18 \mu L}\,}$$

For $St \ll 1$, the particle faithfully follows the streamline — the foundational design criterion for PIV tracers and smoke particles.

## 3.6 Worked Example — Fringe Shift in a Vehicle Wake

**Problem (KTU-style):** A wind-tunnel test section of length $L = 0.5$ m contains air. Behind a model car, the integrated density difference along a light path is measured to be $\int (\rho_2 - \rho_1) dz = 0.04$ kg/m². If the laser wavelength is $\lambda = 632.8$ nm and $K_{GD} = 2.27 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/kg, find the fringe shift $\Delta N$.

**Step 1 — Write the formula**

$$\Delta N = \dfrac{K_{GD}}{\lambda}\int_{0}^{L}(\rho_2 - \rho_1)\,dz$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$\Delta N = \dfrac{2.27 \times 10^{-4}}{632.8 \times 10^{-9}} \times 0.04$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator and denominator**

$$\dfrac{2.27 \times 10^{-4}}{6.328 \times 10^{-7}} = 358.7\;\text{m}^{-1}\cdot\text{kg}^{-1}\cdot\text{kg/m}^2$$

Wait — careful with units. $K_{GD}$ has units of m³/kg, $\lambda$ has units of m, $\int \rho\, dz$ has units of kg/m². So the ratio is dimensionally:

$$\Delta N = \dfrac{(\text{m}^3/\text{kg})}{(\text{m})}\times(\text{kg}/\text{m}^2) = \text{dimensionless}\;✓$$

$$\Delta N = 358.7 \times 0.04 = 14.35$$

**Step 4 — Result**

$$\boxed{\,\Delta N \approx 14.35 \text{ fringes}\,}$$

A large fringe shift indicates a strong density gradient in the wake, typical of a poorly streamlined rear-end geometry.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree — Flow Visualization Techniques

The diagram below is a Mermaid block-level architecture mapping the **functional taxonomy** of flow visualization techniques, classified by the physical property exploited.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FLOW VISUALIZATION TECHNIQUES] --> B[TRACER METHODS]
    A --> C[OPTICAL METHODS]
    A --> D[SURFACE METHODS]
    A --> E[THERMAL METHODS]
    A --> F[MODERN TECHNIQUES]

    B --> B1[Smoke and Fog]
    B --> B2[Dye Injection]
    B --> B3[Hydrogen Bubbles]
    B --> B4[Helium Bubbles]

    C --> C1[Schlieren Photography]
    C --> C2[Shadowgraph]
    C --> C3[Mach Zehnder Interferometer]
    C --> C4[Laser Speckle]

    D --> D1[Oil Flow Technique]
    D --> D2[Tuft and Streamer]
    D --> D3[Sublimation Method]
    D --> D4[Chalk Dust Method]

    E --> E1[Infrared Thermography]
    E --> E2[Liquid Crystal Coatings]

    F --> F1[Particle Image Velocimetry]
    F --> F2[Pressure Sensitive Paint]
    F --> F3[Tomographic PIV]

    C1 --> C1A[Knife Edge Intensity]
    C1 --> C1B[Rainbow Schlieren]
    C2 --> C2A[Parallel Beam Setup]
    C3 --> C3A[Fringe Shift Quant]

    F1 --> F1A[2D PIV Plane]
    F1 --> F1B[Stereo PIV]
    F1 --> F1C[TR PIV]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — A Typical Vehicle Wind-Tunnel Smoke Visualization Setup

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Fog Generator] -->|Tracer Injection| nodeB[Settling Chamber]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Contraction Cone]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Test Section With Vehicle Model]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Light Sheet from Above]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Camera Array]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Optical Bench]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Acquisition Computer]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Streamline Map Output]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Velocity Field PIV]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Surface Oil Pattern Map]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Schlieren Imaging Pipeline for Vehicle Wakes

```mermaid
graph LR
    pSrc[Point Light Source] --> pColl[Collimating Lens]
    pColl --> pTest[Test Section With Vehicle]
    pTest --> pFoc[Focusing Lens]
    pFoc --> pKnife[Knife Edge at Focal Plane]
    pKnife --> pScr[Screen or Camera Sensor]
    pScr --> pProc[Image Processing Unit]
    pProc --> pOut1[Qualitative Density Gradient Image]
    pProc --> pOut2[Quantitative Refractive Index Map]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Block — Selection Heuristic for Vehicle Aero Lab

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Quantitative or Qualitative?}
    Q1 -->|Qualitative| Q2{What region?}
    Q1 -->|Quantitative| Q3{Quantity of interest?}

    Q2 -->|Surface| R1[Oil Flow or Tuft]
    Q2 -->|Wake| R2[Smoke Visualization]
    Q2 -->|Compressible| R3[Schlieren or Shadowgraph]

    Q3 -->|Velocity| R4[PIV or LDV]
    Q3 -->|Pressure| R5[PSP or Pressure Tap]
    Q3 -->|Temperature| R6[IR Thermography]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading hint for the diagrams:** The arrows indicate *flow of information or physical substance*. Start at the root node (top of each graph) and trace a path to a leaf node to identify the appropriate technique for a given experimental requirement.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define *Flow Visualization*. List any **two** surface flow visualization techniques used in vehicle aerodynamics.

**Model Answer:**
Flow visualization is the experimental technique used to make the normally invisible flow field around a vehicle (or any body) directly observable, by introducing tracers or by exploiting the optical properties of air.

Two surface flow visualization techniques are:
1. **Oil-flow technique** — A kerosene–dye mixture painted on the body migrates under the local wall shear stress, leaving streak patterns that indicate attached flow, separation, and reattachment.
2. **Tuft method** — Lightweight yarn tufts attached at grid points align with the local flow direction, giving a qualitative time-averaged map of the surface streamline direction.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** State the **Gladstone–Dale relation** and explain its significance in flow visualization.

**Model Answer:**
The Gladstone–Dale relation is:

$$n - 1 = K_{GD}\,\rho$$

where $n$ is the refractive index of the gas, $\rho$ is its density, and $K_{GD}$ is the Gladstone–Dale constant ($K_{GD} \approx 2.27 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/kg for air at visible wavelengths).

**Significance:** It is the bridge between a *thermodynamic* property (density) and an *optical* property (refractive index). All optical density-based flow visualization methods — Schlieren, Shadowgraph, and Interferometry — fundamentally depend on this linear relationship to convert flow features into visible images.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 1 | 14 Marks]**

### Question A (Choice 1) — Schlieren & Interferometry

**(a)** With the help of a **schematic block diagram**, explain the working principle of a *Schlieren system* used in vehicle aerodynamic studies. Derive the relation between the light deflection angle and the density gradient in the test section. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the working of the *Mach–Zehnder interferometer* as a quantitative flow visualization tool. Derive the fringe-shift equation in terms of density variation along the optical path. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Step 1 — Schematic:**
Refer to the functional block diagram in Section 4.3. The essential elements are:
1. Point light source
2. Collimating lens
3. Test section containing the vehicle model
4. Focusing lens
5. Knife-edge at the focal point of the focusing lens
6. Screen / camera sensor

**Step 2 — Working principle:**
Light from the source is collimated into parallel rays that traverse the test section. Where density gradients exist (e.g., over a windshield or in a wake), the rays are refracted. The focusing lens brings the rays to the focal plane where the **knife-edge** blocks half of the focused image, converting angular deflection into intensity variation.

**Step 3 — Derivation of deflection angle:**
Using the Eikonal approximation for small deflections:

$$\varepsilon_y = \int_{0}^{L}\dfrac{1}{n}\dfrac{\partial n}{\partial y}\,dz$$

Substituting the Gladstone–Dale relation $n - 1 = K_{GD}\rho$ and approximating $n \approx 1$ for air:

$$\varepsilon_y \approx K_{GD}\,L\,\left(\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial y}\right)_{\text{avg}}$$

**Step 4 — Result and interpretation:**
A larger density gradient (e.g., near a windshield or in a strong wake) produces a larger light deflection, which the knife-edge converts into a stronger dark/bright contrast on the screen.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Schematic:** A coherent laser beam is split by a half-silvered mirror into two paths — the *reference* path (through still air) and the *test* path (through the wind tunnel section containing the vehicle). After traversing their respective paths, the beams are recombined and projected onto a screen, producing interference fringes.

**Step 2 — Optical path difference:**
The OPD between the two beams is:

$$\Delta \Phi = \int_{0}^{L}\!\left[n_2(x,y,z) - n_1\right]dz$$

**Step 3 — Apply Gladstone–Dale:**

$$\Delta \Phi = K_{GD}\!\int_{0}^{L}\!\left[\rho_2(x,y,z) - \rho_1\right]dz$$

**Step 4 — Convert OPD to fringe shift:**
Since one wavelength $\lambda$ corresponds to one fringe:

$$\Delta N = \dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{\lambda} = \dfrac{K_{GD}}{\lambda}\int_{0}^{L}(\rho_2 - \rho_1)\,dz$$

**Step 5 — Application:** For axisymmetric density fields, Abel inversion can be applied to extract $\rho(r)$ from the measured $\Delta N(y)$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — PIV & PSP

**(a)** Describe the **Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV)** technique for measuring velocity fields around a vehicle. Derive the velocity vector expression from the displacement measured between two laser pulses. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **Pressure-Sensitive Paint (PSP)** technique. State the Stern–Volmer equation and discuss its use in vehicle aerodynamic testing. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Step 1 — Seeding:** The wind-tunnel flow is seeded with fine tracer particles (typically DEHS or olive oil droplets, ~1 µm diameter) such that $St \ll 1$, ensuring faithful flow tracing.

**Step 2 — Light sheet illumination:** A pulsed Nd:YAG laser is shaped into a thin (~1 mm) light sheet that illuminates the measurement plane at time $t_1$.

**Step 3 — Second pulse:** After a precisely controlled inter-pulse delay $\Delta t$ (typically 1–100 µs), a second laser pulse illuminates the same plane at $t_2 = t_1 + \Delta t$.

**Step 4 — Image capture:** A CCD/CMOS camera captures both frames. The image is divided into small **interrogation windows** (e.g., 32 × 32 pixels).

**Step 5 — Cross-correlation:** For each window, the spatial cross-correlation $R(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ between frame 1 and frame 2 is computed. The location of the correlation peak gives the **displacement vector** $\Delta \vec{x}$.

**Step 6 — Velocity:**
$$\vec{V} = \dfrac{M\cdot \Delta\vec{x}}{\Delta t}$$

where $M$ is the optical magnification factor.

**Step 7 — Output:** A vector field of typically $10^4$–$10^5$ instantaneous velocity vectors.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Principle:**
A luminescent coating is applied to the vehicle model surface. The paint absorbs light at one wavelength and re-emits at another. **Oxygen molecules** in the surrounding air *quench* this luminescence in proportion to their partial pressure.

**Step 2 — Stern–Volmer equation:**

$$\dfrac{I_0}{I} = A(p) + B(p)\left[1 + K_{SV}(T)\cdot p_{O_2}\right]$$

where:
- $I_0$ = luminescence intensity in absence of oxygen
- $I$ = measured intensity
- $K_{SV}(T)$ = Stern–Volmer constant (temperature dependent)
- $p_{O_2}$ = local partial pressure of oxygen
- $A(p), B(p)$ = calibration polynomial coefficients

**Step 3 — Application in vehicle aerodynamics:**
PSP allows full-surface pressure mapping (thousands of points) in a single run — orders of magnitude faster than discrete pressure-tap scans. Critical for resolving leading-edge pressure gradients on wings, A-pillar vortices, and underbody flows.

**Step 4 — Limitations:**
- Temperature-sensitive (must be calibrated or simultaneously imaged in two wavelengths)
- Time resolution limited by paint response (~ms to s)
- Requires in-situ calibration against a known pressure reference

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mistake in Gladstone–Dale:** Students often write $n - 1 = K_{GD}/\rho$ (inverted). Always cross-check by substituting STP values: $n - 1 = 2.93 \times 10^{-4}$, which is correctly obtained from $K_{GD} \cdot \rho = 2.27 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.225 = 2.78 \times 10^{-4}$ ✓
> 2. **Confusing Schlieren with Shadowgraph:** Schlieren uses a *first-derivative* ($\partial \rho / \partial y$) signal with a knife-edge. Shadowgraph uses a *second-derivative* ($\nabla^2 \rho$) signal without a knife-edge. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this mix-up.
> 3. **Skipping the boundary assumptions in derivations:** Always state that the flow is assumed *quasi-steady*, *two-dimensional*, and *isothermal* before applying the Eikonal equation.
> 4. **Drawing a Schlieren diagram without the knife-edge:** This loses 1–2 marks even if the rest of the answer is correct. The knife-edge is the signature element of the system.
> 5. **Writing "PIV measures pressure":** PIV measures **velocity**. Pressure is measured by PSP or pressure taps.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Flow Visualization Techniques (Module 1):**

- **Core definition:** Flow visualization makes invisible fluid-flow features observable by tracers or by exploiting optical properties of the medium.
- **Gladstone–Dale Law:** $n - 1 = K_{GD}\rho$ — the **fundamental optical equation** for Schlieren/Shadowgraph/Interferometry.
- **Schlieren** uses a *first-derivative* density signal + knife-edge; **Shadowgraph** uses a *second-derivative* density signal + no knife-edge; **Interferometry** measures *integrated* density via fringe shift $\Delta N$.
- **PIV formula:** $\vec{V} = M \cdot \Delta \vec{x} / \Delta t$ — the cornerstone of modern quantitative vehicle aero testing.
- **Tracer faithfulness criterion:** Stokes number $St \ll 1$ for the particle to follow the streamline faithfully.
- **Surface methods:** Oil-flow (most quantitative for separation detection), Tuft (simplest, cheapest), Sublimation (steady-state boundary-layer transition visualization).
- **PSP:** Stern–Volmer equation relates luminescence intensity to oxygen partial pressure; ideal for full-surface pressure maps.
- **IR Thermography:** Based on recovery-temperature change in compressible flow; used for hot surfaces (brake discs, exhaust manifolds).
- **Vehicle-specific applications:** A-pillar vortex identification (smoke), rear-glass separation (oil-flow), front-wing flow attachment (tuft), underbody flow (water channel + dye), brake-disc cooling (IR thermography).
- **Selection heuristic:** Surface flow → oil/tuft; Wake → smoke; Compressible → Schlieren; Velocity → PIV; Pressure → PSP; Temperature → IR.
- **Gladstone–Dale constant value (air, 589 nm):** **$K_{GD} = 2.27 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/kg** — remember for numericals.
- **Standard atmospheric values to memorize:** $n = 1.000293$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of Aerodynamics - Resistances to Vehicle Motion - Aerodynamic Drag
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Aerodynamics - Resistances to Vehicle Motion - Aerodynamic Drag # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Fundamentals of Aerodynamics — The Engineering Definition > [!NOTE] > **Aerodynamics** is the branch of fluid mechanics that studies the motion of air (and the forces it exerts) around solid bodies. In the context of ground vehicles, **Vehicle Aerodynamics** deals specifically with the interaction between the vehicle body and the surrounding air stream, and its influence on **performance**, **stability**, **fuel economy**, and **emissions**. For KTU examinations, the most critical sub-branch is **external compressible/incompressible flow** past a *bluff body* (a car is a bluff body — it does not allow the flow to converge smoothly to a single trailing point like an airfoil does). The **four fundamental physical properties** you must internalize before tackling vehicle drag are: | # | Property | Standard Symbol | Typical SI Unit | Vehicle Relevance | |---|----------|-----------------|-----------------|-------------------| | 1 | Air Density | $\rho$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | **1.225 kg/m³** at sea-level, 15 °C ISA | | 2 | Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | $\text{N·s/m}^2$ or Pa·s | $1.789 \times 10^{-5}$ at 15 °C | | 3 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu/\rho$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | $1.46 \times 10^{-5}$ at 15 °C | | 4 | Speed of Sound | $a$ | $\text{m/s}$ | **340.3 m/s** at 15 °C | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Continuum Assumption:** For all KTU vehicle-aerodynamics problems, air is treated as a **continuous fluid (continuum)**. This is valid because the mean free path of air molecules ($\approx 68$ nm at sea level) is vastly smaller than any feature on a car (millimetres to metres). Knudsen number $Kn \ll 0.001$. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Open-Top Bicycle" Analogy Imagine you are riding an **open-top bicycle** on a calm day. - At **walking speed (1–2 m/s)**, you barely feel the air. A piece of paper held in front of you droops — the air behaves almost like a *thick, lazy syrup*. This is the world of **viscous, low-Reynolds-number flow**. - At **scooter speed (8 m/s ≈ 28 km/h)**, the paper flutters; you must squint. Air has begun to resist being *sliced*. - At **car speed (30 m/s ≈ 108 km/h)**, the air is now a *relentless wall* you must constantly push aside. Energy is being dumped into: 1. **Pushing air forward** (momentum change → *pressure/form drag*). 2. **Rubbing along every square centimetre of your body and the bicycle** (*skin-friction drag*). 3. **Whirling little tornadoes off your shoulders** (vortex shedding → *induced/interference drag*). - At **racing speed (70 m/s ≈ 252 km/h)**, the air has become 100× more resistive than at walking pace — because drag grows with the **square of velocity**. > [!TIP] > **Key takeaway for the KTU exam:** Doubling the speed of a car **quadruples** the aerodynamic drag force and requires **eight times** the power to overcome (since $P = F \cdot V \propto V^3$). This single fact is the heart of modern vehicle fuel-economy design. --- ## 1.3 Resistances to Vehicle Motion — The Four "Horsemen" When a car travels at constant speed on a level road, the **tractive force** delivered by the engine/tyres must exactly balance **four distinct resistive forces**: $$F_{\text{tractive}} = F_{\text{rolling}} + F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{acceleration}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Tag:** The 2024 scheme explicitly groups these as *"Resistances to Vehicle Motion"*. Out of these, **Aerodynamic Drag ($F_{\text{aero}}$)** is the only one that *grows with $V^2$* and is the dominant resistance above ~80 km/h. | Resistance | Symbol | Velocity Dependence | Dominant Range | |---|---|---|---| | **Rolling Resistance** | $F_r$ | $\propto V$ (approx.) | 0 – 100 km/h | | **Grade/Climbing Resistance** | $F_g$ | Independent of $V$ | Always (on slopes) | | **Aerodynamic Drag** | $F_D$ | $\propto V^2$ | Above ~80 km/h | | **Acceleration/Inertia Resistance** | $F_a$ | $\propto \dot{V}$ | Transient only | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Resistances — Operational Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Rolling Resistance ($F_r$) Caused by the **hysteresis loss** in tyres and the **relative sliding** at the tyre–road contact patch. It is mathematically expressed as: $$F_r = f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$$ - $f_r$ = coefficient of rolling resistance (≈ **0.012–0.015** for a modern radial passenger-car tyre on asphalt). - $m$ = vehicle mass (kg). - $g$ = 9.81 m/s². - $\theta$ = road gradient angle. > **Why it matters:** A heavier car always consumes more fuel, but rolling resistance grows *linearly* with mass, while aerodynamic drag is **mass-independent**. ### 2.1.2 Grade Resistance ($F_g$) Purely a gravity component along the slope: $$F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$ For a 10 % grade, $\theta = \arctan(0.10) \approx 5.71°$, $\sin\theta \approx 0.0995$. ### 2.1.3 Acceleration Resistance ($F_a$) Newton's second law for the entire vehicle (including rotating inertias of engine, wheels, driveshaft): $$F_a = m \cdot (1 + k) \cdot a$$ where $k$ is the *effective rotational inertia factor* (typically 0.04–0.08 for a passenger car). ### 2.1.4 Aerodynamic Drag ($F_D$) — *The KTU Showstopper* Detailed in §2.2 below. --- ## 2.2 Aerodynamic Drag — The Master Equation The **total aerodynamic drag force** opposing a vehicle's motion is given by the **drag equation** (also called the *Froude formula* in road-vehicle literature): $$\boxed{F_D = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^2\,C_D\,A_f}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the single most important equation in the entire KTU PEAUT752 syllabus. Memorize it verbatim.** | Symbol | Name | Units | Typical Value | |---|---|---|---| | $F_D$ | Aerodynamic drag force | N | — | | $\rho$ | Air (mass) density | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | **1.225** (sea-level, 15 °C) | | $V$ | Free-stream vehicle speed | m/s | — | | $C_D$ | Drag coefficient (dimensionless) | — | 0.25 (Tesla Model S) – 0.38 (typical SUV) | | $A_f$ | Frontal (projected) area | $\text{m}^2$ | 1.8 – 2.8 | --- ##
