> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly use the **frontal area $A$** instead of the **base area $A_b$** in the base-drag formula. This yields the wrong answer and costs 2–3 marks. Always identify *which* area corresponds to *which* formula.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Compare the wake structure and drag coefficient of a notchback with that of a fastback. Discuss why the fastback is aerodynamically superior. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturer offers two rear-end treatments for a notchback: (i) a **boat-tail extension** of length 200 mm that raises $C_{p,b}$ from −0.45 to −0.30, and (ii) a **rear lip spoiler** of 50 mm that raises $C_{p,b}$ from −0.45 to −0.38. The base area is $A_b = 1.5$ m² and the car travels at 100 km/h. Take $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. Which treatment gives greater drag reduction, and by how many Newtons? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

| Feature | Notchback | Fastback |
|---|---|---|
| Rear profile | Abrupt 3-box truncation | Continuous sloping roofline |
| Separation point | Fixed at sharp edge | Further rearward, often attached |
| Wake size | Large, ~0.6 H | Small, ~0.3 H |
| $C_D$ typical | 0.30 – 0.40 | 0.22 – 0.28 |
| $C_{p,b}$ | −0.30 to −0.50 | −0.15 to −0.30 |

The fastback is superior because its continuous, gently curving geometry produces a **smaller adverse pressure gradient**, allowing the boundary layer to remain **attached** much further rearward. This results in a **smaller wake**, **higher (less negative) base pressure**, and consequently **lower drag**. The notchback's sharp geometric corner forces separation regardless of boundary-layer state, locking in a large wake and high drag.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Common speed conversion:**

$$
V = 100 \times \tfrac{5}{18} = 27.78 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(27.78)^2 = 472.6 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Treatment (i) — Boat-tail:**

$$
\Delta C_{p,b} = -0.30 - (-0.45) = +0.15
$$

$$
\Delta F_{D,\text{base}}^{(i)} = -\Delta C_{p,b}\cdot q_\infty \cdot A_b = -(0.15)(472.6)(1.5) = -106.3 \text{ N}
$$

**Treatment (ii) — Spoiler:**

$$
\Delta C_{p,b} = -0.38 - (-0.45) = +0.07
$$

$$
\Delta F_{D,\text{base}}^{(ii)} = -(0.07)(472.6)(1.5) = -49.6 \text{ N}
$$

**Conclusion:** The **boat-tail extension** provides a greater drag reduction by **≈ 56.7 N** more than the rear lip spoiler.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Notchback Drag**

- **Definition:** Notchback = three-box body with abrupt rear truncation; dominant drag is **base (pressure) drag**.
- **Key Range:** Modern notchback $C_D$ is **0.30 – 0.40**; base area $A_b$ is typically **60–75%** of frontal area $A$.
- **Core Equation:** $F_{D,\text{base}} = -C_{p,b} \cdot q_\infty \cdot A_b$.
- **Sign Convention:** $C_{p,b} < 0$ always for separated flow. The minus sign in the formula converts suction into positive drag.
- **Flow Stages:** (1) Acceleration over roof, (2) Convection down rear glass, (3) **Forced separation** at sharp edge, (4) Shear-layer roll-up, (5) Recirculating low-pressure wake.
- **Geometric Levers:** Increase rear glass angle (to ~45°), shorten boot deck, round C-pillars, add boat-tail or rear lip spoiler.
- **Comparison Anchor:** Fastback $C_D$ ≈ **0.25**; Notchback $C_D$ ≈ **0.35** — remember this for short-answer questions.
- **Unit Conversion:** 1 km/h = **5/18** m/s. KTU papers frequently test this.
- **Common Error:** Using $A$ (frontal) instead of $A_b$ (base) in the base drag formula. **Always identify the correct area.**
- **Industrial Significance:** A 0.01 reduction in $C_D$ saves ~0.5–1.0 g/km $CO_2$ — important for EV range and fleet emissions.
- **Wake Physics:** The C-pillar horseshoe vortices dominate the near-wake structure; their strength scales with rear glass angle and C-pillar radius.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hatch Back Drag - Effect of Downforce on Vehicle Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hatch Back Drag - Effect of Downforce on Vehicle Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hatchback Drag — Formal Definition

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, **Hatchback Drag** refers to the total aerodynamic resistance experienced by a two-box passenger vehicle whose rear terminates in a near-vertical tailgate (typically inclined between 25°–50° from horizontal). Because the rear glass and tailgate present an abrupt geometric discontinuity to the oncoming flow, the boundary layer cannot remain attached all the way to the trailing edge. The flow **separates** at the upper rear edge, generating a large, low-pressure, recirculating **wake** behind the car. The resulting pressure imbalance between the high-pressure front stagnation region and the low-pressure base region produces a large **pressure drag (form drag)** component.

Mathematically, the total drag is decomposed as:

$$D = D_{pressure} + D_{skin\,friction} + D_{interference} + D_{induced}$$

For a typical hatchback, **pressure drag constitutes 55%–65%** of the total drag, and skin friction only about 10%–15%.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A hatchback is classified as a **bluff body** in ground-vehicle aerodynamics because the rear geometry is *abruptly truncated*. The drag coefficient (Cd) of a stock hatchback lies in the range **0.30 – 0.40**, whereas a well-streamlined sedan reaches **0.25 – 0.30**, and a modern EV can achieve **0.20 – 0.24**.

### 1.2 Downforce — Formal Definition

**Downforce (FL)** is the vertical component of the aerodynamic force generated by pressure differences around a moving vehicle, directed *downwards* into the road surface. It is mathematically the *negative* of lift (L) and is produced deliberately by wings, splitters, diffusers, and shaped underbodies. Downforce increases the **vertical load (W)** on the tyres, thereby raising the maximum transmissible friction force at the tyre–road interface.

$$F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,C_L\,A$$

For road cars, the downforce coefficient $C_L$ is small and negative (typically $-0.1$ to $-0.4$). For high-downforce race cars (F1, LMP), $C_L$ can reach **$-2.5$ to $-3.5$**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Parachute vs. Vacuum Cleaner" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump:**
> - **Hatchback drag** behaves like holding an open umbrella behind you while running — the rear "pocket" of swirling air sucks the car backwards.
> - **Downforce** behaves like pressing the car harder onto the road with an invisible hand — the harder you press, the more friction your tyres have, just like pushing a chair harder into the floor before sliding it.
> - The catch? You can't have a free lunch: most downforce-generating devices also create drag. A race team wants a *low* drag-to-downforce ratio — they want "downforce without the parachute."

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Air density (sea level) | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Kinematic viscosity of air | $\nu$ | **1.46 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** |
| Hatchback drag coefficient | $C_d$ | **0.30 – 0.40** |
| Sedan drag coefficient | $C_d$ | **0.25 – 0.30** |
| Race car downforce coeff. | $C_L$ | **−2.5 to −3.5** |
| Road car downforce coeff. | $C_L$ | **−0.1 to −0.4** |
| Reference frontal area (hatchback) | $A$ | **2.0 – 2.4 m²** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physics of Flow Over a Hatchback — Step-by-Step

1. **Oncoming Air (Free Stream):** Air approaches the front of the vehicle with velocity $V_\infty$ and static pressure $p_\infty$.
2. **Stagnation at Front Bumper:** At the nose, kinetic energy converts into pressure, raising the local pressure to $p_0 = p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}$ (Bernoulli). This creates a high-pressure *bulb* on the nose.
3. **Acceleration Over the Bonnet and Roof:** The air is forced to accelerate over the curved upper surface, causing local pressure to drop (favourable pressure gradient → attached flow).
4. **Critical Rear Glass Section (The Bottleneck):** As the roof curves down toward the tailgate, the slope steepens. The adverse pressure gradient grows.
5. **Separation at the Upper Rear Edge:** The boundary layer, no longer possessing enough momentum to climb against the rising pressure, detaches from the surface — typically within 5–15% of the roof-to-glass transition.
6. **Wake Formation:** Behind the car, a large recirculating zone forms. Inside the wake, static pressure drops well below ambient. This pressure deficit *sucks* the car rearward.
7. **Base Pressure Coefficient:** Measured on production hatchbacks, $C_{p,base} \approx -0.6$ to $-0.9$, confirming why base drag dominates.
8. **Vortex Shedding:** The shear layers roll up into two counter-rotating **C-type vortices** at the rear window edges, which periodically shed — a major source of unsteady drag and acoustic noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why hatchbacks are *worse* than notchbacks and fastbacks:**
> - **Fastback** (e.g., Tesla Model S): long, gently sloping rear → flow stays attached further back → smaller wake → *lower* drag.
> - **Notchback / Sedan** (e.g., Honda City): separated bootlid + small underbody wake → moderate drag.
> - **Hatchback** (e.g., Maruti Swift): abrupt vertical tailgate → immediate separation → largest wake → *highest* drag among the three.

### 2.2 Why Downforce Helps Performance — The Tyre–Road Friction Argument

The maximum lateral acceleration a tyre can transmit is:

$$a_{y,max} = \mu\,g + \tfrac{\mu}{m}\,\big(F_{L,front} + F_{L,rear}\big)$$

- $\mu$ = tyre–road friction coefficient (≈ 1.0–1.6 for performance tyres, dry tarmac)
- $m$ = vehicle mass (kg)
- $F_{L,front}, F_{L,rear}$ = downforce at front and rear axles (N)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²

Thus, more downforce → higher $a_{y,max}$ → faster cornering speeds. Similarly, braking distance reduces because the available friction force is larger.

### 2.3 The Drag–Downforce Trade-Off (Aerodynamic Efficiency)

In motorsport, the metric used is the **L/D ratio** (lift-to-drag, or equivalently, downforce-to-drag):

$$\eta_{aero} = \frac{\vert C_L \vert}{C_d}$$

A high-performance F1 car achieves $\eta_{aero} \approx 3.0$ to $4.5$ (high downforce, manageable drag), while road cars with passive underbody aero achieve only 0.5–1.5.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,C_d\,A$ | Aerodynamic drag force | N |
| 2 | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,C_L\,A$ | Downforce (lift) | N |
| 3 | $P_{drag} = D \cdot V$ | Power required to overcome drag | W |
| 4 | $C_d = C_{D,pressure} + C_{D,friction} + C_{D,interference}$ | Drag decomposition | — |
| 5 | $a_{y,max} = \mu\,g + (\mu/m)(F_{L,f}+F_{L,r})$ | Cornering limit with downforce | m/s² |
| 6 | $\eta_{aero} = \vert C_L \vert / C_d$ | Aero efficiency (L/D) | — |
| 7 | $p_0 - p_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}$ | Stagnation pressure (Bernoulli) | Pa |
| 8 | $C_p = (p - p_\infty) / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2})$ | Pressure coefficient | — |
| 9 | $D_{base} \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} A_{base}(1 - C_{p,base})$ | Base drag (wake suction) | N |
| 10 | $F_{brake,max} = \mu(m g + F_L)$ | Maximum braking force | N |
| 11 | $V_{corner,max} = \sqrt{\mu g\,R + (\mu R/m)F_L}$ | Cornering speed in radius R | m/s |
| 12 | $Re_L = V L / \nu$ | Reynolds number (flow regime) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - Formula 1 + 2 are the **fundamental aerodynamic force equations** — every drag/downforce problem starts here.
> - Formula 5 is the *bridge* between aerodynamics and chassis dynamics — KTU examiners love to test it.
> - Formula 11 explicitly shows how downforce **boosts cornering speed** — the cornerstone of vehicle-performance problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Aerodynamic Drag on a Hatchback

Starting from Newton's second law applied to a control volume enclosing the vehicle, the drag force equals the net rate of momentum outflow plus the integrated pressure and shear forces on the body surface:

$$
D = \oint_{S} \left[ (p - p_\infty)\hat{n}_x + \tau_{w,x} \right] dA
$$

Splitting the pressure and viscous contributions:

$$
D = \underbrace{\oint (p - p_\infty)\hat{n}_x\, dA}_{D_{pressure}} \;+\; \underbrace{\oint \tau_{w,x}\, dA}_{D_{friction}}
$$

Non-dimensionalising by $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} A$:

$$
C_d = C_{d,pressure} + C_{d,friction}
$$

For a typical hatchback, with $A_{base} \approx 1.2$ m² and $A_{frontal} \approx 2.2$ m², $C_{p,base} \approx -0.75$:

$$
C_{d,base} \approx \frac{A_{base}}{A_{frontal}} (1 - C_{p,base}) = \frac{1.2}{2.2}(1 - (-0.75)) = \frac{1.2 \times 1.75}{2.2} \approx 0.955/2.2
$$

$$
C_{d,base} \approx 0.434
$$

This single base-drag term alone can exceed the total drag of a streamlined sedan — *the very reason hatchbacks are drag-prone*.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Downforce Contribution to Maximum Cornering Speed

Consider a vehicle of mass $m$ traversing a curve of radius $R$ at speed $V$. Vertical equilibrium gives the total normal force:

$$
N_{total} = m g + F_L
$$

where $F_L = F_{L,front} + F_{L,rear}$. Horizontal equilibrium (centripetal balance):

$$
m \frac{V^{2}}{R} = \mu\,N_{total} = \mu(m g + F_L)
$$

Solving for $V$:

$$
V_{max} = \sqrt{\mu\,R\left(g + \frac{F_L}{m}\right)}
$$

Substituting $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} C_L A$, we get a *self-consistent* equation. For the case where $F_L \ll m g$ (road car regime), the binomial approximation gives:

$$
V_{max} \approx \sqrt{\mu g R} \left[ 1 + \frac{F_L}{2 m g} \right]
$$

The fractional gain in cornering speed is:

$$
\frac{\Delta V}{V} \approx \frac{F_L}{2 m g}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):**
Given: $m = 1500$ kg, $C_L A = 0.6$ m², $V = 30$ m/s, $R = 100$ m, $\mu = 1.2$.

Step 1 — Compute downforce:

$$
F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(30)^{2}(0.6) = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 900 \times 0.6
$$

$$
F_L = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 540 = 330.75 \text{ N}
$$

Step 2 — Compute $V_{max}$ *with* downforce:

$$
V_{max} = \sqrt{1.2 \times 100 \times \left(9.81 + \frac{330.75}{1500}\right)} = \sqrt{120 \times 10.0305}
$$

$$
V_{max} = \sqrt{1203.66} \approx 34.69 \text{ m/s} = 124.9 \text{ km/h}
$$

Step 3 — Compute $V_{max}$ *without* downforce:

$$
V_{max,0} = \sqrt{1.2 \times 100 \times 9.81} = \sqrt{1177.2} \approx 34.31 \text{ m/s} = 123.5 \text{ km/h}
$$

Step 4 — Net gain:

$$
\Delta V = 1.4 \text{ km/h (a 1.13% gain from only 330 N of downforce).}
$$

This is small for road cars but is **massive** in racing, where $F_L$ can equal or exceed $m g$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Penalty for Downforce-Generating Devices

Suppose a rear wing generates downforce $F_L$ but also produces extra drag $D_{wing}$. The additional power needed at speed $V$ is:

$$
\Delta P = D_{wing} \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{3} C_{D,wing} A
$$

The corresponding *useful* cornering benefit (from the previous derivation) is equivalent to additional mass held in a turn. The trade-off is captured by the **aero efficiency** $\eta = \vert C_L \vert / C_d$. For the same drag budget, a high-$\eta$ device gives more cornering grip.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Key Phenomena vs. Vehicle Performance Metric

| Phenomenon | Top Speed | Cornering | Braking | Fuel Economy | NVH |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Drag | ↓↓ | ↔ | ↔ | ↓↓ | ↑ noise |
| ↑ Downforce (front-biased) | ↓↓ | ↑ (entry) | ↑ | ↓ | ↔ |
| ↑ Downforce (rear-biased) | ↓ | ↑ (exit) | ↔ | ↓ | ↔ |
| ↑ Base pressure recovery (boat-tail) | ↑↑ | ↔ | ↔ | ↑↑ | ↓ noise |
| ↑ Ride height | ↑ | ↓↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ |
| ↓ Ride height | ↓ | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↔ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the matrix:**
> - Downforce is *not* free — every Newton of downforce costs watts of engine power.
> - Front-bias vs. rear-bias determines whether the car is more stable under braking (front-bias) or more agile on corner exit (rear-bias).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hatchback Drag Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Free Stream Air V_infty] --> B[Stagnation at Front Bumper]
    B --> C[Acceleration over Bonnet and Roof]
    C --> D[Adverse Pressure Gradient at Rear Glass]
    D --> E[Boundary Layer Separation]
    E --> F[Base / Wake Recirculation Zone]
    F --> G[Low Base Pressure C_p less than 0]
    G --> H[Net Rearward Pressure Drag]
    F --> I[C-type Vortices at Tail Edges]
    I --> J[Unsteady Buffeting and Noise]
    H --> K[Total Hatchback Drag Force D]
    J --> K
    L[Skin Friction on Body Panels] --> K
    M[Underbody Flow Losses] --> K
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Downforce Distribution and Vehicle Performance Impact

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AeroSources[Aerodynamic Downforce Sources]
        DF1[Front Splitter / Wing]
        DF2[Rear Wing / Spoiler]
        DF3[Underbody Diffuser]
        DF4[Ground Effect Venturi]
    end

    AeroSources --> SumF[Summation: F_L = F_Lf + F_Lr]
    SumF --> VLoad[Increased Vertical Load on Tyres]
    VLoad --> Fric[Higher Tyre Road Friction mu*N]
    Fric --> Corner[Higher Cornering a_ymax]
    Fric --> Brake[Shorter Braking Distance]
    Fric --> Accel[Improved Traction for Acceleration]
    Fric --> Stab[Enhanced High Speed Stability]

    SumF --> PenA[Drag Penalty D_para]
    PenA --> PenB[Reduced Top Speed]
    PenA --> PenC[Higher Fuel Consumption]
    PenA --> PenD[Increased Power Demand P = D*V]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Flow Regimes Around a Hatchback

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FrontStage[Front Stagnation Region]
        S1[p greater than p_infty High Pressure Bulb]
    end
    subgraph TopStage[Roof Region]
        S2[Favourable Pressure Gradient Attached Flow]
    end
    subgraph RearStage[Rear Glass Region]
        S3[Adverse Pressure Gradient]
        S4[Separation Point]
    end
    subgraph WakeStage[Wake Zone]
        S5[Recirculating Vortices]
        S6[Low Base Pressure]
    end
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S4 --> S6
    S5 --> S6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** In the schematic above, the *separation point* in node `S4` is the critical geometric location — moving it further rearward (e.g., by adding a roof-mounted spoiler) reduces wake size and therefore drag.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Why is the drag coefficient of a typical hatchback higher than that of a sedan or a fastback?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A hatchback has an **abrupt, near-vertical tailgate** that causes **premature boundary-layer separation** at the upper rear edge. This produces a **large, low-pressure wake** behind the car, generating high **pressure (form) drag**. A sedan has a separated bootlid and a smaller wake, while a fastback has a long, gently sloping rear that keeps the flow attached for a longer distance, delaying separation and shrinking the wake. Hence, $C_d$ follows the order: **fastback < sedan < hatchback** (typically 0.25 < 0.30 < 0.35). **(1 mark each for: tailgate geometry, wake formation, comparative Cd values.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define aerodynamic downforce and state its effect on a vehicle's cornering ability.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Downforce** is the vertical component of the aerodynamic force that pushes the vehicle onto the road, given by $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} C_L A$, where $C_L$ is negative. **(1 mark)** It increases the **normal load** $N = mg + F_L$ on the tyres, raising the **maximum lateral friction force** $F_{y,max} = \mu N$. **(1 mark)** This raises the maximum cornering speed as $V_{max} = \sqrt{\mu g R\,(1 + F_L/mg)}$, improving high-speed cornering stability. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the physical mechanism of **drag generation over a hatchback car**. Discuss the role of **flow separation**, **wake formation**, and **base pressure** in determining the total drag. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hatchback has a **frontal area of 2.2 m²** and a **drag coefficient of 0.36**. The vehicle travels at **90 km/h** against a headwind of 20 km/h. The air density is **1.2 kg/m³**. Calculate:
   (i) The **total aerodynamic drag force** on the car. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The **power required** to overcome this drag. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) If a small rear spoiler reduces $C_d$ by 8% and increases downforce by 25 N at this speed, find the **new drag force** and comment on the trade-off. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) Mechanism of hatchback drag — 7 marks:**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Air approaching the front bumper stagnates; pressure rises locally to $p_0 = p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}$. This creates a high-pressure region on the nose.
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Over the bonnet and roof, the air accelerates (favourable pressure gradient), and the boundary layer remains attached. Pressure drops below ambient.
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** At the rear glass, the surface slope steepens abruptly. The boundary layer experiences a strong adverse pressure gradient and **separates** at the upper rear edge (typically 80–90% of roof length).
- **Step 4 (2 marks):** Behind the car, a **large recirculating wake** forms with $C_{p,base} \approx -0.7$ to $-0.9$. The pressure imbalance between the high-pressure front and low-pressure base produces the dominant **pressure drag** component.
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** Two counter-rotating **C-vortices** form at the rear-window edges, shedding periodically. This contributes to unsteady drag and cabin buffeting noise. A sketch must show stagnation point, attached flow, separation point, wake, and vortices.

**(b) Numerical solution — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute the relative velocity:**
$V_{rel} = 90 + 20 = 110$ km/h $= 110 \times 5/18 = 30.56$ m/s

**Step 2 — Compute the drag force (3 marks):**

$$
D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} C_d A = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.2)(30.56)^{2}(0.36)(2.2)
$$

$$
D = 0.6 \times 933.91 \times 0.36 \times 2.2 = 0.6 \times 933.91 \times 0.792
$$

$$
D \approx 443.75 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute power required (2 marks):**

$$
P = D \cdot V = 443.75 \times 30.56 \approx 13{,}564 \text{ W} \approx 13.56 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Spoiler effect (2 marks):**
New $C_d = 0.36 \times 0.92 = 0.3312$

$$
D_{new} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times (30.56)^{2} \times 0.3312 \times 2.2 \approx 408.4 \text{ N}
$$

**Drag reduction:** $\Delta D \approx 35.3$ N (≈ 8% reduction)
**Comment:** The spoiler also generates 25 N of downforce, raising tyre grip. The trade-off is a *gain* on both fronts: less drag *and* more grip — an ideal scenario. In racing, the same downforce would normally cost more drag; this aerodynamic design is efficient.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **effect of downforce on vehicle performance**, covering cornering, braking, acceleration, and top speed. Derive the relationship between downforce and **maximum cornering speed** in a turn of radius $R$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sports hatchback of mass **1400 kg** is fitted with an aerodynamic package that produces a **downforce coefficient $C_L A = 0.8$ m²** (constant with speed). The car enters a curve of radius **150 m** at a tyre friction limit $\mu = 1.4$. Air density is **1.225 kg/m³**. Determine:
   (i) The **maximum cornering speed** with the aero package engaged. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The **maximum cornering speed** without downforce. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The **percentage gain** in cornering speed, and comment on whether the drag penalty is justified. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) Effect of downforce — 7 marks:**

- **Cornering (2 marks):** Downforce adds to vertical load, $N = mg + F_L$. The maximum cornering speed in a turn of radius $R$ is derived from $m V^{2}/R = \mu N = \mu(mg + F_L)$, giving $V_{max} = \sqrt{\mu g R(1 + F_L/mg)}$. Higher $F_L$ → higher $V_{max}$.
- **Braking (2 marks):** Maximum braking force $F_{b,max} = \mu(mg + F_L)$. Higher $F_L$ → shorter stopping distance, critical at high speed.
- **Acceleration and Top speed (2 marks):** Downforce also improves traction under acceleration, but **reduces top speed** because the same aero device usually adds drag, with $P = DV$. The trade-off is governed by the **aero efficiency** $\eta = \vert C_L \vert / C_d$.
- **Stability (1 mark):** Downforce, especially *balanced* front–rear, improves high-speed directional stability and reduces lift-induced skittishness.

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks:**

The cornering equation with downforce depends on $V$ (because $F_L \propto V^2$), making the equation **self-consistent**:

$$
\frac{m V^{2}}{R} = \mu \left(m g + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} C_L A\right)
$$

Rearranging:

$$
V^{2} \left(\frac{m}{R} - \tfrac{1}{2}\mu \rho C_L A\right) = \mu m g
$$

$$
V^{2} = \frac{\mu m g}{\dfrac{m}{R} - \tfrac{1}{2}\mu \rho C_L A}
$$

**Plug in numbers (with downforce):**
- Numerator: $1.4 \times 1400 \times 9.81 = 19{,}227.6$
- Denominator: $\dfrac{1400}{150} - 0.5 \times 1.4 \times 1.225 \times 0.8 = 9.333 - 0.686 = 8.647$

$$
V_{max,w}^{2} = \frac{19{,}227.6}{8.647} = 2223.4 \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}
$$

$$
V_{max,w} = 47.15 \text{ m/s} \approx 169.7 \text{ km/h} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}
$$

**Without downforce (set $C_L A = 0$):**

$$
V_{max,0} = \sqrt{\mu g R} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 9.81 \times 150} = \sqrt{2060.1} = 45.39 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
V_{max,0} \approx 163.4 \text{ km/h} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Percentage gain and comment (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta V \% = \frac{47.15 - 45.39}{45.39} \times 100\% \approx 3.88\%
$$

The cornering speed gain is **~3.9%** (≈ 6.3 km/h). Whether this justifies the drag penalty depends on the L/D ratio of the device and the track characteristics. On a low-speed, twisty circuit, the gain is valuable; on a high-speed track dominated by straight-line drag, the penalty may outweigh the benefit.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in downforce:** $C_L$ is *negative* for downforce. Many students write $F_L$ with $C_L$ positive and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to add wind velocity to vehicle speed:** When a "headwind" is specified, add it to the car's velocity — relative velocity is what matters.
> 3. **Self-consistent equation oversight:** When downforce is proportional to $V^2$ and asked to find $V_{max}$, the equation is *not* $V = \sqrt{\mu g R}$; students must solve the full quadratic in $V^2$.
> 4. **Confusing $C_L A$ with separate $C_L$ and $A$:** Examiners often combine the two into a single $C_L A$ value (units m²) — be alert.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch in part (a):** A neat labelled diagram showing stagnation, separation, wake, and vortices is worth at least 1.5 marks and is often the difference between 5/7 and 7/7.
> 6. **No unit consistency:** Mixing km/h and m/s without conversion loses a full mark in numericals.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **hatchback** is a bluff body with an abrupt vertical tailgate → high pressure drag.
- [x] **$C_d$** of a stock hatchback ≈ **0.30–0.40**; fastbacks achieve **0.20–0.28**.
- [x] **Base pressure** is the dominant source of hatchback drag; $C_{p,base} \approx -0.7$ to $-0.9$.
- [x] **Downforce** = negative lift, generated by wings, splitters, diffusers, and underbody venturi.
- [x] **Downforce → more vertical load → more tyre friction → higher cornering & braking limits.**
- [x] Key equation: $V_{max} = \sqrt{\mu g R\,(1 + F_L/mg)}$.
- [x] **Drag penalty:** $P = D \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{3} C_d A$ — power demand scales as **$V^3$**.
- [x] **Aero efficiency:** $\eta = \vert C_L \vert / C_d$ — high $\eta$ is the design goal.
- [x] **Front-bias downforce** improves braking and turn-in; **rear-bias** improves corner exit.
- [x] Reynolds number for road cars at highway speeds: $Re \approx 10^{6}$ to $10^{7}$ (turbulent flow).
- [x] Vortex shedding at the rear edges is the source of **buffeting noise** in the cabin.
- [x] Always use **SI units** (m, m/s, kg, N) — KTU exams deduct marks for mixed units.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vortices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vortices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VORTEX (Aerodynamic Definition):** A vortex is a region within a fluid where the flow is primarily rotating around an imaginary axis, either straight or curved. Mathematically, it is a region of concentrated **vorticity** ($\vec{\omega}$), which is the **curl of the velocity field**.

$$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$$

> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** In vehicle aerodynamics, vortices are critical flow structures that govern drag, lift, noise generation, and downforce. They are NOT merely "swirls" — they are mathematically defined rotational flow regions with measurable strength.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Vorticity ($\vec{\omega}$)** | A vector field defined as the curl of velocity, describing local rotation. Units: **s⁻¹ (or rad/s)**. |
| **Circulation ($\Gamma$)** | The line integral of velocity around a closed contour. Units: **m²/s**. |
| **Vortex Sheet** | A thin layer across which there is a discontinuity in tangential velocity. |
| **Vortex Tube** | A tube-like region of fluid whose surface is everywhere tangent to the vorticity vector. |
| **Irrotational Flow** | Flow where $\vec{\omega} = 0$ everywhere; has no rotation (despite possible circulation). |
| **Rotational Flow** | Flow where $\vec{\omega} \neq 0$; possesses true rotation. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Stirred Cup of Coffee**
> Imagine stirring a cup of coffee with a spoon. You see a clear "swirl" — a rotating column of liquid. This is a **forced vortex** near the spoon (fluid rotates like a rigid body). Away from the spoon, the rotation spreads outward, and the fluid spirals inward toward the center — this is a **free vortex** (or "free whirlpool").
>
> **In a moving car, the air does exactly the same thing:**
> - Air rushes past the A-pillars → forms small, fast-spinning vortices (like the spoon).
> - These vortices shed off and interact with the rear window → they may grow, merge, or decay.
> - The energy in these vortices is **stolen from the engine** (this is part of what we call **drag**).
>
> **Key Insight:** Vortices are not just visual phenomena. They represent **stored rotational kinetic energy** that the car had to "pay for" by burning fuel. This is why aerodynamicists spend enormous effort controlling and minimizing them.

### 1.4 Why Vortices Matter in Vehicle Aerodynamics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Critical Effects of Vortices on a Vehicle:**
> 1. **Induced Drag:** Trailing vortices at the rear create low-pressure regions that "pull" the car backward, increasing drag.
> 2. **Lift/Downforce Generation:** Strategic vortex generation (e.g., on a wing or diffuser) can create downforce, improving grip.
> 3. **Flow Attachment/Delay of Separation:** Small, controlled vortices (from vortex generators) re-energize the boundary layer, preventing separation and reducing pressure drag.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Mathematical Definition of Vorticity

In a three-dimensional Cartesian coordinate system $(x, y, z)$, with velocity components $(u, v, w)$:

$$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ \partial/\partial x & \partial/\partial y & \partial/\partial z \\ u & v & w \end{vmatrix}$$

The expanded form:

$$\boxed{\vec{\omega} = \left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial v}{\partial z}\right)\hat{i} + \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)\hat{j} + \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \partial u/\partial y\right)\hat{k}}$$

### 2.2 The Two Classical Vortex Models

#### A. Forced Vortex (Solid-Body Rotation)
> Fluid rotates as if it were a rigid body. Vorticity is uniform and non-zero.

$$V_\theta(r) = r\omega$$

$$\omega_z = 2\omega = \text{constant}$$

> **Vehicle Example:** The air inside a rotating wheel well, or near a spinning wheel itself (the Magnus-effect region).

#### B. Free Vortex (Irrotational Vortex)
> No rotation in the fluid elements themselves; only the bulk flow circulates. Vorticity is zero everywhere EXCEPT at the singular axis.

$$V_\theta(r) = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}$$

$$\omega_z = 0 \quad (r \neq 0)$$

> **Vehicle Example:** A tornado-like vortex shed from the tip of a side mirror, or the trailing vortex from a roof edge.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Students often confuse *circulation* with *vorticity*. A free vortex has circulation $\Gamma \neq 0$ but **vorticity is zero** (away from the core). This is a classic short-answer trap in KTU exams.

### 2.3 Circulation and Its Geometric Meaning

Circulation is the line integral of velocity around a closed curve $C$:

$$\boxed{\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l}}$$

By **Stokes' Theorem**, this is equivalent to the flux of vorticity through any surface $S$ bounded by $C$:

$$\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_S (\nabla \times \vec{V}) \cdot \hat{n} \, dS = \int_S \vec{\omega} \cdot \hat{n} \, dS$$

### 2.4 Helmholtz's Three Vortex Theorems (1885)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD THEOREMS — These appear regularly in 14-mark questions.**

**Theorem 1 — Conservation of Vortex Tube Strength:** The circulation (and hence the strength) of a vortex tube is **constant along its length**.

**Theorem 2 — Vortex Tubes Cannot End in the Fluid:** A vortex tube must either:
- Form a **closed loop** (vortex ring), OR
- Extend to **infinity** / terminate on a solid boundary.

**Theorem 3 — Persistence in Inviscid Flow:** In the absence of rotational external forces, the strength of a vortex tube remains **constant in time** (the vortex moves with the fluid).

### 2.5 Kelvin's Circulation Theorem

> For an **inviscid, barotropic flow** under **conservative body forces**, the circulation around a closed material curve moving with the fluid is constant:

$$\frac{D\Gamma}{Dt} = 0$$

> **Real-world note:** Viscous effects in real vehicle flows break Kelvin's theorem — this is precisely why vortices eventually decay (viscous diffusion).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Typical Application |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$ | Vorticity definition | Any rotational flow analysis |
| $\omega_z = \partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y$ | 2D vorticity component | Cross-flow analysis over a car |
| $\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l}$ | Circulation | Vortex strength measurement |
| $\Gamma = \int_S \vec{\omega} \cdot \hat{n} \, dS$ | Stokes' theorem form | Vortex tube analysis |
| $V_\theta = \Gamma / (2\pi r)$ | Free vortex velocity | Trailing vortices at vehicle edges |
| $V_\theta = r\omega$ | Forced vortex velocity | Wheel-rotation region |
| $p + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = \text{const}$ | Bernoulli in free vortex | Pressure distribution in vortex core |
| $p_c = p_\infty - \rho \Gamma^2 / (8\pi^2 r_c^2)$ | Core pressure of free vortex | Explains low-pressure cores |
| $C_{D,\text{induced}} = L^2 / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 \pi b^2)$ | Induced drag coefficient | Wings, vortex drag on vehicles |
| $T = \rho b^2 / (4\pi)$ | Brunt-Väisälä time scale | Vortex-shedding period (qualitative) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Convention:** Use SI throughout. Vorticity in **s⁻¹**, circulation in **m²/s**, circulation per unit length in **m/s** (used for 2D vortex sheets).

### 2.7 Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Role of Vortices |
|----------------------|------------------|
| **Passenger Cars (Drag Reduction)** | Suppress A-pillar, C-pillar, and base vortices to cut $C_D$ by 0.05–0.15. |
| **Formula 1 / Motorsports** | Use tip vortices, Y250 vortex, and diffuser vortices to generate massive downforce. |
| **Trucks / Heavy Vehicles** | Cab-roof vortices, trailer gap vortices, and base vortices dominate fuel economy loss. |
| **EVs (Range Optimization)** | Every 0.01 reduction in $C_D$ ≈ +2.5 km range — vortex control is critical. |
| **Wind Noise / NVH** | Vortex shedding at A-pillars, mirrors, and wipers is the dominant interior noise source above 80 km/h. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Vorticity from Velocity Field

**Problem:** Given a 2D velocity field:
$$u(x,y) = a y, \quad v(x,y) = -a x, \quad w = 0$$
where $a$ is a constant, determine the vorticity and classify the flow.

**Step 1 — Identify the velocity components.**
The flow is purely 2D in the $x$–$y$ plane, so only the $z$-component of vorticity will be non-zero.

**Step 2 — Apply the vorticity definition.**
For 2D flow:
$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the partial derivatives.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} &= \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(-a x) = -a \\
\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} &= \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(a y) = a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the vorticity equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_z &= (-a) - (a) = -2a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since $\omega_z = -2a = \text{constant} \neq 0$, the flow is **rotational**. The constant vorticity and the linear velocity profile $V_\theta = r \cdot a$ (using $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$) confirm this is a **forced vortex** (solid-body rotation) with angular velocity $\omega = -a$ (negative sign indicates clockwise rotation).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Circulation Around a Free Vortex

**Problem:** For a 2D free vortex with tangential velocity $V_\theta = \Gamma / (2\pi r)$, compute the circulation around a circular contour $C$ of radius $R$ centered on the vortex axis.

**Step 1 — Set up the line integral in polar coordinates.**

On a circle of radius $R$, the tangential unit vector is $\hat{e}_\theta$, and the arc length element is $dl = R \, d\theta$. Thus:

$$d\vec{l} = R \, d\theta \, \hat{e}_\theta$$

**Step 2 — Express velocity in polar form.**

The velocity is purely tangential: $\vec{V} = V_\theta \, \hat{e}_\theta = \dfrac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}\bigg\vert_{r=R} \hat{e}_\theta = \dfrac{\Gamma}{2\pi R}\hat{e}_\theta$.

**Step 3 — Evaluate the dot product.**

$$\vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l} = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi R}\hat{e}_\theta \cdot R\,d\theta\,\hat{e}_\theta = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\,d\theta$$

**Step 4 — Integrate around the closed contour.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Gamma_C &= \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\,d\theta \\
&= \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi} \cdot 2\pi = \Gamma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

> **The circulation around any closed contour encircling the vortex axis is constant and equal to the vortex strength $\Gamma$** — this is the direct mathematical expression of Helmholtz's First Theorem.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Pressure Distribution in a Free Vortex Core

**Problem:** Derive the pressure variation with radius in the core of a free vortex.

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation** (valid because the free vortex is irrotational and steady).

For points on a streamline at the same height:
$$p + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\theta^2(r) = p_\infty + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\theta^2(\infty)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the free boundary condition at infinity.**

For a free vortex, $V_\theta \to 0$ as $r \to \infty$, so:
$$p_\infty + 0 = p_\infty$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $V_\theta = \Gamma / (2\pi r)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(r) + \frac{1}{2}\rho \left(\frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}\right)^2 &= p_\infty \\
p(r) &= p_\infty - \frac{\rho \Gamma^2}{8\pi^2 r^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret physically.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The pressure **drops** as $r$ decreases. At the vortex core ($r \to 0$), pressure becomes very low — sometimes below atmospheric. This explains the **suction effect** that trailing vortices exert on the rear of a vehicle, contributing to **base drag** and **lift** (or reduced downforce).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Verification of Irrotationality of a Free Vortex

**Problem:** Show that the free vortex $V_\theta = \Gamma / (2\pi r)$ is irrotational (except at the axis).

**Step 1 — Convert to Cartesian components.**

In polar: $V_r = 0$ and $V_\theta = \Gamma / (2\pi r)$.

Transformation: $u = V_r \cos\theta - V_\theta \sin\theta$, $v = V_r \sin\theta + V_\theta \cos\theta$.

Since $V_r = 0$ and $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$, $\theta = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$:

$$u = -\frac{\Gamma y}{2\pi(x^2 + y^2)}, \quad v = \frac{\Gamma x}{2\pi(x^2 + y^2)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivatives.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} &= \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{(x^2 + y^2) - x(2x)}{(x^2+y^2)^2} = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{y^2 - x^2}{(x^2+y^2)^2} \\
\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} &= -\frac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{(x^2+y^2) - y(2y)}{(x^2+y^2)^2} = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{y^2 - x^2}{(x^2+y^2)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract to get $\omega_z$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_z &= \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \\
&= \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{y^2 - x^2}{(x^2+y^2)^2} - \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{y^2 - x^2}{(x^2+y^2)^2} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

> The free vortex is irrotational everywhere except at the singular axis ($r = 0$), where the velocity is undefined and vorticity is concentrated as a Dirac delta function. This is a **classic KTU conceptual question**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Vortices in Vehicle Aerodynamics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Aerodynamic Vortices] --> B[Trailing Vortices]
    A --> C[Separation-Induced Vortices]
    A --> D[Shear-Layer Vortices]
    A --> E[Rotational Body Vortices]

    B --> B1[Roof trailing edge vortex]
    B --> B2[Base wake vortex pair]
    B --> B3[Trunk edge vortex]

    C --> C1[A-pillar vortex]
    C --> C2[C-pillar vortex]
    C --> C3[Wheelhouse vortex]
    C --> C4[Underbody vortex]

    D --> D1[Side mirror tip vortex]
    D --> D2[Windscreen header vortex]
    D --> D3[Hood leading edge vortex]

    E --> E1[Brake disc rotor vortex]
    E --> E2[Wheel spin Magnus vortex]
    E --> E3[Engine cooling fan vortex]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Vortex Generation and Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incoming Flow] --> B[Vehicle Body Geometry]
    B --> C{Adverse Pressure Gradient?}

    C -->|No| D[Attached Flow - No Vortex]
    C -->|Yes| E[Boundary Layer Separation]

    E --> F[Shear Layer Roll-up]
    F --> G[Discrete Vortex Formation]

    G --> H[Vortex Convection Downstream]
    H --> I{Vortex Control Strategy?}

    I --> J[Fillets and Radius Edges]
    I --> K[Vortex Generators VG]
    I --> L[Base Cavity / Boat-tail]
    I --> M[Diffuser Geometry]

    J --> N[Reduced Vortex Strength]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N

    N --> O[Lower Drag / Higher Downforce]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Vortex Formation Zones Around a Passenger Car (Side View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Streamlines Approach Car] --> F1[Front Bumper Stagnation]
    F1 --> F2[Hood Boundary Layer]
    F2 --> F3[Windscreen Header Vortex Zone]
    F3 --> F4[Roof Vortex Sheet]
    F4 --> F5[Roof Trailing Edge Separation]
    F5 --> F6[Base / Wake Recirculation Zone]
    F6 --> F7[Trailing Vortex Pair Shedding]

    S2[Underbody Flow] --> U1[Front Splitter Stagnation]
    U1 --> U2[Underbody Acceleration]
    U2 --> U3[Rear Diffuser Pressure Recovery]
    U3 --> U4[Underbody Wake Mixing]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid: Curl and Circulation Physical Meaning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Velocity Field V x y z] --> B[Compute Partial Derivatives]
    B --> C[Form Skew-Symmetric Tensor]
    C --> D[Vorticity Vector omega]
    D --> E[Integrate over Surface S]
    E --> F[Circulation Gamma]

    D --> G[Visualize: Tiny Paddlewheel Rotation Rate]
    F --> H[Visualize: Net Flow Around Closed Loop]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** The diagrams above are functional block representations. They map the *cause–effect* chain — geometry → pressure gradient → shear-layer roll-up → vortex → control → performance. In KTU theory exams, drawing such a chain (even crudely) scores high marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define vorticity. How is it related to the velocity field?**

**Model Answer:**

> Vorticity is defined as the curl of the velocity vector field:
> $$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$$
>
> In Cartesian form:
> $$\omega_x = \partial w/\partial y - \partial v/\partial z, \quad \omega_y = \partial u/\partial z - \partial w/\partial x, \quad \omega_z = \partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y$$
>
> It has units of **s⁻¹** and physically represents **twice the local angular velocity** of a fluid element. A non-zero vorticity indicates **rotational flow**.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for mathematical form and physical meaning.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish between a free vortex and a forced vortex with examples from vehicle aerodynamics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Free Vortex | Forced Vortex |
|----------|-------------|---------------|
| Velocity | $V_\theta = \Gamma/(2\pi r)$ | $V_\theta = r\omega$ |
| Vorticity | Zero (except core) | Constant and non-zero |
| Pressure | Decreases toward center | Increases toward center |
| Energy | Decays outward | Solid-body rotation |
| **Vehicle example** | Tip vortex at side mirror, trailing vortex at roof edge | Air inside wheel well, brake disc rotating region |

*(3 marks: 1 for each distinction, 1 for vehicle examples.)*

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** State and explain **Helmholtz's three vortex theorems** with neat sketches. How are these theorems relevant to the trailing vortices formed behind a passenger car? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2D free vortex is observed at the roof trailing edge of a moving car. The tangential velocity at a distance of $r = 0.05\,\text{m}$ from the vortex center is measured to be $V_\theta = 30\,\text{m/s}$.
   Compute:
   - (i) The circulation $\Gamma$ of the vortex.
   - (ii) The tangential velocity at $r = 0.15\,\text{m}$.
   - (iii) The pressure drop from $r = 0.15\,\text{m}$ to $r = 0.05\,\text{m}$ (take $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$). **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Helmholtz's Three Vortex Theorems**
>
> **Theorem 1:** *The strength of a vortex tube is constant along its length.* Circulation computed across any cross-section of a vortex tube is identical.
> $$\Gamma = \int_{S_1} \vec{\omega} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_{S_2} \vec{\omega} \cdot d\vec{S}$$
>
> **Theorem 2:** *A vortex tube cannot end in the fluid.* It must either close on itself (forming a vortex ring) or terminate on a solid boundary. In vehicle flows, vortex tubes typically terminate on the body surface (e.g., roof edge, A-pillar).
>
> **Theorem 3:** *In inviscid flow under conservative body forces, a vortex tube moves with the fluid.* Its strength is preserved in time. Real vehicle flows are viscous → vortices diffuse and decay.
>
> **Relevance to vehicle:** When flow separates at the roof trailing edge, the shear layer rolls up into a concentrated vortex tube. By Theorem 2, this tube extends downstream into the wake. By Theorem 1, the strength shed from the roof equals the strength in the wake — useful for momentum analysis of drag components.

*(7 marks: 2 per theorem + 1 sketch + 1 for vehicle relevance.)*

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** $V_\theta = 30\,\text{m/s}$ at $r_1 = 0.05\,\text{m}$, $\rho = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$.

**(i) Circulation $\Gamma$:**

For a free vortex: $V_\theta = \Gamma / (2\pi r)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Gamma &= 2\pi r_1 V_\theta = 2\pi (0.05)(30) \\
&= 3\pi \approx 9.4248\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Tangential velocity at $r_2 = 0.15\,\text{m}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\theta,2} &= \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r_2} = \frac{9.4248}{2\pi(0.15)} \\
&= \frac{9.4248}{0.9425} \approx 10.0\,\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Pressure drop between $r_2$ and $r_1$:**

Using the free-vortex pressure relation $p(r) = p_\infty - \rho\Gamma^2/(8\pi^2 r^2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta p = p(r_1) - p(r_2) &= \frac{\rho\Gamma^2}{8\pi^2}\left(\frac{1}{r_2^2} - \frac{1}{r_1^2}\right) \\
&= \frac{(1.225)(9.4248)^2}{8\pi^2}\left(\frac{1}{0.15^2} - \frac{1}{0.05^2}\right) \\
&= \frac{(1.225)(88.82)}{78.957}\left(44.44 - 400\right) \\
&= 1.378 \times (-355.56) \\
&\approx -490\,\text{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** Pressure at the inner radius ($r_1 = 0.05\,\text{m}$) is **lower** than at $r_2 = 0.15\,\text{m}$ by approximately **490 Pa**. This low-pressure core is what creates a localized suction on the vehicle's rear deck.

*(7 marks: 2 marks each for (i) and (ii), 3 marks for (iii) with full substitution.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the **vorticity transport equation** for an incompressible viscous fluid. Briefly explain each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger car moving at $V = 25\,\text{m/s}$ has a pair of trailing vortices at the rear, each of strength $\Gamma = 4\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, separated by a distance $b = 1.6\,\text{m}$ (the vehicle width). Using the simplified induced-drag analogy, estimate the **induced drag force** per unit span on the rear of the car. Take $\rho = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Derivation of the Vorticity Transport Equation (incompressible, Newtonian):**

Start with the **Navier–Stokes momentum equation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} &= -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Take the curl of both sides.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho \nabla \times \left(\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}\right) &= -\nabla \times (\nabla p) + \mu \nabla \times (\nabla^2 \vec{V}) + \nabla \times (\rho\vec{g})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply vector identities.**

- $\nabla \times (\nabla p) = 0$ (gradient has no curl).
- $\nabla \times (\nabla^2 \vec{V}) = \nabla^2 \vec{\omega}$ (curl of Laplacian = Laplacian of curl).
- For incompressible flow, the convective derivative simplifies: $\nabla \times (D\vec{V}/Dt) = D\vec{\omega}/Dt - (\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{\omega}$ (using the vector identity for curl of material derivative in incompressible flow, the vortex-stretching term emerges).

Assuming gravity is irrotational: $\nabla \times (\rho \vec{g}) = 0$.

**Step 3 — Divide by $\rho$ and use kinematic viscosity $\nu = \mu/\rho$.**

$$
\boxed{\frac{D\vec{\omega}}{Dt} = (\vec{\omega} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} + \nu \nabla^2 \vec{\omega}}$$

**Physical meaning of each term:**

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| $D\vec{\omega}/Dt$ | Rate of change of vorticity following a fluid element. |
| $(\vec{\omega}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$ | **Vortex stretching/tilting term** — intensifies vorticity when vortex lines are stretched (3D effect). |
| $\nu\nabla^2 \vec{\omega}$ | **Viscous diffusion term** — causes vorticity to spread and decay. In real vehicle flows, this is why vortices eventually dissipate. |

*(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for identity application, 2 for term-by-term explanation.)*

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** $V = 25\,\text{m/s}$, $\Gamma = 4\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $b = 1.6\,\text{m}$, $\rho = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$.

**Step 1 — Compute the downwash velocity $w$ induced by the vortex pair.**

For a finite-span vortex pair of strength $\pm \Gamma$ separated by distance $b$, the induced downwash at the centre is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= \frac{\Gamma}{\pi b} = \frac{4}{\pi(1.6)} = \frac{4}{5.027} \approx 0.7958\,\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Define equivalent lift (per unit span).**

Using the Kutta–Joukowski theorem per unit span:
$$
L' = \rho V \Gamma = (1.225)(25)(4) = 122.5\,\text{N/m}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the induced-drag formula (per unit span).**

For a trailing-vortex pair (analogous to finite wing theory):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D'_i &= L' \cdot \frac{w}{V} = \frac{L'^2}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \pi b^2} \\
&= \frac{(122.5)^2}{(0.5)(1.225)(25)^2 \pi (1.6)^2} \\
&= \frac{15006.25}{(0.5)(1.225)(625)\pi(2.56)} \\
&= \frac{15006.25}{3078.76} \\
&\approx 4.87\,\text{N/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** The induced drag force at the rear of the car is approximately **4.87 N per meter of span**. For a car of width 1.6 m, this represents a small but non-negligible contribution to total drag, primarily visible at high yaw angles or in crosswind.

*(7 marks: 2 for downwash, 2 for lift per span, 3 for induced drag calculation.)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\omega$ (vorticity) with $\Omega$ (angular velocity):** Vorticity is TWICE the angular velocity. Writing $\vec{V} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}$ in a 2D flow without the factor of 2 will lose 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in circulation problems:** Always state $\Gamma$ in **m²/s** explicitly. Omitting units is a 0.5 mark deduction per KTU norms.
>
> 3. **Applying Bernoulli blindly in vortical flow:** Bernoulli's equation is valid *along a streamline* OR *throughout an irrotational region*. Do NOT apply it across a free-vortex core unless you specifically invoke the irrotational property.
>
> 4. **Sign errors in Stokes' theorem:** The orientation of the surface normal $\hat{n}$ and the direction of traversal of $C$ must follow the right-hand rule. Reversing one sign flips the sign of $\Gamma$.
>
> 5. **Skipping the "irrotational except at the axis" statement for free vortices:** Examiners specifically test this distinction. A free vortex has zero vorticity *only* in the region $r > 0$.

---

### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Points to Memorize Before the KTU Exam:**

- **Vorticity** is the curl of velocity: $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$. Units: **s⁻¹**.
- **Circulation** is the line integral of velocity around a closed loop: $\Gamma = \oint \vec{V}\cdot d\vec{l}$. Units: **m²/s**.
- **Stokes' Theorem** links the two: $\Gamma = \int_S \vec{\omega}\cdot\hat{n}\,dS$.
- A **free vortex** has $V_\theta = \Gamma/(2\pi r)$ and is **irrotational** (except at the core).
- A **forced vortex** has $V_\theta = r\omega$ and is **rotational** everywhere.
- **Helmholtz's 3 Theorems:** (1) constant strength along tube, (2) tubes can't end in fluid, (3) tubes persist in inviscid flow.
- **Kelvin's Theorem:** In inviscid, barotropic flow under conservative forces, $D\Gamma/Dt = 0$.
- **Vorticity Transport Equation:** $\dfrac{D\vec{\omega}}{Dt} = (\vec{\omega}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V} + \nu\nabla^2\vec{\omega}$.
- **Low-pressure vortex core** creates suction: $p(r) = p_\infty - \rho\Gamma^2/(8\pi^2 r^2)$.
- **Vehicle examples to mention:** A-pillar, C-pillar, roof trailing edge, wheelhouse, base wake, side-mirror tip vortices.
- **Engineering controls:** fillets, vortex generators (VG), boat-tails, diffusers, base cavities, underbody fairings.
- **Induced drag analogy:** $D'_i = L'^2/(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \pi b^2)$ — applicable to vehicle trailing vortex pairs.
- **Always state assumptions:** incompressible, steady, inviscid/viscous as relevant.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aerodynamic Testing - Wind Tunnels for Automotive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Aerodynamic Testing - Wind Tunnels for Automotive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **automotive wind tunnel** is a purpose-built experimental facility that generates a controlled, uniform, and quasi-steady airflow over a full-scale or scale model of a road vehicle in order to measure the aerodynamic forces (drag, lift, side force) and moments (pitch, yaw, roll) acting on it, along with the surrounding flow field. The tunnel provides a controlled laboratory substitute for the real on-road moving vehicle, where the **air is moved past the stationary car** instead of moving the car through still air, exploiting the principle of **relative motion in fluid mechanics**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> A wind tunnel is a duct equipped with a fan / propulsion system to drive air through a test section, fitted with contraction, diffuser, corner vanes, honeycomb, and screens to condition the flow, where a vehicle (or its model) is instrumented to obtain aerodynamic coefficients ($C_D$, $C_L$, $C_{YM}$, etc.) under controlled Reynolds number and turbulence intensity conditions.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you stand on a footbridge over a flowing river and want to measure the resistance a stone experiences from the current. You cannot easily throw the stone into the river and chase it with instruments. Instead, you bring the **river to the stone** — you create a controlled water channel, push water past the stone at a known speed, and measure the drag. A wind tunnel does **exactly this for cars and air**: instead of driving the car at 120 km/h through still air (which is what the road does), we keep the car still and move the air at 120 km/h past it. The relative motion — and therefore the aerodynamic forces — is **identical** in both cases, as long as the flow conditions (Reynolds number, turbulence, etc.) are matched.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> The principle of **dynamic similarity** underpins every wind tunnel test. You are NOT replicating the road, you are replicating the **flow physics**. Hence, scaling laws, blockage corrections, and Reynolds number matching are the most frequently asked concepts in KTU ESE papers.

### 1.3 Why Wind Tunnels are Essential for Automotive Aerodynamics

| Real-World Need | Wind Tunnel Solution |
|---|---|
| Cannot measure pressure / velocity on a moving car easily | Sensors are fixed in a stationary car inside the tunnel |
| Road tests are affected by wind gusts, temperature, traffic | Tunnel provides steady, repeatable, calibrated flow |
| CFD requires validation | Tunnel data is the **gold-standard experimental reference** |
| Cost of full prototype iteration is very high | Scale models (1:4 or 1:5) can be tested first |

### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics & Constants

The following are the standard KTU board-exam constants that must be remembered:

- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $p_{atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa}$
- **Density of air at sea level (15 °C):** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Dynamic viscosity of air at 15 °C:** $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/(m·s)}$
- **Kinematic viscosity of air:** $\nu = 1.46 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Speed of sound at 15 °C:** $a = 340.3 \text{ m/s}$

> [!NOTE]
> The most common automotive wind-tunnel test speed is around **120–160 km/h** (≈ 33–44 m/s), corresponding to a **Mach number $M \le 0.13$**, firmly in the **incompressible subsonic** regime. Therefore compressibility corrections are **not** required for car testing — a frequently asked KTU 2-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Types of Automotive Wind Tunnels

Wind tunnels are classified along two orthogonal axes: (i) **circuit geometry** and (ii) **flow regime**.

#### 2.1.1 Classification by Circuit Geometry

**A. Open-Circuit (Open-Return / Eiffel Type) Tunnel**

The air is drawn in from the room (or outside), accelerated through the contraction, passes the test section, and is exhausted back into the room (or atmosphere). The flow loop is **not closed**.

- *Advantages:* Low construction cost, simple layout, easy access.
- *Disadvantages:* Flow quality is sensitive to room layout and temperature; turbulence intensity is higher.
- *Use:* Universities, small-scale research, low-budget facilities.

**B. Closed-Circuit (Closed-Return / Göttingen Type) Tunnel**

The air is recirculated through a closed loop, typically passing through a fan, diffuser, corner vanes (turning sections), contraction, test section, and back to the fan.

- *Advantages:* Excellent flow quality (low turbulence intensity $\le 0.1\%$), tightly controlled temperature, low background noise.
- *Disadvantages:* High construction cost (3–10× open type); requires large floor area.
- *Use:* OEM automotive test facilities (Mercedes-Benz, Toyota, Pininfarina, Tata, Mahindra, ARAI Pune).

**C. Closed-Circuit with Two Test Sections (Return-Type with 2 sections)**

Has both a large full-scale automotive test section and a smaller subsonic/aerospace test section in the same loop — used by advanced research centres.

#### 2.1.2 Classification by Flow Regime

| Type | Mach Range | Application in Automotive |
|---|---|---|
| Subsonic | $M < 0.3$ | **Standard for all car testing** |
| Transonic | $0.8 \le M \le 1.2$ | Race cars (F1) — not common |
| Supersonic | $M > 1.2$ | Not relevant for road vehicles |

### 2.2 Major Sub-Systems of an Automotive Wind Tunnel

1. **Honeycomb / Flow Straightener** — Breaks down large-scale swirl and lateral turbulence.
2. **Screens / Mesh** — Reduces turbulence intensity; typical 2–4 screen layers.
3. **Contraction (Nozzle)** — Accelerates flow and reduces turbulence. The contraction ratio (inlet area / test area) is typically **9:1 to 12:1** for high-quality automotive tunnels.
4. **Test Section** — Where the car is placed. For a full-size car, the cross-section is roughly **$10 \text{ m (W)} \times 5 \text{ m (H)}$** or larger. The test section walls can be **open (free-jet)** or **closed (solid-wall)**.
5. **Diffuser** — Decelerates flow after the test section to recover pressure, reducing fan power.
6. **Corner Vanes / Turning Vanes** — Guide flow around the corners of a closed-circuit tunnel with minimum loss.
7. **Fan / Drive System** — DC motor or AC motor with variable frequency drive (VFD), often 1–3 MW for full-scale automotive tunnels.
8. **Boundary-Layer Suction on Walls** — Removes the wall boundary layer in the test section to prevent it from influencing the model.
9. **Moving-Belt System (Rolling Road)** — The wheels of the car (or its model) rest on a moving belt so that the relative motion of the tyre surface matches the road. **Critical for accurate $C_D$ measurement.**
10. **Force Balance** — A 6-component strain-gauge balance (sting or platform type) under the car to measure $F_x, F_y, F_z, M_x, M_y, M_z$.

### 2.3 Free-Jet vs Closed-Wall Test Sections

**Free-Jet Test Section (Open Walls)**
- Surrounding air is at atmospheric pressure and stagnant.
- Ideal for **full-size car testing** because the flow can wrap around the vehicle realistically.
- Used by nearly all major automotive OEM tunnels.

**Closed-Wall Test Section**
- Walls are solid; flow is enclosed.
- Suited to **scale-model aerospace tests**.
- Suffers from **wall interference / blockage effects** that must be corrected.

### 2.4 Critical Similarity Parameters (Dynamic Similarity)

For the wind tunnel test to faithfully represent real driving, the following non-dimensional numbers must match between model and prototype:

| Parameter | Formula | Required Match? |
|---|---|---|
| Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho U L}{\mu}$ | Yes (or at least in the same regime, $\ge 10^6$) |
| Mach number | $M = \dfrac{U}{a}$ | Auto satisfied (both $M \ll 0.3$) |
| Froude number | $Fr = \dfrac{U}{\sqrt{g L}}$ | Required only for water/free-surface flows — **not relevant for air** |
| Geometric similarity | Exact scaled shape | Mandatory |

For a typical car, **$L = 4 \text{ m}$**, **$U = 30 \text{ m/s}$**, giving $Re \approx 8 \times 10^6$. This is the **"automotive Reynolds number regime"** — KTU examiners expect you to recognize this number.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-saver cheat sheet** for Module 4. Memorize the right-most column by heart.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dynamic pressure | $q_\infty = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho U_\infty^2$ | Pa |
| 2 | Drag force | $F_D = C_D \cdot q_\infty \cdot A$ | N |
| 3 | Lift force | $F_L = C_L \cdot q_\infty \cdot A$ | N |
| 4 | Side force | $F_S = C_S \cdot q_\infty \cdot A$ | N |
| 5 | Pitching moment | $M = C_M \cdot q_\infty \cdot A \cdot L$ | N·m |
| 6 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho U L}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Mach number | $M = \dfrac{U}{a}$ | $a \approx 340$ m/s |
| 8 | Blockage ratio (solid wall) | $B = \dfrac{A_m}{A_t}$ | Typically $\le 5\%$ |
| 9 | Blockage correction (drag) | $C_{D,corr} = C_{D,meas} (1 - \varepsilon)$ | $\varepsilon \approx k \cdot B$ |
| 10 | Power required by fan | $P = \Delta p \cdot Q$ | $\Delta p$ in Pa, $Q$ in m³/s |
| 11 | Contraction loss coefficient | $C_c = 1 - (A_t/A_0)$ | Use Bernoulli to derive |
| 12 | Turbulence intensity | $Tu = \dfrac{u'_{rms}}{U_\infty}$ | $\le 0.1\%$ in good tunnels |
| 13 | Reynolds number scaling | $\dfrac{U_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \dfrac{U_p L_p}{\nu_p}$ | For model (m) & prototype (p) |

**Note on the table:** To avoid breaking markdown syntax, the absolute-value bars inside equations are written using the LaTeX `\vert` command. For example, $\vert x \vert$ is typed as `$\vert x \vert$`.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **$C_D$ validation:** A new vehicle's $C_D$ is measured in a wind tunnel; the measurement is accepted as a legal **type-approval** figure in many markets.
- **Yaw-angle studies:** Cars are rotated by $\pm 6°$ to $\pm 12°$ to study **crosswind stability**.
- **Cooling airflow:** Heat exchangers (radiator, AC condenser) are tested with the actual engine fan running.
- **Soiling / water management:** Spray patterns from tyres are studied with a water-injection system.
- **Wind noise / aero-acoustics:** Microphones in the cabin record noise from A-pillar, side mirror, and wheel-arch flow.
- **CFD validation:** The tunnel provides the **benchmark dataset** that validates every CFD solver.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reynolds Number Scaling Between Model and Prototype

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark question** (frequently asked under "similarity laws" in Module 4).

**Problem Statement:** A full-size car (prototype) of characteristic length $L_p$ and freestream velocity $U_p$ is to be tested in a wind tunnel using a 1:4 scale model. The tunnel uses pressurized air at twice atmospheric pressure. The temperature in both cases is the same. Find the required test-section velocity $U_m$.

**Step 1 — Statement of Dynamic Similarity**

For the test to be aerodynamically similar to road conditions, the Reynolds numbers must be equal:

$$
Re_m = Re_p
$$

**Step 2 — Expand Both Sides**

$$
\frac{\rho_m \, U_m \, L_m}{\mu_m} = \frac{\rho_p \, U_p \, L_p}{\mu_p}
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce Scale Ratios**

Let the geometric scale be $\lambda = L_m / L_p = 1/4$. The same air is used, so viscosity scales as $\mu_m = \mu_p$ (viscosity is only weakly dependent on pressure, by Sutherland's law, and at low Mach numbers we take it as constant).

Density scales as $\rho_m = k_\rho \cdot \rho_p$, where $k_\rho = 2$ (since tunnel air is at twice atmospheric pressure).

**Step 4 — Solve for $U_m$**

$$
U_m = U_p \cdot \frac{L_p}{L_m} \cdot \frac{\mu_m}{\mu_p} \cdot \frac{\rho_p}{\rho_m}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
U_m = U_p \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot 1 \cdot \frac{1}{k_\rho}
$$

$$
U_m = U_p \cdot \frac{1}{(1/4)} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = U_p \cdot 4 \cdot \frac{1}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{U_m = 2 \, U_p}
$$

**Interpretation:** The model must be tested at **twice the prototype's road speed**. For $U_p = 30$ m/s, we need $U_m = 60$ m/s. This is why some automotive tunnels are designed to run up to 60–70 m/s.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Logic:** Many students forget the inverse relationship $L_m/L_p$ and get the scaling backwards. KTU examiners frequently test this trap.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Solid-Wall Blockage Correction for $C_D$

In a closed-wall test section, the car occupies a finite fraction of the cross-section. The walls "choke" the flow, increasing the effective velocity around the car and producing a **higher apparent $C_D$** than free-jet conditions.

**Step 1 — Define the Blockage Ratio**

$$
B = \frac{A_m}{A_t}
$$

where $A_m$ is the model's frontal area and $A_t$ is the test-section area.

**Step 2 — Apply the Linearised Glauert–Allen Correction**

The corrected drag coefficient is:

$$
C_{D,corr} = C_{D,meas} \, (1 - k_s B)
$$

where $k_s$ is a blockage constant, typically:

- $k_s = 1.5$ for **open-jet (free-jet)** test sections.
- $k_s = 2.0$ to $2.5$ for **closed-wall** test sections.

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example**

Suppose $C_{D,meas} = 0.32$, $A_m = 2.0 \text{ m}^2$, $A_t = 20 \text{ m}^2$, closed-wall tunnel with $k_s = 2.0$.

$$
B = \frac{2.0}{20} = 0.10
$$

$$
C_{D,corr} = 0.32 \, (1 - 2.0 \times 0.10) = 0.32 \times 0.80
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{D,corr} = 0.256}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The blockage correction is **negative** — the measured $C_D$ in a closed-wall tunnel is **over-predicted**. The corrected value is smaller. A KTU favourite: "Why are free-jet test sections preferred for full-scale automotive tests?" Answer: blockage and wall-interference are essentially absent.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Power Required by the Wind Tunnel Fan

The fan must overcome (a) the **dynamic pressure lost in the contraction** and (b) the **diffuser pressure recovery** and (c) bearing losses. A simplified energy balance gives:

**Step 1 — Mass Flow Rate**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \, U_t \, A_t
$$

where $U_t$ is the test-section velocity and $A_t$ the test-section area.

**Step 2 — Volumetric Flow Rate**

$$
Q = U_t \, A_t
$$

**Step 3 — Pressure Rise Required Across the Fan**

By Bernoulli + losses:

$$
\Delta p_f = \frac{1}{2} \rho U_t^2 \, \left( \frac{A_0}{A_t} - 1 \right) + \Delta p_{loss}
$$

For a contraction ratio $CR = A_0/A_t = 9$ and ideal flow:

$$
\Delta p_f \approx \frac{1}{2} \rho U_t^2 \times 8
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Fan Power**

$$
P_{fan} = \Delta p_f \times Q
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example**

$U_t = 40$ m/s, $A_t = 50$ m², $CR = 9$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

$$
Q = 40 \times 50 = 2000 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\Delta p_f = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1600 \times 8 = 7840 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
P_{fan} = 7840 \times 2000 = 1.568 \times 10^7 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{fan} \approx 15.7 \text{ MW}}
$$

This is why real automotive OEM tunnels run between 1 MW and 5 MW and rarely exceed 60 m/s — fan power scales as $U^3$!

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Open vs Closed Circuit

| Feature | Open Circuit (Eiffel) | Closed Circuit (Göttingen) |
|---|---|---|
| Cost | Low | High (3–10×) |
| Turbulence intensity | 0.5–1.0 % | $\le 0.1$ % |
| Temperature control | Poor | Excellent (chilled heat exchangers) |
| Blockage effect | None (free-jet) | Significant; must correct |
| Floor area | Long, narrow | Compact (often vertical return) |
| Fan power | Higher (single pass) | Lower (recirculated) |
| Preferred for | University research | OEM full-scale testing |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Scale Model vs Full-Size Test

| Aspect | Scale Model (1:4 or 1:5) | Full-Size Car |
|---|---|---|
| Reynolds number match | Requires pressurised air or high speed | Naturally matched |
| Geometric detail fidelity | Poor (engine bay, wheel wells lost) | Perfect |
| Cost per test | Low | Very high |
| Use | Concept / styling stage | Final $C_D$ sign-off |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners **frequently** test: *"Why can't a 1:4 scale model fully replace a full-size test, even with $Re$ matched?"* Answer: **Geometric similarity of all small features (seals, gaps, OGs, wheel arch liners) cannot be maintained at small scale**, so the **detached/separated flow physics** shifts. The student must mention **boundary-layer transition and surface roughness** as key limitations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Circuit (Göttingen) Automotive Wind Tunnel

The figure below shows the high-level functional architecture of a closed-circuit automotive wind tunnel and the data-acquisition path from the model to the analyst's display.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Fan And Motor Drive] --> A2[Diffuser Section 1]
    A2 --> A3[Corner Vanes Turning Section 1]
    A3 --> A4[Heat Exchanger Temperature Control]
    A4 --> A5[Honeycomb Flow Straightener]
    A5 --> A6[Anti Turbulence Screens 2 To 4 Layers]
    A6 --> A7[Contraction Nozzle Ratio 9 To 12]
    A7 --> A8[Test Section Car And Balance]
    A8 --> A9[Boundary Layer Suction Slot On Floor]
    A8 --> A10[Diffuser Section 2]
    A10 --> A11[Corner Vanes Turning Section 2]
    A11 --> A12[Return Duct Back To Fan]
    A12 --> A1
    A8 --> B1[Six Component Force Balance]
    A8 --> B2[Surface Pressure Taps And Scanivalve]
    A8 --> B3[Laser Doppler Anemometer LDA Probe]
    A8 --> B4[Particle Image Velocimetry PIV Camera]
    A8 --> B5[Smoke Probe For Flow Visualisation]
    B1 --> C1[Data Acquisition System DAQ]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Wind Tunnel Control Console And Operator HMI]
    C2 --> C3[Coefficient Reduction And Reporting Module]
    C3 --> C4[Cd Cl Cy Cm Plots And Yaw Sweep Report]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Test Execution Procedure

This block diagram captures the exact sequence an engineer follows inside a wind tunnel test campaign.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Define Test Objective Example Measure Cd At Multiple Yaw Angles] --> S2[Select Model Or Full Size Vehicle]
    S2 --> S3[Instrument With Pressure Taps And Balances]
    S3 --> S4[Align Model At Yaw Zero Degree]
    S4 --> S5[Power Up Fan And Ramp To Target Velocity]
    S5 --> S6[Wait For Flow To Stabilise About 10 Seconds]
    S6 --> S7[Acquire Force And Pressure Data Over 30 Seconds]
    S7 --> S8[Compute Coefficients Using q Infinity]
    S8 --> S9[Apply Blockage And Buoyancy Corrections If Needed]
    S9 --> S10[Rotate Yaw To Next Angle Say Plus 6 Degree]
    S10 --> S5
    S5 --> S11[All Yaw Angles Completed]
    S11 --> S12[Generate Final Report With Cd Versus Yaw Curve]
```

### 4.3 Comparison Tree — Wind Tunnel Categories

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Automotive Wind Tunnels] --> R1[Circuit Geometry]
    R --> R2[Flow Regime]
    R1 --> R1A[Open Circuit Eiffel]
    R1 --> R1B[Closed Circuit Goettingen]
    R1B --> R1B1[Single Test Section]
    R1B --> R1B2[Dual Test Section]
    R2 --> R2A[Subsonic M Below 0.3 Standard Automotive]
    R2 --> R2B[Transonic M 0.8 To 1.2 F1 Race Cars]
    R2 --> R2C[Supersonic Not Used For Road Vehicles]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define an automotive wind tunnel. Why are closed-circuit (Göttingen) tunnels preferred over open-circuit types for full-scale vehicle testing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An automotive wind tunnel is a ducted facility in which a controlled, uniform airflow is generated over a stationary vehicle (or its model) to measure aerodynamic forces and moments using a force balance and pressure instrumentation (1 mark).

Closed-circuit (Göttingen) tunnels are preferred because: (a) the air is recirculated, giving tight control of temperature and humidity; (b) turbulence intensity is very low ($\le 0.1\%$); (c) the flow is highly repeatable; and (d) fan power is lower due to pressure recovery in the diffuser (2 marks).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Explain why a moving-belt (rolling-road) system is essential in an automotive wind tunnel test.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

When a car drives on a road, the contact patch of each tyre is momentarily stationary relative to the road surface (1 mark). In a wind tunnel, if the car simply sits on a fixed floor, the boundary layer on the road surface is wrong and a stagnant air film forms under the car, contaminating the underbody and wheel-arch flow (1 mark). A moving-belt system matches the relative motion of the tyre/road, producing the correct underbody, wheel, and wake flow, and therefore a more realistic drag and lift measurement (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks – Internal Choice Option A)

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major sub-systems of a **closed-circuit automotive wind tunnel**. Discuss the role of the contraction, diffuser, and corner vanes.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1:5 scale model of a car is tested in a pressurised wind tunnel at 4 atm and the same temperature as the prototype. The full-scale car travels at 108 km/h. Determine the tunnel test-section velocity required for Reynolds number similarity. Take the viscosity of air to be constant. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B, Question A

**Part (a) — Block Diagram and Sub-systems (7 marks)**

A closed-circuit automotive wind tunnel consists of the following sub-systems arranged in a continuous loop (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the diagram):

1. **Drive fan with variable-speed motor** (1 mark) — supplies the mechanical energy to the airflow. The fan is typically downstream of the test section so that the air entering the test section is undisturbed by the fan's own swirl.

2. **Diffuser** (1 mark) — located immediately after the test section, the diffuser expands the cross-section gradually. By the continuity equation, the velocity drops and by Bernoulli, the static pressure rises. This **pressure recovery** dramatically reduces the fan power requirement.

3. **Turning section with corner vanes** (1 mark) — the duct must turn 180° in a closed loop. Sharp corners would cause massive flow separation and energy loss. **Aerofoil-shaped corner vanes** guide the flow around the bend with minimum loss, keeping the turbulence intensity low.

4. **Heat exchangers** (½ mark) — to maintain a constant air temperature, the closed circuit contains chilled water coils that absorb the heat added by the fan motor and the model.
