5. **Honeycomb and screens** (1 mark) — the **honeycomb** (a matrix of small hexagonal or circular cells) breaks down large transverse swirl components, while the **fine wire mesh screens** (typically 2 to 4 layers of decreasing mesh size) attenuate the small-scale axial turbulence. Together they lower the turbulence intensity from ~5 % to less than 0.1 %.

6. **Contraction (nozzle)** (1 mark) — a carefully shaped convergent duct that accelerates the flow into the test section. The contraction ratio (inlet area / test area) is typically **9:1 to 12:1**. A high contraction ratio reduces turbulence intensity by a factor equal to the ratio, since $u'/U \propto 1/CR$.

7. **Test section with model and 6-component balance** (1 mark) — the car is mounted on a strain-gauge balance that resolves the three forces ($F_x, F_y, F_z$) and three moments ($M_x, M_y, M_z$).

8. **Boundary-layer suction on the floor and walls** (½ mark) — porous walls with suction slots prevent the wall boundary layer from growing and choking the test section.

**Part (b) — Reynolds Number Scaling Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Scale: $L_m / L_p = 1/5$, hence $\lambda = 1/5$
- Pressure ratio: $p_m / p_p = 4$, so $k_\rho = 4$
- Viscosity ratio: $\mu_m / \mu_p = 1$
- Prototype velocity: $U_p = 108$ km/h $= 30$ m/s

**Step 1 — Reynolds Number Equality**

$$
Re_m = Re_p
$$

$$
\frac{\rho_m U_m L_m}{\mu_m} = \frac{\rho_p U_p L_p}{\mu_p}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $U_m$**

$$
U_m = U_p \, \frac{L_p}{L_m} \, \frac{\mu_m}{\mu_p} \, \frac{\rho_p}{\rho_m}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$
U_m = 30 \times \frac{1}{1/5} \times 1 \times \frac{1}{4}
$$

$$
U_m = 30 \times 5 \times \frac{1}{4}
$$

$$
U_m = 30 \times 1.25
$$

$$
\boxed{U_m = 37.5 \text{ m/s} \; = 135 \text{ km/h}}
$$

**Interpretation (½ mark):** The model must be tested at 135 km/h, achievable in a subsonic wind tunnel without compressibility issues since $M = 37.5 / 340 \approx 0.11 \ll 0.3$.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks – Internal Choice Option B)

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** What is **blockage effect** in a closed-wall wind tunnel? Derive the linear blockage correction formula for the drag coefficient, and explain why free-jet (open) test sections do not require this correction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car of frontal area 2.1 m² is tested in a closed-wall test section of cross-section $10 \text{ m} \times 5 \text{ m}$. The measured drag coefficient is $C_{D,meas} = 0.34$. Apply the Glauert–Allen correction with $k_s = 2.0$ and determine the corrected $C_D$. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B, Question B

**Part (a) — Blockage Effect Derivation (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** The blockage effect is the **artificial increase in apparent drag** caused by the model occupying a finite fraction of the test-section cross-section in a closed-wall tunnel. The walls restrict the air's freedom to expand sideways, accelerating the flow around the model, which produces higher velocities (and lower static pressure) than would exist in an unconfined flow.

**Derivation of the Linear Correction (4 marks):**

Consider a 2D model of frontal area $A_m$ in a closed test section of area $A_t$. Define the blockage ratio:

$$
B = \frac{A_m}{A_t}
$$

For small $B$ ($< 10\%$), the wall constraint can be modelled as a source-flow in potential-flow theory. The induced velocity at the model is proportional to $B$:

$$
u_{ind} = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{\pi}} \, U_\infty
$$

The measured dynamic pressure is therefore larger than the freestream value by a factor $(1 + k B)$:

$$
q_{meas} = q_\infty \, (1 + k B)
$$

Since $C_D = F_D / (q A)$ and $F_D$ itself is also slightly modified, the **net linear correction** is written in the Glauert–Allen form:

$$
C_{D,corr} = C_{D,meas} \, (1 - k_s B)
$$

For open-jet (free-jet) test sections, the surrounding air is at rest and the model is surrounded by an effectively infinite region, so $A_t \to \infty$ and $B \to 0$; hence no correction is needed (2 marks).

**Part (b) — Numerical Application (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $A_m = 2.1$ m²
- $A_t = 10 \times 5 = 50$ m²
- $C_{D,meas} = 0.34$
- $k_s = 2.0$

**Step 1 — Blockage Ratio**

$$
B = \frac{A_m}{A_t} = \frac{2.1}{50} = 0.042
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Glauert–Allen Correction**

$$
C_{D,corr} = 0.34 \times (1 - 2.0 \times 0.042)
$$

$$
C_{D,corr} = 0.34 \times (1 - 0.084)
$$

$$
C_{D,corr} = 0.34 \times 0.916
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{D,corr} = 0.3114 \approx 0.311}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The corrected drag coefficient is about **8.4% lower** than the measured value, confirming that closed-wall tunnels over-predict drag. The rule of thumb is to keep $B \le 5\%$ to limit the correction to $\le 10\%$, which is satisfied here.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Mach and Reynolds matching** — for cars, $M$ is automatically matched (both $\ll 0.3$); only $Re$ scaling requires attention.
> 2. **Forgetting the inverse length ratio** in the Reynolds scaling derivation — many students write $U_m = U_p \cdot (1/\lambda) \cdot k_\rho$ instead of the correct $U_m = U_p \cdot (1/\lambda) \cdot (1/k_\rho)$ when density is increased.
> 3. **Applying blockage correction incorrectly** — the correction is a **multiplier less than 1** (it reduces $C_D$), not an addition.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** in numerical problems — this is the single most common 1-mark loss in KTU papers.
> 5. **Not labelling the diagram** — when drawing the wind-tunnel block diagram, label **every** sub-system; missing labels cost up to 2 marks.
> 6. **Ignoring the role of the moving-belt** — a question on "sources of error in a wind tunnel" almost always includes underbody flow contamination, and the moving belt is the fix.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Definition:** Wind tunnel = controlled duct where air is moved past a stationary car to measure aero forces.
- [ ] **Two main types:** Open-circuit (Eiffel, cheap) and Closed-circuit (Göttingen, OEM-grade).
- [ ] **Test speed for cars:** 30–50 m/s, $M < 0.13$ → **incompressible subsonic** regime.
- [ ] **Contraction ratio:** typically 9:1 to 12:1 — reduces turbulence.
- [ ] **Honeycomb + screens:** reduce turbulence intensity from ~5% to < 0.1%.
- [ ] **Corner vanes:** guide flow around the 180° loop with minimum loss.
- [ ] **Diffuser:** recovers pressure, reduces fan power.
- [ ] **6-component balance:** measures $F_x, F_y, F_z, M_x, M_y, M_z$.
- [ ] **Moving belt:** essential for realistic underbody and wheel flow.
- [ ] **Free-jet vs closed-wall:** free-jet has no blockage; closed-wall needs Glauert–Allen correction.
- [ ] **Blockage formula:** $C_{D,corr} = C_{D,meas} (1 - k_s B)$, with $B = A_m/A_t$.
- [ ] **Reynolds scaling:** $U_m = U_p (L_p/L_m) (\rho_p/\rho_m) (\mu_m/\mu_p)$.
- [ ] **Dynamic similarity for cars:** match $Re$; $M$ auto-matched; $Fr$ not relevant for air.
- [ ] **Standard reference values:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s), $a = 340$ m/s.
- [ ] **Real-world $Re$ for a car:** $\sim 8 \times 10^6$ at 30 m/s.
- [ ] **Fan power scales as $U^3$** — this is the fundamental reason automotive tunnels are speed-limited.
- [ ] **Free-jet test sections** are the global standard for full-scale OEM testing.
- [ ] **KTU-most-asked 2-mark:** "Why is a closed-circuit tunnel preferred?" / "Why moving belt?" / "Why free-jet?"
- [ ] **KTU-most-asked 14-mark:** Reynolds scaling derivation OR blockage correction derivation with a worked example.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principles of Wind Tunnel Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **wind tunnel** is a meticulously engineered facility that artificially generates a controlled, uniform flow of air (or other gas) past a stationary test model, replicating the real-world relative motion between a body and the surrounding fluid. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEAUT752 - Module 4), the **Principles of Wind Tunnel Technology** encompass the foundational design philosophy, classification taxonomy, governing non-dimensional similarity parameters, and measurement instrumentation that permit accurate, repeatable aerodynamic experimentation on ground vehicles, aircraft, and bluff bodies.

In the specific context of **Vehicle Aerodynamics**, the wind tunnel is the single most important experimental instrument. It enables engineers to quantify drag coefficient ($C_D$), lift coefficient ($C_L$), side-force coefficient ($C_S$), and their respective moments ($C_M$) of a full-scale or scale-model automobile, truck, or racing car under precisely regulated Reynolds number ($\mathrm{Re}$) and Mach number ($M$) conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim standard):** *A wind tunnel is a closed or open-throated duct system equipped with a prime mover (fan/motor) that accelerates a working fluid to prescribed velocities so that aerodynamic forces, moments, and surface pressures on a stationary model can be measured with high fidelity.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Giant Hairdryer" Mental Model

Imagine you place a small toy car on a long conveyor belt moving at 100 km/h — the car "feels" the wind. Now imagine the opposite: keep the car perfectly still on a giant desk and blow air over it at 100 km/h with a colossal industrial hairdryer. **That hairdryer is your wind tunnel.** The car is fixed to a sensitive weighing scale (a force balance), and as the air rushes past, the scale registers the aerodynamic forces exactly as if the car were actually driving at that speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use a wind tunnel instead of a real road test?**
> - **Repeatability:** Weather (rain, gusts, sun) cannot disturb the test.
> - **Control:** Speed, temperature, and pressure can be dialed precisely.
> - **Instrumentation:** Sensors, cameras, and balances can be mounted *inside* the model.
> - **Safety:** A 350 km/h prototype does not need to be driven.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Constants & Metrics

- **Standard Sea-Level Density:** $\rho = 1.225 \ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$
- **Dynamic Viscosity of Air:** $\mu = 1.789 \times 10^{-5} \ \mathrm{kg/(m \cdot s)}$
- **Speed of Sound at SL:** $a = 340.29 \ \mathrm{m/s}$
- **Kinematic Viscosity:** $\nu = \mu / \rho = 1.46 \times 10^{-5} \ \mathrm{m^2/s}$
- **Reference Velocity for Vehicle Tunnels:** $V_{\infty} = 30$ to $200$ km/h typical
- **Test Section Turbulence Intensity Target:** $\mathrm{Tu} \leq 0.1\%$ (for high-quality subsonic tunnels)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Operating Principle

The wind tunnel exploits the **Galilean invariance** of fluid mechanics. The aerodynamic forces on a body depend *only* on the **relative velocity** between the body and the fluid. By holding the model stationary and moving the fluid, we obtain the same force coefficients as the inverse scenario (stationary fluid, moving body). This is the central philosophical pivot of the entire discipline.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Wind Tunnel Design

Every KTU 2024 Scheme question on this topic rotates around three pillars. Memorize this triangular logic:

**Pillar 1 — Geometric Similarity**
The model must be a true, scaled replica of the prototype. If the prototype is length $L_p$ and the model is length $L_m$, the geometric scale ratio is:
$$\lambda = \frac{L_m}{L_p}$$

Every feature (wheelbase, hood angle, ground clearance, mirror position) must be scaled by the same $\lambda$ to avoid distortion of the flow field.

**Pillar 2 — Kinematic Similarity**
The streamlines around the model must be geometrically similar to the prototype streamlines. This is guaranteed by matching the **Reynolds number** and **Mach number**:
$$\mathrm{Re} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} \qquad \quad M = \frac{V}{a}$$

**Pillar 3 — Dynamic Similarity**
Forces and moments must scale proportionally. This follows automatically when Pillar 1 and Pillar 2 are satisfied and the same fluid (typically air) is used.

## 2.3 Classification of Wind Tunnels — Structured Logic

### 2.3.1 By Circuit Type (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Type | Description | Pressure Recovery | Power Cost | Automotive Use |
|------|-------------|-------------------|------------|----------------|
| **Open-Circuit (Blow-Down / Eiffel / Göttingen)** | Air is drawn in from the room, accelerated, and exhausted back to the room. No recirculation. | Very Low | High (single-pass) | Educational, low-speed |
| **Closed-Circuit (Closed-Return / Prandtl)** | Air circulates in a closed loop through a return passage; the same air is reused. | Very High | Low (recirculated) | **Industrial standard** (Pininfarina, MIRA, FKFS) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Closed-circuit wind tunnels** are universally used for vehicle testing because the temperature stays stable, the test section is shielded from room drafts, and acoustic isolation is far superior.

### 2.3.2 By Speed Regime (Mach Number $M$)

| Tunnel Class | Mach Range | Vehicle Relevance |
|--------------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Subsonic (Low-Speed)** | $M < 0.3$ | **All road & race cars** — primary domain of PEAUT752 |
| Transonic | $0.8 < M < 1.2$ | Uncommon for vehicles |
| Supersonic | $1.2 < M < 5.0$ | Not for ground vehicles |
| Hypersonic | $M > 5.0$ | Re-entry vehicles, missiles |

### 2.3.3 By Test Section Geometry

- **Closed (Solid Wall):** Rectangular or octagonal cross-section. Used for subsonic flow where blockage is corrected analytically.
- **Open (Slotted / Perforated):** Test section has perforated walls or slots, used in transonic tunnels to suppress shock reflections.
- **Free-Jet (NFFT — None-Flow-Through):** The model sits in an open jet with no walls. **Most representative of free-flight conditions for road vehicles.**

## 2.4 The Canonical Subsonic Closed-Return Wind Tunnel Architecture

A vehicle wind tunnel has nine major components in the flow path. Trace the flow direction:

1. **Bell-Mouth / Entry Cone** — Smoothly accelerates air into the contraction.
2. **Contraction (Convergent Nozzle)** — Increases velocity and *decreases* turbulence intensity.
3. **Honeycomb / Flow Straightener / Screens** — Removes lateral swirl and large-scale eddies.
4. **Settling Chamber** — Provides uniform, low-turbulence inflow.
5. **Test Section** — Where the model resides; force balance, pressure taps, and flow visualization are performed.
6. **Diffuser** — Decelerates the flow gradually to prevent separation and recover pressure.
7. **Corner Turning Vanes** — In closed-return tunnels, redirect the flow $180^\circ$ with minimal loss.
8. **Fan / Prime Mover** — Provides the pressure rise to drive the loop.
9. **Drive Section / Silencer** — Houses the motor and dampens acoustic noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Contraction Ratio ($C_R$)** is the ratio of settling-chamber area to test-section area. Typical values: $C_R = 9$ to $25$. A higher $C_R$ yields a lower **turbulence intensity** ($\mathrm{Tu}$) and better flow uniformity, but the diffuser must be longer to recover pressure without stall.

## 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mandatory Memorization)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | $V = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}}$ | Bernoulli velocity from Pitot-static differential | $\mathrm{m/s}$ |
| 2 | $\mathrm{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number — viscous similarity | dimensionless |
| 3 | $M = \dfrac{V}{a}$ | Mach number — compressibility similarity | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_D = \dfrac{D}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A}$ | Drag coefficient | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_L = \dfrac{L}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A}$ | Lift coefficient | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_M = \dfrac{M}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A \, L}$ | Moment coefficient (about reference length) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\mathrm{Tu} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\overline{u'^2}}}{U}$ | Turbulence intensity (RMS fluctuating / mean) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C_p = \dfrac{P - P_\infty}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2}$ | Pressure coefficient | dimensionless |
| 9 | $C_R = \dfrac{A_{\text{settling}}}{A_{\text{test}}}$ | Contraction ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_{\text{block}} = \dfrac{A_m}{A_t}$ | Solid blockage ratio (model frontal area to test area) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $C_{D,\text{corr}} = C_{D,\text{meas}}\left(1 - k \varepsilon\right)^2$ | Maskell blockage correction | dimensionless |
| 12 | $C_p^* = C_p (1 - k \varepsilon)^{2}$ | Maskell wall-pressure correction | dimensionless |
| 13 | $P_{\text{fan}} = \Delta p_{\text{total}} \cdot Q$ | Fan power required = pressure rise × volume flow | $\mathrm{W}$ |
| 14 | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Speed of sound in ideal gas | $\mathrm{m/s}$ |
| 15 | $V_{\infty} = M \cdot a$ | Velocity from Mach number | $\mathrm{m/s}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant $k$ in Maskell correction:** For an open jet, $k \approx 0.4$ to $0.6$ for streamlined bodies; for bluff bodies (cars), empirical calibrations with reference geometries (Ahmed body) are mandatory.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM Use:** Volkswagen, BMW, Tata Motors, and Mahindra all operate full-scale climatic wind tunnels (e.g., VW's Wolfsburg tunnel is 4.7 m diameter) to measure $C_D$ to within $\pm 0.001$ repeatability.
- **Motorsport:** Formula 1 teams use 60% scale models in 1:1 scale moving-belt tunnels (rolling road) so the contact patch of the tire and boundary-layer ingestion under the floor are correctly simulated.
- **Regulatory:** Homologation of EV range requires wind-tunnel validation of the production-intent body.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Velocity from Pitot-Static Probe Using Bernoulli's Equation

**Statement:** A Pitot-static tube measures stagnation pressure $P_0$ and static pressure $P$. Show that the free-stream velocity is given by $V = \sqrt{2(P_0 - P)/\rho}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the streamline.** Connect the freestream point ($P_\infty$, $V_\infty$) to the stagnation point on the probe nose ($P_0$, $V = 0$).

**Step 2 — Apply incompressible, steady, inviscid Bernoulli equation along the streamline:**
$$P_\infty + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 = P_0 + \frac{1}{2}\rho (0)^2$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange the equation to isolate $V_\infty^2$:**
$$\frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 = P_0 - P_\infty$$

$$V_\infty^2 = \frac{2(P_0 - P_\infty)}{\rho}$$

**Step 4 — Take the positive square root (velocity is non-negative):**
$$V_\infty = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_0 - P_\infty)}{\rho}}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering replacement.** Since the static tap on the probe measures $P_\infty$ directly, we may simply write:
$$V = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}} \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta P = P_0 - P$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a vehicle wind tunnel operating at $V = 50$ m/s, this gives $\Delta P \approx 1.53 \times 10^3$ Pa, easily resolved by a high-accuracy differential pressure transducer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Contraction Ratio Effect on Turbulence Intensity

**Statement:** Demonstrate that the contraction cone reduces turbulence intensity by the inverse square of its area ratio (for irrotational decay).

**Step 1 — Definition of turbulence intensity:**
$$\mathrm{Tu} = \frac{u'}{U}$$

where $u'$ is the RMS of the fluctuating axial velocity component and $U$ is the local mean velocity.

**Step 2 — Continuity (mass conservation) at the inlet (area $A_1$) and exit (area $A_2$) of the contraction:**
$$A_1 U_1 = A_2 U_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad U_2 = U_1 \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$

**Step 3 — For a well-designed contraction, the fluctuating component $u'$ (being a small perturbation) is governed by linearized momentum and obeys the irrotational-flow result that the *amplitude* of a potential disturbance decays as $A_1 / A_2$ in the streamwise direction, while its RMS scales similarly.**

**Step 4 — Therefore the new turbulence intensity at the test section is:**
$$\mathrm{Tu}_2 = \frac{u_2'}{U_2} = \frac{u_1' (A_1/A_2)}{U_1 (A_1/A_2)} = \frac{u_1'}{U_1} \cdot \frac{1}{C_R}$$

Wait — this is a simplistic potential-flow scaling. The widely accepted empirical result (Bell & Mehta, 1988) is:
$$\mathrm{Tu}_{\text{test}} \approx \frac{\mathrm{Tu}_{\text{settling}}}{C_R}$$

and the **RMS static-pressure fluctuations** (which dominate noise in force measurements) decay even more strongly:
$$\frac{p'_{\text{test}}}{p'_{\text{settling}}} \propto \frac{1}{C_R^2}$$

**Step 5 — Design takeaway:** Doubling $C_R$ (e.g., from 9 to 36) reduces the *force-balance noise floor* by approximately a factor of 4 in pressure fluctuations — this is why the **contraction cone is the single most important piece of flow-conditioning hardware** in a vehicle tunnel.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maskell Blockage Correction (Bluff Body in Closed Test Section)

**Statement:** For a bluff body (e.g., a car) of frontal area $A_m$ placed in a closed test section of area $A_t$, the measured drag is artificially high because the model displaces fluid and effectively acts as a "nozzle" in the test section, accelerating the flow. Derive the Maskell correction.

**Step 1 — Define the solid blockage ratio:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{A_m}{A_t}$$

For a typical car in a 10 m² test section with $A_m = 2$ m², $\varepsilon = 0.20$ (a non-trivial correction).

**Step 2 — The flow sees an effective area reduction, so the local dynamic pressure at the model is amplified by a factor $(1 - k\varepsilon)^{-2}$ where $k \approx 0.4$ for typical bluff bodies (Maskell, 1963).**

**Step 3 — The measured drag coefficient must be corrected downward:**
$$C_{D,\text{true}} = C_{D,\text{meas}} \left(1 - k \varepsilon\right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.** Let $\varepsilon = 0.20$, $k = 0.5$:
$$(1 - 0.5 \times 0.20)^2 = (0.90)^2 = 0.81$$
$$C_{D,\text{true}} = 0.81 \, C_{D,\text{meas}}$$

A 19% correction — absolutely non-negligible for a vehicle manufacturer reporting homologated $C_D$ values.

**Step 5 — Wall pressure correction.** Static pressure on the test-section wall is also affected:
$$C_p^* = C_p \left(1 - k\varepsilon\right)^2$$

This correction is applied to all surface pressure taps before publishing pressure distributions.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Required Fan Power

**Step 1 — The total pressure rise $\Delta p_{\text{total}}$ required to drive the loop equals the sum of all losses: contraction (small), honeycomb + screens (dominant), test-section model drag, diffuser, and turning vanes.**

**Step 2 — The volumetric flow rate $Q = V_t \cdot A_t$ where $V_t$ is the test-section velocity and $A_t$ is its area.**

**Step 3 — Hydraulic power delivered to the air:**
$$P_{\text{fan}} = \Delta p_{\text{total}} \cdot Q = \Delta p_{\text{total}} \cdot V_t \cdot A_t$$

**Step 4 — Substituting $V_t = 50$ m/s, $A_t = 10$ m², $\Delta p_{\text{total}} = 2000$ Pa:**
$$P_{\text{fan}} = 2000 \times 50 \times 10 = 1{,}000{,}000 \ \mathrm{W} = 1 \ \mathrm{MW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A full-scale vehicle wind tunnel consumes roughly 1–5 MW of electrical power at peak operation — equivalent to a small town. This is why efficient diffuser and screen design is critical to operating cost.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Measurement Techniques Comparison

| Technique | Measures | Spatial Resolution | Temporal Resolution | Intrusive? | Typical Use in Vehicle Tunnel |
|-----------|----------|--------------------|--------------------|------------|-------------------------------|
| **Pitot-Static Probe** | $V$, $P_0$, $P_\infty$ | Point | Continuous | Yes (rake supports) | Reference velocity, $V_\infty$ calibration |
| **Multi-Hole Pressure Probe (Cobra Probe)** | 3-component $V$ vector | Point | kHz | Yes | Three-dimensional wake surveys |
| **Pressure-Sensitive Paint (PSP)** | $C_p$ on entire surface | Full surface (pixel) | Single snapshot | No (non-contact) | Pressure map on full body |
| **Hot-Wire Anemometry** | Instantaneous $V$ (1D) | $\sim$ 1 mm | MHz | Yes | Turbulence intensity, Tu measurement |
| **Laser Doppler Anemometry (LDA)** | $V$ at point | Point | kHz | No (laser) | Non-intrusive boundary layer profiles |
| **Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV)** | 2D $V$ field | mm-scale, planar | Hz | No (laser sheet) | Wake flow structures, vortex identification |
| **Strain-Gauge Force Balance** | 6-component forces/moments | Integrated over body | Static or dynamic | No (model mounted) | Direct $C_D$, $C_L$, $C_M$ measurement |
| **Pneumatic Balance** | Lift only (early design) | Integrated | Static | No | Historical, low accuracy |
| **Boundary Layer Rake** | Velocity profile | Spanwise | Static | Yes | Ground-plane boundary layer simulation |

> [!NOTE]
> The **rolling-road (5-belt or 1-belt moving belt system)** is the *defining* feature of modern automotive wind tunnels. It moves the ground under the stationary wheels so that the boundary layer on the road mimics the moving-ground condition of a real car at speed. Without it, the measured underbody and wheel $C_D$ values are wildly wrong.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Closed-Return Subsonic Wind Tunnel (Block Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> A1[Bell Mouth Entry Cone]
    A1 --> A2[Honeycomb Flow Straightener]
    A2 --> A3[Anti Turbulence Screens]
    A3 --> A4[Settling Chamber]
    A4 --> A5[Contraction Cone CR 9 to 25]
    A5 --> A6[Test Section with Car Model on Force Balance]
    A6 --> A7[Diffuser Pressure Recovery]
    A7 --> A8[Corner Turning Vanes 180 degree]
    A8 --> A9[Fan Prime Mover 1 to 5 MW]
    A9 --> A10[Heat Exchanger and Silencer]
    A10 --> A0
    A6 -. Surface pressure taps .-> B1[Pressure Scanner 64 ch]
    A6 -. Strain gauge outputs .-> B2[Force Balance Amplifier]
    B1 --> C1[Data Acquisition DAQ]
    B2 --> C1
    A5 -. Velocity probe .-> B3[Pitot Static Reference]
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Computation of CD CL CM Cp]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The outer loop (A0 → A10) is the **closed return circuit**. The bottom dashed lines show the **instrumentation signal path** from the test section to the data-acquisition system. The fan (A9) is the only prime mover; once steady state is reached, it merely overcomes the cumulative aerodynamic losses.

## 4.2 Diagram B — Test Section Internal Architecture (Vehicle Wind Tunnel)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Inflow from Contraction] --> P2[Boundary Layer Suction Slot at Test Section Floor]
    P2 --> P3[Moving Belt Rolling Road]
    P3 --> P4[Car Model Mounted on 6 Component Balance Below Floor]
    P4 --> P5[Wheel Spinning Motors in Each Wheel Hub]
    P5 --> P6[Boundary Layer Rake Upstream of Car]
    P6 --> P7[Car Body with Pressure Tap Array]
    P7 --> P8[Wake Survey Probe Traversing 3D Traverse Gear]
    P8 --> P9[Diffuser Entry]
    P1 -. Reference Pitot Static .-> P10[Reference Velocity Probe]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Every modern vehicle wind tunnel includes a **moving belt** (P3) and **spinning wheels** (P5) so the local flow at the tire contact patch and the rotating wheel wake match the real driving condition. The **boundary-layer suction slot** (P2) and **upstream rake** (P6) tune the simulated ground boundary layer to the correct thickness and displacement.

## 4.3 Diagram C — Force Balance Coordinate System (6-Component)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Forces
    F1[Drag D along X] 
    F2[Side Force S along Y]
    F3[Lift L along Z]
    end
    subgraph Moments
    M1[Roll Moment about X]
    M2[Pitch Moment about Y]
    M3[Yaw Moment about Z]
    end
    F1 --> R[Resultant 6 Component Force Vector]
    F2 --> R
    F3 --> R
    M1 --> R
    M2 --> R
    M3 --> R
    R --> BAL[Strain Gauge Balance Wheatstone Bridge]
    BAL --> AMP[Amplifier and DAQ]
    AMP --> CAL[Calibration Matrix Application]
    CAL --> COUT[Output CD CS CL CM roll CM pitch CM yaw]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A **6-component force balance** is essentially three orthogonal force transducers and three orthogonal moment transducers that share a common rigid element (the "balance beam"). Calibration uses a known deadweight-and-pulley rig that applies independent forces/moments to populate the $6 \times 6$ interaction matrix.

## 4.4 Diagram D — Pitot-Static Probe Schematic (Functional Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Freestream Velocity V at Test Section] --> B[Hemispherical Nose Stagnation Tap]
    A --> C[Sidewall Static Taps Ring of 4]
    B --> D[Tube to Differential Transducer High Port]
    C --> E[Tube to Differential Transducer Low Port]
    D --> F[P0 P infinity Differential]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Apply Bernoulli V equals 2 DeltaP over rho]
    G --> H[Calibrated V output]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The Pitot-static tube is the *primary velocity reference* for the entire tunnel. Without an accurate $V_\infty$, all force coefficients ($C_D$, $C_L$, etc.) carry proportional error because they scale as $1/V^2$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a wind tunnel. List the three types based on the flow circuit.

**Model Answer:**

A wind tunnel is a ducted facility that uses a prime mover (fan) to produce a controlled airstream over a stationary test model so that aerodynamic forces, moments, and pressures can be measured precisely, exploiting the principle of Galilean invariance.

The three types based on flow circuit are:
1. **Open-circuit (open-return / induction tunnel):** Air is drawn from the room, passes through the test section, and is exhausted back to the room. Simple and cheap, but poor temperature and acoustic control.
2. **Closed-circuit (closed-return / Prandtl tunnel):** Air circulates in a sealed loop. The same air is reused, giving better flow quality, temperature stability, and low noise.
3. **Open-jet (NFFT — None-Flow-Through):** A special open-circuit variant where the test section has no walls; the model sits in a free jet issuing into a large collection chamber. Most representative of free-flight flow.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is meant by the contraction ratio of a wind tunnel? Why is a high contraction ratio desirable?

**Model Answer:**

The contraction ratio ($C_R$) is defined as the ratio of the settling-chamber cross-sectional area to the test-section cross-sectional area:
$$C_R = \frac{A_{\text{settling}}}{A_{\text{test}}}$$

A high contraction ratio is desirable because:
- It **reduces the turbulence intensity** in the test section (empirically $\mathrm{Tu} \propto 1/C_R$).
- It improves **flow uniformity** (the velocity profile becomes flatter).
- It suppresses **RMS pressure fluctuations**, which directly lowers the noise floor of the force balance.
- Typical high-quality subsonic tunnels use $C_R$ between 9 and 25; values above 25 require impractically long contraction cones.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Set 1]**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a closed-circuit subsonic wind tunnel. State the function of each component.

**Model Solution:**

A closed-circuit subsonic wind tunnel recirculates the same air continuously in a closed loop. The block diagram is shown below (in the order of flow):

1. **Bell-mouth / Entry cone:** Smoothly draws air into the system, avoiding flow separation at the inlet lip.
2. **Honeycomb (Flow straightener):** Breaks up large-scale lateral swirl and secondary flows; composed of hexagonal or square cells of length $\sim 8\times$ cell diameter.
3. **Antiturbulence screens:** Multiple fine wire meshes (typically 2–4) further reduce large-scale turbulence and equalize the mean velocity profile.
4. **Settling chamber:** Provides a region of nearly uniform, low-turbulence flow upstream of the contraction.
5. **Contraction cone:** Accelerates the flow to the test-section velocity. The contraction ratio $C_R$ is usually 9 to 25.
6. **Test section:** The model is mounted on a 6-component force balance; pressure taps, flow visualization, and reference probes are used here.
7. **Diffuser:** Decelerates the flow gradually (typically 6°–8° half-angle) to recover static pressure with minimum loss.
8. **Corner turning vanes:** In a closed-return tunnel, redirect the flow by 180° with low total-pressure loss; designed for equal-area flow distribution.
9. **Fan / Prime mover:** Provides the pressure rise $\Delta p$ to overcome the cumulative losses; typically 1–5 MW for vehicle tunnels.
10. **Heat exchanger + Silencer:** Removes the heat added by the fan and attenuates acoustic noise (especially important in climatic tunnels).

**Working principle:** The fan pressurizes the loop. Air enters, is conditioned (straightened, de-swirled, accelerated), passes the stationary model where forces are measured, decelerates in the diffuser, and returns to the fan. The dynamic equilibrium gives a steady test-section velocity.

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Define the Reynolds number and Mach number for a wind tunnel. Why must they match the prototype conditions?

**Model Solution:**

The **Reynolds number** characterizes the ratio of inertial to viscous forces in the flow:
$$\mathrm{Re} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

where $L$ is the characteristic length (e.g., wheelbase or frontal height of the vehicle), $V$ is the free-stream velocity, $\rho$ is the air density, and $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity.

The **Mach number** characterizes compressibility effects:
$$M = \frac{V}{a} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{\gamma R T}}$$

**Why matching is mandatory (dynamic similarity principle):**

If $\mathrm{Re}_{\text{model}} \neq \mathrm{Re}_{\text{prototype}}$, the relative importance of viscous forces (boundary layer thickness, separation point, wake structure) changes. For a car, this directly affects $C_D$. Mismatched $\mathrm{Re}$ may incorrectly predict flow separation over the rear window, boot, or underbody diffuser.

If $M_{\text{model}} \neq M_{\text{prototype}}$ and $M > 0.3$, compressibility (density variation) effects become non-negligible, introducing wave drag and changing pressure distributions. For road vehicles $M < 0.3$ in normal operation, so compressibility is not the main concern, but Reynolds similarity is **critical**.

For a $1:4$ scale model tested at the same $V$, $\mathrm{Re}$ is reduced by $4\times$, so tunnels often use elevated pressure (pressurized wind tunnel) or cryogenic temperature to restore $\mathrm{Re}$ matching.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Set 2]**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the concept of similarity in wind tunnel testing. What are the corrections applied for blockage and wall interference?

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of similarity:** For a wind tunnel test to faithfully reproduce the aerodynamic behavior of the full-scale prototype (the "real car"), three conditions must be satisfied:
1. **Geometric similarity:** Every linear dimension is scaled by the same factor $\lambda$.
2. **Kinematic similarity:** Streamlines and velocity ratios are identical; enforced by matching $\mathrm{Re}$ and $M$.
3. **Dynamic similarity:** The ratios of all relevant forces (inertia, viscosity, gravity, elasticity) are identical. When the same fluid is used and $\mathrm{Re}$, $M$ are matched, dynamic similarity is automatically obtained.

**Blockage and wall-interference corrections:**

In a closed test section, the model displaces air and effectively "chokes" the cross-section, artificially increasing the local dynamic pressure and hence the measured forces. The key corrections are:

**1. Solid Blockage Correction (Maskell, 1963):**
$$C_{D,\text{true}} = C_{D,\text{meas}} \left(1 - k \varepsilon\right)^2 \quad ; \quad \varepsilon = \frac{A_m}{A_t}$$

For bluff bodies, $k \approx 0.4$ to $0.6$.

**2. Wake Blockage Correction:** The model's wake displaces additional fluid and adds a velocity-induced drag increment. The complete Maskell formulation gives:
$$C_{D,\text{true}} = C_{D,\text{meas}} \left(1 - k_s \varepsilon_s\right)^2 - \frac{C_D^2}{4} \frac{\pi^2}{C_t^2} \varepsilon_w$$
where $\varepsilon_s$ is the solid-blockage ratio and $\varepsilon_w$ is the wake-blockage parameter.

**3. Wall Pressure Correction (Closed Section):**
$$C_p^* = C_p \left(1 - k\varepsilon\right)^2$$

**4. Open-Jet Correction:** For free-jet tunnels, the dominant correction is the jet-boundary entrainment effect, modeled with virtual source/sink methods.

**Practical guideline:** Keep $\varepsilon < 0.05$ to keep corrections below 5% of $C_D$ (this requires a large test section, e.g., $> 4 \times$ model frontal height in each direction for a car).

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Explain the working principle and application of the following in a vehicle wind tunnel: (i) Force balance, (ii) Pressure-sensitive paint (PSP).

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Force Balance:**

A force balance is a precisely calibrated mechanical transducer that measures the three forces ($D$, $S$, $L$) and three moments (roll, pitch, yaw) exerted by the airflow on the model. The model is rigidly attached to the balance, which is in turn fixed to the tunnel floor or a sting.

**Working principle:** The aerodynamic loads deform a precision-machined elastic element. Strain gauges (Wheatstone bridges) bonded to the element convert the strain into proportional voltage outputs. A $6 \times 6$ calibration matrix maps the six raw voltages to the six physical force/moment components, removing cross-axis coupling.

**Types used in vehicle tunnels:**
- **Platform balance (under-floor):** Mounted beneath the moving belt; the car sits on top. Used in full-scale automotive tunnels.
- **Sting balance:** The model is cantilevered from a single rear strut; used for scale models.
- **Internal balance:** Housed inside the model itself for aircraft testing.

**Application:** Direct measurement of $C_D$, $C_L$, $C_S$, and moments over a swept yaw ($\beta$) and pitch ($\alpha$) range, used for drag-bookmarking, lift-curve slope, and yaw-stability derivatives.

**(ii) Pressure-Sensitive Paint (PSP):**

PSP is a non-intrusive, full-field pressure measurement technique in which the model surface is coated with a luminescent paint containing an oxygen-sensitive dye.

**Working principle:** The paint is excited by UV or blue light (from LEDs or a laser). The dye fluoresces, and the **intensity of the emitted light is quenched by local oxygen concentration**. Since oxygen concentration is proportional to local air pressure (Henry's law at the surface), the **fluorescence intensity maps inversely to surface pressure**:
$$I / I_0 = f(P, T)$$

A high-resolution scientific CCD camera captures the intensity map, and in-situ calibration (using a few discrete pressure taps) converts the intensity field to a full $C_p$ distribution over the entire body.

**Application:** Captures the full pressure field on a car body, hood, roof, and rear screen in a single snapshot — including the complex separated flows under the rear, which would require thousands of discrete pressure taps otherwise. Particularly valuable for **Y250-style wake region** and **front-end stagnation analysis** in Formula 1.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on wind-tunnel questions:**
> 1. **Confusing open-jet (NFFT) with open-circuit.** A "free-jet tunnel" is a *type of test section*, not a circuit topology. State both clearly.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the reference area** for $C_D$ of a car. KTU strictly follows **frontal area** ($A_f$); writing wing area in an automotive question costs a mark.
> 3. **Maskell correction sign error:** the *measured* $C_D$ is **higher** than the *true* $C_D$ in a closed test section; students often divide instead of multiply.
> 4. **Skipping the rolling road / moving belt** in any description of a vehicle wind tunnel. The examiner expects the belt to be mentioned.
> 5. **Mixing up $C_p$ and $C_p^*$:** $C_p$ is the local measurement, $C_p^*$ is the wall-corrected value.
> 6. **Fan power:** Several students write $P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^3 A$ (kinetic energy flux). That is the *kinetic* power, not the *fan* power. The fan must overcome **all** pressure losses, not just the dynamic pressure.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Wind tunnel** = facility that produces a controlled flow over a stationary model, exploiting Galilean invariance.
- [x] **Closed-return (Prandtl)** tunnels are the industry standard for vehicle testing.
- [x] **Open-jet (NFFT)** tunnels are most representative of free-flight conditions.
- [x] **Vehicle tunnels** are *always* subsonic ($M < 0.3$).
- [x] **The 9 components:** Bell-mouth → Honeycomb → Screens → Settling chamber → Contraction → Test section → Diffuser → Turning vanes → Fan.
- [x] **Contraction ratio** $C_R = A_{\text{settling}} / A_{\text{test}}$, typically 9 to 25; reduces turbulence and improves uniformity.
- [x] **Similarity** = geometric + kinematic + dynamic; require $\mathrm{Re}$ and $M$ matching.
- [x] **Reynolds number** $\mathrm{Re} = \rho V L / \mu$; **Mach number** $M = V / a$.
- [x] **Reference area for cars = frontal area** $A_f$.
- [x] **Drag coefficient** $C_D = D / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_f)$; **lift** $C_L = L / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_f)$; **moment** $C_M = M / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_f L)$.
- [x] **Turbulence intensity** $\mathrm{Tu} = \sqrt{\overline{u'^2}} / U$; high-quality tunnels achieve $\mathrm{Tu} \leq 0.1\%$.
- [x] **Pressure coefficient** $C_p = (P - P_\infty) / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2_\infty)$.
- [x] **Maskell blockage correction:** $C_{D,\text{true}} = C_{D,\text{meas}}(1 - k\varepsilon)^2$, with $k \approx 0.4$–$0.6$ for bluff bodies.
- [x] **Rolling road (moving belt) + spinning wheels** are *mandatory* for credible vehicle $C_D$ measurement.
- [x] **Force balance** = strain-gauge transducer; gives 6-component force/moment measurement.
- [x] **PSP** = oxygen-quenched luminescent paint, gives full-field $C_p$ from one camera shot.
- [x] **Hot-wire / LDA / PIV** are the velocity-measurement tools; PIV is the gold standard for wake flow mapping.
- [x] **Fan power** $P_{\text{fan}} = \Delta p_{\text{total}} \cdot V_t \cdot A_t$, on the order of 1–5 MW for full-scale vehicle tunnels.
- [x] **Speed of sound** $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T} = 340.29$ m/s at sea level standard atmosphere.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types and Components of Wind Tunnel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types and Components of Wind Tunnel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wind Tunnel** is a rigorously engineered, ground-based experimental facility designed to produce a **controlled, steady, uniform, and reproducible flow of air** over a scaled test model, thereby allowing aerodynamicists to simulate the relative motion between a vehicle and the surrounding fluid (air) without requiring the vehicle itself to move.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A wind tunnel is classified not merely as a "tube with a fan," but as a **calibrated metrology instrument** capable of producing quantifiable flow fields characterized by specific Reynolds Number ($Re$), Mach Number ($M$), Turbulence Intensity ($TI$), and Flow Angularity ($\alpha, \beta$) values.

Mathematically, the fundamental purpose of a wind tunnel is to replicate the **Reynolds Number similarity** between the full-scale prototype (real vehicle) and the sub-scale model, ensuring dynamic similarity of the flow field.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:**
> Imagine you want to test how a **paper boat** behaves in a river without actually floating it in a fast-moving river. Instead of putting the boat in the river, you bring the river to the boat! You pour water from a controlled tap over the stationary paper boat at the exact speed the river would flow. The wind tunnel does the exact same thing, but with **air** instead of water, and a **vehicle** (or its scaled model) instead of a paper boat. The "tap" is the powerful fan, and the "river" is the carefully conditioned airflow inside the tunnel.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The performance and classification of every wind tunnel is benchmarked against these foundational parameters:

- **Standard Sea-Level Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_0 = 101325 \text{ Pa} \approx 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- **Standard Sea-Level Air Density:** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Dynamic Viscosity of Air at 15°C:** $\mu = 1.789 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$
- **Speed of Sound at 15°C:** $a = 340.29 \text{ m/s}$
- **Kinematic Viscosity:** $\nu = \mu / \rho = 1.46 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Tip:** In vehicle aerodynamics, the *Reynolds Number* ($Re$) is the single most important similarity parameter because it governs the **boundary layer behavior** (laminar vs. turbulent transition, separation point, wake formation) which directly impacts drag, lift, and downforce on road vehicles.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Wind Tunnels — Taxonomy of Types

Wind tunnels are classified along **two orthogonal axes**: (i) **Circuit Topology** (geometry of the airflow path) and (ii) **Speed Regime** (compressibility range / Mach Number).

### 2.1.1 Classification by Circuit Topology

**A. Open-Circuit (Open-Return / Eiffel Type) Wind Tunnel**
- Air is drawn from the atmosphere, passed once through the test section, and exhausted back to the atmosphere.
- **Advantages:** Simple construction, low capital cost, no cooling required.
- **Disadvantages:** Flow is highly sensitive to ambient atmospheric disturbances (temperature, pressure fluctuations), high noise levels.
- **KTU Note:** Frequently used in **automotive industry** (e.g., subsonic full-scale vehicle testing facilities by OEMs like Mercedes-Benz, Ford).

**B. Closed-Circuit (Closed-Return / Prandtl Type) Wind Tunnel**
- Air circulates in a sealed loop; the same air mass is continuously reused.
- **Advantages:** Superior flow quality, low ambient noise, lower operating cost over long term, climate-controlled.
- **Disadvantages:** High initial capital cost, requires heat exchangers (since energy from the fan continuously heats the air), complex structural design to withstand pressure differentials.

**C. Open vs. Closed Circuit Comparison Table**

| Parameter | Open-Circuit | Closed-Circuit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Initial Cost | **Low** | **High** |
| Flow Quality | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| Energy Efficiency | Low (single pass) | **High (recirculated)** |
| Acoustic Signature | Loud | Quieter |
| Climate Control | None | **Required (Cooling Coils)** |
| Typical Application | Educational, small-scale | **Automotive OEMs, Aerospace** |

### 2.1.2 Classification by Speed Regime (Mach Number)

| Tunnel Type | Mach Range ($M$) | Vehicle/Aerospace Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Low-Subsonic** | $0 < M < 0.3$ | Passenger cars, trucks, race cars |
| **High-Subsonic (Transonic)** | $0.3 \le M < 0.8$ | High-speed trains, propeller aircraft |
| **Supersonic** | $1.2 < M < 5.0$ | Fighter jets, rockets (early stage) |
| **Hypersonic** | $M > 5.0$ | Re-entry vehicles, hypersonic missiles |
| **Special: Automotive Full-Scale** | $M \approx 0.05$–$0.25$ | Road cars at 60–250 km/h |

## 2.2 Major Structural Components of a Wind Tunnel

A modern subsonic wind tunnel comprises the following sequential components arranged along the flow direction:

### 2.2.1 Settling Chamber
- **Function:** Houses the **flow conditioning devices** (honeycomb and turbulence screens) to remove large-scale turbulent eddies and lateral velocity components from the incoming air.
- **Design Ratio:** Cross-sectional area should be $\approx 6$–$9$ times the test section area.

### 2.2.2 Honeycomb (Flow Straightener)
- **Function:** A grid of thin-walled hexagonal or rectangular cells (typically $L/D \approx 6$–$8$, where $L$ is cell depth and $D$ is cell diameter).
- **Why & How:** Breaks up large transverse swirling motions by forcing flow into parallel streamlines. Effectively reduces **lateral turbulence intensity** without significant loss of total pressure.

### 2.2.3 Contraction Cone (Nozzle / Convergent Section)
- **Function:** **Accelerates** the flow from low velocity in the settling chamber to the desired high velocity in the test section.
- **Critical Parameter:** The **Contraction Ratio (CR)** is defined as the ratio of the inlet area ($A_1$) to the exit (test section) area ($A_2$).
- **Why & How:** A larger CR reduces turbulence intensity and improves flow uniformity at the test section. According to energy conservation, the kinetic energy gain comes from the conversion of static pressure energy (Bernoulli's principle). Typical CR for subsonic tunnels: **CR = 9 to 16**.

### 2.2.4 Test Section
- **Function:** The **measurement chamber** where the vehicle model is mounted on a **force balance** (sting, strut, or ground plane).
- **Vehicle Specific:** In automotive tunnels, it is typically an **open-jet test section** (Plenum-style) with a **moving ground plane belt** to simulate the relative motion between tire and road.
- **Why & How:** Walls may be solid (closed test section, e.g., pressurized tunnels) or open (open test section, used for car testing to permit wake visualization and ground plane simulation).

### 2.2.5 Diffuser (Divergent Section)
- **Function:** **Decelerates** the flow exiting the test section back to a low velocity before reaching the fan, in order to minimize fan power requirements.
- **Design:** Has a small divergence angle (typically $2.5°$–$5°$ half-angle) to prevent **adverse pressure gradient induced flow separation**.

### 2.2.6 Corner Turning Vanes (in closed-circuit tunnels)
- **Function:** Redirect the flow smoothly through $90°$–$180°$ turns at the corners of the closed loop with minimum loss and no flow separation.

### 2.2.7 Drive Section / Fan
- **Function:** Provides the necessary **power input** to overcome frictional and form drag losses and to maintain the desired flow velocity.
- **Typical Power:** For full-scale automotive tunnels, fan motors range from **500 kW to 5 MW**.

### 2.2.8 Auxiliary Systems
- **Heat Exchangers:** Remove heat added to the air by the fan and model.
- **Boundary Layer Suction:** Removes the growing boundary layer along tunnel walls (in closed return tunnels).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Cheat Sheet for Wind Tunnel Analysis**

| # | Equation / Parameter | Formula | Description / Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Contraction Ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}$ | Ratio of inlet to test section area; higher $CR$ = better flow quality |
| 2 | **Contraction Loss Coefficient** | $C_L = \dfrac{A_2/A_1 \cdot (1 - A_2/A_1)^2}{...}$ | Quantifies non-uniformity reduction across contraction |
| 3 | **Reynolds Number** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Critical for dynamic similarity between model and prototype |
| 4 | **Mach Number** | $M = \dfrac{V}{a}$ | Determines compressibility effects; $a \approx 340.29 \text{ m/s}$ |
| 5 | **Dynamic Pressure** | $q = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2$ | Used in force coefficient definitions |
| 6 | **Velocity from Continuity** | $V_1 A_1 = V_2 A_2$ | Mass conservation through contraction |
| 7 | **Velocity from Bernoulli** | $V_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho} + V_1^2}$ | Energy conversion across contraction |
| 8 | **Turbulence Intensity** | $TI = \dfrac{u'}{U_{\infty}} \times 100\%$ | Quality metric; should be $< 0.1\%$ for high-quality tunnels |
| 9 | **Power Required (Ideal)** | $P = \Delta p \cdot Q = \Delta p \cdot A V$ | Fan power; $\Delta p$ is total pressure loss |
| 10 | **Flow Uniformity Index** | $\Delta U / U_{\infty} < 0.5\%$ | Specification for test section flow quality |
| 11 | **Boundary Layer Displacement** | $\delta^* = \int_0^\infty \left(1 - \dfrac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy$ | Wall boundary layer growth inside the test section |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** In automotive design, $Re \approx 1.5 \times 10^6$ for a typical sedan (L = 4 m, V = 130 km/h). Wind tunnel tests at 1:4 scale must achieve $Re = 3.75 \times 10^5$ to maintain similarity — this requires pressurized tunnels or cryogenic facilities (e.g., the famous **Pininfarina Wind Tunnel**).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Velocity at Test Section Using Continuity and Bernoulli

**Problem Setup:** Air enters the contraction cone of a wind tunnel from the settling chamber (station 1) at velocity $V_1$ and pressure $P_1$, and exits into the test section (station 2) with area $A_2$ at velocity $V_2$ and pressure $P_2$. Given $A_1$, $A_2$, $P_1$, $P_2$, and $\rho$, derive an expression for $V_2$.

### Step 1 — Apply Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation) for Steady, Incompressible Flow

For a control volume enclosing the contraction, the volumetric flow rate is conserved because air density is assumed constant (subsonic, low-speed regime):

$$Q_1 = Q_2 \implies A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the Settling Chamber Velocity $V_1$ in Terms of $V_2$

Rearranging the continuity equation:

$$V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} \cdot V_2$$

### Step 3 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation (Inviscid, Steady, Incompressible Flow Along a Streamline)

Between stations 1 and 2, assuming no frictional losses (ideal contraction) and horizontal flow:

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $V_1$ From Step 2 Into Bernoulli's Equation

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2\right)^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$$

$$\implies P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 - \frac{1}{2}\rho \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 V_2^2$$

### Step 5 — Factor Out $V_2^2$ on the Right-Hand Side

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $V_2^2$

$$V_2^2 = \frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]}$$

### Step 7 — Take the Positive Square Root for the Physical Velocity

$$\boxed{V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]}}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** As the contraction ratio $A_1/A_2 \to \infty$ (very strong contraction), the term $(A_2/A_1)^2 \to 0$, and the equation reduces to the **Torricelli-like discharge equation** $V_2 = \sqrt{2(P_1 - P_2)/\rho}$, confirming that for a very large plenum, the test section velocity is governed purely by the pressure drop across the nozzle.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reynolds Number Scaling for Vehicle Models

**Problem Setup:** A full-scale passenger car has characteristic length $L_p = 4.5 \text{ m}$ and is tested at $V_p = 130 \text{ km/h} = 36.11 \text{ m/s}$ in atmospheric conditions ($\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$). A geometrically similar 1:4 scale model is to be tested in a wind tunnel. Find the **required wind tunnel velocity** to maintain dynamic similarity, assuming the same fluid (air).

### Step 1 — Compute Full-Scale Reynolds Number

$$Re_p = \frac{V_p L_p}{\nu} = \frac{36.11 \times 4.5}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re_p = \frac{162.5}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.083 \times 10^7$$

### Step 2 — Apply Reynolds Number Similarity Condition

For dynamic similarity between model ($m$) and prototype ($p$):

$$Re_m = Re_p \implies \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{V_p L_p}{\nu_p}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Model Length and Equal Fluid Properties

Since the same fluid (air) is used in both, $\nu_m = \nu_p = \nu$, and $L_m = L_p / 4$:

$$V_m = Re_p \cdot \frac{\nu}{L_m} = (1.083 \times 10^7) \cdot \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}{4.5/4}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate Numerator and Denominator

$$V_m = (1.083 \times 10^7) \cdot \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.125}$$

$$V_m = (1.083 \times 10^7) \cdot (1.333 \times 10^{-5})$$

### Step 5 — Compute Final Required Test Section Velocity

$$\boxed{V_m = 144.4 \text{ m/s} \approx 520 \text{ km/h}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** Maintaining **exact** Reynolds similarity would require a sub-scale model to be tested at **unrealistically high speeds** (often supersonic), which violates other similarity conditions (Mach number, flow regime). This is why automotive OEMs either:
> (a) Use **pressurized tunnels** (increase $\rho$, decrease $\nu$ via higher pressure),
> (b) Use **cryogenic tunnels** (decrease $\nu$ by cooling air, e.g., $N_2$ at $-150°C$), or
> (c) Accept a **lower Reynolds number** and apply empirical correction factors.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Contraction Ratio Effect on Turbulence Intensity

**Problem Setup:** The turbulence intensity at the test section is empirically related to the contraction ratio by:

$$\frac{TI_2}{TI_1} = \frac{1}{CR^{1.5}}$$

For an initial settling-chamber turbulence intensity $TI_1 = 5\%$ and a target test section turbulence intensity $TI_2 < 0.1\%$, find the minimum contraction ratio.

### Step 1 — Rearrange the Empirical Relation

$$CR^{1.5} = \frac{TI_1}{TI_2}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$CR^{1.5} = \frac{0.05}{0.001} = 50$$

### Step 3 — Solve for CR by Raising Both Sides to the Power $2/3$

$$CR = 50^{2/3}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Exponent

$$CR = (50)^{0.6667} = (50)^{2/3}$$

Using logarithms: $\log(50) = 1.69897$

$$CR = 10^{(1.69897 \times 2/3)} = 10^{1.1326} \approx 13.56$$

$$\boxed{CR \approx 13.6}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A contraction ratio of approximately **13.6** is required to suppress the turbulence intensity from 5% down to 0.1%. This is consistent with industry-standard subsonic wind tunnel designs (typically CR = 9 to 16).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Closed-Circuit Subsonic Wind Tunnel

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **sequential functional flow** of air through a closed-circuit subsonic wind tunnel — the standard configuration for full-scale automotive testing facilities.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CornerTurningVane1] --> A2[DiffuserSection]
    A2 --> A3[FanDriveSection]
    A3 --> A4[HeatExchangerCoolingCoil]
    A4 --> A5[SettlingChamber]
    A5 --> A6[HoneycombFlowStraightener]
    A6 --> A7[TurbulenceScreen1]
    A7 --> A8[TurbulenceScreen2]
    A8 --> A9[ContractionCone]
    A9 --> A10[TestSectionWithVehicleModel]
    A10 --> A11[DiffuserAndBoundaryLayerSuction]
    A11 --> A12[CornerTurningVane2]
    A12 --> A1
    A10 -.ForceBalance.-> B1[DataAcquisitionSystem]
    B1 -.PressureProbes.-> A10
    style A10 fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style A3 fill:#ccddff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Yellow Block (A10):** The test section — the only place the model is installed.
- **Blue Block (A3):** The fan drive section — the heart of energy injection.
- **Dashed Lines:** Instrumentation and measurement data flow.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Open-Circuit (Eiffel Type) Wind Tunnel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[AtmosphericAirIntake] --> B2[HoneycombAndScreens]
    B2 --> B3[ContractionCone]
    B3 --> B4[TestSectionVehicleModel]
    B4 --> B5[Diffuser]
    B5 --> B6[FanExhaustSection]
    B6 --> B7[AtmosphericExhaust]
    B4 -.PressureProbe.-> C1[MultiPressureScanner]
    B4 -.ForceBalance.-> C2[SixComponentLoadCell]
    style B4 fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.3 Functional Taxonomy of Wind Tunnel Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[WindTunnelSystem] --> D2[FlowConditioning]
    D1 --> D3[FlowAcceleration]
    D1 --> D4[TestAndMeasurement]
    D1 --> D5[FlowRecovery]
    D1 --> D6[PowerAndDrive]
    D2 --> D2A[Honeycomb]
    D2 --> D2B[TurbulenceScreens]
    D2 --> D2C[SettlingChamber]
    D3 --> D3A[ContractionCone]
    D4 --> D4A[TestSection]
    D4 --> D4B[ForceBalance]
    D4 --> D4C[PressureProbes]
    D4 --> D4D[MovingGroundPlane]
    D5 --> D5A[Diffuser]
    D5 --> D5B[CornerTurningVanes]
    D6 --> D6A[FanMotor]
    D6 --> D6B[HeatExchanger]
    style D1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style D4 fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to Use These Diagrams for KTU Exams:** When a question asks to "draw a labeled schematic of a wind tunnel," reproduce a simplified version of the **Block Diagram 4.3** highlighting the **Settling Chamber → Contraction Cone → Test Section → Diffuser** sequence. This is the **most frequently asked** diagram in KTU ESE Module 4 questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define a wind tunnel. List any **four** major components of a subsonic wind tunnel.

**Model Answer:**
A wind tunnel is a ground-based experimental facility that produces a **controlled, uniform, and steady flow of air** over a stationary model to simulate aerodynamic flow conditions encountered by a moving vehicle or aircraft, enabling measurement of aerodynamic forces, pressures, and flow visualization.

The four major components are:
1. **Settling Chamber** with honeycomb and screens (flow conditioning).
2. **Contraction Cone** (nozzle) — accelerates flow to test section.
3. **Test Section** — houses the vehicle model and force balance.
4. **Diffuser** — decelerates flow after the test section to recover pressure.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "fan" as a component but forget to mention the **contraction cone** or **diffuser**. Both are essential — without them, no useful test section velocity can be achieved. Always list **conditioning + accelerating + testing + recovery** in sequence.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Differentiate between **open-circuit** and **closed-circuit** wind tunnels. State one advantage of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open-Circuit (Eiffel Type) | Closed-Circuit (Prandtl Type) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Air Path | Single pass from atmosphere to atmosphere | Sealed loop; air recirculated |
| Advantage | **Low initial capital cost** and simple construction | **Superior flow quality** with low turbulence intensity and climate control |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **components and working principle of a closed-circuit subsonic wind tunnel** used for automotive vehicle testing.

**Sub-Parts:**
- **(a)** Draw and explain the working of any **six major components** in sequence. **[7 Marks]**
- **(b)** Explain the role of the **contraction ratio** in determining test section flow quality, and derive the relationship between settling chamber pressure and test section velocity. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The closed-circuit subsonic wind tunnel consists of the following **six major components arranged sequentially** along the airflow direction:

**1. Settling Chamber:**
This is a large plenum (reservoir) preceding the contraction cone. Its primary function is to **house the flow conditioning devices** (honeycomb and screens) and provide a region of low-velocity, high-pressure air before acceleration. The chamber cross-section is typically 6–9 times the test section area.

**2. Honeycomb (Flow Straightener):**
A grid of thin-walled hexagonal cells (aspect ratio $L/D \approx 6$–$8$) that **breaks up large transverse swirling motions** in the incoming air. It ensures that flow enters the contraction cone with streamlines parallel to the tunnel axis, reducing lateral turbulence intensity.

**3. Turbulence Screens:**
Fine-mesh screens (typically 16–40 mesh count) placed downstream of the honeycomb. They **dissipate small-scale turbulent eddies** into heat through viscous shear, reducing the **axial turbulence intensity** to $< 0.1\%$.

**4. Contraction Cone (Nozzle):**
A smoothly contoured convergent section with **contraction ratio (CR) typically between 9 and 16**. Its function is to **accelerate the flow** from low velocity in the settling chamber to the high test-section velocity. The acceleration is governed by the **continuity equation** and **Bernoulli's principle**, with pressure energy converting to kinetic energy.

**5. Test Section:**
The **measurement chamber** where the scaled vehicle model (or full-scale car) is mounted. For automotive applications, the test section features a **moving ground plane belt** to simulate the relative motion between the tire and road surface. Force balances (typically six-component strain gauge types) measure drag, lift, side force, and the corresponding moments.

**6. Diffuser:**
A divergent section placed immediately after the test section with a small half-angle ($2.5°$–$5°$) to **prevent flow separation**. It decelerates the flow, recovering static pressure, which significantly **reduces the power requirement** of the fan downstream.

**Additional Components:** Fan drive section (power injection), heat exchangers (cooling), and corner turning vanes (for closed-loop return).

#### Model Solution for (b):

The **contraction ratio (CR)** is defined as:

$$CR = \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$

where $A_1$ is the settling chamber cross-sectional area and $A_2$ is the test section area.

**Role of CR in Flow Quality:**

The contraction cone acts as a **low-pass filter** for turbulence. Empirical and theoretical analyses show that the turbulence intensity reduction across the contraction follows:

$$\frac{TI_2}{TI_1} \approx \frac{1}{CR^{1.5}}$$

A higher CR produces a more uniform flow (lower turbulence intensity) and reduces flow angularity at the test section inlet.

**Derivation of Velocity in Terms of Pressure:**

Applying **Bernoulli's equation** between station 1 (settling chamber) and station 2 (test section):

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$$

From **continuity**: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \implies V_1 = V_2 \cdot (A_2/A_1)$

Substituting into Bernoulli's equation and rearranging (as shown in Section 3.1):

$$V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]}}$$

This equation demonstrates that **higher contraction ratio** (larger $A_1/A_2$) means the test section velocity approaches the ideal Torricelli value $V_2 = \sqrt{2(P_1 - P_2)/\rho}$, indicating efficient pressure-to-velocity conversion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** Discuss the **classification of wind tunnels** based on (i) circuit topology and (ii) speed regime. Explain the concept of **Reynolds Number similarity** in the context of subsonic vehicle testing.

**Sub-Parts:**
- **(a)** Classify wind tunnels by circuit topology and speed regime. Explain the working of an **open-circuit wind tunnel** with a block diagram. **[7 Marks]**
- **(b)** A 1:5 scale model of a truck is to be tested in a wind tunnel. The full-scale truck operates at 90 km/h in air with kinematic viscosity $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. If the model has a characteristic length of 2 m, calculate: (i) the full-scale Reynolds number, and (ii) the wind tunnel velocity required for dynamic similarity if the same air is used. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Classification by Circuit Topology:**
- **Open-Circuit (Eiffel Type):** Single-pass airflow; air drawn from atmosphere, passed once through test section, and exhausted back to atmosphere.
- **Closed-Circuit (Prandtl Type):** Sealed recirculating loop; the same air mass is continuously reused.

**Classification by Speed Regime:**
- **Subsonic:** $M < 0.3$ — automotive, low-speed aircraft.
- **Transonic:** $0.3 \le M < 1.2$ — propeller aircraft, high-speed trains.
- **Supersonic:** $1.2 < M < 5.0$ — fighter jets.
- **Hypersonic:** $M > 5.0$ — re-entry vehicles.

**Working of Open-Circuit Wind Tunnel (Eiffel Type):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[AtmosphericInlet] --> P2[HoneycombAndScreens]
    P2 --> P3[ContractionCone]
    P3 --> P4[TestSectionVehicleModel]
    P4 --> P5[Diffuser]
    P5 --> P6[FanAndExhaust]
    P6 --> P7[AtmosphericOutlet]
    style P4 fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

**Working Principle:**
Atmospheric air enters the inlet (P1), passes through a **flow conditioning section** (P2) where the honeycomb and screens straighten the flow and reduce turbulence. The conditioned air then enters the **contraction cone** (P3), which accelerates it to the required test-section velocity (typically 30–60 m/s for automotive testing). In the **test section** (P4), the scaled vehicle model is mounted on a force balance, and aerodynamic data are recorded. After the test section, the air decelerates in the **diffuser** (P5) before being expelled by the **fan** (P6) through the atmospheric outlet (P7).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Full-scale truck velocity: $V_p = 90 \text{ km/h} = 25 \text{ m/s}$
- Model scale: 1:5, so $L_m = 2 \text{ m} \implies L_p = 5 \times 2 = 10 \text{ m}$
- Kinematic viscosity: $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- Same fluid (air) is used in both tests.

**(i) Full-Scale Reynolds Number:**

$$Re_p = \frac{V_p \cdot L_p}{\nu} = \frac{25 \times 10}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\boxed{Re_p = \frac{250}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.667 \times 10^7}$$

**(ii) Required Wind Tunnel Velocity for Dynamic Similarity:**

For Reynolds number similarity: $Re_m = Re_p$

$$\frac{V_m \cdot L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{V_p \cdot L_p}{\nu_p}$$

Since the same fluid is used ($\nu_m = \nu_p$), and $L_m = L_p / 5$:

$$V_m = Re_p \cdot \frac{\nu}{L_m} = (1.667 \times 10^7) \cdot \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}{2}$$

$$V_m = (1.667 \times 10^7) \cdot (7.5 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$\boxed{V_m = 125 \text{ m/s} \approx 450 \text{ km/h}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistakes:** Failing to convert km/h to m/s is the #1 reason students lose marks. Always state: $V = (\text{km/h}) \times (5/18) \text{ m/s}$.
> 2. **Scale Direction Errors:** A 1:5 scale model means $L_m = L_p / 5$, **NOT** $L_m = 5 \times L_p$. Re-read the problem statement carefully.
> 3. **Forgetting to State Assumptions:** Always explicitly write: "Assuming the same fluid (air) is used in both tests and $\nu_m = \nu_p$." This shows the examiner you understand the underlying physics.
> 4. **Skipping Final Units:** The final answer must include **units** ($\text{m/s}$, $\text{kg/m}^3$, etc.). Missing units = −0.5 to −1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick Revision for KTU ESE Module 4**

- [ ] **Definition:** A wind tunnel produces a **controlled, uniform, steady** airflow over a stationary model to simulate real-world aerodynamic conditions.
- [ ] **Two Classification Axes:** (i) Circuit topology (open vs. closed), (ii) Speed regime (subsonic, transonic, supersonic, hypersonic).
- [ ] **Open-Circuit (Eiffel):** Single-pass, low cost, sensitive to ambient conditions.
- [ ] **Closed-Circuit (Prandtl):** Recirculating, high cost, excellent flow quality, requires cooling.
- [ ] **Component Sequence (in flow direction):** Settling Chamber → Honeycomb → Screens → **Contraction Cone** → **Test Section** → **Diffuser** → Fan → [Corner Vanes → back to start].
- [ ] **Contraction Ratio:** $CR = A_1/A_2$, typically **9 to 16** for subsonic tunnels; higher CR = better flow uniformity.
- [ ] **Diffuser Angle:** Half-angle of **$2.5°$–$5°$** to avoid flow separation.
- [ ] **Honeycomb:** Breaks transverse motion; aspect ratio $L/D \approx 6$–$8$.
- [ ] **Test Section:** Houses the model; for cars — includes a **moving ground plane**.
- [ ] **Reynolds Number Formula:** $Re = VL/\nu$ — governs boundary layer and flow separation.
- [ ] **Bernoulli Across Contraction:** $V_2 = \sqrt{2(P_1 - P_2) / [\rho(1 - (A_2/A_1)^2)]}$.
- [ ] **Turbulence Intensity Decay:** $TI_2/TI_1 \approx 1/CR^{1.5}$.
- [ ] **Dynamic Similarity Rule:** For vehicle model testing, match $Re_m = Re_p$, which often forces high test velocities (use pressurized or cryogenic tunnels).
- [ ] **Automotive Standard Reynolds Number:** $Re \approx 10^6$–$10^7$ for full-scale cars.
- [ ] **Fan Power:** $P = \Delta p \cdot Q$; requires **heat exchangers** to remove compressor heat.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limitation of Simulation - Stress with Scale Models - Full Scale Wind Tunnels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Limitation of Simulation - Stress with Scale Models - Full Scale Wind Tunnels # Module 4: Limitations of Simulation, Scale Model Stress & Full-Scale Wind Tunnels ## 1.1 Introduction to the Problem of Simulation in Vehicle Aerodynamics In automotive aerodynamics, the engineer has three primary tools to evaluate the aerodynamic behaviour of a road vehicle: 1. **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** – numerical simulation on a digital mesh. 2. **Scale-Model Wind-Tunnel Testing** – a geometrically reduced physical replica tested in a conventional subsonic tunnel. 3. **Full-Scale Wind-Tunnel Testing** – the real production vehicle placed inside a very large test section. Each method has an irreducible set of limitations. The purpose of this chapter is to understand **why scale models fail to perfectly represent the real car**, what stresses this places on the experimentalist, and **how full-scale wind tunnels were developed to overcome these stresses**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is a *high-weightage* item in Module 4 because it justifies the existence and expense of full-scale facilities, and it tests the student's ability to identify *dynamic similarity* and *Reynolds-number scaling* problems – both classic KTU ESE favourites. ### 1.2 Formal Definition: Limitation of Simulation > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Terminology):** > *Limitation of simulation* refers to the inherent inability of either a numerical (CFD) or scale-model experimental technique to simultaneously satisfy **all** the dimensionless similarity parameters (Re, Fr, M, Pr, etc.) that govern the flow physics around a road vehicle. Because the governing equations of viscous, incompressible, bluff-body flow are non-linear, the simplified or scaled problem cannot reproduce the full-scale flow in every detail. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Boat-in-the-Bathtub" Problem Imagine you want to study how an ocean liner cuts through water. You cannot afford a real ocean, so you put a small toy boat in a bathtub and push it. - The **water viscosity** behaves differently at the small scale (the toy sits in a *thicker* relative fluid). - The **waves** you generate are too small (gravity scaling breaks down). - The **boundary layer** on the toy hull is laminar, while on the real ship it is turbulent. Similarly, a 1:4 scale car tested in a 2 m × 2 m tunnel will *feel* an air viscosity that is effectively 4 times more "sticky" relative to its size. The car *appears* to behave as if it were driving through honey, not air. This single statement captures the essence of the **scale-model stress problem**. ### 1.4 Three Pillars of Limitation | Pillar | Physical Cause | Practical Symptom | |---|---|---| | **Geometric Similarity** | Surface roughness, panel gaps, mirror stalks cannot be scaled linearly | Boundary-layer transition moves on the model | | **Kinematic Similarity** | Velocity ratio must match | No issue – we set the wind speed | | **Dynamic Similarity** | Re, Fr, M, Sr all need to match simultaneously | **Impossible to satisfy all at once** | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write "we increase the wind speed to keep Re constant." This is correct in *principle* but ignores the compressibility limit (M < 0.3) and the drive-power limit of the fan. Always state the *capped* Re achievable, not the ideal one. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Governing Non-Dimensional Numbers For a road vehicle at highway speed (V ≈ 30 m/s, L ≈ 4–5 m) the dominant dimensionless groups are: ### 2.1.1 Reynolds Number (Viscous Inertia Ratio) $$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$ - $\rho$ = air density ≈ **1.225 kg/m³** - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity ≈ **1.81 × 10⁻⁵ N·s/m²** - $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity ≈ **1.5 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** - $V$ = free-stream velocity - $L$ = characteristic length (usually wheelbase or vehicle length) For a real car at 30 m/s: **Re ≈ 0.8 × 10⁷ to 1.0 × 10⁷** (≈ 10 million). ### 2.1.2 Froude Number (Gravitational Inertia Ratio) $$\text{Fr} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g L}}$$ Relevant only when **ground simulation with a moving belt** is used, since the boundary layer in the ground plane grows under gravity. ### 2.1.3 Mach Number (Compressibility) $$\text{M} = \frac{V}{a}, \qquad a \approx 343 \text{ m/s}$$ For road vehicles, **M < 0.3** is always satisfied, so compressibility is not a concern. ### 2.1.4 Strouhal Number (Unsteady Flow) $$\text{Sr} = \frac{f L}{V}$$ Important for vortex shedding and aero-acoustic studies. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **KTU ESE favourites**.
