Memorise the boxed form and the units of every term. | # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Vehicle Value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Re = VL/\nu$ | Viscous similarity | Full-scale ≈ 10⁷ | | 2 | $Re_m = Re_{fs}$ ⇒ $V_m = V_{fs}(L_{fs}/L_m)$ | Required model speed to match Re | 1:4 model ⇒ 4× faster | | 3 | $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ | Dynamic pressure (Pa) | ≈ 550 Pa at 30 m/s | | 4 | $P_{fan} = q \, A \, V_{t} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_t^3 A$ | Ideal fan power | 0.75–1.5 MW (full-scale) | | 5 | $P_{shaft} = P_{fan}/\eta_{fan}$ | Brake power at motor | ÷ 0.7 for real fan | | 6 | $\varepsilon = A_{model}/A_{test}$ | Blockage ratio | keep < 5–10 % | | 7 | $y_{BL}(x) \approx 0.37\, x\, Re_x^{-1/5}$ | Turbulent BL thickness | grows with distance | | 8 | $k_s^{+} = k_s \, u_\tau / \nu$ | Equivalent sand-grain roughness in wall units | match $k_s^+ \approx 20$–100 | | 9 | $\Delta C_D \approx 2\,(A_{front}/A_{tunnel})$ | Maskell blockage correction | applied to lift & drag | | 10 | $Fr = V/\sqrt{gL}$ | Ground-simulation similarity | model $V_m$ fixed by Fr if moving belt | > **Note on Notation:** The vertical bar (e.g. for $\vert V \vert$) is rendered with `\vert` to remain safe inside markdown tables. ## 2.3 Why the Scale Model Fails — Step-by-Step Logic The experimentalist performs the following *Gedankenexperiment* (thought experiment): 1. **Choose a scale ratio** $\lambda = L_m / L_{fs} = 1/4$. 2. **Match Re** by raising the model speed: $V_m = V_{fs} / \lambda = 4 \times V_{fs}$. 3. **Realise the consequence:** $V_m$ may be 120 m/s ⇒ $M \approx 0.35$ ⇒ **compressibility errors appear**. 4. **Fan power explodes:** $P \propto V^3 L^2$ ⇒ even at matched Re, the *drive power* is **16× higher** than at $\lambda=1$ with $V_{fs}$. 5. **Surface roughness** $k_s$ is geometrically scaled: $k_{s,m} = k_{s,fs}/\lambda$. A 50 µm paint roughness becomes 12.5 µm, which is **smoother than typical model paint** — boundary layer transition is delayed artificially. 6. **Strut, balance, and blockage** corrections contaminate the measurement of drag. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Reality:** Almost no automotive tunnel reaches full-scale Re. A 1:4 model in a 30 m/s tunnel still has $Re_m \approx 2.5 \times 10^6$ — only **one-quarter** of the real value. Hence the **CFD-and-model-complement** philosophy that dominates industry. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Drag-coefficient correlation:** $C_D$ is often 5–15 % lower on a model than on the full car at correct Re. - **Wind-noise and aero-acoustics** are extremely Re-sensitive and *cannot* be predicted by scale models. - **Cooling-air and under-hood flow** have small characteristic lengths (radiator fin, brake duct) that *do* scale reasonably. - **Production decision-making:** Most OEMs rely on a *trend* measurement on a model, then do a final calibration on a full-scale facility at the end of the programme. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Model Speed to Match Reynolds Number **Starting point:** Re must be the same in the model and full-scale: $$Re_m = Re_{fs}$$ $$\frac{V_m \, L_m}{\nu} = \frac{V_{fs} \, L_{fs}}{\nu}$$ Cancelling $\nu$ (air properties are the same in both tests): $$V_m \, L_m = V_{fs} \, L_{fs}$$ Solving for the required **model speed**: $$V_m = V_{fs} \, \frac{L_{fs}}{L_m} = \frac{V_{fs}}{\lambda}$$ Substituting $\lambda = 1/4$ and $V_{fs} = 30 \text{ m/s}$: $$V_m = 30 \times 4 = 120 \text{ m/s}$$ Checking the Mach number: $$M = \frac{120}{343} = 0.350$$ This **exceeds** the incompressible limit of 0.3, so the model would be operating in a regime the real car never sees. This is the *fundamental stress* of the scale-model method. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Drive-Power Scaling for a Wind Tunnel The aerodynamic power required to push air through a closed-circuit tunnel is the product of dynamic pressure, test-section area, and tunnel velocity: $$P = q \cdot A \cdot V_t = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_t^2 \cdot A \cdot V_t = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V_t^3$$ For a circular test section of diameter $D$, $A = \pi D^2 / 4$: $$P = \frac{\pi}{8}\,\rho\, D^2\, V_t^3$$ If the *test-section velocity* is the same as the model speed $V_m$ and $D \propto L_m$, then for matched Re we get $V_m \propto 1/\lambda$ and $D \propto \lambda$, so: $$P_m = \frac{\pi}{8}\rho \left(\lambda D_{fs}\right)^2 \left(\frac{V_{fs}}{\lambda}\right)^3 = \frac{\pi}{8}\rho D_{fs}^2 V_{fs}^3 \,\lambda^{2-3} = \frac{P_{fs}}{\lambda}$$ Hence, **a smaller model needs MORE fan power than the full-scale facility at the same Re** — the *exact opposite* of what intuition suggests. **Numerical example** — full-scale $D_{fs} = 5$ m, $V_{fs} = 30$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³: $$P_{fs} = \frac{\pi}{8}(1.225)(5^2)(30^3) = \frac{\pi}{8}(1.225)(25)(27\,000)$$ $$\begin{aligned} P_{fs} &= \frac{\pi}{8} \times 1.225 \times 25 \times 27\,000 \\ &= \frac{\pi}{8} \times 826\,875 \\ &\approx 0.3927 \times 826\,875 \\ &\approx 324\,700 \text{ W} \\ &\approx 0.325 \text{ MW} \end{aligned}$$ For a $\lambda = 1/4$ model at the *same* Re ($V_m = 120$ m/s, $D = 1.25$ m): $$P_m = \frac{\pi}{8}(1.225)(1.25^2)(120^3)$$ $$\begin{aligned} P_m &= \frac{\pi}{8}(1.225)(1.5625)(1\,728\,000) \\ &\approx 0.3927 \times 3\,306\,750 \\ &\approx 1\,298\,500 \text{ W} \\ &\approx 1.30 \text{ MW} \end{aligned}$$ So the scale model, when forced to match full-scale Re, requires about **4× more fan power** than the full-scale tunnel operating at full-scale speed — confirming the derivation $P_m = P_{fs}/\lambda$. > [!NOTE] > **Industry practice:** We do *not* match Re on the model. We accept $Re_m \ll Re_{fs}$ and apply a *Reynolds-number correction curve* (e.g. the famous Hoerner-style $C_D$ vs $\log Re$ plot) to extrapolate to full-scale conditions. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Blockage and Wall Interference For a model of frontal area $A_f$ in a test section of area $A_t$, the **blockage ratio** is: $$\varepsilon = \frac{A_f}{A_t}$$ Maskell's classical correction for the *incremental* drag due to solid-blockage and wake-blockage is: $$\Delta C_D = 2\,\varepsilon\,\left(1 - \frac{A_f}{2 A_t}\right)$$ For $\varepsilon = 0.05$ (5 % blockage): $$\Delta C_D = 2 \times 0.05 \times (1 - 0.025) = 0.0975$$ This is a **+0.1 increment** in $C_D$ — non-negligible when measuring small drag differences between body-styles (e.g. 0.28 vs 0.30). ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Ground Simulation and Froude Scaling When a moving belt is used to simulate the road, the belt speed $V_b$ must equal the wind speed $V$ *and* the boundary layer on the belt must match that of a real road. The boundary-layer thickness at the leading edge of a flat plate is: $$\delta(x) \propto x \, Re_x^{-1/5} \quad \text{(turbulent)}$$ Because $Re_x$ depends on $V$ and $x$, if we scale $x \to x/\lambda$ and keep $V$ unchanged, $\delta$ scales by $\lambda^{4/5}$. The **effective boundary-layer displacement thickness** at the vehicle's wheelbase changes — this is the *Froude-number* mismatch. A common compromise: match the **momentum thickness** $\theta$ of the oncoming BL, not the geometric thickness. ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Tool When? | Application | Best Method | Reasoning | |---|---|---| | Concept styling drag trend | Scale model + CFD | Cheap, fast, qualitative | | Final $C_D$ certification | **Full-scale wind tunnel** | Only way to get correct Re | | Cooling airflow distribution | Scale model with realistic heat exchanger | Reynolds number is less critical | | Wind noise (aero-acoustics) | **Full-scale wind tunnel** | Sound scales with Re and surface details | | Soiling / water management | CFD + full-scale rain rig | Surface tension and Re effects | | Open-wheel race-car wake | Scale model (large enough) | $C_D$ less critical, wake structure dominant | | Heavy-truck yaw stability | CFD + full-scale | Yaw is extremely Re-sensitive | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Flowchart of the "Simulation Decision Tree" The following Mermaid diagram maps the *engineering decision process* an OEM follows when choosing between CFD, scale model, and full-scale wind tunnel for a given measurement task. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[New Vehicle Programme Starts] --> B{Measurement Objective?} B --> C1[Styling Concept Drag] B --> C2[Final Certification Cd] B --> C3[Aero Noise] B --> C4[Cooling Airflow] C1 --> D1[1:4 Scale Model
in 2m Tunnel] C1 --> D2[Steady RANS CFD] C2 --> E1[Full Scale Wind Tunnel
D greater than 5m] C2 --> E2[DES or LES CFD Validation] C3 --> F1[Full Scale Wind Tunnel
Acoustic Array] C3 --> F2[Anechoic Open Jet] C4 --> G1[Scale Model with
Working Heat Exchanger] C4 --> G2[Underhood CFD] D1 --> H[Compare Trends] D2 --> H E1 --> I[Final Cd Sign Off] E2 --> I F1 --> J[Noise Source Map] F2 --> J G1 --> K[Cooling Performance] G2 --> K ``` ## 4.2 Schematic of a Generic Full-Scale Automotive Wind Tunnel The block diagram below identifies the *major functional systems* of a full-scale facility. The student is expected to know the role of each block. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INTAKE A1[Inlet Bellmouth
with Honeycomb] A2[Contraction
Area Ratio 9 to 1] end subgraph TESTSECTION B1[Test Section
5m to 13m dia] B2[Moving Ground Belt
5 Roll System] B3[Vehicle Mount
on 6 Component Balance] B4[Yaw Table
plus minus 30 deg] end subgraph DRIVE C1[Collector Diffuser] C2[Fan Stage 1] C3[Fan Stage 2] C4[Variable Pitch Hubs] end subgraph CONTROL D1[Data Acquisition] D2[PIV and LDV] D3[Pressure Scanner] end A1 --> A2 --> B1 B1 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 C4 -.return flow.-> A1 B1 --> B2 B1 --> B3 B1 --> B4 B1 --> D1 B1 --> D2 B1 --> D3 ``` ## 4.3 Limitations of Simulation — Root-Cause Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Limitation of Simulation] --> S1[CFD Limitations] R --> S2[Scale Model Limitations] R --> S3[Full Scale Limitations] S1 --> S11[Turbulence Model
RANS under predicts
separation] S1 --> S12[Mesh Induced
Drag Errors] S1 --> S13[Boundary Condition
Yaw Sweep Gaps] S1 --> S14[CPU Cost of
DES and LES] S2 --> S21[Reynolds Number
Under Matched] S2 --> S22[Surface Roughness
Not Scaled] S2 --> S23[Gap and Joint
Detail Lost] S2 --> S24[Blockage and
Support Sting] S2 --> S25[Ground BL
Froude Mismatch] S3 --> S31[Drive Power
MW Class] S3 --> S32[Building Footprint
greater than 50m long] S3 --> S33[Moving Belt
Heat and Wear] S3 --> S34[Yaw Mechanism
Cost and Stiffness] ``` ## 4.4 The Re-Scaling Stress — Cause-Effect Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Choose Scale 1 colon 4] --> P2[L model is L over 4] P2 --> P3[Re Match requires V m greater than 4V fs] P3 --> P4[Mach approaches 0.35] P4 --> P5[Compressibility distorts Cd] P3 --> P6[Power rises 4x] P6 --> P7[Fan Motor Saturated] P3 --> P8[Roughness k s scales to k s over 4] P8 --> P9[BL Transition Premature] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Style] **"State three major limitations of computational simulation (CFD) in vehicle aerodynamics."** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** 1. **Turbulence-model dependence:** Standard RANS models (k–ε, k–ω SST) fail to capture large-scale unsteady separation that dominates the wake of a bluff vehicle. DES/LES are accurate but prohibitively expensive for full-car meshes. 2. **Mesh-induced drag error:** Even a fine wall-resolved mesh can introduce 2–5 % error in $C_D$ due to prism-layer stretching and far-field boundary placement. 3. **Boundary-condition gaps:** True road turbulence, yaw-sweep angle, and transient cross-wind gusts are simplified to idealised uniform inlets, missing the real unsteadiness that influences the side-mirror and A-pillar flow. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often answer only with "CFD is slow." That is not a *physical* limitation. Always tie the answer to a *modelling assumption* (e.g. turbulence closure, transition model, or wall-function treatment). ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style] **"Define blockage ratio. Why must it be kept below 10 % in a scale-model test?"** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** The **blockage ratio** is defined as the ratio of the model's maximum frontal area to the test-section cross-sectional area: $$\varepsilon = \frac{A_{model}}{A_{test section}}$$ It must be kept below **10 %** because the model's presence accelerates the flow locally (solid blockage) and modifies the static-pressure field (wake blockage). Both effects *artificially raise* the measured drag coefficient by an amount $\Delta C_D \approx 2\varepsilon$ (Maskell). Above 10 % the corrections become non-linear and unreliable, and the flow around the model may even exhibit **constriction choking** for very bluff bodies. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question has *internal choice* — the student answers *either* Q(A) *or* Q(B). Each carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each. We model both alternatives below. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Limitation of Simulation & Scale-Model Stress #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **"Explain with neat sketches the limitations of scale-model wind-tunnel testing of road vehicles. Discuss the Reynolds-number scaling problem in detail."** (7 Marks) **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** 1. **Geometric scaling failure (1 Mark):** Panel gaps, mirror stalks, door handles and surface paint roughness cannot be linearly scaled. The relative roughness $k_s/L$ becomes smaller, artificially *delaying* boundary-layer transition. 2. **Reynolds-number mismatch (2 Marks):** For a 1:4 model, $Re_m = Re_{fs}/4$. At $V_m = 30$ m/s, $Re_m \approx 2.5 \times 10^6$ instead of $10^7$. This causes $C_D$ to be under-predicted by 5–10 % because the *real* turbulent boundary layer is thicker and the wake is wider. 3. **Drive-power consequences (1 Mark):** To match Re we would need $V_m = 120$ m/s, $M \approx 0.35$, demanding $\sim 4\times$ the shaft power of a full-scale facility — economically absurd. 4. **Blockage and support interference (1 Mark):** The strut and balance contribute 2–5 % of the drag reading; wall proximity accelerates the flow and must be corrected via Maskell or similar. 5. **Ground simulation (1 Mark):** A moving belt with $V_b = V$ can match the *no-slip* condition, but the boundary-layer growth is governed by the Froude number, which is not preserved. 6. **Concluding remark (1 Mark):** Hence scale models are excellent for *trends* but inadequate for *absolute* drag certification. > [!WARNING] > **Common Loss of Marks:** Students forget to mention **wall-interference / blockage correction** explicitly. Just stating "the model is too small" loses 1–2 marks. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **"A 1:5 scale model of a car is tested in a wind tunnel at 40 m/s. The full-scale car is 4.5 m long and travels at 30 m/s. Calculate (i) the Reynolds number of the model and the prototype, (ii) the speed required on the model to match the prototype Re, and (iii) comment on the practicality."** (7 Marks) **Given:** - Scale ratio $\lambda = 1/5$, so $L_m = 0.9$ m - $V_m = 40$ m/s, $V_{fs} = 30$ m/s, $L_{fs} = 4.5$ m - $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s (air) **(i) Reynolds numbers (3 Marks):** $$Re_{fs} = \frac{V_{fs} L_{fs}}{\nu} = \frac{30 \times 4.5}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$\begin{aligned} Re_{fs} &= \frac{135}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.0 \times 10^6 \\ Re_m &= \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu} = \frac{40 \times 0.9}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{36}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.4 \times 10^6 \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Required model speed for Re match (2 Marks):** $$V_m^{*} = \frac{V_{fs}}{\lambda} = 30 \times 5 = 150 \text{ m/s}$$ **(iii) Practical comment (2 Marks):** $M = 150/343 = 0.437$, which is well into the *transonic* regime. Compressibility and shock-like losses on the model's bluff after-body would completely alter the pressure distribution. The model is therefore tested at a *lower* Re and the data extrapolated to full scale using a $C_D$–Re correlation curve. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Full-Scale Wind Tunnels #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **"What is a full-scale wind tunnel? List its advantages over a scale-model tunnel. Sketch the major systems of a typical full-scale automotive wind tunnel."** (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** A **full-scale wind tunnel** is a closed-circuit or open-return facility whose test section is large enough (typically **4 m to 13 m** in cross-section) to accept the *real production road vehicle* without geometric scaling. **Advantages over scale models (4 Marks):** 1. **True Reynolds number** is achieved: $Re \approx 10^7$ for a normal saloon. 2. **True surface details:** paint roughness, panel gaps, mirror stalks, underbody components — all present at the correct scale. 3. **Real boundary-layer transition** and real wake structure. 4. **Direct measurement of absolute $C_D$** with no extrapolation needed. 5. **Aero-acoustic, water-management, and thermal** tests can be performed on the actual car. 6. **Yaw and cross-wind** sensitivity can be quantified with full-scale sensors and wheels. **Major systems of a full-scale facility (3 Marks):** 1. **Inlet bellmouth + contraction** (area ratio ≈ 9:1) for uniform flow. 2. **Test section** with **moving ground belt** (5-roll system) to simulate the road. 3. **6-component force balance** beneath the vehicle. 4. **Yaw table** (turntable) for ±30° cross-flow testing. 5. **Collector-diffuser + drive fans** (often 2-stage, variable pitch) of MW-class power. 6. **Acoustic / PIV / pressure** instrumentation. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **"A full-scale wind tunnel has a circular test section of diameter 6 m and a free-stream velocity of 35 m/s. The fan efficiency is 70 %. Estimate (i) the ideal aerodynamic power, (ii) the brake power at the motor shaft, and (iii) the cost of one 8-hour test at ₹8 per kWh."** (7 Marks) **Given:** $D = 6$ m, $V = 35$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\eta_{fan} = 0.70$, $t = 8$ h, rate = ₹8/kWh. **(i) Ideal aerodynamic power (3 Marks):** $$P_{ideal} = \frac{\pi}{8}\rho D^2 V^3$$ $$\begin{aligned} P_{ideal} &= \frac{\pi}{8} \times 1.225 \times 36 \times 42\,875 \\ &= 0.3927 \times 1.225 \times 36 \times 42\,875 \\ &= 0.3927 \times 1.890 \times 10^6 \\ &\approx 0.742 \text{ MW} \\ &\approx 742 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Brake (shaft) power (2 Marks):** $$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{ideal}}{\eta} = \frac{742}{0.70} \approx 1\,060 \text{ kW} \approx 1.06 \text{ MW}$$ **(iii) Cost of 8-hour run (2 Marks):** $$\text{Energy} = 1\,060 \text{ kW} \times 8 \text{ h} = 8\,480 \text{ kWh}$$ $$\text{Cost} = 8\,480 \times 8 = ₹67\,840$$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — must memorise before the KTU exam:** - ☐ **Reynolds number** for a real car at highway speed is **~10⁷** (≈ 10 million). - ☐ A 1:4 model reaches only **Re/4** unless the wind speed is quadrupled. - ☐ Matching Re on the model requires $V_m = V_{fs}/\lambda$ and a **compressor-class fan** ($M > 0.3$ risk). - ☐ Drive power scales as $P \propto \rho D^2 V^3$ — the **V³** term is the killer. - ☐ Blockage ratio $\varepsilon = A_f/A_t$ must stay **< 5–10 %**; correction $\Delta C_D \approx 2\varepsilon$. - ☐ **Froude number** matters only when a moving-belt ground simulation is used. - ☐ **Surface roughness** $k_s$ must be scaled: $k_{s,m} = k_{s,fs}/\lambda$. Real production paint is *not* smooth. - ☐ **Full-scale wind tunnels** are 4–13 m in diameter, 50–100 m long, and consume **0.5–1.5 MW**. - ☐ Famous full-scale facilities: **Pininfarina (11 m × 22 m)**, **BMW (5 m)**, **Ford (5 m)**, **Toyota (13 m)**, **Volkswagen (10 m × 22 m)**. - ☐ **Maskell wall correction** formula: $\Delta C_D \approx 2\varepsilon$ is a 1-liner worth 2 marks in ESE. - ☐ CFD limitations: RANS under-predicts separation, DES/LES are too expensive, yaw transient hard to model. - ☐ In KTU answers, always state the **equation**, **substitute the numbers**, and give the **answer with units** — three things, three marks. - ☐ Do **not** say "scale model gives wrong answer." Say "scale model under-predicts $C_D$ by 5–10 % because of Re under-match and roughness scale-down." --- > **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Limitation of Simulation, Scale-Model Stress & Full-Scale Wind Tunnels** > *Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education framework and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1–L4 coverage).*

topics.title: Measurement Techniques - Equipment and Transducers - Road Testing Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Measurement Techniques - Equipment and Transducers - Road Testing Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Vehicle Aerodynamics (PEAUT752)**, *Measurement Techniques* refer to the systematic, calibrated, and traceable experimental methodologies used to quantify aerodynamic phenomena acting on a ground vehicle — namely pressure distributions, aerodynamic forces and moments, velocity fields, and flow visualization patterns. These techniques are categorically divided into two parallel experimental domains:

1. **Wind-Tunnel (Bench) Measurement Techniques** — controlled laboratory conditions using scaled or full-scale models in a simulated airstream.
2. **Road-Testing (On-Road) Methods** — uncontrolled, real-world, in-situ measurements performed on the actual vehicle moving through the ambient atmosphere.

A **Transducer**, as per the KTU 2024 instrumentation framework, is a device that converts one form of physical energy (mechanical pressure, force, displacement) into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, frequency), enabling digital acquisition and post-processing. In vehicle aerodynamics, the most frequently encountered transducers are **pressure transducers, strain-gauge force balances, piezoelectric sensors, and thermal anemometers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 demands familiarity with BOTH controlled (wind tunnel) and uncontrolled (road test) environments. Students are expected to identify the type of transducer best suited for a given measurement task and articulate its operating principle, sensitivity, and limitations.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a cardiologist examining a patient. The cardiologist uses a **stethoscope** (transducer) to listen to the heart, a **blood-pressure cuff** (force balance) to measure force, and **blood reports** (data acquisition) to diagnose. The patient cannot be taken apart — the doctor must use external instruments to infer internal conditions.

A vehicle aerodynamicist does exactly the same thing:

- The **car is the patient**.
- The **wind tunnel is the clinical lab** — clean, controlled, repeatable.
- **Pressure taps drilled on the body** are the "blood samples."
- **Force balances** under the wheels measure the "heartbeat" of drag and lift.
- **Smoke or tufts** reveal "blockages" (separated flow) the way an X-ray reveals fractures.
- **Road testing** is the field check — the doctor visits the patient at home, where conditions are messy, variable, and noisy.

Just as a doctor chooses a stethoscope vs. an MRI based on the diagnostic question, the aerodynamicist chooses a **multi-hole pressure probe** vs. a **six-component balance** based on whether the goal is surface mapping or total force resolution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Measurement Matters:** Without validated measurement, Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) remains a conjecture. Wind-tunnel and road-test data form the **empirical ground truth** against which every CFD simulation must be benchmarked before it can be trusted for production design.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Measurement Equipment — Overview Map

| Category | Typical Device | Physical Variable Measured | Working Principle |
|----------|---------------|----------------------------|-------------------|
| Pressure Transducers | Pitot-static tube, Scanivalve | Static / Total Pressure | Differential pressure → strain gauge / piezo |
| Force Transducers | Six-component balance | Aerodynamic forces & moments | Strain gauge on flexure beams |
| Velocity Transducers | Hot-wire / Hot-film probe | Local flow velocity | Heat-loss → King's Law |
| Velocity (Optical) | LDV, PIV | Velocity field | Doppler shift / particle tracking |
| Visualization | Smoke generator, Tufts, Schlieren | Flow topology, separation | Visual contrast |
| Road-Test Specific | GPS, OBD-II, Coastdown logger | Speed decay, yaw angle, fuel rate | Kinematic & time-based |

---

### 1.4 Standard Reference Quantities (SI Units Used Throughout KTU)

- Air density at standard conditions: **ρ = 1.225 kg/m³**
- Dynamic viscosity at 15 °C: **μ = 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s**
- Speed of sound at 15 °C: **a = 340.3 m/s**
- Reference dynamic pressure: **q∞ = ½ρV²** (Pa)
- Reference area for cars: **frontal projected area A** (m²)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pressure Measurement — Operational Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Sensing element:** A diaphragm deflects under differential pressure between the test point and a reference.

**Step 2 — Strain conversion:** Deflection is read by bonded strain gauges arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge**. The bridge converts mechanical strain into an electrical potential difference:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex}\cdot \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Piezoelectric alternative (for fluctuating pressures):** Piezoelectric crystals (e.g., quartz) generate a charge $Q$ proportional to applied force $F$. Because static calibration is impossible (charge leaks), piezoelectric transducers are used **only for fluctuating pressures** (e.g., wake unsteadiness, cavity tones).

**Step 4 — Signal conditioning:** Amplification, anti-alias filtering at the Nyquist rate $f_{Nyq} = f_s/2$, and A/D conversion.

**Step 5 — Multi-channel pressure scanning:** Modern wind tunnels use **electronically scanned pressure (ESP) modules** (e.g., Scanivalve DSA 3217) capable of sampling up to 512 channels at rates exceeding 100 kHz.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use ESP modules?** They replace tedious manual manometer reading, eliminate human parallax error, and capture unsteady pressure fluctuations during yawed runs. They are standard equipment in KTU-referenced automotive wind tunnels such as the MIRA Full-Scale Wind Tunnel.

---

### 2.2 Force Measurement — Strain-Gauge Balance Theory

A **force balance** is the single most critical instrument in vehicle aerodynamic testing because it directly yields **CD, CL, CM** without needing pressure integration.

**Operational principle:**
- The model is mounted on a **rigid support** connected to the balance through precisely machined flexures.
- External aerodynamic loads (drag, lift, side-force) cause **micro-strain** in the flexures.
- Strain gauges bonded to the flexures measure strain $\varepsilon$.
- Through Hooke's law and geometry, strain is converted to force.

**The Six-Component Balance:**
A production-grade automotive balance resolves the **complete aerodynamic load vector** on the vehicle:

$$F_{total} = F_{x}\hat{i} + F_{y}\hat{j} + F_{z}\hat{k}$$
$$M_{total} = M_{x}\hat{i} + M_{y}\hat{j} + M_{z}\hat{k}$$

| Component | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|--------|------------------|
| Drag | $F_x$ | Streamwise resistance force |
| Side force | $F_y$ | Lateral force in crosswind |
| Lift | $F_z$ | Vertical force (positive upward) |
| Roll moment | $M_x$ | About longitudinal axis |
| Pitch moment | $M_y$ | About lateral axis |
| Yaw moment | $M_z$ | About vertical axis |

**Sensitivity Note:** A typical KTU-spec balance resolution is **~0.1 N** at full-scale loads of 5 kN — sufficient to detect a Cd change of **0.0005** at 130 km/h.

---

### 2.3 Velocity Measurement — Thermal Anemometry (Hot-Wire)

The **Constant-Temperature Anemometer (CTA)** is the workhorse for **turbulence measurement**. Its operation rests on **King's Law**:

$$E^{2} = A + B\cdot U^{n}$$

where:
- $E$ = bridge output voltage (V)
- $U$ = local flow velocity (m/s)
- $A, B, n$ = empirically fitted calibration constants
- Typical $n \approx 0.45$ for air

**Why hot-wire?** Its frequency response exceeds **100 kHz**, enabling resolution of turbulent eddies down to the Kolmogorov microscale. However, it is **fragile** and breaks on impact with rain or debris — hence road tests use the more rugged **hot-film** variant.

---

### 2.4 Velocity Measurement — Optical Methods

**Laser Doppler Velocimetry (LDV):** Two intersecting coherent beams produce a **fringe pattern** in the measurement volume. Particles crossing the fringes scatter light at a frequency $f_{Doppler}$ proportional to velocity:

$$U = \dfrac{f_{Doppler}\cdot \lambda}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}$$

where $\lambda$ = laser wavelength and $\theta$ = half-angle between beams. LDV is **non-intrusive**, single-point, and gives velocity magnitude — but is **direction-ambiguous** (resolved via frequency shifting) and gives no pressure or force directly.

**Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV):** A planar technique that images seed particles twice in rapid succession, then cross-correlates the two images to yield the **2-D or 3-D velocity vector field** across a plane. Modern **Time-Resolved Stereo-PIV (TR-SPIV)** captures the wake of full-scale vehicles at 1 kHz+ rates.

---

### 2.5 Road Testing Methods — Operational Framework

Road testing is fundamentally different from wind tunnel testing:

| Aspect | Wind Tunnel | Road Test |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| Reynolds number | Matched by fan power | Inherently correct (real scale, real speed) |
| Boundary layer | Artificial (ground plane) | Real (moving ground) |
| Atmospheric stability | Controlled | Variable (wind, rain, temperature) |
| Cost per data point | High (model build, tunnel time) | Low (production car) |
| Repeatability | Excellent | Requires statistical averaging |
| Yaw capability | Turntable or moving belt | Natural crosswinds |

**Principal Road Test Methods for Vehicle Aerodynamics:**

1. **Coastdown Test** — vehicle allowed to decelerate from a known speed; the rate of speed decay gives the road-load equation (rolling resistance + aerodynamic drag + drivetrain losses).
2. **On-Road Constant-Speed Test** — fuel consumption measured at constant speed; aerodynamic portion extracted by subtraction of mechanical losses.
3. **Plume Chasing (or Tracer Gas Method)** — release of CO₂ or smoke from a moving vehicle to map the wake in still air.
4. **On-Board Pressure Scanning** — miniature ESP modules mounted inside a moving test car with pneumatic tubes to surface taps.
5. **GPS-Based Yaw-Angle Logging** — record instantaneous heading, speed, and GPS-derived crosswind component; correlate with measured fuel rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common-Pitfall:** Students often assume road tests yield *true* aerodynamic drag. In reality, **wind variability, turbulence intensity, and yaw angle fluctuations** mean road-test drag values must be corrected using the **standard deviation of yaw angle** and a yaw-sensitivity coefficient (typically $\partial C_D / \partial \beta \approx 0.0001$ to $0.0003$ per degree).

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Equation | Name | Variables | Units | KTU Use |
|----------|------|-----------|-------|---------|
| $q_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}$ | Dynamic pressure | $\rho, V$ | Pa | Reference pressure, $C_p$ normalization |
| $C_{p} = \dfrac{p - p_{\infty}}{q_{\infty}}$ | Pressure coefficient | $p$ | dimensionless | Pressure tap data reduction |
| $C_{D} = \dfrac{F_x}{q_{\infty} A}$ | Drag coefficient | $F_x, A$ | dimensionless | Balance output |
| $C_{L} = \dfrac{F_z}{q_{\infty} A}$ | Lift coefficient | $F_z$ | dimensionless | Balance output |
| $E^{2} = A + B U^{n}$ | King's Law | $U$ | V, m/s | Hot-wire calibration |
| $U = \dfrac{f_{Doppler}\lambda}{2\sin(\theta/2)}$ | LDV velocity | $f, \lambda$ | m/s | Optical velocity |
| $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = -\dfrac{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V^{2} + C_{r}mg}{m + I/r^{2}}$ | Coastdown ODE | $V, t$ | m/s² | Road test drag extraction |
| $\text{Re}_L = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | $L$ | dimensionless | Similarity check |
| $f_{Nyq} = f_s/2$ | Nyquist rate | $f_s$ | Hz | Sampling rule |
| $\beta = \arctan(V_{cross}/V_{long})$ | Yaw angle | $V$ | rad/deg | Road-test crosswind correction |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown-Syntax Safeguard Applied:** The vertical pipe character `|` inside formulas has been deliberately replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve table integrity during PDF/Markdown conversion.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Formula 1 teams** use full-scale wind tunnels with **moving ground planes** + **rotating wheels** (e.g., Toyota Cologne, Sauber Wind-tech) — measurement fidelity is so high that wind-tunnel drag measurements now agree with track-based GPS coastdown within **±1.5 %**.
- **Electric-vehicle manufacturers** rely heavily on coastdown testing to certify **WLTP range** because aerodynamic drag dominates above 80 km/h.
- **Trucks and Class-8 vehicles** use **side-force balance measurements** for crosswind stability — critical for high-profile box trucks.
- **Aircraft/aerospace applications** of the same balances (with much higher load capacities) validate CFD for wing design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pitot-Static Tube Relation to Velocity

A **pitot-static tube** is a concentric dual-tube device. The inner tube senses **stagnation pressure** $p_0$, and the outer static ports sense the **local static pressure** $p$. The pressure differential is **dynamic pressure** $q$.

**Start:** Apply Bernoulli along a streamline between the freestream and the stagnation point:

$$p_{\infty} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2} = p_0$$

**Rearrange:**

$$p_0 - p_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}$$

**Identify the differential:**

$$\Delta p = p_0 - p = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}$$

**Solve for velocity:**

$$V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\Delta p}{\rho}}$$

This is the foundational relation KTU examiners use to test student understanding of why pitot tubes work in wind tunnels *and* in road-test data acquisition (when sensing flow velocity on a probe mounted ahead of a moving car).

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Correction:** Real pitot tubes require a calibration coefficient $C_p^{pt} \approx 0.98$–$1.02$ to account for viscous effects and finite geometry. The corrected velocity becomes $V_{true} = C_p^{pt} \sqrt{2\Delta p/\rho}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hot-Wire Calibration via King's Law (Step-by-Step)

**Goal:** Convert measured bridge voltage $E$ into velocity $U$.

**Step 1 — Set up the calibration equation (King's Law form):**

$$E^{2} = A + B \cdot U^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Take natural log of both sides:**

$$\ln(E^{2} - A) = \ln(B) + n \ln(U)$$

**Step 3 — Linearise as $y = m x + c$:**

Let $y = \ln(E^{2} - A)$, $x = \ln U$. Then a least-squares fit of calibration data points $(U_i, E_i)$ yields slope $m = n$ and intercept $c = \ln B$.

**Step 4 — Solve calibration constants numerically:**

Suppose a CTA is calibrated at three reference velocities in a wind tunnel:

| $U$ (m/s) | $E$ (V) |
|-----------|---------|
| 5.0       | 2.31    |
| 15.0      | 3.78    |
| 30.0      | 4.92    |

Assume $A = 1.0$ (offset from zero-flow voltage). Then:

$$y_1 = \ln(2.31^{2} - 1.0) = \ln(4.336) = 1.467$$
$$y_2 = \ln(3.78^{2} - 1.0) = \ln(13.288) = 2.587$$
$$y_3 = \ln(4.92^{2} - 1.0) = \ln(23.206) = 3.144$$

$$x_1 = \ln(5.0) = 1.609$$
$$x_2 = \ln(15.0) = 2.708$$
$$x_3 = \ln(30.0) = 3.401$$

**Step 5 — Two-point fit using points 1 and 3:**

$$n = \dfrac{y_3 - y_1}{x_3 - x_1} = \dfrac{3.144 - 1.467}{3.401 - 1.609} = \dfrac{1.677}{1.792} = 0.936$$

$$\ln B = y_3 - n x_3 = 3.144 - (0.936)(3.401) = 3.144 - 3.183 = -0.039$$
$$B = e^{-0.039} = 0.962$$

**Step 6 — Verify with point 2 (predict $\ln(E^{2} - A)$ at $U = 15$):**

$$\hat{y_2} = \ln B + n \ln(15.0) = -0.039 + (0.936)(2.708) = -0.039 + 2.535 = 2.496$$
$$\hat{E_2}^{2} = A + e^{2.496} = 1.0 + 12.131 = 13.131$$
$$\hat{E_2} = 3.624 \text{ V}$$

Measured value was 3.78 V → **error = 4.1 %** — within typical CTA calibration tolerance.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Coastdown Test Drag Extraction

A coastdown test is performed by accelerating the vehicle to a high speed (typically 120 km/h), shifting to neutral, and recording the decay of velocity versus time. The resistive forces are:

$$F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V^{2}$$
$$F_{roll} = C_r m g$$
$$F_{driveline} \approx 0 \quad (\text{neutral gear, modern vehicles})$$

**Newton's second law (translational + rotational inertia):**

$$(m + I_{w}/r^{2})\dfrac{dV}{dt} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V^{2} - C_r m g$$

Let $m_{eff} = m + I_{w}/r^{2}$. Rearrange:

$$\dfrac{dV}{dt} = -\dfrac{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A}{m_{eff}} V^{2} - \dfrac{C_r m g}{m_{eff}}$$

**Integrate** between two time instances $t_1$ and $t_2$ with corresponding velocities $V_1$ and $V_2$:

**Closed-form solution (valid for constant $C_D$ and $C_r$):**

$$\dfrac{1}{V_2} - \dfrac{1}{V_1} = \dfrac{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A}{m_{eff}}(t_2 - t_1) \cdot \dfrac{1}{V_1 V_2} \quad \text{(approximate)}$$

A more common engineering formulation uses **two-speed method (SAE J1263 + J2263)**:

For each coastdown run, fit the deceleration curve to obtain **A, B, C** road-load coefficients in:

$$F_{resist} = A + B V + C V^{2}$$

The $C V^{2}$ term maps directly to the aerodynamic drag:

$$C = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A$$

Therefore:

$$C_D = \dfrac{2C}{\rho A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this derivation is high-yield at KTU:** Examiners love coastdown because it combines kinematics, fluid mechanics, and instrumentation into a single closed-loop problem. They often provide fictitious A, B, C coefficients and ask students to back-calculate $C_D$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pressure Coefficient from Wind-Tunnel Tap Data

Given a pressure tap reading $p_{tap}$ measured via a scanivalve with reference to the tunnel settling-chamber static pressure $p_{\infty}$, and freestream dynamic pressure $q_{\infty}$ measured by a pitot-static probe in the test section:

**Step 1:** Convert raw transducer voltage to pressure via the **calibration certificate**:

$$p = a_{0} + a_{1} V + a_{2} V^{2}$$

(typically linear; second-order coefficient often negligible.)

**Step 2:** Compute $C_p$ directly:

$$C_p = \dfrac{p_{tap} - p_{\infty}}{q_{\infty}}$$

**Step 3:** Uncertainty propagation (essential in KTU viva):

$$\delta C_p = C_p \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\delta q}{q}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{\delta p_{tap}}{p_{tap} - p_{\infty}}\right)^{2}}$$

For typical wind-tunnel runs, $\delta C_p \approx \pm 0.005$ — small but non-trivial when integrating $C_p$ over a curved surface to recover $C_D$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensor Selection Decision Tree

| Measurement Goal | Best Sensor | Why | Limitation |
|-----------------|-------------|-----|-----------|
| Steady surface pressure map | ESP / scanivalve | High channel count, low cost per channel | ~1 kHz bandwidth |
| Unsteady wake pressure | Piezoelectric transducer | High frequency response | No DC (static) response |
| Total vehicle drag | Six-component balance | Direct force, no integration error | Requires careful installation |
| Local turbulence spectrum | Hot-wire CTA | Sub-millisecond response | Fragile, calibration drift |
| Planar velocity field | PIV | Whole-field, non-intrusive | Expensive, requires seeding |
| Field-route drag | Coastdown + GPS | No tunnel needed | Statistical variability |
| On-road yaw stability | GPS + 6-DOF IMU | Captures real crosswind | Sensor fusion complexity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define a pressure transducer and explain with a neat sketch the working of a strain-gauge-based pressure transducer used in vehicle aerodynamic measurements.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **pressure transducer** is a device that converts a fluid pressure input into a proportional electrical output signal. In vehicle aerodynamics, it is used at pressure taps drilled on the body surface.

**Working principle:**
- A **diaphragm** deflects proportionally to the applied differential pressure.
- **Strain gauges** bonded on the diaphragm form the four arms of a **Wheatstone bridge**.
- Deflection changes gauge resistance: $\Delta R / R = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$, where $G_F$ is the gauge factor (~2.0 for constantan).
- The bridge produces an output voltage proportional to the pressure.
- Output is amplified, filtered, and digitised.

**Sketch (3 marks requires labelled diagram — describe):**

```
        ┌─────────────┐
Pressure→│  Diaphragm  │→ Differential Pressure
        │  with Strain │
        │   Gauges     │
        └──────┬──────┘
               │ Wheatstone Bridge
               ▼
         Amplifier + Filter
               │
               ▼
           DAQ Output
```

**Mark distribution:** 1 mark definition + 1 mark working + 1 mark sketch.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three differences between wind-tunnel testing and road-testing methods for vehicle aerodynamic drag measurement.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each for three differences):**

| S. No. | Aspect | Wind Tunnel | Road Test |
|--------|--------|-------------|-----------|
| 1 | Reynolds number | Matched via fan power / pressure | Inherently real-scale |
| 2 | Repeatability | Excellent (controlled) | Statistical (variable wind) |
| 3 | Cost per data point | High (model build, tunnel time) | Low (production car) |
| 4 | Ground boundary layer | Artificial (boundary-layer suction, moving belt) | Real (moving ground) |
| 5 | Yaw control | Turntable or yaw table | Passive (crosswind variability) |

Any **three valid differences** carry 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the working of a **six-component strain-gauge balance** used in a vehicle wind tunnel. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A passenger car of mass $m = 1500$ kg and effective inertia $m_{eff} = 1620$ kg (including rotating masses) is subjected to a coastdown test from 150 km/h. The deceleration curve, after correction for rolling resistance, yields the following velocity-time pairs:

| Time (s) | Velocity (km/h) | Velocity (m/s) |
|----------|-----------------|----------------|
| 0        | 150             | 41.67          |
| 30       | 130             | 36.11          |
| 60       | 113             | 31.39          |
| 90       | 98              | 27.22          |

The frontal area of the car is $A = 2.2$ m² and air density is $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. Determine the **drag coefficient** $C_D$. *(7 marks)*

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Six-component balance (7 marks):**

A six-component balance simultaneously measures the three force components ($F_x, F_y, F_z$) and three moment components ($M_x, M_y, M_z$) acting on the vehicle. The balance consists of an **upper flexure block**, a **middle flexure block** (instrumented with strain gauges), and a **lower foundation block** anchored to the tunnel floor.

**Working:**
1. Aerodynamic loads applied at the model support point deform the flexures.
2. Each flexure has **strain gauges bonded** in specific orientations.
3. Strain gauges arranged in **Wheatstone bridges** convert deformation to voltage.
4. Through **calibration matrices**, voltage readings are converted to forces and moments.
5. The balance output is **six simultaneous channels** corresponding to $F_x, F_y, F_z, M_x, M_y, M_z$.

**Block diagram (describe):**

```
     Vehicle Model
          │
          ▼
   ┌──────────────┐
   │ Load Point   │
   └──────┬───────┘
          │
   ┌──────▼───────┐    Strain gauges on
   │ Upper Flexure│    flexures in
   │ Block        │    Wheatstone bridges
   └──────┬───────┘
          │
   ┌──────▼───────┐    Six independent
   │ Middle Block │ → calibrated channels
   │ (instrumented│    of force/moment
   └──────┬───────┘
          │
   ┌──────▼───────┐
   │ Foundation   │    Anchored to tunnel
   │ Block        │    floor/turntable
   └──────────────┘
          │
          ▼
    Amplifier → DAQ → Computer
```

**Mark distribution:**
- 2 marks — labelled block diagram
- 2 marks — flexure / strain gauge principle
- 2 marks — six-channel calibration matrix
- 1 mark — real-world example

---

**(b) Coastdown drag extraction (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert velocities to m/s (already done in table).**

**Step 2 — Compute average deceleration between consecutive points:**

$$a_{0-30} = \dfrac{V_0 - V_{30}}{30} = \dfrac{41.67 - 36.11}{30} = 0.1853 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$a_{30-60} = \dfrac{36.11 - 31.39}{30} = 0.1573 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$a_{60-90} = \dfrac{31.39 - 27.22}{30} = 0.1390 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute average velocity for each segment:**

$$\bar{V}_{0-30} = \dfrac{41.67 + 36.11}{2} = 38.89 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\bar{V}_{30-60} = \dfrac{36.11 + 31.39}{2} = 33.75 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\bar{V}_{60-90} = \dfrac{31.39 + 27.22}{2} = 29.31 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Use Newton's second law. Since rolling resistance has been corrected for, the decelerating force is purely aerodynamic:**

$$m_{eff} \cdot a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V^{2}$$

**Solving for $C_D$:**

$$C_D = \dfrac{2 m_{eff} a}{\rho A V^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate for each segment:**

**Segment 1:**

$$C_D = \dfrac{2 \cdot 1620 \cdot 0.1853}{1.225 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (38.89)^{2}}$$

Numerator: $2 \cdot 1620 \cdot 0.1853 = 600.37$ N

Denominator: $1.225 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 1512.4 = 4074.6$ N

$$C_{D,1} = \dfrac{600.37}{4074.6} = 0.1473$$

**Segment 2:**

$$C_D = \dfrac{2 \cdot 1620 \cdot 0.1573}{1.225 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (33.75)^{2}}$$

Numerator: $2 \cdot 1620 \cdot 0.1573 = 509.65$ N

Denominator: $1.225 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 1139.06 = 3069.0$ N

$$C_{D,2} = \dfrac{509.65}{3069.0} = 0.1661$$

**Segment 3:**

$$C_D = \dfrac{2 \cdot 1620 \cdot 0.1390}{1.225 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (29.31)^{2}}$$

Numerator: $2 \cdot 1620 \cdot 0.1390 = 450.36$ N

Denominator: $1.225 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 859.08 = 2314.1$ N

$$C_{D,3} = \dfrac{450.36}{2314.1} = 0.1946$$

**Step 6 — Realisation of inconsistency:**

The three values differ because of **neglected rolling resistance**. The variation is the giveaway that rolling-resistance correction was incomplete. KTU expects the student to note this and use either the high-speed segment (least RR contribution) or a least-squares fit.

**Step 7 — Engineering judgment answer:**

Best estimate using highest-speed segment (least rolling-resistance contamination):

$$\boxed{C_D \approx 0.15}$$

**Mark distribution:**
- 2 marks — setting up the force balance equation
- 3 marks — correct substitution and numerical evaluation
- 1 mark — recognition of segment dependence
- 1 mark — final answer with engineering interpretation

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** With relevant diagrams, explain the working of a **Constant-Temperature Hot-Wire Anemometer (CTA)**. Show how King's Law is used for calibration. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A wind-tunnel test on a 1:5 scale model of a car is conducted at a freestream velocity of 60 m/s. The full-scale vehicle has a length of 4.5 m. Atmospheric conditions are $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. Determine:
   (i) The model-scale Reynolds number. *(2 marks)*
   (ii) Whether the test satisfies Reynolds-number similarity with the full-scale vehicle at 120 km/h. *(2 marks)*
   (iii) The required tunnel speed if the test is to be conducted at the same Reynolds number as the full-scale car at 200 km/h. *(3 marks)*

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) CTA working + King's Law (7 marks):**

A **Constant-Temperature Anemometer (CTA)** is a thermal anemometer where a fine wire (typically 5 µm diameter tungsten or platinum) is heated by an electric current and exposed to the flow. The wire's **resistance is held constant** by an electronic feedback loop (Wheatstone bridge with amplifier), so the **voltage across the wire is the measured variable**.

**Operating principle:**
- The wire is heated to ~250 °C above ambient.
- Flowing fluid **cools** the wire by forced convection.
- To maintain constant temperature (resistance), the bridge increases voltage $E$.
- $E$ is monotonically related to flow velocity $U$.

**King's Law (calibration):**

$$E^{2} = A + B U^{n}$$

**Calibration procedure (describe with diagram):**

1. Mount the probe in a reference wind tunnel.
2. Record $E$ at a series of known velocities $U$ (e.g., 5, 10, 20, 40, 60 m/s).
3. Apply least-squares fit to determine constants $A, B, n$.
4. Validate against an independent reference (e.g., pitot tube).

**Block diagram (describe):**

```
   Reference Tunnel Flow
            │
            ▼
   ┌────────────────┐
   │ Hot-Wire Probe │ ← Heated Wire
   └────────┬───────┘
            │ Resistance R(T)
            ▼
   ┌────────────────────┐
   │ Wheatstone Bridge  │ ← with feedback op-amp
   │ + Amplifier        │   (constant T control)
   └────────┬───────────┘
            │ Output E (V)
            ▼
   ┌────────────────────┐
   │ A/D + King's Law   │ → Convert to U (m/s)
   │ Conversion         │
   └────────────────────┘
```

**Mark distribution:**
- 2 marks — principle
- 2 marks — King's Law and calibration
- 2 marks — block diagram
- 1 mark — limitations (fragile, calibration drift, direction ambiguity)

---

**(b) Reynolds-number similarity (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Scale ratio = 1:5, so model length $L_m = 4.5 / 5 = 0.9$ m.
- Tunnel velocity $V_m = 60$ m/s.
- Full-scale test speed at 120 km/h: $V_p = 120 \cdot (1000/3600) = 33.33$ m/s.
- Full-scale test speed at 200 km/h: $V_p = 200 \cdot (1000/3600) = 55.56$ m/s.
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s.

**(i) Model Reynolds number (2 marks):**

$$Re_m = \dfrac{\rho V_m L_m}{\mu} = \dfrac{1.225 \cdot 60 \cdot 0.9}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}}$$

Numerator: $1.225 \cdot 60 \cdot 0.9 = 66.15$ kg/(m·s)

$$Re_m = \dfrac{66.15}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.65 \times 10^{6}$$

$$\boxed{Re_m \approx 3.65 \times 10^{6}}$$

**(ii) Similarity check at 120 km/h (2 marks):**

Full-scale $Re_p$ at 120 km/h:

$$Re_p = \dfrac{\rho V_p L_p}{\mu} = \dfrac{1.225 \cdot 33.33 \cdot 4.5}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}}$$

Numerator: $1.225 \cdot 33.33 \cdot 4.5 = 183.74$ kg/(m·s)

$$Re_p = \dfrac{183.74}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.015 \times 10^{7}$$

**Comparison:**

$$\dfrac{Re_m}{Re_p} = \dfrac{3.65 \times 10^{6}}{1.015 \times 10^{7}} \approx 0.36$$

The model Reynolds number is **only 36 % of the full-scale value**. The test **does not satisfy Reynolds-number similarity** — it under-predicts by a factor of ~2.8.

**Critical Reynolds number for cars is typically around $Re \approx 3 \times 10^{6}$**, where boundary-layer transition occurs. Therefore the model test may exhibit **laminar separation** that would not occur at full scale, leading to erroneous drag readings.

$$\boxed{\text{Test does NOT satisfy Reynolds similarity.}}$$

**(iii) Required tunnel speed at 200 km/h full-scale (3 marks):**

For similarity, $Re_m = Re_p$:

$$\dfrac{\rho V_m L_m}{\mu} = \dfrac{\rho V_p L_p}{\mu}$$

$$V_m = V_p \cdot \dfrac{L_p}{L_m} = 55.56 \cdot \dfrac{4.5}{0.9} = 55.56 \cdot 5 = 277.78 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{V_m \approx 278 \text{ m/s} \; (\approx 1000 \text{ km/h})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical Reality Check:** This speed is supersonic for the car body shape and creates **Mach-number and compressibility distortions** that did not exist in the full-scale flow. Therefore **Reynolds-number similarity at this scale is physically impossible** in a subsonic wind tunnel — a classic KTU discussion point leading into the concept of **boundary-layer tripping** and **cryogenic tunnels**.

**Mark distribution:**
- 2 marks — Re formula and substitution for part (i)
- 2 marks — comparison and judgment in part (ii)
- 2 marks — similarity equation and algebra in part (iii)
- 1 mark — engineering reality / Mach-number caveat

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Measurement Techniques — Module 4:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units:** $C_p$ is dimensionless, but you must show the numerator and denominator in **Pa / Pa** form. Marks are deducted if units are dropped mid-calculation.
>
> 2. **Confusing pressure coefficient with pressure:** A common error is writing "C_p = 0.4 Pa." It is **dimensionless**.
>
> 3. **Neglecting the calibration coefficient of pitot tubes:** Real pitot tubes do not give exact stagnation pressure; introducing $C_p^{pt}$ is a **board-exam bonus point**.
>
> 4. **Mixing up balance axes:** $F_x$ is **drag**, $F_z$ is **lift**. The KTU examiner expects the student to use the **vehicle coordinate system** consistently (x = forward, z = upward).
>
> 5. **Coastdown sign convention:** The deceleration term is **negative** because velocity is decreasing. Students often drop the minus sign and get physically impossible positive accelerations.
>
> 6. **Hot-wire non-linearity:** King's Law exponent $n$ is **not 0.5**. Forcing $n = 0.5$ (square-root dependence) is a frequent student error and loses 1 mark.
>
> 7. **Road-test data scatter:** Writing $C_D = 0.147$, $C_D = 0.166$, $C_D = 0.195$ without explaining the segment dependence **invites a 2-mark deduction** for lack of engineering judgment.
>
> 8. **Re-similarity overshoot:** Computing a supersonic tunnel speed and **not commenting on Mach-number issues** shows incomplete understanding and loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Pressure tap → scanivalve → Cp:** Standard wind-tunnel surface-pressure workflow.
- **Force balance direct read:** Six-component balance yields $C_D, C_L, C_{MY}$ in a single measurement without integration.
- **Pitot-static equation:** $V = \sqrt{2\Delta p / \rho}$.
- **King's Law:** $E^2 = A + B U^n$ — calibrate hot-wire, never assume $n = 0.5$.
- **LDV equation:** $U = f_{Doppler} \lambda / [2 \sin(\theta/2)]$ — non-intrusive, single point.
- **PIV:** Planar whole-field velocity; needs seeding, lasers, cameras.
- **Coastdown ODE:** $(m + I_w/r^2) \, dV/dt = -\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V^2 - C_r m g$.
- **Two-speed road-load form:** $F = A + B V + C V^2$; coefficient $C = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A$.
- **Reynolds similarity:** $Re_m = Re_p \Rightarrow V_m = V_p (L_p / L_m)$.
- **Crit-Re for cars:** ~$3 \times 10^6$; below this, laminar separation distorts data.
- **Wind tunnel vs road test:** Tunnel = controlled, repeatable; Road = real-scale, real-ground, statistical.
- **Yaw correction:** Road-test $C_D$ must be corrected for crosswind; $\partial C_D / \partial \beta \approx 10^{-4}$ per degree.
- **Nyquist rule:** Sample at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
- **Wheatstone bridge:** Strain-gauge-based pressure and force measurement; quarter-, half-, full-bridge configurations.
- **Calibration matrix:** Balance voltages → forces/moments through a 6×6 matrix obtained by loading the balance with known weights and moments.
- **Unit consistency:** Always keep $\rho$ in kg/m³, $V$ in m/s, $A$ in m², $\mu$ in Pa·s — KTU examiners **deduct marks for unit errors**.
- **Engineering judgment:** Real data is noisy. Always report a **range or band**, not a single false-precision value.

---

*End of Module 4 — Measurement Techniques, Equipment & Transducers, Road Testing Methods.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Methods - CFD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Numerical Methods - CFD # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** is the branch of fluid mechanics that uses **numerical methods and algorithms** to solve and analyze problems involving fluid flows, governed by the **Navier–Stokes equations**, on a discretized domain using a computer. In the context of **Vehicle Aerodynamics**, Numerical Methods in CFD refer to the mathematical and algorithmic techniques used to convert the **continuous partial differential equations (PDEs)** of fluid motion (conservation of mass, momentum, energy) into a system of **algebraic equations** that can be solved iteratively over a finite number of spatial cells (the **mesh/grid**) representing the vehicle and its surrounding flow field. > [!NOTE] > **Core Idea:** Real vehicles (cars, trucks, F1 cars, aircraft) cannot be solved analytically. The geometry is too complex, the flow is **turbulent**, and the Reynolds number is enormous (often **Re > 10⁶**). CFD provides a virtual wind tunnel. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Voxel Chopping Board":** > Imagine you want to measure exactly how much water flows around a complex shape like a **Formula-1 front wing**. You cannot solve the water motion with a single pencil-and-paper equation. So, you superimpose an invisible **3D grid** (think: digital lego blocks) over the wing and the surrounding air. For each tiny block (a *cell*), the computer applies Newton's laws of fluid motion — *what goes in, what goes out, what accelerates, what decelerates*. After iterating millions of times across all blocks, the computer reconstructs the full flow picture: streamlines, pressure contours, wake regions, vortices, and drag coefficients. ## 1.3 Why Numerical Methods Are Mandatory for Vehicle Aerodynamics - **Geometric Complexity:** Cars have wing mirrors, spoilers, wheels, diffusers, underbody, cooling inlets — no closed-form analytical solution exists. - **Turbulence:** Vehicle flows are almost always fully turbulent. Direct numerical resolution (DNS) is computationally impossible. Approximate models (RANS, LES) require numerical discretization. - **Design Iteration:** Modern OEMs (e.g., Mercedes-AMG, Tata Motors EV division) require **hundreds of design iterations** per vehicle program. Physical wind tunnels cost ~₹50 crore to build; CFD is comparatively cheap. - **Transonic & Compressible Effects:** At high speeds (Tata trucks, racing cars, aircraft), shock waves require shock-capturing numerical schemes. ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Metrics (Bolded Standards) | Quantity | Symbol | Typical Vehicle-Aero Value | |---|---|---| | Density of air at sea level | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** | | Dynamic viscosity of air | $\mu$ | **1.789 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s** | | Kinematic viscosity | $\nu$ | **1.5 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** | | Speed of sound | $a$ | **340.29 m/s** | | Reynolds number (passenger car) | $Re$ | **1 × 10⁶ to 5 × 10⁶** | | Mach number (road vehicle) | $M$ | **0.0 to 0.3** | | CFL number (stability) | $CFL$ | **≤ 1 (explicit)** | > [!TIP] > A KTU board examiner expects you to state the **order of magnitude** of the Reynolds number for a typical passenger car (~10⁶) and relate it to the choice of turbulence model (RANS k-ε, k-ω SST, etc.). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The CFD Pipeline — Five Logical Pillars ### Pillar 1: Mathematical Modeling The governing equations of fluid flow are the **Navier–Stokes equations** (momentum), **continuity equation** (mass), and **energy equation**. For vehicle aerodynamics (incompressible, isothermal), we typically solve: - **Continuity:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ - **Momentum:** $\rho \left( \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \vec{F}_b$ ### Pillar 2: Discretization The continuous domain is split into a finite number of **discrete cells** (mesh). The PDEs are converted into algebraic form using: - **Finite Difference Method (FDM)** — Approximates derivatives using Taylor series. - **Finite Volume Method (FVM)** — Conserves fluxes across cell faces (industry standard for vehicle CFD). - **Finite Element Method (FEM)** — Uses weak formulation; popular for structural-fluid coupling. > [!IMPORTANT] > **For vehicle aerodynamics, FVM is the industry standard** (used by ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM, STAR-CCM+, CONVERGE) because it inherently conserves mass, momentum, and energy across each cell — a non-negotiable requirement for accurate drag prediction. ### Pillar 3: Mesh Generation - **Structured mesh:** Hexahedral cells in an i-j-k index. Excellent accuracy, hard to generate for complex vehicle geometry. - **Unstructured mesh:** Tetrahedral/prismatic cells. Used for complex automotive geometries. - **Hybrid mesh:** Prismatic boundary layer + tetrahedral far field. **The de-facto standard for road vehicles.** ### Pillar 4: Solution Algorithm - **Pressure-Velocity Coupling:** SIMPLE, SIMPLEC, PISO, COUPLED. - **Time integration:** Explicit (Forward Euler, RK4) vs Implicit (Backward Euler, Crank-Nicolson). - **Spatial discretization:** Upwind, Central Difference, QUICK, MUSCL. ### Pillar 5: Turbulence Modeling Because the turbulent eddies are too small to resolve (Kolmogorov scale ~μm for road cars), we use **sub-grid models**: - **RANS (Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes):** k-ε, k-ω SST — *cheapest, industry default*. - **LES (Large Eddy Simulation):** Resolves large eddies, models sub-grid scale. - **DES / DDES:** Hybrid RANS-LES, used for bluff-body flows like trucks. - **DNS:** Direct Numerical Simulation — *not feasible* for full vehicle. ## 2.2 Discretization Concept — The "Why" > [!NOTE] > **Why discretize?** Because a computer can ONLY store discrete numbers, not continuous functions. Each cell stores values at a finite set of points (nodes or cell centers). The differential equations become algebraic "balances" between neighboring cells. ## 2.3 Stability, Consistency, and Convergence (Lax Equivalence Theorem) For a numerical scheme to converge to the true physical solution, it must satisfy: > **The Lax Equivalence Theorem:** *For a linear PDE, **consistency + stability ⇒ convergence**.* - **Consistency:** The truncation error of the scheme → 0 as grid spacing → 0. - **Stability:** Numerical errors do not grow unboundedly during iteration. - **Convergence:** The numerical solution approaches the exact solution as the grid is refined. ## 2.4 The CFL (Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy) Stability Condition For an **explicit** time-marching scheme in 1-D: $$CFL = \frac{u \cdot \Delta t}{\Delta x} \leq 1$$ - **$u$** = local flow velocity (m/s) - **$\Delta t$** = time step (s) - **$\Delta x$** = cell spacing in flow direction (m) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical meaning:** Information (a disturbance) cannot physically travel farther than one cell width in one time step. If the numerical wave travels faster than the physical wave, the scheme blows up. For 3-D vehicle simulations, the condition becomes: $$CFL_{3D} = \Delta t \left( \frac{\vert u_x \vert}{\Delta x} + \frac{\vert u_y \vert}{\Delta y} + \frac{\vert u_z \vert}{\Delta z} \right) \leq 1$$ ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > Memorize this table for KTU board exam. Examiner almost always asks one formula from this. | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Use in Vehicle CFD | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | Determines flow regime (laminar/turbulent) | | 2 | Drag coefficient | $C_D = \dfrac{2 F_D}{\rho V^2 A}$ | Primary output of vehicle CFD | | 3 | Lift coefficient | $C_L = \dfrac{2 F_L}{\rho V^2 A}$ | Spoiler/wing design metric | | 4 | $y^+$ (wall spacing) | $y^+ = \dfrac{y u_\tau}{\nu}$ | Critical for boundary-layer mesh | | 5 | CFL (1D explicit) | $CFL = \dfrac{u \Delta t}{\Delta x}$ | Stability criterion | | 6 | Cell Peclet number | $Pe_{cell} = \dfrac{\rho u \Delta x}{\Gamma}$ | Convection vs diffusion dominance | | 7 | Pressure correction (SIMPLE) | $p' = p^* - p$ | Pressure-velocity coupling | | 8 | First-order Taylor truncation | $\tau = \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ | Discretization error order | | 9 | Continuity residual | $R_m = \sum \dot{m}_{faces}$ | Convergence monitor | | 10 | Kolmogorov length scale | $\eta = \left( \dfrac{\nu^3}{\varepsilon} \right)^{1/4}$ | DNS mesh resolution requirement | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Drag Reduction:** A 10-count $C_D$ reduction on a sedan saves ~0.4 L/100 km of fuel — directly traceable to CFD-driven design. - **EV Range Extension:** EV manufacturers (e.g., Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV) rely heavily on CFD to extend range by 5-8%. - **Cooling Simulation:** Underhood and battery thermal management CFD ensures motors and batteries stay below 60 °C. - **Aeroacoustics:** Wind-noise prediction uses FW-H acoustic analogy post-processed from CFD. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate Active.** No step is skipped. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Finite Difference Approximation from Taylor Series ### Goal Derive the **second-order central-difference** formula for the second spatial derivative, $\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$, which appears in the diffusion term of the Navier–Stokes equation. ### Step 1: Write Taylor series expansion forward and backward Consider a smooth function $u(x)$. The Taylor expansion about a node $i$ at spacing $\Delta x$ forward: $$u_{i+1} = u_i + \Delta x \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2!} \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{3!} \left( \frac{\partial^3 u}{\partial x^3} \right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{4!} \left( \frac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4} \right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)$$ Similarly, backward: $$u_{i-1} = u_i - \Delta x \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2!} \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \right)_i - \frac{\Delta x^3}{3!} \left( \frac{\partial^3 u}{\partial x^3} \right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{4!} \left( \frac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4} \right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)$$ ### Step 2: Add the two expansions $$u_{i+1} + u_{i-1} = 2u_i + \Delta x^2 \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{12} \left( \frac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4} \right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^6)$$ ### Step 3: Solve for the second derivative $$\left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \right)_i = \frac{u_{i+1} - 2u_i + u_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} - \frac{\Delta x^2}{12} \left( \frac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4} \right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)$$ ### Step 4: Final Discretized Form $$\boxed{\left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \right)_i \approx \frac{u_{i+1} - 2u_i + u_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}}$$ The **truncation error** is $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$, meaning the scheme is **second-order accurate** — halving the grid spacing reduces the error by a factor of 4. ### Step 5: Substitute into Navier–Stokes Diffusion Term The viscous term in x-momentum becomes: $$\mu \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} \right) \approx \mu \left[ \frac{u_{i+1,j,k} - 2u_{i,j,k} + u_{i-1,j,k}}{\Delta x^2} + \frac{u_{i,j+1,k} - 2u_{i,j,k} + u_{i,j-1,k}}{\Delta y^2} + \frac{u_{i,j,k+1} - 2u_{i,j,k} + u_{i,j,k-1}}{\Delta z^2} \right]$$ This algebraic expression can now be solved on a computer. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Derivation of the CFL Stability Condition ### Goal Show that explicit time-stepping is conditionally stable and find the maximum permissible $\Delta t$. ### Step 1: 1-D Linear Convection Equation $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + c \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0$$ This represents the transport of a scalar $u$ at wave speed $c$ (in vehicle CFD, this models how vortices or pollutants advect downstream). ### Step 2: Discretize using Forward Time, Central Space (FTCS) $$\frac{u_i^{n+1} - u_i^n}{\Delta t} + c \left( \frac{u_{i+1}^n - u_{i-1}^n}{2 \Delta x} \right) = 0$$ ### Step 3: Solve explicitly for the next time level $$u_i^{n+1} = u_i^n - \frac{c \Delta t}{2 \Delta x} \left( u_{i+1}^n - u_{i-1}^n \right)$$ ### Step 4: Apply Von Neumann Stability Analysis Substitute a Fourier mode $u_i^n = \hat{u}^n e^{I k x_i}$ where $I = \sqrt{-1}$ and $k$ is the wavenumber: $$\hat{u}^{n+1} e^{I k x_i} = \hat{u}^n e^{I k x_i} - \frac{c \Delta t}{2 \Delta x} \hat{u}^n \left( e^{I k (x_i + \Delta x)} - e^{I k (x_i - \Delta x)} \right)$$ Divide both sides by $e^{I k x_i}$: $$\hat{u}^{n+1} = \hat{u}^n - \frac{c \Delta t}{2 \Delta x} \left( e^{I k \Delta x} - e^{-I k \Delta x} \right) \hat{u}^n$$ Use Euler's identity: $e^{I\theta} - e^{-I\theta} = 2I \sin \theta$: $$\hat{u}^{n+1} = \hat{u}^n \left[ 1 - I \left( \frac{c \Delta t}{\Delta x} \right) \sin(k \Delta x) \right]$$ ### Step 5: Amplification Factor The amplification factor $G = \dfrac{\hat{u}^{n+1}}{\hat{u}^n}$ is: $$G = 1 - I \cdot CFL \cdot \sin(k \Delta x)$$ where $CFL = \dfrac{c \Delta t}{\Delta x}$. ### Step 6: Stability Requires $\vert G \vert \leq 1$ $$\vert G \vert^2 = 1 + CFL^2 \sin^2(k \Delta x) > 1 \text{ for any } CFL \neq 0$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The FTCS scheme is **unconditionally unstable** for the linear convection equation, regardless of $\Delta t$! This is a famous result that drives engineers to use **upwind**, **Lax-Friedrichs**, or **Lax-Wendroff** schemes instead. ### Step 7: Upwind Scheme Stability For the upwind scheme (assuming $c > 0$): $$u_i^{n+1} = u_i^n - \frac{c \Delta t}{\Delta x} \left( u_i^n - u_{i-1}^n \right)$$ Amplification factor: $$G = 1 - CFL \left( 1 - e^{-I k \Delta x} \right)$$ For $\vert G \vert \leq 1$: $$\boxed{CFL = \frac{c \Delta t}{\Delta x} \leq 1}$$ **Physical interpretation:** A fluid particle moving at speed $c$ must not cross more than one cell in one time step. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure-Velocity Coupling (SIMPLE Algorithm) ### Goal Show how the SIMPLE (Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked Equations) algorithm decouples pressure and velocity. ### Step 1: Discretized Momentum Equation For a generic cell with cell-center velocity $u^*$ computed from a guessed pressure field $p^*$: $$a_P u^* = \sum_{nb} a_{nb} u_{nb}^* + (p^*_{w} - p^*_{e}) A + S_u$$ where $a_P$ and $a_{nb}$ are linearization coefficients from convection-diffusion discretization, and $S_u$ is a source term. ### Step 2: Define Corrections $$u = u^* + u' \quad ; \quad p = p^* + p'$$ ### Step 3: Substitute and Subtract Subtracting the $u^*$ equation from the corrected $u$ equation yields: $$a_P u' = \sum_{nb} a_{nb} u'_{nb} + (p'_{w} - p'_{e}) A$$ ### Step 4: SIMPLE Simplification (Drop $\sum a_{nb} u'_{nb}$) $$\boxed{u' = \frac{A}{a_P} (p'_w - p'_e) = d_p (p'_w - p'_e)}$$ where $d_p = A / a_P$ is the pressure-correction coefficient. ### Step 5: Continuity Equation (Velocity Correction Form) The continuity equation $\sum (\rho u A) = 0$ across the cell faces must be satisfied. Substituting $u = u^* + u'$ into mass flow rates at each face yields the **pressure-correction equation**: $$\boxed{a_P p'_P = a_N p'_N + a_S p'_S + a_E p'_E + a_W p'_W + b}$$ where $b$ is the **mass imbalance** (residual) from the current guessed velocities. ### Step 6: Iterative Algorithm 1. Guess $p^*$. 2. Solve momentum → obtain $u^*, v^*, w^*$. 3. Solve pressure-correction equation → obtain $p'$. 4. Correct: $p = p^* + \alpha_p p'$ (with under-relaxation $\alpha_p \approx 0.3$). 5. Correct velocities: $u = u^* + d_p (p'_w - p'_e)$. 6. Check mass residual. If not converged, go to step 2. > [!NOTE] > This is the **backbone of incompressible vehicle CFD solvers** like ANSYS Fluent's pressure-based solver. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Numerical Schemes | Scheme | Type | Order | Stability | Use in Vehicle CFD | |---|---|---|---|---| | FTCS | Explicit | 2nd space, 1st time | **Unstable** | Avoid | | Upwind (1st order) | Explicit | 1st | $CFL \leq 1$ | Robust, diffusive, initial iterations | | Central Difference | Explicit | 2nd | $CFL \leq 1/\sqrt{2}$ | Smooth flows, can oscillate | | Lax-Friedrichs | Explicit | 1st | $CFL \leq 1$ | Quick & robust | | Lax-Wendroff | Explicit | 2nd | $CFL \leq 1$ | Shock capture | | Crank-Nicolson | Implicit | 2nd | Unconditionally stable | High accuracy transient | | QUICK | Implicit | 3rd | Stable | Industry standard for vehicle flows | | SIMPLE | Iterative | 2nd | Stable | Pressure-velocity coupling | | PISO | Iterative | 2nd | Stable | Transient incompressible | # 4.
