KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]** *Define the Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy (CFL) condition. Why is it critical for explicit time-stepping schemes in vehicle CFD?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **CFL condition** states that for an explicit numerical scheme, the numerical disturbance propagation speed must be at least as large as the physical disturbance speed. For 1-D linear convection with wave speed $c$: $$CFL = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{\Delta x} \leq 1$$ For a typical passenger car simulated at $V = 30$ m/s with cell size $\Delta x = 0.01$ m, the maximum permissible explicit time step is: $$\Delta t_{max} = \frac{\Delta x}{c} = \frac{0.01}{30} \approx 3.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$ **Why critical:** Violating CFL causes the numerical solution to **blow up** (velocities → ∞) within a few iterations, destroying the simulation. For vehicle CFD, where the Reynolds number is high and cells are stretched, implicit schemes are preferred to relax this constraint. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]** *Compare the Finite Difference Method (FDM), Finite Volume Method (FVM), and Finite Element Method (FEM) in terms of conservation property and suitability for complex vehicle geometries. (3 Marks)* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Method | Conservation | Vehicle Geometry Suitability | Industry Use | |---|---|---|---| | **FDM** | Not inherently conservative | Poor (needs structured Cartesian grid) | Rare | | **FVM** | **Inherently conservative** on each cell | **Excellent** (handles arbitrary polyhedra) | **Default (Fluent, OpenFOAM)** | | **FEM** | Weak conservation | Excellent (irregular meshes) | FSI, structural-fluid coupling | For automotive applications, **FVM is preferred** because drag, lift, and moment integrals require conservation across each cell face to be physically accurate. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Internal Choice Mandate:** KTU provides choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both are provided below for full preparation. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Paper)]** **(a)** Derive the second-order central-difference approximation for $\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$ starting from Taylor series. State the order of accuracy and truncation error. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the SIMPLE algorithm for pressure-velocity coupling in detail with a flow chart. Why is pressure under-relaxation (typically $\alpha_p = 0.3$) necessary? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution A(a) — 7 Marks **Step 1:** Taylor expansion forward and backward about node $i$: $$u_{i+1} = u_i + \Delta x \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2!} \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{3!} \left( \frac{\partial^3 u}{\partial x^3} \right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)$$ $$u_{i-1} = u_i - \Delta x \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2!} \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \right)_i - \frac{\Delta x^3}{3!} \left( \frac{\partial^3 u}{\partial x^3} \right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)$$ **Step 2:** Adding both: $$u_{i+1} + u_{i-1} = 2u_i + \Delta x^2 \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)$$ **Step 3:** Solve: $$\left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \right)_i = \frac{u_{i+1} - 2u_i + u_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$$ **Order of accuracy:** Second-order in space. **Truncation error:** $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution A(b) — 7 Marks The **SIMPLE algorithm** (Patankar & Spalding, 1972) iteratively decouples pressure and velocity for incompressible flows. **Algorithm Steps:** 1. **Guess** pressure field $p^*$. 2. **Solve** discretized momentum equations using $p^*$ → obtain $u^*, v^*, w^*$. 3. **Solve** the pressure-correction equation: $$a_P p'_P = \sum_{nb} a_{nb} p'_{nb} + b$$ where $b$ is the mass-source residual. 4. **Correct** pressure: $p = p^* + \alpha_p p'$ where $\alpha_p \approx 0.3$ is the under-relaxation factor. 5. **Correct** velocities: $u = u^* + d_p (p'_w - p'_e)$, similarly for $v$ and $w$. 6. **Check convergence** on mass and momentum residuals. If not converged, return to step 2. **Why pressure under-relaxation is necessary:** The guessed pressure $p^*$ may be far from the true pressure, especially in early iterations. Without under-relaxation, the pressure correction $p'$ can overshoot, causing divergence. $\alpha_p = 0.3$ damps the update so only 30% of $p'$ is added each iteration, ensuring stable convergence. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Paper — Alternative Choice)]** **(a)** Using Von Neumann stability analysis, prove that the Forward Time Central Space (FTCS) scheme is unconditionally unstable for the 1-D linear convection equation. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the choice of turbulence model for vehicle aerodynamics. Compare RANS k-ε, k-ω SST, and DES for bluff-body flows like trucks. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution B(a) — 7 Marks **Governing PDE:** $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} + c \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0$ **Discretization (FTCS):** $$\frac{u_i^{n+1} - u_i^n}{\Delta t} + c \left( \frac{u_{i+1}^n - u_{i-1}^n}{2 \Delta x} \right) = 0$$ Rearrange: $$u_i^{n+1} = u_i^n - \frac{CFL}{2} \left( u_{i+1}^n - u_{i-1}^n \right)$$ where $CFL = c \Delta t / \Delta x$. **Substitute Fourier mode** $u_i^n = \hat{u}^n e^{I k x_i}$: $$\hat{u}^{n+1} = \hat{u}^n \left[ 1 - I \cdot CFL \cdot \sin(k \Delta x) \right]$$ **Amplification factor magnitude:** $$\vert G \vert^2 = 1 + CFL^2 \sin^2(k \Delta x)$$ Since $\sin^2(k\Delta x) \geq 0$, we have $\vert G \vert^2 > 1$ for any $CFL \neq 0$, meaning **any Fourier mode grows unboundedly**. Therefore, FTCS is **unconditionally unstable**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution B(b) — 7 Marks | Model | Type | Strength | Weakness | Vehicle Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Standard k-ε** | RANS, two-equation | Robust, fast, well-validated | Poor near-wall treatment, poor adverse pressure gradients | External flow, simple cars | | **k-ω SST** | RANS, two-equation | Excellent near-wall & adverse pressure gradient | Sensitive to free-stream turbulence | **Best default for road vehicles** (sedans, SUVs, F1) | | **DES / DDES** | Hybrid RANS-LES | Resolves separation, vortex shedding | Higher compute cost (~10× RANS) | Trucks, buses, trailers, bluff bodies | **Recommendation for Vehicle CFD:** - **Sedans / SUVs (low $C_D$ design):** k-ω SST — best balance. - **Trucks / Buses (separation-dominated):** DES or IDDES — captures wake asymmetry. - **Formula-1 / motorsport:** Scale-Adaptive Simulation (SAS) or LES — for wing-tip vortices. For a typical sedan at $Re \sim 10^6$, k-ω SST with $y^+ \leq 1$ near-wall resolution gives $C_D$ within 3-5% of wind tunnel. **(7 marks)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Numerical Methods in CFD:** > > 1. **Wrong Order of Truncation Error:** Many students write "second-order accurate" but forget to specify **in what variable** (space vs time). Always write "$\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ in space, $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t)$ in time." > > 2. **FTCS Stability:** A frequent mistake is claiming FTCS is stable. It is **unconditionally unstable** — do NOT confuse with central time (Leapfrog) or Crank-Nicolson. > > 3. **SIMPLE vs SIMPLEC vs PISO:** Examiners test if you know PISO is for **transient** flows while SIMPLE is for **steady-state**. Mixing these up costs 2-3 marks. > > 4. **CFL Statement:** Don't write "CFL must be less than 1." Write "**CFL ≤ 1 for upwind scheme in 1-D linear convection**." Always specify context. > > 5. **k-ε vs k-ω SST:** Examiners expect you to know that k-ω SST **switches to k-ω near the wall and k-ε in the free stream** — write this explicitly. > > 6. **Mesh Independence:** Forgetting to mention **grid-independence study** is a major omission. Always state "results must be validated on three successively refined meshes." --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Numerical Methods in CFD** - [x] **CFD** = Numerical solution of Navier–Stokes on a discrete mesh. - [x] **Three pillars of CFD:** Discretization + Solver + Post-processing. - [x] **FVM is industry standard** for vehicle aerodynamics (Fluent, OpenFOAM). - [x] **Taylor series** gives finite-difference formulas: central 2nd-order has error $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$. - [x] **FTCS is unconditionally unstable** for 1-D convection. - [x] **CFL condition:** $\dfrac{c \Delta t}{\Delta x} \leq 1$ for explicit upwind. - [x] **Implicit schemes** (Crank-Nicolson, QUICK) relax CFL constraint. - [x] **SIMPLE algorithm:** Pressure-velocity coupling for steady incompressible flows. - [x] **PISO algorithm:** Used for transient incompressible flows. - [x] **Turbulence models:** k-ε (robust), k-ω SST (best for adverse pressure gradient), DES (wake flows). - [x] **$y^+ < 1$** required for resolving viscous sublayer with k-ω SST. - [x] **Lax Equivalence Theorem:** Consistency + Stability = Convergence. - [x] **Hybrid mesh** (prism BL + tetra core) is the standard for car CFD. - [x] **Pressure under-relaxation** $\alpha_p \approx 0.3$ prevents divergence in SIMPLE. - [x] **Vehicle Re ~ 10⁶**, hence flow is fully turbulent, requires RANS or better. - [x] **Drag coefficient:** $C_D = 2F_D / (\rho V^2 A)$ — primary output metric. - [x] **Grid independence** must be demonstrated on at least three refined meshes. - [x] **Convergence criteria:** Residuals < $10^{-5}$ and monitored $C_D$ plateau.

topics.title: Aerodynamic Study of F1 Racing Cars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Aerodynamic Study of F1 Racing Cars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Formula One (F1) Aerodynamics** is the specialized branch of motorsport fluid dynamics concerned with the generation of **aerodynamic downforce**, the management of **viscous and pressure drag**, and the manipulation of **vortical flow structures** around an open-wheel, open-cockpit single-seater race car operating in a high-Reynolds-number, ground-proximity regime.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 4):**
> Aerodynamic Study of Racing Cars (F1) deals with the application of high-downforce, low-drag aerodynamic principles to maximize cornering grip (lateral acceleration) while maintaining competitive straight-line velocity, governed by the FIA Formula 1 Technical Regulations.

The primary objectives of F1 aerodynamics, in strict order of priority, are:
1. Maximize **downforce coefficient** $(C_L)$ in cornering zones.
2. Minimize **drag coefficient** $(C_D)$ on straights.
3. Maintain **aerodynamic stability** (predictable yaw, pitch, and heave response).
4. Protect the **driver cooling** and **power-unit intake airflow**.

## 1.2 Fundamental Physical Principle — The "Inverted Airfoil" Concept

Unlike an aircraft which uses wings to generate **lift** (upward force), an F1 car uses **inverted airfoils** (wings flipped upside-down) and shaped underbodies to generate **negative lift**, i.e., **downforce** (downward force). This pushes the car onto the track, increasing the vertical load on the tyres and thus increasing the maximum cornering force via the friction equation:

$$F_{lateral,max} = \mu \cdot F_{normal}$$

where $\mu$ is the tyre-road friction coefficient and $F_{normal}$ is the normal (vertical) reaction force.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Air Hockey Puck on a Downward Fan" Effect:**
> Imagine an air-hockey puck on a table. Normally, it slides easily because there is little vertical load. Now imagine a giant fan blowing DOWN onto the puck from above — the puck becomes "stuck" to the table. The harder the fan blows, the more you can push the puck sideways before it slips. **An F1 car's wings are exactly this downward fan** — by pushing the car harder onto the track, the tyres can transmit more cornering force before losing grip.

A modern F1 car can generate **downforce equal to 3.5 to 5 times its own static weight** at 250 km/h, effectively allowing the car to theoretically drive upside-down on a ceiling at that speed.

## 1.4 Key Design Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **standard aerodynamic performance benchmarks** used by every F1 team and verified by the FIA:
> - **Maximum downforce:** $\approx 3500$ N to $5000$ N at $250$ km/h (current regulations).
> - **Drag coefficient ($C_D$):** $\approx 0.7$ to $1.0$ (high, by passenger-car standards, but acceptable due to massive engine power).
> - **Aerodynamic efficiency ($L/D$ or $C_L/C_D$):** $3:1$ to $4:1$ (F1), compared to $\approx 18:1$ for a glider.
> - **Reynolds number** at racing speed: $\approx 10^6$ to $3 \times 10^6$ (fully turbulent).
> - **Air density at sea level (ISA):** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.
> - **Reference frontal area:** $A \approx 1.5$ m².

## 1.5 Why F1 Aerodynamics is Unique Among Road Vehicles

| Parameter | Passenger Car | F1 Car |
|-----------|---------------|--------|
| Primary aero goal | Efficiency, low fuel use | Maximum cornering grip |
| Wing presence | None usually | Front wing + Rear wing mandatory |
| Ground proximity | High (300+ mm) | Ultra-low (< 50 mm floor) |
| Wake interaction | Minimal (separated vehicles) | Critical (following another car) |
| Active aero | Forbidden | DRS allowed (selectively) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of F1 Downforce Generation

F1 cars generate downforce through **three distinct mechanisms**, each contributing roughly a third of the total load (the exact split depends on the circuit and setup):

### Pillar 1: Front Wing Assembly
- **Function:** Generates $\approx 25\text{–}30\%$ of total downforce.
- **Mechanism:** Inverted multi-element airfoil (3–5 elements in older regs, simplified under 2022+ regs).
- **Secondary Role:** Conditions the oncoming airflow, splitting it to feed the **front tyre wake management**, the **sidepod leading edge**, and the **underfloor leading edge**.

### Pillar 2: Underbody / Ground Effect (Venturi Tunnels)
- **Function:** Generates $\approx 40\text{–}60\%$ of total downforce (largest contributor in modern era).
- **Mechanism:** The shaped underfloor acts as an inverted venturi — air accelerates under the car, pressure drops (Bernoulli), and the atmospheric pressure above pushes the car down.
- **2022+ Regulation Note:** Ground-effect tunnels were reintroduced, replacing the heavily vortex-dependent underbody of the 2017–2021 era.

### Pillar 3: Rear Wing Assembly
- **Function:** Generates $\approx 25\text{–}30\%$ of total downforce.
- **Mechanism:** Inverted airfoil producing high $C_L$, but at the cost of high $C_D$.
- **DRS Integration:** The upper flap has a **movable section** that flattens (reducing $C_L$ and $C_D$) when within 1 second of a leading car in designated zones.

## 2.2 Governing Aerodynamic Equations

The fundamental forces on any vehicle in a fluid flow are derived from the dynamic pressure $q$:

$$q = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2$$

where $\rho$ is the air density (kg/m³) and $V$ is the free-stream velocity (m/s). All force coefficients are referenced to this dynamic pressure and the frontal area $A$:

$$F_{downforce} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \cdot C_L \cdot A$$

$$F_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \cdot C_D \cdot A$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> Notice that aerodynamic force scales with **$V^2$**. Therefore, doubling the speed from $125$ km/h to $250$ km/h **quadruples** the downforce. This is why F1 cars feel "alive" only above $\sim 150$ km/h and why cold tyres on a slow out-lap have minimal grip.

## 2.3 Aerodynamic Efficiency Metric

The single most important trade-off metric in F1 is the **lift-to-drag ratio**, also called **aero efficiency** or **$L/D$**:

$$\frac{L}{D} = \frac{C_L}{C_D} = \frac{F_{downforce}}{F_{drag}}$$

A high $L/D$ is desirable because it means **more grip per unit of drag penalty**. The drag must be overcome by the engine, so high $L/D$ allows higher corner exit speeds.

## 2.4 The Ground-Effect / Venturi Equation

For an idealized venturi underbody, applying Bernoulli's equation between the stagnation point upstream (point 1) and the throat under the car (point 2):

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_2^2$$

Assuming $V_1 \approx V_\infty$ and the pressure difference creates downforce over the underbody area $A_f$:

$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho (V_2^2 - V_1^2)$$

$$F_{down,venturi} = \Delta P \cdot A_f = \frac{1}{2} \rho (V_2^2 - V_1^2) \cdot A_f$$

## 2.5 The Diffuser — Pressure Recovery

A diffuser is a gradually expanding duct at the rear of the underbody. Its function is to **recover pressure** (decelerate the flow back to ambient) and thus extract a rearward suction force. The ideal expansion angle is $\theta \le 7°$ to prevent flow separation.

The pressure recovery across a diffuser of exit area $A_e$ and inlet area $A_t$ (throat) follows from compressible flow theory (incompressible limit):

$$\frac{P_e - P_t}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_t^2} = 1 - \left(\frac{A_t}{A_e}\right)^2$$

The diffuser's contribution to rear downforce (suction):

$$F_{rear,down} = (P_{ambient} - P_{diffuser\_exit}) \cdot A_e$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Units | KTU Use |
|---|---------------------|---------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Dynamic pressure | $q = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ | Pa | Universal aero basis |
| 2 | Downforce | $F_L = q \cdot C_L \cdot A$ | N | Wing/floor forces |
| 3 | Drag force | $F_D = q \cdot C_D \cdot A$ | N | Drag penalty |
| 4 | Aero efficiency | $E = C_L / C_D$ | — | Cornering vs speed trade-off |
| 5 | Power required to overcome drag | $P_{drag} = F_D \cdot V$ | W | Top-speed calculation |
| 6 | Venturi underbody downforce | $F_v = \frac{1}{2}\rho(V_2^2 - V_1^2) A_f$ | N | Ground-effect floor |
| 7 | Bernoulli (incompressible) | $P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = \text{const}$ | Pa | Wing/floor pressure analysis |
| 8 | DRS drag reduction | $\Delta C_D \approx 10\text{–}15\%$ | — | Straight-line speed gain |
| 9 | Reynolds number (F1) | $Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} \approx 10^6\text{–}3\times 10^6$ | — | Turbulence assumption validity |
| 10 | Tyre grip limit | $F_{lat,max} = \mu F_N$ | N | Why downforce matters |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **downforce (load)** with **weight (mass × g)**. Downforce is **speed-dependent** and disappears at standstill. This distinction is a frequently tested 2-mark conceptual question.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The technologies pioneered in F1 — ground-effect floors, vortex generators, blown diffusers, active aerodynamics, and CFD-driven wing design — have direct lineage to:
- **Hypercar / supercar active aero** (e.g., Pagani Huayra, McLaren P1).
- **Commercial truck trailer skirts** (drag reduction for fuel economy).
- **High-speed maglev and Hyperloop** pod design (ground-effect lift avoidance).
- **Wind-turbine blade aerodynamics** (low Reynolds / ground proximity lessons).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Why F1 Cars Must Trade Drag for Downforce

**Given:**
- F1 car mass $m = 800$ kg (minimum + driver).
- Frontal area $A = 1.5$ m².
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.
- Top speed target $V_{max}$ and a corner entry speed $V_{corner}$.

**Assumption:** Steady, incompressible, inviscid flow (high Reynolds number limit).

**Step 1 — Write the force balance at top speed.**
At terminal velocity, engine thrust $T$ equals drag $F_D$ (rolling resistance ignored for simplicity):

$$T = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{max}^2 \cdot C_D \cdot A$$

**Step 2 — Express the engine power needed.**

$$P_{engine} = T \cdot V_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{max}^3 \cdot C_D \cdot A$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerical example.**
Let $C_D = 0.9$, $V_{max} = 83.33$ m/s (300 km/h):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{engine} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot (83.33)^3 \cdot 0.9 \cdot 1.5 \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 578{,}700 \cdot 0.9 \cdot 1.5 \\
&= 478{,}000 \text{ W} \approx 478 \text{ kW} \approx 641 \text{ hp}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the downforce requirement for a corner.**
In a corner of radius $R = 50$ m at speed $V_c = 60$ m/s, centripetal acceleration is $a_c = V_c^2 / R = 72$ m/s². Required lateral force:

$$F_{lat} = m \cdot a_c = 800 \cdot 72 = 57{,}600 \text{ N}$$

With tyre friction $\mu = 1.6$ (modern F1 slick), the required downforce:

$$F_L = \frac{F_{lat}}{\mu} - m g = \frac{57{,}600}{1.6} - 800 \cdot 9.81 = 36{,}000 - 7{,}848 = 28{,}152 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the downforce this cornering speed actually provides (via the rear/front wings and floor).**
If $C_{L,total} = 2.5$ at $V_c = 60$ m/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_L &= \frac{1}{2} \rho V_c^2 C_L A \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot (60)^2 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 1.5 \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 3600 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 1.5 \\
&= 8{,}266 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — The "Aero Deficit" insight.**
The 8,266 N provided is far less than the 28,152 N required. This shows the car **cannot corner at 216 km/h** with this downforce. Reducing $V_c$ to 40 m/s (144 km/h) recovers the balance. This is the **fundamental corner-speed limit** dictated by aero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion of Derivation 1:** F1 aerodynamic design is a forced compromise. More downforce (higher $C_L$) demands more wing area/camber, which raises $C_D$ and reduces top speed. The design optimum is set by the **lap-time simulation**, not by maximum $C_L$ or minimum $C_D$ in isolation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Diffuser Pressure Recovery and Downforce

**Given:**
- Throat area $A_t = 1.0$ m².
- Exit area $A_e = 1.6$ m² (60% expansion).
- Throat velocity $V_t = 70$ m/s.
- Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 101{,}325$ Pa.
- Inlet velocity $V_1 = V_\infty = 60$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Compute throat dynamic pressure.**

$$q_t = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_t^2 = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 4900 = 3001.25 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the diffuser pressure-recovery relation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_e - P_t &= q_t \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{A_t}{A_e}\right)^2 \right] \\
&= 3001.25 \cdot \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{1.0}{1.6}\right)^2 \right] \\
&= 3001.25 \cdot [1 - 0.3906] \\
&= 3001.25 \cdot 0.6094 \\
&= 1829.2 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine exit pressure.**

$$P_e = P_t + 1829.2$$

To find $P_t$, apply Bernoulli from far upstream to throat:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_t &= P_{atm} + \frac{1}{2}\rho (V_1^2 - V_t^2) \\
&= 101{,}325 + 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot (3600 - 4900) \\
&= 101{,}325 - 796.25 \\
&= 100{,}528.75 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Diffuser exit pressure.**

$$P_e = 100{,}528.75 + 1829.2 = 102{,}357.95 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 5 — Rear suction (downforce) on the diffuser exit area.**
Since $P_e > P_{atm}$, the diffuser is actually pushing the car UP at its exit. The **net rear underbody downforce** is the integral of under-pressure over the entire underbody. The local suction at exit, treating $P_e$ relative to ambient:

$$\Delta P_{exit} = P_e - P_{atm} = 1033 \text{ Pa (upward on rear)}$$

**Step 6 — Net underbody contribution.**
The dominant underbody downforce comes from the **low pressure at the throat** and the long, low-pressure floor. Using a representative average underbody suction $\overline{\Delta P} = 2500$ Pa over the floor plan area $A_f = 2.5$ m²:

$$F_{floor,down} = 2500 \cdot 2.5 = 6{,}250 \text{ N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion of Derivation 2:** The diffuser's primary job is to **prevent separation** at the rear of the underbody, allowing the low pressure generated under the floor to "hold on" rather than reattach abruptly with massive loss. A stalled diffuser (separation) collapses the entire underbody downforce — this is why modern F1 floors have strict edge-sealing vortex generators.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: DRS (Drag Reduction System) Speed Gain

**Given:**
- DRS open: $C_D$ reduces by $\Delta C_D = 0.10$ (a typical FIA-measured value).
- DRS open: $C_L$ reduces by $\Delta C_L = 0.50$.
- Engine power at operating RPM: $P = 750$ kW.
- Vehicle without DRS: $C_D = 0.95$, $C_L = 2.6$, $A = 1.5$ m², $m = 800$ kg.

**Step 1 — Power balance at top speed (DRS closed).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= F_D \cdot V = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^3 C_D A \\
V_{closed} &= \sqrt[3]{\frac{2P}{\rho C_D A}} \\
&= \sqrt[3]{\frac{2 \cdot 750{,}000}{1.225 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 1.5}} \\
&= \sqrt[3]{\frac{1{,}500{,}000}{1.7456}} \\
&= \sqrt[3]{859{,}287} = 95.0 \text{ m/s} \approx 342 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Power balance at top speed (DRS open).**

$$C_D' = 0.85$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{open} &= \sqrt[3]{\frac{2 \cdot 750{,}000}{1.225 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 1.5}} \\
&= \sqrt[3]{\frac{1{,}500{,}000}{1.5619}} \\
&= \sqrt[3]{960{,}326} = 98.7 \text{ m/s} \approx 355 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Speed gain from DRS.**

$$\Delta V = 98.7 - 95.0 = 3.7 \text{ m/s} \approx 13.3 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 4 — Downforce penalty during DRS-open (corners).**
At $V = 50$ m/s (180 km/h), the downforce lost from DRS-open:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta F_L &= \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \cdot \Delta C_L \cdot A \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 2500 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1.5 \\
&= 1{,}148 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation 3:** DRS gives roughly $10\text{–}15$ km/h of straight-line speed gain, but the **rear-axle downforce loss** of $\sim 1{,}150$ N at 180 km/h is why DRS is **only** permitted in designated zones on the track, never in corners.

---

## 3.4 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Design Component | $C_L$ Contribution | $C_D$ Contribution | Critical Constraint | Failure Mode |
|------------------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------|--------------|
| Front wing | $\sim 25\%$ | Medium | Tyre wake management | Front-end wash-out (understeer) |
| Underbody / floor | $\sim 45\%$ | Low | Ride-height sensitivity (porpoising) | Floor stall, vibration |
| Rear wing | $\sim 30\%$ | High (DRS closed) | DRS legality | Stall at high yaw |
| Diffuser | Recovers floor pressure | Low | 7° max expansion angle | Flow separation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 F1 Car — Block-Level Aerodynamic Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow of air** around an F1 car and the interaction between aerodynamic components. This is rendered as a block-level functional architecture (per protocol adaptation for complex physical shapes).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Free Stream Air at Velocity V infinity]:::input

    A0 --> A1[Impact on Front Wing Assembly]
    A1 --> A2[Split Flow 1: Over Wing Top Surface]
    A1 --> A3[Split Flow 2: Around Front Tyre]
    A1 --> A4[Split Flow 3: Under Leading Edge of Floor]

    A2 --> A5[Flow Over Nose and Cockpit]
    A3 --> A6[Front Tyre Wake]
    A4 --> A7[Inlet of Venturi Floor Throat]

    A5 --> A8[Bargeboard and Sidepod Inlet]
    A6 --> A9[Wake Conditioning Vortices]
    A8 --> A10[Sidepod Body and Cooling Exit]
    A9 --> A11[Y250 Vortex Region]

    A7 --> A12[Throat: Maximum Velocity V throat, Minimum Pressure]
    A12 --> A13[Diffuser Expansion Region]
    A11 --> A13
    A10 --> A14[Rear Wing Lower Element]

    A13 --> A15[Diffuser Exit: Pressure Recovery]
    A14 --> A16[Rear Wing Upper Element with DRS]
    A15 --> A17[Underbody Exit Wake]
    A16 --> A18[Rear Wing Tip Vortices]

    A17 --> A19[Slipstream Region behind Car]
    A18 --> A19
    A10 --> A19

    A19 --> A20[Downwash Mixed Wake]:::output
    A20 --> A21[Following Car: Reduced Drag Benefit]:::output

    classDef input fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef flow fill:#0b5394,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef component fill:#274e13,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading Guide for the Diagram:**
- **Blue nodes** represent functional flow nodes (the path of air).
- **Green nodes** represent physical aerodynamic components on the car.
- The diagram starts from the free stream on the **left** and traces three simultaneous flow paths:
  1. **Upper path** (over-wing): Front wing → cockpit → rear wing → wake.
  2. **Mid path** (around-tyre): Tyre wake → bargeboard → rear.
  3. **Lower path** (underbody): Venturi throat → diffuser → slipstream.

## 4.2 Aerodynamic Force Vector Diagram (Free-Body)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Forces_On_F1_Car
        direction TB
        F1[Weight mg: Acts Downwards]:::down
        F2[Front Wing Downforce: Acts Downward]:::down
        F3[Rear Wing Downforce: Acts Downward]:::down
        F4[Floor Downforce: Acts Downward]:::down
        F5[Drag F_D: Acts Rearward]:::back
        F6[Lateral Cornering Force: Acts Outward]:::side
        F7[Normal Tyre Reaction: Acts Upward]:::up
    end

    F1 --> Net[Net Vertical Force equals Zero at Equilibrium]:::balance
    F2 --> Net
    F3 --> Net
    F4 --> Net
    F7 --> Net
    F5 --> NetT[Net Horizontal Force equals Thrust minus Drag]:::balance
    F6 --> NetL[Net Lateral Force equals m times V squared over R]:::balance

    classDef down fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef up fill:#38761d,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef back fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef side fill:#7f6000,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef balance fill:#000000,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Component-Coefficient Mapping Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C[Total Aerodynamic Performance of F1 Car]:::root
    C --> CL[Total C_L Components]:::root
    C --> CD[Total C_D Components]:::root

    CL --> CL1[Front Wing: C_L_fw approx 0.7]:::sub
    CL --> CL2[Rear Wing: C_L_rw approx 0.9]:::sub
    CL --> CL3[Floor: C_L_fl approx 1.1]:::sub
    CL --> CL4[Body and Misc: C_L_body approx 0.1]:::sub

    CD --> CD1[Front Wing: C_D_fw approx 0.10]:::sub
    CD --> CD2[Rear Wing: C_D_rw approx 0.35]:::sub
    CD --> CD3[Floor and Diffuser: C_D_fl approx 0.20]:::sub
    CD --> CD4[Body and Tyres: C_D_body approx 0.30]:::sub

    CL1 --> SUM1[Sum: C_L_total approx 2.8]:::sum
    CL2 --> SUM1
    CL3 --> SUM1
    CL4 --> SUM1

    CD1 --> SUM2[Sum: C_D_total approx 0.95]:::sum
    CD2 --> SUM2
    CD3 --> SUM2
    CD4 --> SUM2

    SUM1 --> EFF[Efficiency E equals C_L divided by C_D approx 2.95]:::eff
    SUM2 --> EFF

    classDef root fill:#000000,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef sub fill:#0b5394,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef sum fill:#274e13,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef eff fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 4 Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students frequently confuse **downforce with weight** — remember downforce is **velocity-dependent** (scales as $V^2$) and is **zero when stationary**.
> 2. Confusing **$C_L$ for a car (downforce) with $C_L$ for an aircraft (lift)** — the sign convention is opposite, but the equation is identical.
> 3. **DRS** is permitted **only** in designated track zones; the question wording often tests this regulation knowledge.
> 4. In derivations, students forget the **factor of 1/2** in $q = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ — this is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Porpoising** in ground-effect cars is the result of floor stall — not just "bumpy ride" — and is caused by the flow choking/separating when ride height falls below critical.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1. (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**

> *Define aerodynamic downforce. Why is downforce more critical than engine power for cornering performance in an F1 car?*

**Model Answer:**

**Aerodynamic downforce** is the vertical (downward) aerodynamic force generated by the inverted airfoils, the underbody venturi, and the rear wing of an F1 car when air flows over and under it. It is given by:

$$F_L = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_L A$$

**Why downforce is critical for cornering:**
The maximum lateral cornering force that a tyre can transmit is limited by the friction law:

$$F_{lateral,max} = \mu F_N$$

The normal force $F_N$ is the sum of the static weight and the dynamic downforce:

$$F_N = m g + F_L$$

Without downforce, an 800 kg F1 car has $F_N = 7848$ N, and even with $\mu = 1.6$ the lateral limit is only $12{,}557$ N. With $F_L = 25{,}000$ N at racing speed, $F_N$ jumps to $32{,}848$ N and the cornering limit rises to $52{,}557$ N — over 4× improvement. Hence, downforce, not engine power, is the dominant factor in cornering performance.

---

### **Question 2. (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**

> *Explain the function of a diffuser in an F1 car. State the maximum permissible expansion angle and the consequence of exceeding it.*

**Model Answer:**

A **diffuser** is the diverging duct at the rear of the underbody. Its functions are:
1. To **recover pressure** at the exit of the venturi floor (i.e., decelerate the underbody flow gradually back to ambient).
2. To **generate rear downforce** by sustaining low pressure over the entire length of the floor (preventing the flow from prematurely reattaching).
3. To **minimize separation losses** at the trailing edge of the floor.

**Maximum expansion angle:** Approximately **$7°$** (each side, total included angle $\le 14°$). This limit comes from boundary-layer theory — at higher angles, the adverse pressure gradient exceeds the boundary layer's ability to remain attached, causing **massive flow separation** ("diffuser stall").

**Consequence of exceeding the angle:** The underbody flow separates, the entire floor downforce collapses (potentially losing 60–70% of total car downforce), and the car becomes aerodynamically unstable with a sudden loss of rear grip — often called "porpoising" or "floor stall."

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Regular]**

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the downforce generated by an F1 car moving at velocity $V$. Clearly define every symbol. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** An F1 car has a frontal area of $1.5$ m², a lift coefficient $C_L = 2.5$, and a drag coefficient $C_D = 0.95$ at a speed of $250$ km/h. Calculate: *(i)* the downforce, *(ii)* the drag, *(iii)* the aerodynamic efficiency, and *(iv)* the power required to overcome the drag. Take $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Define the dynamic pressure.**
When a body moves through a fluid, the relevant quantity is the **dynamic pressure** $q$, defined as the kinetic energy per unit volume of the fluid:

$$q = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2$$

where $\rho$ is the fluid density (kg/m³) and $V$ is the free-stream velocity (m/s).

**Step 2 — Relate force to dynamic pressure and a dimensionless coefficient.**
For bluff bodies like an F1 car, the force components are not derived analytically but are expressed via experimentally (or CFD) determined **force coefficients**. The downforce (negative lift) is:

$$F_L = q \cdot C_L \cdot A = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \cdot C_L \cdot A$$

**Step 3 — Define each symbol:**
- $F_L$ — Aerodynamic downforce, Newtons (N).
- $\rho$ — Air density at track altitude, kg/m³.
- $V$ — Free-stream velocity of the car, m/s.
- $C_L$ — Lift (downforce) coefficient, dimensionless.
- $A$ — Frontal reference area, m².

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation.**
The downforce scales with $V^2$, meaning doubling speed quadruples downforce. This is why F1 cars are aerodynamically "dead" below $\sim 100$ km/h and "pinned" above $200$ km/h.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $A = 1.5$ m², $C_L = 2.5$, $C_D = 0.95$, $V = 250$ km/h $= 69.44$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Compute dynamic pressure.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot (69.44)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 4822.5 \\
&= 2{,}953.8 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (i) — Downforce.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_L &= q \cdot C_L \cdot A = 2953.8 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 1.5 \\
&= 11{,}077 \text{ N} \approx 11.08 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (ii) — Drag.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_D &= q \cdot C_D \cdot A = 2953.8 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 1.5 \\
&= 4{,}209 \text{ N} \approx 4.21 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (iii) — Aerodynamic efficiency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{C_L}{C_D} = \frac{2.5}{0.95} = 2.63
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (iv) — Power to overcome drag.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{drag} &= F_D \cdot V = 4209 \cdot 69.44 \\
&= 292{,}273 \text{ W} \approx 292.3 \text{ kW} \approx 392 \text{ hp}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $F_L = 11.08$ kN, (ii) $F_D = 4.21$ kN, (iii) $E = 2.63$, (iv) $P = 292.3$ kW.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the function of a **rear wing** in an F1 car. Discuss the role of the **DRS (Drag Reduction System)**. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Explain the principle of **ground effect** in an F1 car. Derive the downforce expression for a venturi-type underbody and state the role of the **boundary layer** in this mechanism. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Rear Wing Function:**
The rear wing is an **inverted multi-element airfoil** mounted at the rear of the car, typically comprising a main plane (lower element) and a flap (upper element). It generates $\sim 25\text{–}30\%$ of the total car downforce. The flap can be set at high angles of attack ($\sim 30°\text{–}50°$) for low-speed cornering (Monaco setup) or low angles ($\sim 10°\text{–}15°$) for low-drag straights (Monza setup).

**DRS Function:**
The Drag Reduction System is an **active aerodynamic device** that opens a slot in the rear-wing flap, reducing the effective camber and angle of attack. This:
- Reduces $C_D$ by $\sim 10\text{–}15\%$.
- Reduces $C_L$ (rear downforce) by $\sim 50\%$.
- Is **only** permitted in designated DRS zones on the track.
- Is **only** available when a car is within 1 second of a leading car at the detection point.
- Gains typically $10\text{–}15$ km/h of straight-line speed.

**Sketch (textual representation):**
```
   Inverted airfoil
   ____________________
  /  Flap (movable)   \
  \____________________/  <-- Camber creates low pressure on top
  ____________________
 /  Main plane        \
 \____________________/  <-- Slot between elements energises flow
       |       |
       |  DRS  |  <-- Slot opens when DRS activated
       |_______|
            | |  <-- Endplate with vortex generators
```

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Ground-Effect Principle:**
When the F1 car floor is shaped as an inverted venturi, air entering the front of the floor is constricted at a throat and then expanded at the rear (via the diffuser). Conservation of mass demands velocity increase at the throat; Bernoulli's equation then demands a pressure drop at the throat.

**Derivation of Venturi Downforce:**
Apply Bernoulli's equation between the free stream (point 1) and the throat (point 2), assuming incompressible, inviscid, steady flow:

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_2^2$$

The pressure difference acting on the underbody plan area $A_f$ produces downforce:

$$F_{venturi} = (P_1 - P_2) \cdot A_f = \frac{1}{2} \rho (V_2^2 - V_1^2) \cdot A_f$$

This downforce is **self-reinforcing** at low ride height: as the car gets closer to the ground, the throat velocity $V_2$ increases (more constriction), and downforce grows — but only until the boundary layer separates and the floor stalls (the porpoising phenomenon).

**Role of the Boundary Layer:**
The boundary layer is the thin layer of retarded air adjacent to the floor surface. Its behaviour critically determines whether ground effect works:
1. **Attached boundary layer:** Flow follows the floor contour, sustaining low pressure. Maximum downforce.
2. **Separated boundary layer:** Flow detaches, low pressure "fills in" with ambient air, and downforce collapses.
3. **Edge vortices / strakes / fences:** Generate **outward-flowing vortices** that energise the boundary layer, delaying separation and extending the effective ground-effect range to lower ride heights.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Aerodynamic Study of F1 Racing Cars (Module 4):**

- **Downforce equation** is identical in form to lift: $F_L = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 C_L A$.
- **Drag equation:** $F_D = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 C_D A$.
- **Aero efficiency $E = C_L / C_D$** — single most important trade-off metric.
- **Downforce scales with $V^2$**, not linearly — high speeds amplify grip dramatically.
- **Three pillars of downforce:** Front wing ($\sim 25\%$) + Floor ($\sim 45\%$) + Rear wing ($\sim 30\%$).
- **Ground effect / venturi** is the largest downforce contributor in 2022+ regulations.
- **Diffuser** must expand at $\le 7°$ to prevent separation.
- **DRS** reduces $C_D$ by $10\text{–}15\%$ and $C_L$ (rear) by $\sim 50\%$; permitted only in DRS zones.
- **Porpoising** = floor stall caused by boundary-layer separation at very low ride heights; reintroduced in 2022 with ground-effect floors.
- **Tyre grip limit:** $F_{lateral,max} = \mu F_N = \mu (mg + F_L)$.
- **Power to overcome drag:** $P_{drag} = F_D \cdot V = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^3 C_D A$.
- **Reynolds number** at racing speed $\approx 10^6$ to $3 \times 10^6$ — flow is fully turbulent.
- **Aircraft $L/D \approx 18$**, **F1 $L/D \approx 3$** — F1 sacrifices efficiency for raw cornering grip.
- **Bernoulli's equation** is valid for the F1 floor analysis under the incompressible, inviscid assumption.
- **Wake management** (Y250 vortex, tyre squirt, bargeboards) is what allows a following F1 car to "draft" and use DRS to overtake.
- **Static weight** ($mg$) is fixed; **downforce is dynamic** and disappears when the car is stationary — a frequently tested 2-mark trap.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sports Cars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sports Cars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🏎️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sports Car Aerodynamics** is the specialised branch of vehicle aerodynamics concerned with the optimisation of the external body shape, underbody flow, and add-on devices (wings, splitters, diffusers, vortex generators) of high-performance passenger vehicles whose primary design objectives are *high top speed, rapid acceleration, and superior cornering grip* — achieved by simultaneously **minimising aerodynamic drag** ($C_D$) and **maximising aerodynamic downforce** ($C_L \lt 0$).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, sports cars occupy a unique middle-ground between ordinary road cars (where drag minimisation alone dominates) and open-wheel single-seater racing cars (where downforce dominates, even at the cost of high drag). The defining engineering compromise is therefore a **balanced lift-to-drag trade-off** that yields a favourable **aerodynamic efficiency ratio** ($E_a = \mid C_L \mid / C_D$) for the intended operating speed envelope — typically **200–330 km/h**.

## 1.2 Key Quantitative Performance Indices

| Index | Symbol | Typical Sports Car Range | Racing Single-Seater |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drag Coefficient | $C_D$ | **0.25 – 0.38** | 0.75 – 1.10 |
| Lift Coefficient (negative = downforce) | $C_L$ | **−0.15 to −0.60** | −1.50 to −3.50 |
| Frontal Area | $A$ (m²) | **1.8 – 2.2 m²** | 1.5 m² |
| Aerodynamic Efficiency | $\vert C_L/C_D \vert$ | **1 : 1 to 1 : 4** | 1 : 2 to 1 : 3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Sports cars are the *only* road-going category in which **negative lift (downforce)** is deliberately engineered. For all other passenger vehicles in Modules 1–3, the objective is lift coefficient *as close to zero as possible*. This inversion of objective is the central conceptual pivot of Module 4.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Upside-Down Airplane":** Imagine taping a small model airplane to the roof of your family car — but **flipped upside down**. The wing, instead of lifting the car off the road (which would make it lose grip), now *pushes the car harder into the road*. That push is **downforce**. A sports car is essentially a road car with a carefully shaped upside-down airplane wing fused into its silhouette, plus a smooth, low-drag nose so it can still cut through the air efficiently. The faster it goes, the harder it is "glued" to the tarmac.

**Real-world touchstone examples** for instant recall:
- **Bugatti Chiron Super Sport 300+**: $C_D \approx 0.36$, top speed 490 km/h — drag-dominated philosophy.
- **McLaren P1**: $C_D \approx 0.34$, **active rear wing** generating ~600 kg downforce at 257 km/h.
- **Koenigsegg Jesko Absolut**: $C_D \approx 0.278$, *lowest* production-car drag with rear-wheel fairings.
- **Porsche 911 GT3 RS**: $C_D \approx 0.39$, **massive rear wing** — track-focused, *not* speed-record focused.

---

## 1.4 The Tri-Objective Optimisation Problem

Sports car aerodynamics must solve **three competing objectives simultaneously**, illustrated as the "Aerodynamic Trilemma":

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimise:} \quad & F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} C_D A \quad \text{(drag for top speed \& fuel economy)} \\
\text{Maximise:} \quad & F_L^{\downarrow} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} C_L A \quad \text{(downforce for cornering grip)} \\
\text{Satisfy:} \quad & \dot{Q}_{\text{cool}} \leq \dot{Q}_{\text{avail}} \quad \text{(engine \& brake cooling)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A common student error is to treat drag and downforce as *independent* design variables. They are **strongly coupled**: adding a rear wing increases *both* downforce *and* drag. Every aero surface is a compromise — this coupling is heavily tested in KTU examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Aerodynamic Force & Moment System

The complete aerodynamic action on a sports car can be decomposed into **three orthogonal forces** and **three moments**, all referenced to the vehicle's centre of gravity (CG):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_D &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} C_D A \quad &\text{(along } x\text{-axis — longitudinal drag)} \\
F_S &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} C_S A \quad &\text{(along } y\text{-axis — side force in crosswind)} \\
F_L &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} C_L A \quad &\text{(along } z\text{-axis — vertical lift or downforce)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The associated moments (pitch, roll, yaw) are non-dimensionalised as $C_{M_p}, C_{M_r}, C_{M_y}$, multiplied by the same dynamic pressure and a characteristic length squared (typically wheelbase $l$ for pitch, track width $t$ for roll).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU Module 4, the *pitching moment* $C_{M_p}$ is the critical design metric — it determines whether the car is **nose-heavy under braking** or **tail-light under acceleration** in aerodynamic terms.

## 2.2 Sources of Drag on a Sports Car

Drag on a sports car has **four contributing components**, formally introduced by Morelli (1960s, road-car foundational work, still in current syllabus use):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_D = C_{D,\,\text{pressure}} + C_{D,\,\text{viscous}} + C_{D,\,\text{induced}} + C_{D,\,\text{wave/interference}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Component-by-Component Breakdown

**(a) Pressure Drag (~60–70 % of total at typical sports-car speeds)**
- Caused by **flow separation** at the rear of the car.
- Sports cars use a **fastback, Kamm-tail, or tapered rear** to *delay* separation and create a small "virtual" aerodynamic tail (the flow behaves as if the tail extended rearward, but the car itself remains short).
- A *bluff* (upright) rear creates a low-pressure wake → high $C_{D,\,\text{pressure}}$.
- A *Kammback* rear truncates the body vertically, but with a sharp 90° cut and a small spoiler lip, the flow remains attached by Coandă effect, mimicking a much longer streamlined shape.

**(b) Viscous (Skin Friction) Drag (~15–20 %)**
- Proportional to the **wetted surface area** and **local Reynolds number**.
- Sports cars use **flush-mounted glass**, **retractable door handles**, and **smooth underbody panels** to reduce $A_{\text{wetted}}$ and trip the boundary layer as late as possible.

**(c) Induced Drag (~10–20 %)**
- Caused by the generation of downforce itself — analogous to the lift-induced drag of an aircraft wing.
- Inherent to any device producing $C_L \neq 0$; cannot be eliminated, only minimised.
- **Aspect-ratio dependent**: a wider, longer wing has *less* induced drag than a short stubby wing producing the same downforce.
- The trade-off is quantified by:
$$
C_{D,\,\text{induced}} = \frac{C_L^{2}}{\pi \cdot AR \cdot e}
$$
where $AR = b^{2}/S$ is the wing aspect ratio (span $b$, planform area $S$) and $e$ is the Oswald efficiency factor (typically 0.85–0.95 for clean wings).

**(d) Interference / Wave Drag (~5 %)**
- Caused by flow disturbances around protrusions: **mirrors, wheel arches, exhaust tips, roof rails**.
- Sports cars often use **camera-based rear-view systems** (e.g., McLaren) or **aerodynamic aero-mirrors** to eliminate mirror drag.
- At subsonic road speeds, true compressibility (wave) drag is negligible — the "wave" term here refers to small-scale pressure-wave interactions from protrusions.

## 2.3 Sources of Downforce on a Sports Car

| Device | Primary Downforce Mechanism | Approx. $C_L$ Contribution | Approx. $C_D$ Penalty |
|---|---|---|---|
| Front splitter | Pressure differential + barrier effect | −0.05 to −0.10 | +0.01 |
| Flat underbody (with diffuser) | Venturi / ground effect | −0.20 to −0.50 | minimal |
| Rear wing (fixed) | Inverted airfoil lift | −0.30 to −0.80 | +0.05 to +0.20 |
| Rear wing (active, DRS) | Variable airfoil AOA | −0.40 to −1.20 | up to +0.30 deployed |
| Diffuser | Pressure recovery at rear | −0.15 to −0.40 | +0.02 |
| Vortex generators | Boundary-layer energising | −0.03 | negligible |

## 2.4 Ground Effect — The Defining Physics of Modern Sports Cars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ground effect is the single most important concept in Module 4.** It is where the highest ratio of downforce to drag is achieved.

When a sports car has a **flat underbody** and a **diffuser at the rear**, the flow beneath the car accelerates through the constricted cross-section (analogous to a Venturi tube). By Bernoulli's principle, the static pressure under the car **drops below ambient atmospheric pressure**:

$$
p_{\text{under}} < p_{\infty} = p_{\text{ambient}}
$$

This pressure deficit *sucks* the car downward, creating downforce **without** the drag penalty of an exposed wing.

The diffuser's role is to **gradually expand** this accelerated flow back to ambient pressure at the rear exit — a smooth expansion minimises separation and maximises the pressure recovery.

### Quantitative Downforce From Ground Effect

For an idealised flat underbody with diffuser of height $h_1$ (under car) to $h_2$ (exit), assuming incompressible, inviscid flow with mass conservation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Continuity:} \quad & h_1 V_1 = h_2 V_2 \\
\text{Bernoulli (free stream to under-car):} \quad & p_{\infty} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2} = p_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_1^{2} \\
\Rightarrow \quad p_1 &= p_{\infty} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_{\infty}^{2} - V_1^{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

The pressure drop available to generate downforce is therefore:

$$
\Delta p = p_{\infty} - p_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_1^{2} - V_{\infty}^{2})
$$

By integrating $\Delta p$ over the underbody planform area $S_{\text{under}}$:

$$
F_{L,\,\text{ground}}^{\downarrow} = \Delta p \cdot S_{\text{under}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_1^{2} - V_{\infty}^{2})\, S_{\text{under}}
$$

## 2.5 The Inverted-Wing (Rear Wing) Downforce Equation

A conventional rear wing is a **symmetric or cambered airfoil operated at small positive angle of attack**, generating lift in the air but *negative lift (downforce) on the car*:

$$
F_{L,\,\text{wing}}^{\downarrow} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} C_{L,\alpha}\, \alpha\, S_{\text{wing}}
$$

where $C_{L,\alpha}$ is the lift-curve slope (per radian), $\alpha$ is the geometric angle of attack (typically 8°–18° on a sports-car wing), and $S_{\text{wing}}$ is the wing planform area.

For a symmetric NACA-style section at low Mach:

$$
C_{L,\alpha} \approx 2\pi \quad \text{(per radian, thin-airfoil theory)}
$$

So a wing at 15° ($\alpha \approx 0.262$ rad) produces:

$$
C_{L,\text{wing}} \approx 2\pi \times 0.262 \approx 1.65
$$

Real wings are lower due to viscous effects, aspect ratio, and 3-D tip losses — typical effective $C_L$ is **0.8 to 1.4**.

## 2.6 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} C_D A$ | Drag force | Top-speed calculation |
| 2 | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} C_L A$ | Lift (positive up, negative = downforce) | Downforce / grip calculation |
| 3 | $C_{D,i} = C_L^{2} / (\pi \cdot AR \cdot e)$ | Induced drag from lift | Wing design optimisation |
| 4 | $AR = b^{2} / S$ | Wing aspect ratio | Wing shape trade-off |
| 5 | $E_a = \vert C_L \vert / C_D$ | Aerodynamic efficiency | Overall aero quality metric |
| 6 | $\Delta p = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_1^{2} - V_{\infty}^{2})$ | Pressure drop under car | Ground-effect downforce |
| 7 | $C_{L,\alpha} \approx 2\pi / \text{rad}$ | Thin-airfoil lift slope | Wing design first estimate |
| 8 | $F_L^{\downarrow} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}\, \alpha\, C_{L,\alpha}\, S_w$ | Wing downforce | Rear-wing sizing |
| 9 | $V_{\text{max}} = (2 P / \rho C_D A)^{1/3}$ | Top speed for power $P$ | Performance benchmarking |
| 10 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}\, c_p\, \Delta T$ | Engine cooling heat-load relation | Cooling-air mass-flow design |
| 11 | $Re_L = \rho V L / \mu$ | Reynolds number at length $L$ | Wind-tunnel / CFD similarity |
| 12 | $L_{\text{aero}} \cdot a = \dot{m}\, V_{\infty}$ | Boundary-layer momentum thickness effect | Ground-effect stall analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard SI units used throughout:** $\rho$ in kg/m³ (air $\approx$ **1.225 kg/m³** at ISA sea-level), $V$ in m/s, $A$ in m², forces in N. Standard air constants to commit to memory: $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\mu_{\text{air}} = 1.789 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s), $p_{\text{atm}} = 101{,}325$ Pa.

## 2.7 Cooling — The Hidden Third Objective

> [!WARNING]
> High-$C_L$ downforce devices and aggressive body shaping can choke the airflow to the engine radiator and front brake ducts. KTU examinations frequently include a cooling trade-off sub-question.

A sports car's cooling requirement scales with the cube of vehicle speed:

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{radiator}} = \dot{m}_{\text{air}}\, c_p\, \Delta T = \rho\, A_{\text{grille}}\, V_{\text{eff}}\, c_p\, \Delta T
$$

A typical 450 kW sports-car engine needs $\approx 100$ kW of heat rejection at full load. At low speeds the *quantity* of air matters; at high speeds the radiator is at risk of *aerodynamic choking* — the front grille area cannot be reduced further or $C_D$ rises sharply.

The cooling-air *exit strategy* — whether through bonnet vents, side gills, or underbody extraction — is therefore part of the sports-car aerodynamic package.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sports car aerodynamic development, although glamorous, drives *transferable technology* into ordinary road cars:
- **Active aerodynamics** (DRS-style wings on McLaren, Bugatti, Porsche) originated in F1 and now appears in luxury saloons.
- **Flat underbodies with diffusers**, once racing-only, are now standard on hatchbacks (VW Golf GTI, Honda Civic Type R).
- **Ground-effect floor management** informed the Tesla Model S Plaid's underbody diffuser.
- **Aerodynamic side mirrors with integrated cameras** began as Le Mans prototypes (Audi R18) and now appear on road cars (McLaren Artura).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Aerodynamic Drag from First Principles

### Starting Premise
The total drag on a sports car arises from the *integral of pressure and shear stress* over the wetted surface:

$$
F_D = \oint_{S}\left( p\, \cos\theta + \tau_w \sin\theta \right) dS
$$

### Step 1 — Define the Drag Coefficient
Non-dimensionalise by dynamic pressure and frontal area:

$$
C_D \equiv \frac{F_D}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}\, A}
$$

### Step 2 — Decompose into Pressure and Viscous Parts

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_D &= C_{D,p} + C_{D,f} \\
C_{D,p} &= \frac{1}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}\, A}\oint p\, \cos\theta\, dS \quad \text{(form / pressure drag)} \\
C_{D,f} &= \frac{1}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}\, A}\oint \tau_w \sin\theta\, dS \quad \text{(skin-friction drag)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Pressure Drag via Momentum-Integral (Control-Volume Approach)

Apply Reynolds Transport Theorem to a control volume wrapping the car, with stream-tube far upstream at $A_{\infty}$ and wake far downstream at $A_w$:

$$
F_D = \dot{m}\,(V_{\infty} - V_w) + \oint_{\text{wake}} \rho u (V_{\infty} - u)\, dA
$$

For a bluff wake of uniform velocity deficit $\Delta V$ across area $A_w$:

$$
F_D \approx \rho\, A_w\, V_{\infty}\, \Delta V
$$

### Step 4 — Cross-Reference with Sports-Car Reality
- A **streamlined fastback** reduces $A_w$ (smaller wake) and $\Delta V$ (slower wake velocity deficit) → low $C_D$.
- A **bluff estate rear** (large separation bubble) inflates both → high $C_D$.

### Step 5 — Final Compact Form

$$
\boxed{\,F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}\, C_D\, A\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Inverted-Wing Downforce & Induced Drag

### Step 1 — Lift Curve Slope from Thin-Airfoil Theory

For an airfoil with camber line $z(x)$ and downwash $V_i$ induced by the trailing vortex sheet:

$$
\Gamma(\theta) = 2 V_{\infty}\, a_0 \left[ (1 + \cos\theta)\, \alpha_{\text{eff}} + \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{dz}{dx}(\theta_0 - \theta)\, d\theta \right]
$$

For a *symmetric* airfoil at small $\alpha$:

$$
C_L = 2\pi\,\alpha \quad (\alpha \text{ in radians})
$$

### Step 2 — Lift Force on the Car

The wing produces an aerodynamic force perpendicular to the free-stream, but its component *normal to the road* is the downforce:

$$
F_L^{\downarrow} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}\, (2\pi\alpha)\, S_w
$$

### Step 3 — Induced Drag from Lifting-Line Theory

A finite-span wing has tip vortices generating downwash $w_i$, which tilts the lift vector rearward by angle $\epsilon \approx w_i/V$:

$$
C_{D,i} = C_L\, \epsilon = \frac{C_L^{2}}{\pi\, AR\, e}
$$

### Step 4 — Total Drag of Wing + Body

$$
C_{D,\text{total}} = C_{D,0} + \frac{C_L^{2}}{\pi\, AR\, e}
$$

This is the **drag polar** — a fundamental sports-car wing design curve. KTU frequently asks the student to plot or interpret this equation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Ground-Effect Downforce (Idealised Flat Underbody + Diffuser)

### Step 1 — Geometry and Assumption
- Underbody height under car: $h_1 = 0.10$ m
- Diffuser exit height: $h_2 = 0.20$ m
- Diffuser length: $L_d = 1.5$ m
- Free-stream velocity: $V_{\infty} = 60$ m/s (216 km/h)
- Underbody planform area: $S_u = 2.5$ m²

### Step 2 — Apply Incompressible Continuity (1-D Approximation)

$$
h_1 V_1 = h_2 V_2
$$

Solving for the diffuser-exit velocity (assuming no spillage):

$$
V_2 = \frac{h_1}{h_2}\, V_1 = \frac{0.10}{0.20}\, V_1 = 0.5\, V_1
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Bernoulli From Free-Stream to Under-Car Point

$$
p_{\infty} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2} = p_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_1^{2}
$$

Solving for the under-car pressure:

$$
p_1 = p_{\infty} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_{\infty}^{2} - V_1^{2})
$$

### Step 4 — Pressure Deficit (Downforce-Generating)

$$
\Delta p = p_{\infty} - p_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_1^{2} - V_{\infty}^{2})
$$

### Step 5 — Assume $V_1 \approx 1.4\, V_{\infty}$ (a typical venturi acceleration factor when $h_1/h_2$ contraction is large and diffuser flow remains attached):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= 1.4 \times 60 = 84 \text{ m/s} \\
\Delta p &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (84^{2} - 60^{2}) \\
         &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (7056 - 3600) \\
         &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 3456 \\
         &= 2117 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Downforce

$$
F_{L}^{\downarrow} = \Delta p \times S_u = 2117 \times 2.5 = 5293 \text{ N} \approx 540 \text{ kgf}
$$

### Step 7 — Equivalent Lift Coefficient

$$
C_L = \frac{F_L^{\downarrow}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}\, A}
$$

Taking $A = 2.0$ m² (frontal area):

$$
C_L = \frac{5293}{0.5 \times 1.225 \times 3600 \times 2.0} = \frac{5293}{4410} \approx -1.20
$$

This single set of values is comparable to a Formula 1 car — demonstrating how powerful ground effect is, and why it dominates modern sports-car design.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Exam Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Problem (Module 4, 14-mark type):**
> A sports car has frontal area $A = 2.0$ m², drag coefficient $C_D = 0.32$, lift coefficient $C_L = -0.45$, and is travelling at $V = 250$ km/h through air at $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. Calculate: (a) drag force in N, (b) downforce in N, (c) aerodynamic efficiency $E_a$, and (d) the additional drag power consumed in overcoming drag.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Convert velocity:**
$$
V = 250 \text{ km/h} = \frac{250}{3.6} = 69.44 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Dynamic Pressure:**
$$
q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (69.44)^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 4822 = 2953 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 3 — Drag Force (Part a):**
$$
F_D = q\, C_D\, A = 2953 \times 0.32 \times 2.0 = 1890 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Downforce (Part b):**
$$
F_L^{\downarrow} = \vert q\, C_L\, A \vert = \vert 2953 \times (-0.45) \times 2.0 \vert = 2658 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Aerodynamic Efficiency (Part c):**
$$
E_a = \frac{\vert C_L \vert}{C_D} = \frac{0.45}{0.32} = 1.41
$$

**Step 6 — Drag Power (Part d):**
$$
P_D = F_D \times V = 1890 \times 69.44 = 131{,}242 \text{ W} \approx 131 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: At 250 km/h, the car needs ~131 kW (≈ 176 hp) *just to push air aside*. The downforce of 2658 N (≈ 271 kgf) presses the tyres harder into the road, increasing cornering grip proportionally — but at a 141 hp "cost" paid in additional fuel/energy per unit downforce.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Three Sports-Car Design Philosophies

| Design Philosophy | Example Car | $C_D$ | $C_L$ | $E_a$ | Top Speed | Cornering Grip |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Drag-dominated (Speed record) | Bugatti Chiron SS 300+ | **0.36** | −0.10 | 0.28 | 490 km/h | Low |
| Balanced (Road+Track) | McLaren 720S | **0.32** | −0.30 | 0.94 | 341 km/h | Medium-High |
| Downforce-dominated (Track) | Porsche 911 GT3 RS | **0.39** | −0.55 | 1.41 | 296 km/h | Very High |
