# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📋 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Define *aerodynamic efficiency* for a sports car. What is the typical range of this parameter for a high-performance sports car compared to a Formula 1 car?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Aerodynamic efficiency $E_a$ is the ratio of the magnitude of the lift coefficient to the drag coefficient:

$$
E_a = \frac{\vert C_L \vert}{C_D}
$$

It quantifies how much downforce is produced per unit of drag penalty. For a high-performance road-going sports car (e.g., McLaren 720S), $E_a$ ranges from **1 : 1 to 1 : 4** (i.e., 1 to 4). For a Formula 1 car with high downforce wings, $E_a$ ranges from **1 : 2 to 1 : 3** — *lower* than a wing-dominated sports car because F1 wings are highly induced-drag-loaded, but the absolute downforce is far higher.

> **Mark allocation:** Definition 1 mark, formula 1 mark, ranges 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Explain the term *ground effect* as applied to sports car aerodynamics. Why is it preferred over a rear wing for high-speed cornering?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Ground effect is the phenomenon in which a flat or contoured underbody, combined with a diffuser, accelerates the airflow beneath the car, creating a region of **low static pressure** by Bernoulli's principle. This pressure deficit *sucks* the car toward the road, generating downforce without exposing a wing to the free stream.

It is preferred over a rear wing for high-speed cornering because:
1. It generates downforce with **minimal drag penalty** (high $E_a$).
2. It acts **distributed across the entire underbody**, avoiding large pitch moments that an isolated rear wing would impose.
3. It is less susceptible to stall at high yaw angles (important in transient cornering).

> **Mark allocation:** Definition 1 mark, mechanism 1 mark, advantages 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 📋 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Module 4]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the various aerodynamic add-on devices used on a modern sports car for downforce generation. Explain how each contributes to overall aerodynamic performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sports car travels at $V = 280$ km/h through air at $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. The car has frontal area $A = 2.1$ m², drag coefficient $C_D = 0.34$, and lift coefficient $C_L = -0.42$. The car's wheelbase is 2.65 m, and the centre of pressure of the wing is 1.40 m behind the centre of gravity. Calculate:
1. Drag force $F_D$.
2. Total downforce $F_L^{\downarrow}$.
3. Pitching moment about the centre of gravity due to the wing-generated downforce only.
4. Aerodynamic efficiency $E_a$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

> **Marking scheme:** List of devices with description — 4 marks. Diagram — 1 mark. Synthesis / overall performance integration — 2 marks.

**Devices used on a modern sports car for downforce generation:**

1. **Front Splitter** — A horizontal blade at the front bumper edge that creates a *pressure barrier* between high-pressure air above and low-pressure air below. It produces ~5–10 % of total downforce with negligible drag penalty.

2. **Flat Underbody with Diffuser** — A smooth floor that accelerates flow underneath (Venturi effect) and a gradually expanding rear section that recovers pressure. **This is the dominant downforce source** on modern cars (McLaren, Koenigsegg, Bugatti). Downforce coefficient from ground effect can reach −1.0 or lower.

3. **Rear Wing** — A conventional or asymmetric airfoil operated at positive angle of attack to produce *negative lift on the car*. Modern sports cars use active (motorised) rear wings (e.g., McLaren P1, Porsche 911 Turbo S). They contribute 30–50 % of total downforce but cost 0.05–0.20 in $C_D$.

4. **Vortex Generators** — Small fins on the roof or boot-lid that energise the boundary layer, delaying separation. Used on Corvette Z06, Ferrari 488.

5. **Side Sill Extensions / Dive Planes** — Small winglets ahead of the front wheels that manage the front wheel-arch vortex and add incremental front downforce.

6. **Louvres / Bonnet Vents** — Extract hot air from the engine bay and manage boundary-layer behaviour over the rear deck.

**Synthesis:** Effective sports-car design *integrates* all six — splitter and front downforce for balanced grip, underbody+diffuser for the bulk of downforce, rear wing as a tunable supplement, vortex generators and dive planes for stability, bonnet vents for thermal management.

**Diagram (to be drawn by student):**

```
              ↑ Rear Wing (inverted airfoil)
        ┌─────┴─────┐
        │           │
        │   Body    │  ← Bonnet vents / louvres
        │           │
   ─────┴───────────┴─────  ← Front splitter
        Vortex   Vortex
       Generators Generators
   ═══════════════════════════ ← Ground / Road
   ▒▒▒▒▒▒▒ Underbody ▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒
        ◄─── Diffuser ────►
```

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

> **Marking scheme:** Each sub-calculation 1.5 marks; final answer boxed — 0.5 marks.

**Given:**
- $V = 280$ km/h $= 280/3.6 = 77.78$ m/s
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $A = 2.1$ m², $C_D = 0.34$, $C_L = -0.42$
- Lever arm $= 1.40$ m

**Step 1 — Dynamic Pressure:**
$$
q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (77.78)^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 6049.8 = 3705 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Drag Force:**
$$
F_D = q\, C_D\, A = 3705 \times 0.34 \times 2.1 = 2646 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Downforce:**
$$
F_L^{\downarrow} = \vert q\, C_L\, A \vert = \vert 3705 \times (-0.42) \times 2.1 \vert = 3268 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Pitching Moment About CG (Wing Only):**
Assume the wing contributes the *entire* lift coefficient magnitude ($C_{L,\text{wing}} = -0.42$) at the rear centre of pressure, $1.40$ m behind the CG:

$$
M_{\text{pitch}} = F_L^{\downarrow} \times \ell = 3268 \times 1.40 = 4575 \text{ N·m (nose-up)}
$$

**Step 5 — Aerodynamic Efficiency:**
$$
E_a = \frac{\vert C_L \vert}{C_D} = \frac{0.42}{0.34} = 1.235
$$

> **Final Answer Summary:**
> $F_D = 2646$ N, $F_L^{\downarrow} = 3268$ N, $M_{\text{pitch}} = 4575$ N·m (nose-up), $E_a = 1.24$.

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Sign of $C_L$**: Students often write $F_L$ as positive when it should be reported as a magnitude (downforce). Use $\vert C_L \vert$ or write explicitly *"downforce = 3268 N"*.
> 2. **Unit conversion of km/h**: Forgetting to divide by 3.6 is the single most common error. Always state the converted value in m/s **before** any calculation.
> 3. **Pitching moment sign convention**: Define positive (nose-up vs. nose-down) at the start of the answer.
> 4. **Forgetting lever arm**: Students often forget to multiply downforce by lever arm to get moment — this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.

---

### ⭐ Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Module 4]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for aerodynamic drag force and lift force on a sports car from first principles. State clearly the assumptions and define each term in the final equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rear wing on a sports car has planform area $S_w = 1.2$ m², span $b = 1.8$ m, and is operated at angle of attack $\alpha = 12°$. The Oswald efficiency factor is $e = 0.92$. Using thin-airfoil theory and assuming incompressible flow, calculate:
1. The lift-curve slope $C_{L,\alpha}$ (per radian).
2. The lift coefficient of the wing $C_{L,\text{wing}}$.
3. The aspect ratio of the wing $AR$.
4. The induced drag coefficient $C_{D,i}$.
5. The downforce at $V = 220$ km/h. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Starting from the integral form of pressure and shear on the body surface:**

$$
F_D = \oint_S \left( p\, n_x + \tau_w\, t_x \right) dS
$$

**Apply Reynolds Transport Theorem to a control volume around the vehicle:**

$$
\sum F_x = \int_{CS} \rho\, u\, (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n})\, dA
$$

**Assume:**
1. Steady, incompressible flow.
2. Far-field uniform velocity $V_{\infty}$.
3. Wake is a uniform velocity-deficit region of area $A_w$ with deficit $\Delta V$.

**Resulting drag equation:**

$$
F_D = \rho\, A_w\, V_{\infty}\, \Delta V
$$

**Non-dimensionalising** with dynamic pressure $q_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}$ and frontal area $A$:

$$
C_D = \frac{F_D}{q_{\infty} A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}\, C_D\, A
$$

**Identical procedure** along the $z$-axis gives the lift (or downforce):

$$
F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}\, C_L\, A
$$

> **Mark allocation:** Integral form 2 marks, RTT application 2 marks, non-dimensionalisation 2 marks, definitions 1 mark.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $S_w = 1.2$ m², $b = 1.8$ m, $\alpha = 12° = 0.2094$ rad, $e = 0.92$
- $V = 220$ km/h $= 61.11$ m/s
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Lift-Curve Slope (thin-airfoil theory):**
$$
C_{L,\alpha} = 2\pi \text{ rad}^{-1} = 6.283 \text{ rad}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Lift Coefficient of Wing:**
$$
C_{L,\text{wing}} = C_{L,\alpha} \times \alpha = 6.283 \times 0.2094 = 1.316
$$

**Step 3 — Aspect Ratio:**
$$
AR = \frac{b^{2}}{S_w} = \frac{(1.8)^{2}}{1.2} = \frac{3.24}{1.2} = 2.70
$$

**Step 4 — Induced Drag Coefficient:**
$$
C_{D,i} = \frac{C_L^{2}}{\pi \cdot AR \cdot e} = \frac{(1.316)^{2}}{\pi \times 2.70 \times 0.92} = \frac{1.732}{7.80} = 0.222
$$

**Step 5 — Downforce at $V = 220$ km/h:**
$$
F_L^{\downarrow} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}\, C_{L,\text{wing}}\, S_w = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (61.11)^{2} \times 1.316 \times 1.2
$$

Calculate term by term:
- $0.5 \times 1.225 = 0.6125$
- $(61.11)^{2} = 3734.4$
- $0.6125 \times 3734.4 = 2287.8$
- $1.316 \times 1.2 = 1.5792$
- $F_L^{\downarrow} = 2287.8 \times 1.5792 = 3613 \text{ N}$

> **Final Answer Summary:**
> $C_{L,\alpha} = 6.283$ rad⁻¹, $C_{L,\text{wing}} = 1.316$, $AR = 2.70$, $C_{D,i} = 0.222$, $F_L^{\downarrow} = 3613$ N (≈ 369 kgf).

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning for Question B:**
> 1. **Angle of attack in radians**: Always convert from degrees before using $2\pi\alpha$. Forgetting this yields a 1.316 vs. 0.230 — factor-of-6 error.
> 2. **Aspect ratio uses planform area**, not frontal area. A common slip.
> 3. **Thin-airfoil $2\pi$** assumes incompressible, attached, low-AOA flow. For compressible or stalled conditions, $C_{L,\alpha}$ decreases. State assumptions in the solution.
> 4. **Induced drag ≠ total drag**. The $C_{D,i}$ calculated is *only* the wing-induced part; total car drag includes body pressure drag and skin friction too.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Sports Cars (Module 4)**

### Core Conceptual Points
- ✅ Sports cars are the **only road category** that engineers *negative lift* deliberately.
- ✅ Two competing objectives: **minimise drag** (top speed), **maximise downforce** (grip).
- ✅ Aerodynamic efficiency $E_a = \vert C_L \vert / C_D$ quantifies the trade-off.

### Numerical / Formula Points
- ✅ $F_D = q\, C_D\, A$, $F_L = q\, C_L\, A$, $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}$.
- ✅ Thin-airfoil lift slope: $C_{L,\alpha} = 2\pi$ rad⁻¹.
- ✅ Induced drag: $C_{D,i} = C_L^{2} / (\pi \cdot AR \cdot e)$.
- ✅ Wing downforce: $F_L^{\downarrow} = q\, \alpha\, C_{L,\alpha}\, S_w$.
- ✅ Ground-effect pressure drop: $\Delta p = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_1^{2} - V_{\infty}^{2})$.
- ✅ Drag polar: $C_D = C_{D,0} + C_L^{2}/(\pi AR e)$.

### Physical Constants to Memorise
- ✅ $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.225$ kg/m³ (ISA sea-level).
- ✅ $V_{\text{conversion}}: 1$ km/h $= 1/3.6$ m/s.
- ✅ Standard atmosphere: $p_{\text{atm}} = 101.325$ kPa.

### Real-World Typical Values
- ✅ Road sports car $C_D$: 0.25 – 0.38.
- ✅ Road sports car $C_L$: $-0.15$ to $-0.60$.
- ✅ F1 car $C_D$: 0.75 – 1.10, $C_L$: $-1.5$ to $-3.5$.

### Design Devices to Recognise
- ✅ Front splitter, flat underbody, diffuser, rear wing, vortex generators, dive planes, bonnet vents, Kamm tail, active aero (DRS).

### Common KTU Pitfalls
- ⚠️ Sign confusion on $C_L$ (negative = downforce — always report magnitude).
- ⚠️ km/h to m/s conversion (forgetting $/3.6$).
- ⚠️ Pitching-moment lever arm ignored.
- ⚠️ $C_{D,i}$ mistaken for total drag.
- ⚠️ Treating drag and downforce as independent.

### Top Exam Phrases to Use
- *"Aerodynamic efficiency quantifies the downforce gained per unit of drag penalty."*
- *"Ground effect generates downforce with minimal induced drag."*
- *"The rear wing induces drag that scales with the square of the lift coefficient."*

---

> **End of Module 4 — Sports Cars | KTU PEAUT752 | 2024 Scheme**
> *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 (NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SUVs
topics.content:
# SUVs in Vehicle Aerodynamics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT752 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** SUVs ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Sports Utility Vehicle (SUV)** in the context of vehicle aerodynamics is defined as a light-duty, high-ground-clearance passenger vehicle characterized by a **bluff body geometry**, a **large frontal projected area (A)**, a **raised center of gravity (CG)**, and a **box-like rear geometry** that combines the load-carrying capacity of a utility vehicle with passenger comfort. From a fluid dynamics perspective, an SUV is classified as a **bluff body** — a body whose flow separation is dominated by pressure drag rather than viscous (friction) drag. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** SUVs are treated as a distinct aerodynamic category because they represent the **worst-case scenario** for passenger vehicle drag, lift, and cross-wind sensitivity due to their geometric proportions (height-to-length ratio often exceeds **0.45**, compared to ~0.30 for a sedan). **Standard SUV Geometric & Aerodynamic Metrics (Industry Benchmarks):** | Parameter | Typical Sedan | Typical SUV | Effect on Aerodynamics | |---|---|---|---| | Drag Coefficient $C_D$ | 0.25 – 0.30 | 0.35 – 0.45 | SUV ~40 % higher | | Frontal Area $A$ | 2.0 – 2.2 m² | 2.5 – 3.0 m² | SUV ~35 % larger | | Ground Clearance | 150 mm | 200 – 250 mm | SUV ~50 % higher | | Height-to-Length Ratio | 0.28 – 0.32 | 0.45 – 0.55 | SUV ~60 % higher | | Center of Gravity Height | 500 mm | 700 – 800 mm | SUV ~50 % higher | > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constant Reminder:** Standard air density at sea level is $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$. The drag equation uses this value unless altitude correction is specified. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine pushing a **flat rectangular brick** through water versus pushing a **streamlined fish** of the same length. The brick pushes a huge wall of water ahead of it, creates turbulent swirls behind it, and water spills sideways off its top and bottom — this is exactly what air does around an SUV. The fish, on the other hand, slices through the water with minimal disturbance. **In simple terms:** An SUV is the "brick" of the road. Its tall, flat front face acts like a wall hitting the oncoming air, and its truncated, vertical rear cannot "hold onto" the airflow — so the air **separates** violently, creating a large, low-pressure wake behind the vehicle. This wake effectively "sucks" the vehicle backward, which is what we call **pressure drag**. > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — The Sail Effect:** Hold a large flat board out of a car window at high speed. You will feel two things: (1) a strong **rearward push** (drag), and (2) the board wants to lift or dive unpredictably (lift/yaw). An SUV's broadside is exactly that board, just integrated into the body — which is why SUVs feel "twitchy" in crosswinds compared to sedans. ### 1.3 Why SUVs are Aerodynamically Distinct The unique aerodynamic profile of SUVs stems from three geometric realities: 1. **Large Frontal Area ($A$):** Drag is **linearly proportional** to frontal area. A 35 % larger face means 35 % more drag at identical $C_D$ and speed. 2. **High Bluff-Body Proportions:** The near-vertical front and rear of an SUV trigger **massive flow separation** at the leading and trailing edges, producing a wide, energetic wake. 3. **Raised Center of Gravity + Underbody Cavity:** The high CG amplifies **crosswind-induced yaw and roll moments**, while the open underbody allows air to swirl underneath, increasing both drag and lift. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Aerodynamic Forces and Moments on an SUV Every SUV in motion is subjected to a complete set of aerodynamic loads acting at the **center of pressure (CP)**, which is typically located near the geometric centroid of the projected side area. ### 2.1.1 Aerodynamic Drag ($F_D$) Drag is the **rearward force** opposing vehicle motion. It has two principal components: - **Pressure Drag ($F_{D,p}$):** Dominant in SUVs (≈ 55–65 % of total drag). Caused by the pressure difference between the stagnation point at the front and the low-pressure wake at the rear. - **Skin Friction Drag ($F_{D,f}$):** Caused by viscous shearing of air along the body's surface. Contributes ≈ 25–30 % in SUVs (lower % than sedans due to relatively smaller wetted area in the separated region). - **Induced Drag ($F_{D,i}$):** Caused by three-dimensional lift distribution near the vehicle's ends. Contributes ≈ 10–15 % in SUVs due to the wide body and pronounced shoulder lines. ### 2.1.2 Aerodynamic Lift ($F_L$) SUVs generate **positive lift** (upward force) because the curved or flat upper surface combined with the bluff rear geometry accelerates flow over the roof, lowering local pressure (Bernoulli effect). Positive lift is **undesirable** because it: - Reduces front-tire grip (understeer tendency) - Reduces steering precision at high speed - Increases rollover risk during evasive maneuvers ### 2.1.3 Side Force ($F_S$) Generated when the wind is not perfectly head-on. Acts perpendicular to the vehicle's longitudinal axis. In SUVs, the **large side projected area** makes them especially sensitive to side forces, which can cause **sidewind-induced drift**. ### 2.1.4 Aerodynamic Moments ($M_X, M_Y, M_Z$) - **Yaw Moment ($M_Z$):** Tendency to rotate the vehicle about a vertical axis. **Critical for SUVs** — the high CG and bluff rear make yaw moments strong during crosswind. - **Roll Moment ($M_X$):** Tendency to tip the vehicle sideways. Amplified by the high CG. - **Pitch Moment ($M_Y$):** Tendency to rotate nose-up or nose-down. Less critical than yaw for SUVs. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic: Why SUVs Suffer Aerodynamically **Step 1 — The Stagnation Problem:** The near-vertical front face of an SUV causes the oncoming air to come to a near-complete stop at the front grille area. This creates a high-pressure zone (the **stagnation point**), where dynamic pressure is fully converted to static pressure: $$p_{stag} = p_\infty + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2$$ **Step 2 — The Underbody and Wheel Wake Problem:** The high ground clearance and exposed wheels create **multiple discrete wakes**: - The four wheels each shed turbulent wakes (wheel drag can account for **25–30 %** of total drag on an SUV). - The underbody cavity allows air to enter, swirl, and exit chaotically, contributing to drag and lift. **Step 3 — The Roof Flow Acceleration Problem:** The roof curvature (or lack thereof) on an SUV often causes the boundary layer to remain attached for too short a distance, after which it **separates** at the rear edge. This generates a **large, low-pressure recirculation zone** behind the vehicle. **Step 4 — The Rear Separation Problem:** The vertical or near-vertical rear face of an SUV causes **abrupt flow separation** along the entire upper trailing edge and at the rear window-glass interface. This produces a wide, turbulent wake — the primary source of pressure drag. **Step 5 — The Net Result:** The combination of high stagnation pressure at the front, low pressure at the rear, turbulent underbody flow, and wheel wakes produces a net rearward force (drag) and a net upward force (lift) that is significantly greater than that of a sedan. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Memorize these equations verbatim. Every numerical problem on SUVs in the KTU ESE will derive from one of these. | # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | KTU Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_D A$ | $\rho$ = kg/m³, $V$ = m/s, $A$ = m², $C_D$ = – | Compute drag force | | 2 | Aerodynamic Lift | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_L A$ | $C_L$ = lift coefficient (–) | Compute lift force | | 3 | Drag Power Required | $P_D = F_D \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^3 C_D A$ | $P_D$ = W | Compute power loss | | 4 | Frontal Area (Rectangle) | $A = W \times H$ | $W$ = width, $H$ = height (m) | SUV geometry | | 5 | Reynolds Number (Vehicle Scale) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | $L$ = length, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity | Flow regime check | | 6 | Specific Resistance | $r = \dfrac{C_D A}{m}$ | $m$ = mass (kg) | Compare vehicles | | 7 | Yaw Sensitivity (Empirical) | $\beta_{crit} \approx \dfrac{C_{S\beta}}{C_{D}} \cdot \dfrac{h}{l}$ | $h$ = CG height, $l$ = wheelbase | Crosswind stability | | 8 | Drag Reduction % | $\Delta C_D \% = \dfrac{C_{D,old} - C_{D,new}}{C_{D,old}} \times 100$ | – | Quantify mods | | 9 | Effective Drag Area | $f_A = C_D \cdot A$ (m²) | – | Single-number comparison | | 10 | Side Force | $F_S = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_S A_S$ | $A_S$ = side area (m²) | Crosswind load | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Alert:** $V$ must always be in **m/s** in the standard drag equation. KTU questions frequently give $V$ in km/hr — convert using $V_{\text{m/s}} = V_{\text{km/h}} / 3.6$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The aerodynamic study of SUVs is not merely academic — it directly impacts: - **Fuel Economy & Emissions:** At 100 km/h, ~60–70 % of an SUV's fuel energy is consumed overcoming drag. A **0.05 reduction in $C_D$** saves roughly **3–5 %** in fuel consumption. - **Electric Vehicle (EV) Range:** For electric SUVs, every watt-hour saved on drag directly extends driving range. A 10 % drag reduction can yield a **5–7 % range improvement**. - **High-Speed Stability:** SUV rollover incidents are strongly correlated with aerodynamic lift and crosswind sensitivity — making the study of $C_L$ and yaw moments a **safety-critical** engineering concern. - **NVH Engineering (Wind Noise):** The A-pillar, side mirror, and roof-rail regions of an SUV are major sources of wind noise; their design is governed by aerodynamic principles. - **Active Aerodynamics:** Modern SUVs use grille shutters, active rear spoilers, and air curtains to dynamically reduce $C_D$ based on speed and cooling demand. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Drag Equation from First Principles The drag force on a vehicle is derived by integrating the **pressure distribution** and the **viscous shear stress** over the entire body surface. We begin with the integral form of the momentum equation for a control volume enclosing the vehicle. **Starting Point — The Momentum Flux Balance:** For a steady, incompressible flow around a body, the net force on the body equals the rate of change of momentum of the fluid: $$F_{aero} = \int_{CS} \rho \vec{V}(\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$ **Step 1 — Decompose into Pressure and Viscous Components:** By the Cauchy stress theorem, the force on the body is: $$F_{aero} = -\oint_{CS} (p \hat{n}) \, dA + \oint_{CS} (\tau \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$ The first integral gives the **pressure force** (normal to the surface), and the second gives the **viscous force** (tangential to the surface). For a bluff body like an SUV, the pressure term dominates. **Step 2 — Non-Dimensionalize Using Dynamic Pressure:** To express drag in the form used in vehicle aerodynamics, we divide the dimensional force by the **freestream dynamic pressure** $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ and the **reference area** $A$ (frontal area for ground vehicles): $$C_D = \frac{F_D}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for Drag Force:** Rearranging the coefficient definition: $$F_D = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_D A$$ This is the **fundamental drag equation** that every KTU numerical problem on SUVs will use. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** Drag scales with the **square of velocity**. Doubling speed quadruples drag. This is why SUVs suffer dramatically at highway speeds — a sedan at 100 km/h and an SUV with the same $C_D \cdot A$ product at 200 km/h experience 4× the drag force. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Drag Power and the "Cube Law" The power required to overcome aerodynamic drag is a critical quantity for fuel/energy consumption calculations. **Step 1 — Power is Force times Velocity:** $$P_D = F_D \cdot V$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the Drag Equation:** $$P_D = \left( \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_D A \right) \cdot V$$ **Step 3 — Combine the Velocity Terms:** $$P_D = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^3 C_D A$$ This is the **aerodynamic drag power** equation. The **cubic dependence on velocity** is the single most important practical result in vehicle aerodynamics. **Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):** > **Problem (KTU 2024 Style):** An SUV has a frontal area of $A = 2.7 \; \text{m}^2$ and a drag coefficient of $C_D = 0.40$. The vehicle cruises at $V = 108 \; \text{km/h}$. The air density is $\rho = 1.2 \; \text{kg/m}^3$. Calculate (a) the drag force, and (b) the drag power. **Solution:** **(a) Drag Force:** Convert velocity: $$V = \frac{108}{3.6} = 30 \; \text{m/s}$$ Substitute: $$F_D = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.2 \times (30)^2 \times 0.40 \times 2.7$$ Step by step: - $(30)^2 = 900$ - $\frac{1}{2} \times 1.2 = 0.6$ - $0.6 \times 900 = 540$ - $540 \times 0.40 = 216$ - $216 \times 2.7 = 583.2$ $$F_D = 583.2 \; \text{N}$$ **(b) Drag Power:** $$P_D = F_D \times V = 583.2 \times 30$$ $$P_D = 17496 \; \text{W} \approx 17.5 \; \text{kW}$$ > [!TIP] > **Comparison Check:** A typical sedan with $A = 2.0 \; \text{m}^2$ and $C_D = 0.28$ at the same speed would have $F_D = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 900 \times 0.28 \times 2.0 = 302.4 \; \text{N}$ — about **half** the drag. This demonstrates the SUV's aerodynamic penalty quantitatively. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Effective Drag Area Concept To fairly compare vehicles of different sizes, engineers use the **effective drag area** (also called the **drag area** or **$f_A$**): **Step 1 — Start with the Drag Force:** $$F_D = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_D A$$ **Step 2 — Group the Geometry:** Define: $$f_A = C_D \cdot A$$ This single number (with units of m²) represents the **aerodynamic "size"** of a vehicle independent of velocity and air density. **Step 3 — The Simplified Drag Equation:** $$F_D = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 f_A$$ **Step 4 — Practical Comparison Table:** | Vehicle | $C_D$ | $A$ (m²) | $f_A$ (m²) | Relative Drag | |---|---|---|---|---| | Sedan (Toyota Camry) | 0.28 | 2.10 | 0.59 | 1.00× (reference) | | Hatchback (VW Golf) | 0.31 | 2.05 | 0.64 | 1.08× | | Crossover SUV (Honda CR-V) | 0.36 | 2.70 | 0.97 | 1.64× | | Full-size SUV (Range Rover) | 0.40 | 2.85 | 1.14 | 1.93× | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** The Range Rover has **nearly double** the aerodynamic "size" of a Camry, even though both seat five passengers. This is the central design challenge SUV manufacturers face. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Why Each SUV Geometric Feature Hurts Aerodynamics | Geometric Feature | Effect on $C_D$ | Effect on $C_L$ | Effect on Crosswind | Engineering Mitigation | |---|---|---|---|---| | High ground clearance | ↑↑ (underbody flow) | ↑ (air underneath) | Minimal | Underbody fairings / shields | | Vertical front face | ↑↑ (stagnation area) | Neutral | Minimal | Sloped hood, grille shutters | | Boxy roofline | ↑ (large wake) | ↓ (slight — pushes down) | ↑ (sail effect) | Roof spoiler, tapered roof | | Vertical rear | ↑↑ (early separation) | Neutral | ↑↑ (rear yaw) | Rear spoiler, boat-tail | | Large side mirrors | ↑ (mirror drag) | ↑ (small) | ↑ (side force) | Camera-based mirrors | | Exposed wheels | ↑↑ (wheel drag) | ↑ (small) | Minimal | Wheel covers, flat rims | | Roof rails | ↑ (turbulence) | Neutral | ↑ (side drag) | Flush / retractable rails | | Flat windshield | ↓ (attached flow) | Neutral | ↑ (vortex shedding) | Curved / raked windshield | | Stepped underbody | ↑↑↑ (chaotic) | ↑ (air cushion) | Minimal | Full underbody panel | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often assume a "taller" vehicle has lower $C_D$ because of a more "streamlined" shape. **The opposite is true** for SUVs. Bluff-body geometry always increases $C_D$ regardless of height. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: SUV Aerodynamic Flow Regions The following diagram maps the **interactions between flow regions** around a typical SUV, identifying the dominant physics in each zone and the resulting design challenge. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Oncoming Air Stream
Freestream Velocity V_infinity] --> B[Front Stagnation Zone
High Pressure Region
p_stag = p_inf + 0.5 rho V^2] B --> C[Front Grille and Bumper
Cooling Air Intake Region
Engine Heat Exchange] B --> D[Hood and Windshield Region
Attached Flow Zone
Boundary Layer Growth] D --> E[Roof and A-Pillar Region
Flow Acceleration Zone
Local Pressure Drop] E --> F[Rear Window and Roof Edge
Primary Separation Zone
Shear Layer Formation] F --> G[Recirculation Wake Zone
Low Pressure Region
Primary Pressure Drag Source] B --> H[Front Wheel Wells
Wheel Wake Zone
Vortex Shedding] H --> I[Underbody Cavity
Chaotic Turbulent Flow
Lift and Drag Contributor] I --> G H --> J[Rear Wheel Wells
Secondary Wheel Wake
Amplified by Front Wake] J --> G F --> K[Crosswind Interaction Layer
Side Force Generation
Yaw Moment Inducer] K --> G G --> L[Aerodynamic Drag Force F_D
Vector Acts at Center of Pressure
Opposes Vehicle Motion] G --> M[Aerodynamic Lift Force F_L
Vector Acts at Center of Pressure
Reduces Tire Normal Load] K --> N[Side Force F_S and Yaw Moment M_Z
Critical for Crosswind Stability] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: SUV Drag Reduction Strategy Hierarchy The following block diagram presents the **cascading decision logic** used by aerodynamicists when reducing SUV drag, from passive geometric design to active systems. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[SUV Drag Reduction Strategy] --> B[Level 1 Passive Geometry] A --> C[Level 2 Underbody Management] A --> D[Level 3 Local Surface Treatment] A --> E[Level 4 Active Aerodynamics] B --> B1[Slope Hood Angle 8 to 12 deg] B --> B2[Rake Windshield 28 to 32 deg] B --> B3[Roof Taper and Boat Tail] B --> B4[Side Glass Curvature] C --> C1[Full Underbody Panel] C --> C2[Engine Bay Lower Cover] C --> C3[Fuel Tank and Rear Axle Shields] C --> C4[Diffuser at Rear Underbody] D --> D1[A-Pillar Fillet] D --> D2[C-Pillar Boat Tail] D --> D3[Wheel Arch Liners] D --> D4[Flush Door Handles] D --> D5[Side Mirror Design] E --> E1[Active Grille Shutters] E --> E2[Active Rear Spoiler] E --> E3[Air Curtain at Front] E --> E4[Active Suspension Leveling] B1 --> F[Target Delta C_D 0.04 to 0.06] B2 --> F B3 --> F B4 --> F C1 --> G[Target Delta C_D 0.03 to 0.05] C2 --> G C3 --> G C4 --> G D1 --> H[Target Delta C_D 0.01 to 0.03] D2 --> H D3 --> H D4 --> H D5 --> H E1 --> I[Target Delta C_D 0.01 to 0.02] E2 --> I E3 --> I E4 --> I F --> J[Final C_D Target 0.30 to 0.34] G --> J H --> J I --> J ``` ## 4.3 Flow Diagram: SUV Drag Force Computation Pipeline This diagram shows the **complete analytical pipeline** for computing aerodynamic drag on an SUV — the exact sequence a KTU examiner follows in a numerical problem. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Step 1 Input Vehicle Geometry] --> A1[Width W meters] A --> A2[Height H meters] A --> A3[Frontal Area A equals W times H square meters] B[Step 2 Input Aerodynamic Coefficient] --> B1[Drag Coefficient C_D] C[Step 3 Input Operating Conditions] --> C1[Velocity V kilometers per hour] C --> C2[Convert V to meters per second V divided by 3.6] C --> C3[Air Density rho 1.225 kg per cubic meter] D[Step 4 Assemble Equation] --> D1[F_D equals 0.5 times rho times V squared times C_D times A] E[Step 5 Evaluate] --> E1[Compute V squared] E --> E2[Multiply by rho and C_D and A] E --> E3[Multiply by 0.5] F[Step 6 Output Drag Force in Newtons] --> F1[Optional Compute Power P_D equals F_D times V in Watts] F --> F2[Optional Compute Energy per km equals P_D times 1000 divided by V in Joules] ``` ## 4.4 Schematic Block Diagram: SUV Crosswind Instability Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Crosswind Gust Velocity V_cross] --> B[SUV Side Projected Area A_S] B --> C[Side Force F_S equals 0.5 rho V_cross squared C_S A_S] C --> D[Force Acts at Center of Pressure CP Height] D --> E[Yaw Moment M_Z equals F_S times CP Height minus CG Height] E --> F[High CG in SUV amplifies M_Z] F --> G[Yaw Instability and Steering Correction Required] G --> H[Vehicle Drifts Sideways or Rotates] A --> I[Pressure Differential Across Vehicle Body] I --> J[Asymmetric Pressure Distribution] J --> K[Vortex Shedding at A-Pillar and C-Pillar] K --> G ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **KTU Direct Short-Answer Format:** Two sentences plus a formula where applicable. No long derivations. ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Explain why the drag coefficient of a typical SUV is higher than that of a typical sedan, even when both vehicles have comparable wheelbases.** **Model Answer:** An SUV has a higher drag coefficient ($C_D \approx 0.35$–$0.45$) than a sedan ($C_D \approx 0.25$–$0.30$) due to its **bluff-body geometry**. The near-vertical front and rear faces, high ground clearance, and large frontal area promote **early flow separation** and generate a **wide, low-pressure wake** behind the vehicle. This wake increases the **pressure differential** between the front (high pressure) and rear (low pressure), which is the dominant component of pressure drag. Additionally, the exposed wheels and open underbody of an SUV contribute significant **wheel drag** and **underbody turbulence**, further raising $C_D$. ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define "effective drag area" and state its significance in comparing SUVs with other vehicle categories.** **Model Answer:** The **effective drag area** ($f_A$) is defined as the product of the drag coefficient and the frontal projected area: $$f_A = C_D \cdot A \;\; (\text{m}^2)$$ It is significant because it collapses two vehicle-specific parameters into a **single, scale-independent metric** of aerodynamic "size," allowing fair comparison between vehicles of different physical dimensions. A Range Rover SUV with $f_A = 1.14 \; \text{m}^2$ has nearly **double** the aerodynamic penalty of a Camry sedan with $f_A = 0.59 \; \text{m}^2$, even though both seat five passengers — quantifying the SUV's aerodynamic disadvantage. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Full derivations, diagrams, and numerical evaluations expected. ### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]** **A crossover SUV has the following specifications:** - Width $W = 1.85 \; \text{m}$, Height $H = 1.70 \; \text{m}$ - Drag coefficient $C_D = 0.38$ - Lift coefficient $C_L = 0.25$ (positive = upward) - Cruise speed $V = 90 \; \text{km/h}$ - Air density $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Calculate the aerodynamic drag force, the drag power, and the percentage of a 100 kW engine output consumed by drag at this speed.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute the Frontal Area:** $$A = W \times H = 1.85 \times 1.70 = 3.145 \; \text{m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Convert Velocity:** $$V = \frac{90}{3.6} = 25 \; \text{m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the Drag Force:** $$F_D = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_D A$$ Substituting: $$F_D = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (25)^2 \times 0.38 \times 3.145$$ Step by step: - $(25)^2 = 625$ - $0.5 \times 1.225 = 0.6125$ - $0.6125 \times 625 = 382.8125$ - $382.8125 \times 0.38 = 145.469$ - $145.469 \times 3.145 = 457.5$ $$\boxed{F_D \approx 457.5 \; \text{N}}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the Drag Power:** $$P_D = F_D \times V = 457.5 \times 25$$ $$\boxed{P_D = 11437.5 \; \text{W} \approx 11.44 \; \text{kW}}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the % Engine Output Consumed:** $$\% = \frac{11.44}{100} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\% = 11.44 \; \%}$$ > **Interpretation:** At 90 km/h, **over 11 %** of the engine's output is consumed solely to overcome air resistance — and this scales with $V^3$, so at 150 km/h it would be roughly **3.7× higher** (~42 %). #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **The manufacturer introduces (i) a full underbody panel that reduces $C_D$ to 0.34, and (ii) a rear roof spoiler that reduces $C_L$ to 0.15. Calculate the new lift force, the new drag force, and the percentage reduction in both compared to the original values. Comment on the engineering significance.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — New Frontal Area and Velocity (Unchanged):** $$A = 3.145 \; \text{m}^2, \quad V = 25 \; \text{m/s}, \quad \rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$$ **Step 2 — New Drag Force:** $$F_{D,new} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 625 \times 0.34 \times 3.145$$ Step by step: - $0.5 \times 1.225 \times 625 = 382.8125$ (same as before) - $382.8125 \times 0.34 = 130.156$ - $130.156 \times 3.145 = 409.34$ $$\boxed{F_{D,new} \approx 409.3 \; \text{N}}$$ **Step 3 — New Lift Force:** $$F_{L,new} = 0.5 \rho V^2 C_{L,new} A$$ $$F_{L,new} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 625 \times 0.15 \times 3.145$$ Step by step: - $382.8125 \times 0.15 = 57.422$ - $57.422 \times 3.145 = 180.6$ $$\boxed{F_{L,new} \approx 180.6 \; \text{N}}$$ **Step 4 — Percentage Reduction in Drag:** $$\Delta C_D\% = \frac{457.5 - 409.3}{457.5} \times 100 = \frac{48.2}{457.5} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\Delta F_D \approx 10.5 \; \%}$$ **Step 5 — Percentage Reduction in Lift:** $$\Delta F_L\% = \frac{0.5 \times 1.225 \times 625 \times 0.25 \times 3.145 - 180.6}{0.5 \times 1.225 \times 625 \times 0.25 \times 3.145} \times 100$$ Original lift: $$F_{L,orig} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 625 \times 0.25 \times 3.145 = 301.0 \; \text{N}$$ $$\Delta F_L\% = \frac{301.0 - 180.6}{301.0} \times 100 = \frac{120.4}{301.0} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\Delta F_L \approx 40 \; \%}$$ **Engineering Significance:** The underbody panel produces a modest **10.5 % drag reduction**, but the rear spoiler delivers a **40 % lift reduction** — a much larger proportional safety benefit. This illustrates a key principle in SUV aerodynamics: **lift reduction often yields greater safety gains per unit $C_D$ change** than drag reduction, because positive lift directly degrades tire grip and increases rollover risk. Hence, SUV manufacturers prioritize **lift-reducing features** (rear spoilers, diffusers) alongside drag-reducing features (underbody panels, grille shutters). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A Part (b):** > Students often forget to recompute the **dynamic pressure** step and simply multiply the new coefficient by the old force. This is **wrong** because the formula is multiplicative, not proportional. Always recompute from the equation: $F_{new} = 0.5 \rho V^2 C_{D,new} A$. A second common error is forgetting the **conversion of V from km/h to m/s** in part (a). Marks are deducted 2 per sub-step missed. --- ### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **(a) — 7 Marks:** With the aid of a clearly labeled schematic, describe the **principal flow regions** around a moving SUV and identify the **physical origin** of drag in each region. Discuss why the **wake region** is the dominant contributor to pressure drag. **(b) — 7 Marks:** A full-size SUV has a frontal area of $2.9 \; \text{m}^2$, $C_D = 0.42$, $C_L = 0.22$, and operates at $V = 120 \; \text{km/h}$ at sea level ($\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$). Compute the drag force, lift force, and the power consumed by drag. If a **boat-tail rear extension** reduces the effective $C_D$ by **15 %** while leaving $C_L$ unchanged, compute the new drag force and the fuel saving in watts assuming the engine delivers 25 % of its 90 kW rating to overcome drag originally. #### Part (a) — 7 Marks — Model Solution **The principal flow regions around an SUV are:** 1. **Stagnation Region (Front):** Located at the grille and front bumper. Air decelerates from freestream velocity to nearly zero, converting dynamic pressure to static pressure. This is a **high-pressure zone**. Drag contribution: minor direct contribution, but it sets up the pressure differential that drives pressure drag at the rear. 2. **Front Hood and Windshield Region:** Attached (laminar or mildly turbulent) flow with gradual boundary layer growth. Contributes mostly **skin friction drag** and sets the boundary layer thickness that will determine where separation occurs at the rear. 3. **Roof and A-Pillar Region:** Flow accelerates over the curved roof due to the **Bernoulli effect**, lowering local static pressure. The A-pillar creates a small **horseshoe vortex** at its base that contributes to local drag. 4. **Front Wheel Wells:** Each wheel acts as a small bluff body in its own right, shedding turbulent wakes. Wheel drag is typically **25–30 %** of total SUV drag. 5. **Underbody Cavity:** The high ground clearance creates an open cavity. Air enters from the front, swirls chaotically, and exits at the rear — contributing to both **lift and drag**. 6. **Rear Window and Roof Trailing Edge:** The boundary layer separates at the **upper rear edge** due to the adverse pressure gradient. A free shear layer forms, bounding the recirculation zone. 7. **Recirculation Wake (Rear):** A large, low-pressure, turbulent region behind the vehicle. This is the **dominant source of pressure drag** in SUVs. **Why the Wake Dominates Pressure Drag:** The wake is dominant because: - The bluff rear geometry causes **abrupt flow separation** along the entire upper trailing edge and at the rear vertical face. - The wake's low pressure (relative to the front stagnation pressure) creates a **large net pressure imbalance** acting on the vehicle. - The wake's width scales with the vehicle's **height and width**, not its length — making tall, wide SUVs especially penalized. - Energy is continuously **fed into turbulent eddies** in the wake, which is the mechanism by which pressure drag performs work on the fluid. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks — Model Solution **Step 1 — Convert Velocity:** $$V = \frac{120}{3.6} = 33.33 \; \text{m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Drag Force:** $$F_D = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (33.33)^2 \times 0.42 \times 2.9$$ Step by step: - $(33.33)^2 = 1110.89$ - $0.5 \times 1.225 = 0.6125$ - $0.6125 \times 1110.89 = 680.42$ - $680.42 \times 0.42 = 285.78$ - $285.78 \times 2.9 = 828.76$ $$\boxed{F_D \approx 828.8 \; \text{N}}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Lift Force:** $$F_L = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1110.89 \times 0.22 \times 2.9$$ Step by step: - $680.42 \times 0.22 = 149.69$ - $149.69 \times 2.9 = 434.1$ $$\boxed{F_L \approx 434.1 \; \text{N}}$$ **Step 4 — Compute Drag Power:** $$P_D = F_D \times V = 828.8 \times 33.33$$ $$\boxed{P_D \approx 27619 \; \text{W} \approx 27.62 \; \text{kW}}$$ **Step 5 — Original Engine Fraction to Drag:** $$P_{drag,orig} = 0.25 \times 90000 = 22500 \; \text{W}$$ (Note: this is the given assumption for engine power used to overcome drag.) **Step 6 — New Drag Coefficient:** $$C_{D,new} = 0.42 \times (1 - 0.15) = 0.42 \times 0.85 = 0.357$$ **Step 7 — New Drag Force:** $$F_{D,new} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1110.89 \times 0.357 \times 2.9$$ Step by step: - $680.42 \times 0.357 = 242.91$ - $242.91 \times 2.9 = 704.4$ $$\boxed{F_{D,new} \approx 704.4 \; \text{N}}$$ **Step 8 — New Drag Power and Fuel Saving:** $$P_{D,new} = 704.4 \times 33.33 = 23478 \; \text{W} \approx 23.48 \; \text{kW}$$ $$\text{Fuel Saving} = P_{D,orig} - P_{D,new}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Fuel Saving} \approx 22500 - 23478 = -978 \; \text{W}}$$ > **Note on Negative Result:** The 15 % $C_D$ reduction yields $P_{D,new} \approx 23.48 \; \text{kW}$, which is **higher** than the assumed engine fraction of 22.5 kW. This indicates that the assumed engine power fraction was already less than the actual aerodynamic power. The **true saving** based on actual computed powers is: > > $$\text{True Saving} = 27619 - 23478 = 4141 \; \text{W} \approx 4.14 \; \text{kW}$$ > > This corresponds to a **15 % reduction in aerodynamic power consumption** — confirming the proportional benefit of the boat-tail design. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B Part (b):** > Two critical mistakes to avoid: > > 1. **Velocity Conversion Error:** Forgetting to convert 120 km/h to 33.33 m/s is the **#1 most common error** in KTU SUV numericals. Marks lost: 2–3. > 2. **The 15 % Interpretation:** The problem states the boat-tail "reduces effective $C_D$ by 15 %." This means the **new $C_D$ is 85 % of the original**, not that you subtract 15 from 0.42. Many students incorrectly compute $C_{D,new} = 0.27$, which gives a drastically wrong answer. Marks lost: 3–4. > 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing kW and W in the final answer. Always express final answers in clean SI units. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 4 — SUVs: Rapid Revision Checklist** ### 🔑 Core Concepts - An **SUV is a bluff body** — its flow physics is dominated by **pressure drag**, not skin friction. - SUV drag coefficients are **35–50 % higher** than comparable sedans due to geometric bluffness. - SUV frontal areas are **30–40 % larger** than comparable sedans, multiplying the drag. - The **recirculation wake** behind the SUV is the single largest contributor to pressure drag. - **Wheel drag** alone accounts for **25–30 %** of an SUV's total drag — a unique characteristic of high-clearance vehicles. ### 🔑 Key Numerical Values to Memorize - Standard sea-level air density: $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ - Typical SUV $C_D$ range: **0.35 – 0.45** - Typical SUV frontal area: **2.5 – 3.0 m²** - Velocity conversion: $V_{\text{m/s}} = V_{\text{km/h}} / 3.6$ - Cube law: $P_D \propto V^3$ (doubling speed → 8× power) - Square law: $F_D \propto V^2$ (doubling speed → 4× force) ### 🔑 Essential Formulas (Memorize Verbatim) - $F_D = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_D A$ - $F_L = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_L A$ - $P_D = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^3 C_D A$ - $f_A = C_D \cdot A$ (effective drag area) - $\Delta \% = \frac{\text{Old} - \text{New}}{\text{Old}} \times 100$ ### 🔑 Engineering Insights - A **0.05 reduction in $C_D$** saves ~3–5 % fuel at highway speeds. - A **rear spoiler** reduces lift by ~40 % but only drag by ~5 % — a key trade-off. - **Underbody panels** are the highest-leverage passive drag-reduction feature for SUVs. - **Lift reduction > Drag reduction** in safety impact (lift affects rollover risk). - **Crosswind sensitivity** scales with the ratio of CG height to wheelbase — SUVs are inherently more sensitive. ### 🔑 Common Exam Traps - ❌ Treating $C_D$ as a constant across vehicle categories (it varies by 60 %+). - ❌ Forgetting velocity unit conversion (km/h vs m/s). - ❌ Misinterpreting "% reduction" as subtraction rather than multiplication. - ❌ Ignoring the underbody contribution to total drag in qualitative answers. - ❌ Confusing lift and drag force directions in diagram-based questions. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Strategy:** If a 14-mark question on SUVs is asked, the examiner expects: (1) a labeled flow-region diagram (4 marks), (2) the drag equation with definitions (3 marks), (3) a complete numerical evaluation (5 marks), and (4) a brief engineering comment on the result (2 marks). Practice the velocity conversion until it becomes automatic — it is the single most common point of error in KTU exam scripts.
